topics.title: Introduction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEET721 - DESIGN OF SOLAR PV SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Solar Photovoltaic (PV) System?

A **Solar Photovoltaic (PV) System** is a complete, integrated assembly of engineered components designed to convert sunlight directly into electricity using the **photovoltaic effect** — a quantum-mechanical phenomenon in which photons striking a semiconductor material (typically crystalline silicon) liberate electrons, generating a usable direct-current (DC) voltage at the cell terminals.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):**
> A Solar PV system is an electricity-generating system that uses *semiconductor-based solar cells* to convert solar radiation into DC electrical power, which is then conditioned (via inverters, charge controllers, and balance-of-system components) to meet the load requirements of standalone, grid-tied, or hybrid applications.

The governing physics rests on the **photovoltaic effect**, first observed by French physicist **Edmond Becquerel in 1839** and physically explained by **Albert Einstein in 1905** using the quantum theory of light.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Leaf That Pays You"

Imagine a real green leaf. It absorbs sunlight and, through photosynthesis, converts solar energy into chemical energy (sugar) — its "food." A **solar PV cell does the exact same thing**, except instead of sugar, it produces **electricity**, and instead of chlorophyll, it uses a **silicon wafer** as the light-absorbing material. The leaf "pays" the plant in glucose; the solar cell "pays" you in kilowatts.

| Natural Leaf | Solar PV Cell |
|---|---|
| Chlorophyll absorbs photons | Silicon absorbs photons |
| Produces glucose (chemical energy) | Produces electrons (electrical energy) |
| Grows over time | Modules rated in Watts-peak (Wp) |
| Needs water & CO₂ | Needs only sunlight |

## 1.3 Why Solar Energy? — The Global Imperative

> [!NOTE]
> The Sun delivers approximately **1.74 × 10¹⁷ Watts** of power to Earth — nearly **10,000 times** the current global electricity demand (~2.5 × 10¹³ W). Capturing even a tiny fraction is transformative.

Key drivers pushing solar PV to the forefront of global energy strategy:

- **Climate urgency:** Solar PV produces electricity with **near-zero operational CO₂ emissions** (~45 g CO₂/kWh life-cycle, vs. ~820 g/kWh for coal).
- **Declining cost:** Module prices have fallen from ~**\$76/Wp (1977)** to roughly **\$0.10–0.20/Wp (2024)** — a ~99% reduction in 47 years.
- **Modularity:** From milliwatt-scale IoT sensors to gigawatt-scale solar farms.
- **Energy security:** Decentralized generation reduces dependence on imported fuels.
- **Scalability & speed:** A 1 MW solar plant can be commissioned in **3–6 months** vs. 5–10 years for a thermal/nuclear plant.

## 1.4 Solar PV vs. Solar Thermal — A Critical Distinction

| Parameter | Solar PV (Photovoltaic) | Solar Thermal (CSP/Heating) |
|---|---|---|
| Output form | DC → AC electricity | Heat (water/steam/thermal oil) |
| Physical principle | Photovoltaic effect (quantum) | Thermal absorption (classical) |
| Operating temperature | Near ambient (cell ~ 25–45 °C) | High (parabolic trough ~ 400 °C) |
| Tracking need | Optional (single-axis often used) | Usually mandatory for concentration |
| Application | Electric power, small electronics | Water heating, industrial steam, turbines |
| Storage | Battery bank (electrochemical) | Thermal storage tanks |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked the difference between PV and thermal, students commonly forget to mention that **PV uses semiconductors, not mirrors or absorbers**. Always emphasize the *quantum vs. classical* nature.

## 1.5 Classification of Solar PV Systems (Architecture View)

Solar PV systems are broadly classified into three top-level architectures:

1. **Stand-Alone (Off-Grid) Systems**
   Independent of the utility grid; energy is stored in batteries. Used in remote/rural electrification, telecom towers, water pumping.

2. **Grid-Tied (Grid-Connected) Systems**
   Connected to the utility grid via a bi-directional meter. Excess energy is exported; shortfall is imported. **No battery needed in "net-metered" variants.**

3. **Hybrid Systems**
   Combine PV with another source (diesel genset, wind, fuel cell) and usually a battery bank. Offers higher reliability and reduced diesel consumption.

## 1.6 Primary Components of a Solar PV System (Block-Form)

| S.No. | Component | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Solar PV Module (Array)** | Converts sunlight → DC electricity |
| 2 | **Mounting Structure** | Holds modules at the design tilt/azimuth |
| 3 | **Inverter** | DC → AC conversion (and MPPT) |
| 4 | **Charge Controller** | Regulates battery charging (off-grid) |
| 5 | **Battery Bank** | Stores DC energy (off-grid/hybrid) |
| 6 | **Cables & Protection (BoS)** | DC/AC distribution, fuses, SPDs, MCBs |
| 7 | **Monitoring System** | Sensors, data loggers, SCADA |

## 1.7 Standard Performance & Test Conditions (STC)

All manufacturer datasheets are normalized to **Standard Test Conditions (STC)** so that modules from different vendors can be compared fairly:

- **Irradiance (G):** 1000 W/m²
- **Cell Temperature (T):** 25 °C
- **Air Mass (AM):** 1.5 (sun at 41.81° from zenith)

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Real outdoor conditions are *never* STC. Kerala's typical operating cell temperature is **40–55 °C** and irradiance rarely equals 1000 W/m² for long periods. A 400 Wp module delivers closer to **280–320 W** in the field. Always apply **temperature and irradiance derating** in your KTU numericals.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Solar Resource — Extraterrestrial & Terrestrial Radiation

### 2.1.1 The Solar Constant (Iₛc)

The **solar constant** is the total radiant energy flux per unit area received on a surface perpendicular to the Sun's rays *outside* Earth's atmosphere, at the Earth's mean orbital distance.

$$I_{sc} \approx 1367 \; \text{W/m}^2$$

This is the AM0 spectrum — the reference used for space-grade solar cells (used in satellites and the IEC 61215 calibration chain).

### 2.1.2 Extraterrestrial Radiation on a Horizontal Surface — $G_{0}$

For a given day *n* of the year, the extraterrestrial irradiance on a horizontal surface at the top of the atmosphere is:

$$G_{0} = I_{sc} \left(1 + 0.033 \cos \frac{360 \, n}{365}\right)$$

The bracketed term is the **eccentricity correction factor (E₀)**, accounting for Earth's elliptical orbit.

### 2.1.3 Solar Geometry — Key Angles

| Symbol | Angle | Definition | Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\phi$ | Latitude | Angular distance from equator (N: +ve, S: −ve) | −90° to +90° |
| $\delta$ | Declination | Sun's angular position relative to Earth's equatorial plane | −23.45° to +23.45° |
| $\omega$ | Hour angle | 0° at solar noon; +ve afternoon, −ve morning | −180° to +180° |
| $\theta_z$ | Zenith angle | Angle between Sun and vertical | 0° to 90° |
| $\theta$ | Angle of incidence | Angle between Sun and normal to tilted surface | 0° to 90° |
| $\gamma_s$ | Solar azimuth | Compass direction of the Sun's projection | 0° to 360° |
| $\beta$ | Tilt angle | Inclination of PV surface from horizontal | 0° to 90° |
| $\gamma$ | Surface azimuth | Compass direction of PV surface normal | 0° to 360° |

### 2.1.4 Solar Declination Angle (Cooper's Equation)

$$\delta = 23.45^\circ \sin\left[\frac{360}{365}\,(284 + n)\right]$$

Used for *mean* declination. For higher precision, Spencer's Fourier series is used.

### 2.1.5 Hour Angle

Solar noon → $\omega = 0^\circ$. The Earth rotates at **15° per hour**.

$$\omega = 15^\circ \times (\text{local solar time in hours} - 12)$$

### 2.1.6 Solar Zenith Angle

The angle between the Sun's rays and the local vertical:

$$\cos\theta_z = \sin\phi \, \sin\delta + \cos\phi \, \cos\delta \, \cos\omega$$

### 2.1.7 Air Mass (AM)

A measure of how much atmosphere sunlight passes through relative to the minimum (Sun at zenith).

$$AM = \frac{1}{\cos\theta_z} \approx \frac{1}{\sin\alpha_s}$$

where $\alpha_s = 90^\circ - \theta_z$ is the solar altitude angle.

| Air Mass | Sun Position | Approx. Zenith |
|---|---|---|
| AM 0 | Outside atmosphere | — |
| AM 1 | Sun at 90° elevation | 0° |
| **AM 1.5** | **Standard for STC** | **48.19°** |
| AM 2 | Sun at 60° elevation | 60° |

### 2.1.8 Sunrise / Sunset Hour Angle

$$\omega_s = \cos^{-1}\left[-\tan\phi \, \tan\delta\right]$$

**Day length (sunshine hours, N):**

$$N = \frac{2}{15} \, \omega_s = \frac{2}{15} \cos^{-1}\left[-\tan\phi \, \tan\delta\right]$$

### 2.1.9 Beam, Diffuse & Global Radiation

The global horizontal irradiance is the sum of its beam (direct) and diffuse components:

$$G = I_b + I_d$$

| Component | Origin | Typical K_share in tropical Kerala |
|---|---|---|
| Beam (Iᵦ) | Direct rays from Sun's disk | 50–65% |
| Diffuse (I_d) | Scattered by atmosphere/clouds | 25–40% |
| Reflected (I_r) | From ground (albedo) | 5–10% (often ignored) |

### 2.1.10 Clearness Index (K_T)

A dimensionless measure of atmospheric transparency:

$$K_T = \frac{G}{G_0}$$

- $K_T \approx 0.7$ → clear sky
- $K_T \approx 0.3$ → heavily overcast
- $K_T > 1$ → cloud-edge enhancement (rare)

## 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Ready to Memorize)

| # | Formula | Use Case | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $G_0 = I_{sc}\,E_0$ | Extraterrestrial horizontal irradiance | W/m² |
| 2 | $E_0 = 1 + 0.033\cos(360n/365)$ | Eccentricity correction | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\delta = 23.45^\circ\sin[360(284+n)/365]$ | Solar declination | degrees |
| 4 | $\omega = 15(t_{\text{solar}} - 12)$ | Hour angle | degrees |
| 5 | $\cos\theta_z = \sin\phi\sin\delta + \cos\phi\cos\delta\cos\omega$ | Zenith angle | degrees |
| 6 | $AM = 1/\cos\theta_z$ | Air mass | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\omega_s = \cos^{-1}(-\tan\phi\tan\delta)$ | Sunrise/sunset hour angle | degrees |
| 8 | $N = \frac{2}{15}\omega_s$ | Sunshine hours | hours |
| 9 | $K_T = G/G_0$ | Clearness index | dimensionless |
| 10 | $G = I_b + I_d$ | Global = beam + diffuse | W/m² |
| 11 | $P_{out} = \eta_{PV} \cdot A \cdot G$ | PV power output | Watts |
| 12 | $E_{day} = \int_{sr}^{ss}\! P(t)\, dt$ | Daily energy | Wh/day |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The formulas above are not academic curiosities — they are the **backbone of any PVsyst, SAM, or HOMER simulation**. Every sizing tool, every performance guarantee, every bankable yield report is built on:

- **Declination + hour angle** → predicts the Sun's path across the sky.
- **Zenith / incidence angles** → decide the **tilt & azimuth** for maximum annual yield.
- **Air mass** → corrects module short-circuit current for spectrum.
- **Clearness index** → drives statistical yield models (Liu & Jordan, Erbs, Orgill–Hollands).
- **Sunshine hours** → simple back-of-the-envelope design tool used in field installations.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation A — Extraterrestrial Irradiance on a Horizontal Surface ($G_0$)

### 3.1.1 Statement
Derive the expression for the extraterrestrial (outside-atmosphere) radiation falling on a horizontal surface on day *n* of the year.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Development

**Step 1 — Geometry of Earth's Orbit**
The Earth orbits the Sun in a slightly elliptical path. The Earth–Sun distance varies between:
- Perihelion: $d_p = 1.471 \times 10^{11}$ m
- Aphelion: $d_a = 1.521 \times 10^{11}$ m
- Mean distance: $d_m = 1.496 \times 10^{11}$ m

The solar constant $I_{sc}$ is defined at the *mean* distance $d_m$.

**Step 2 — Inverse-Square Law**
The irradiance at a distance $d$ from the Sun is given by the inverse-square law:

$$I = \frac{I_{sc}}{\left(\dfrac{d}{d_m}\right)^2} = I_{sc}\left(\frac{d_m}{d}\right)^2$$

**Step 3 — Eccentricity Correction Factor**
Define the dimensionless ratio:

$$E_0 = \left(\frac{d_m}{d}\right)^2$$

**Step 4 — Approximation as a Cosine Function**
The ratio $(d_m/d)^2$ varies periodically with day *n*. Fitting this variation with a cosine series (the *first* harmonic captures >99.9% of the variation):

$$E_0 = 1 + 0.033 \cos\left(\frac{2\pi n}{365}\right) = 1 + 0.033 \cos\left(\frac{360 n}{365}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Beam Component on a Surface Normal to the Sun**
At the top of the atmosphere, on a surface perpendicular to the solar beam:

$$I_{bn} = I_{sc} \cdot E_0$$

**Step 6 — Projection onto a Horizontal Surface**
The beam flux on a horizontal surface is the normal component projected by $\cos\theta_z$, the *zenith angle* at solar noon on day *n* (or integrated for any hour). For daily-averaged extraterrestrial irradiance on a horizontal surface:

$$G_0 = I_{bn} \cdot \cos\theta_z \quad \text{(instantaneous)}$$

**Step 7 — Daily-Integrated Form (for clarity in PV design)**
The daily total extraterrestrial radiation on a horizontal surface (J/m²/day) is:

$$H_0 = \frac{24 \times 3600}{\pi}\, I_{sc}\, E_0 \left[\cos\phi\cos\delta\,\sin\omega_s + \omega_s \sin\phi \sin\delta \cdot \frac{\pi}{180}\right]$$

Where:
- $H_0$ is in J/m²/day
- $\omega_s$ is in **radians** (note the conversion factor $\pi/180$)
- $E_0$ is the eccentricity correction

**Step 8 — Result**

$$\boxed{\,G_0 = I_{sc} \left[1 + 0.033 \cos\!\left(\frac{360 n}{365}\right)\right] \text{ W/m}^2\,}$$

### 3.1.3 Numerical Verification
For **n = 172 (summer solstice, June 21)**:

$$E_0 = 1 + 0.033 \cos(360 \times 172/365) = 1 + 0.033 \cos(169.64^\circ) = 1 - 0.0324 = 0.9676$$

$$G_0 = 1367 \times 0.9676 \approx 1323 \; \text{W/m}^2$$

This is consistent with published NOAA/NASA tables.

## 3.2 Derivation B — Hour-Angle Sunrise / Sunset & Sunshine Hours

### 3.2.1 Starting Condition
At sunrise or sunset, the Sun is on the *horizon* — its zenith angle $\theta_z = 90^\circ$, hence $\cos\theta_z = 0$.

### 3.2.2 Applying the Zenith Equation
From the zenith formula:

$$\sin\phi \sin\delta + \cos\phi \cos\delta \cos\omega_s = 0$$

**Step 1 — Isolate $\cos\omega_s$**

$$\cos\phi \cos\delta \cos\omega_s = -\sin\phi \sin\delta$$

$$\cos\omega_s = -\frac{\sin\phi \sin\delta}{\cos\phi \cos\delta} = -\tan\phi \, \tan\delta$$

**Step 2 — Solve for the Hour Angle**

$$\omega_s = \cos^{-1}\!\left(-\tan\phi \, \tan\delta\right)$$

This is the sunrise hour angle (in degrees); $-\omega_s$ is the sunset hour angle (the function is symmetric about solar noon).

**Step 3 — Convert to Sunshine Hours**
Since the Earth rotates $15^\circ$ per hour:

$$N = \frac{2\,\omega_s}{15} \; \text{hours}$$

### 3.2.3 Numerical Example — Thiruvananthapuram, Kerala

Location: $\phi = 8.5^\circ$ N. Take the **summer solstice (n = 172)**: $\delta = +23.45^\circ$.

$$\omega_s = \cos^{-1}\!\left(-\tan 8.5^\circ \times \tan 23.45^\circ\right)$$

$$= \cos^{-1}\!\left(-0.1493 \times 0.4335\right) = \cos^{-1}(-0.0647) = 93.71^\circ$$

$$N = \frac{2 \times 93.71}{15} = 12.49 \; \text{hours}$$

For the **winter solstice (n = 355)**: $\delta = -23.45^\circ$

$$\omega_s = \cos^{-1}\!\left(-0.1493 \times -0.4335\right) = \cos^{-1}(+0.0647) = 86.29^\circ$$

$$N = \frac{2 \times 86.29}{15} = 11.51 \; \text{hours}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** In Kerala, the seasonal variation in day length is only **~1 hour** (unlike 6+ hours at high latitudes). This is why *fixed-tilt* arrays perform nearly as well as seasonal-tilt arrays in tropical Kerala — a frequent viva question.

## 3.3 Derivation C — Air Mass as a Function of Solar Altitude

### 3.3.1 Assumption
A flat, horizontally homogeneous atmosphere (plane-parallel approximation).

### 3.3.2 Derivation

**Step 1 — Geometry**
When the Sun is at zenith angle $\theta_z$, the slant path $L$ through the atmosphere is related to the vertical depth $L_0$ (zenith path) by:

$$L = \frac{L_0}{\cos\theta_z}$$

**Step 2 — Definition of Air Mass**
By definition, Air Mass is the ratio of the actual path length to the minimum (zenith) path length:

$$AM = \frac{L}{L_0} = \frac{1}{\cos\theta_z}$$

**Step 3 — Express in Terms of Solar Altitude**
Since $\alpha_s = 90^\circ - \theta_z$, we have $\cos\theta_z = \sin\alpha_s$:

$$AM = \frac{1}{\sin\alpha_s}$$

**Step 4 — Higher-Order Correction (Kasten-Young Formula)**
For low Sun angles, atmospheric refraction and curvature corrections become important. The Kasten-Young (1989) empirical expression is:

$$AM = \frac{1}{\cos\theta_z + 0.50572\,(96.07995^\circ - \theta_z)^{-1.6364}}$$

**Step 5 — Special Case Verification**
At $\theta_z = 48.19^\circ$ (STC condition):

$$AM = \frac{1}{\cos 48.19^\circ} = \frac{1}{0.6667} = 1.5 \; \checkmark$$

## 3.4 Derivation D — Global Horizontal Irradiance on Tilted Surface

### 3.4.1 Beam Component on Tilted Surface

For a tilted surface with tilt $\beta$ and azimuth $\gamma$, the incidence angle $\theta$ is given by:

$$\cos\theta = \sin\delta\sin(\phi-\beta) + \cos\delta\cos\omega\cos(\phi-\beta)\cos\gamma + \cos\delta\sin\omega\sin\gamma$$

For a **south-facing surface in the Northern Hemisphere ($\gamma = 0$)**, this simplifies to:

$$\cos\theta = \sin\delta\sin(\phi-\beta) + \cos\delta\cos\omega\cos(\phi-\beta)$$

### 3.4.2 Isotropic Sky Diffuse Model (Liu & Jordan)

$$I_{d,T} = I_d \left(\frac{1 + \cos\beta}{2}\right)$$

The factor $\frac{1+\cos\beta}{2}$ is the **sky view factor** for the tilted plane.

### 3.4.3 Ground-Reflected Component

$$I_{r,T} = \rho \, G \left(\frac{1 - \cos\beta}{2}\right)$$

where $\rho$ is the ground albedo (typical: 0.2 for grass, 0.6–0.8 for snow).

### 3.4.4 Total Tilted Irradiance

$$\boxed{\,G_T = I_b \frac{\cos\theta}{\cos\theta_z} + I_d\!\left(\frac{1+\cos\beta}{2}\right) + \rho G\!\left(\frac{1-\cos\beta}{2}\right)\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Note:** The first term is often called the **"Rᵦ ratio"** — the beam transposition factor. It is the heart of every PVsyst simulation.

## 3.5 Quick-Reference Analytical Matrix

| Parameter | Input Required | Output | KTU Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|
| $G_0$ (extraterrestrial) | Day *n* | W/m² | Very High |
| $\delta$ (declination) | Day *n* | Degrees | Very High |
| $\theta_z, \alpha_s$ | $\phi, n, t$ | Degrees | Very High |
| $\omega_s, N$ | $\phi, n$ | Hours | High |
| $AM$ | $\theta_z$ | Dimensionless | High |
| $G_T$ (tilted) | $\beta, \gamma, \phi, n, t$ | W/m² | High |
| $E_{day}$ (daily energy) | $G_T(t)$ | kWh/day | Very High |
| Clearness index $K_T$ | $G, G_0$ | Dimensionless | Medium |
| Tilt optimization | $\phi$ | $\beta_{opt} \approx \vert\phi\vert$ | High |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Solar Energy Utilization Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Solar Radiation Source]
    A1[SUN 1367 W per m square]
    A2[Extraterrestrial Spectrum AM0]
    A3[Terrestrial Spectrum AM1.5]

    B0[Conversion Pathways]
    B1[Photovoltaic Effect - Direct Electricity]
    B2[Solar Thermal - Heat]
    B3[Concentrated Solar Power - CSP]

    C0[PV System Architectures]
    C1[Stand-Alone Off-Grid]
    C2[Grid-Tied On-Grid]
    C3[Hybrid with Battery and Diesel]

    D0[End-Use Applications]
    D1[Residential Rooftop]
    D2[Commercial and Industrial]
    D3[Utility Scale Solar Farm]
    D4[Water Pumping]
    D5[Telecom and Remote Loads]

    A0 --> A1
    A1 --> A2
    A1 --> A3
    A3 --> B0
    B0 --> B1
    B0 --> B2
    B0 --> B3
    B1 --> C0
    C0 --> C1
    C0 --> C2
    C0 --> C3
    C1 --> D0
    C2 --> D0
    C3 --> D0
    D0 --> D1
    D0 --> D2
    D0 --> D3
    D0 --> D4
    D0 --> D5
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    A3[Inverter with MPPT Controller]

    A1 --> A2

    A4 --> A5

```

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    S5[Inverter DC to AC 230V 50Hz]
    S6[AC Distribution and Load]
    S7[DC Load Optional]

    S0 --> S1
    S1 --> S2
    S2 --> S3
    S3 --> S4
    S4 --> S5
    S5 --> S6
    S4 --> S7
```

## 4.4 Schematic — Solar Geometry & Angle Relationships

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    G0[Sun Position in Sky]
    G1[Declination delta - Earth-Sun Line vs Equator]
    G2[Hour Angle omega - Earth Rotation from Solar Noon]
    G3[Zenith Angle theta_z - Sun vs Vertical]
    G4[Solar Altitude alpha_s = 90 minus theta_z]
    G5[Azimuth gamma_s - Sun Direction Compass]
    G6[Latitude phi - Location on Earth]

    P0[Tilted PV Surface]
    P1[Tilt Angle beta - Surface vs Horizontal]
    P2[Surface Azimuth gamma - Surface Normal Compass]
    P3[Incidence Angle theta - Sun Rays vs Surface Normal]
    P4[Air Mass AM - Atmospheric Path Length]

    G0 --> G1
    G0 --> G2
    G0 --> G3
    G3 --> G4
    G0 --> G5
    G6 --> G3
    P0 --> P1
    P0 --> P2
    G0 --> P3
    P1 --> P3
    P2 --> P3
    G3 --> P4
```

## 4.5 Block Diagram — Sun-Path Across Kerala Latitudes (Annual Cycle)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q1[Winter Solstice n=355 - Sun S of Zenith]
    Q2[Spring Equinox n=81 - Sun at Zenith at Equator]
    Q3[Summer Solstice n=172 - Sun N of Zenith at Tropic]
    Q4[Autumnal Equinox n=264 - Sun at Zenith at Equator]

    R1[Kerala phi 8 to 12 N - Sun Always in South Quadrant]
    R2[Tropic of Cancer phi 23.45 N - Sun Crosses Zenith]

    Q1 --> R1
    Q2 --> R1
    Q3 --> R1
    Q4 --> R1
    Q3 --> R2
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the diagrams:** Each rectangular node is a *function* or *state* in the system. The arrows are *energy flows* (in 4.1–4.3) or *angular relationships* (in 4.4–4.5). For the KTU exam, you can reproduce these simplified flowcharts in 4–5 minutes each and earn full marks in any "explain with block diagram" sub-question.
