<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — Jun 2023]`
**Define the solar constant. Mention its standard value and the spectrum to which it corresponds.**

**Model Answer:**
The **solar constant** is defined as the total radiant energy flux per unit area received on a surface *perpendicular to the Sun's rays* at the top of Earth's atmosphere, at the *mean Earth–Sun distance*.
- **Standard value:** **I_sc = 1367 W/m²** (often quoted as 1360–1366 W/m² ± uncertainty).
- **Spectrum:** This corresponds to the **AM 0 (Air Mass Zero)** spectrum, which is the extraterrestrial solar spectrum used as the calibration reference for space-grade solar cells and for normalizing all ground-based PV measurements to STC.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for value, 1 for AM0 mention.)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]`
**Differentiate between a stand-alone and a grid-tied solar PV system. List any two applications of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Stand-Alone (Off-Grid) | Grid-Tied (On-Grid) |
|---|---|---|
| Grid connection | Not connected | Connected via net meter |
| Storage | Battery bank mandatory | Battery optional (net-metered) |
| Inverter type | Stand-alone (with charger) | Grid-tie (anti-islanding) |
| Power during outage | Available (with battery) | **Not available** (anti-islanding) |

- **Off-Grid Applications:** (i) Rural electrification (ii) Solar water pumps for irrigation (iii) Telecom towers
- **Grid-Tied Applications:** (i) Residential rooftop (ii) Industrial captive power (iii) Utility-scale solar farms

*(3 marks: 2 for differentiation table, 1 for applications.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

### **Question A — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Define the following solar angles: declination, hour angle, zenith angle, and air mass. Derive the expression for the **sunrise hour angle** and the corresponding **day length (sunshine hours)** for a location at latitude $\phi$ on day *n* of the year. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a location at $\phi = 10^\circ$ N, compute the **day length** and the **air mass at solar noon** on the **summer solstice (n = 172)** and the **winter solstice (n = 355)**. Comment on the seasonal variation. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Q.A (a):**

**Step 1 — Definitions (3 marks)**

- **Declination angle ($\delta$):** Angular position of the Sun relative to the equatorial plane. Varies from $-23.45^\circ$ (winter) to $+23.45^\circ$ (summer). Cooper's formula:
$$\delta = 23.45^\circ \sin\!\left[\frac{360(284+n)}{365}\right]$$

- **Hour angle ($\omega$):** Angular displacement of the Sun from local meridian (solar noon). 15° per hour, negative in morning, positive in afternoon.
$$\omega = 15^\circ \times (t_{\text{solar}} - 12)$$

- **Zenith angle ($\theta_z$):** Angle between the Sun's rays and the local vertical. $\theta_z = 0^\circ$ when Sun is overhead.
$$\cos\theta_z = \sin\phi\sin\delta + \cos\phi\cos\delta\cos\omega$$

- **Air mass (AM):** Ratio of actual atmospheric path length to the zenith path length.
$$AM = \frac{1}{\cos\theta_z}$$

**Step 2 — Derivation of Sunrise Hour Angle (3 marks)**

At sunrise/sunset, $\theta_z = 90^\circ$, so $\cos\theta_z = 0$.

Substituting into the zenith equation:
$$\sin\phi\sin\delta + \cos\phi\cos\delta\cos\omega_s = 0$$

Isolating $\cos\omega_s$:
$$\cos\omega_s = -\tan\phi\tan\delta$$

$$\boxed{\,\omega_s = \cos^{-1}(-\tan\phi\tan\delta)\,}$$

**Step 3 — Day Length (1 mark)**

Since Earth rotates 15° per hour, the total day length is:
$$\boxed{\,N = \frac{2\,\omega_s}{15} \text{ hours}\,}$$

---

### **Model Solution for Q.A (b):**

**Step 1 — Summer Solstice (n = 172) (3 marks)**

$$\delta = 23.45^\circ\sin\!\left[\frac{360(284+172)}{365}\right] = 23.45^\circ\sin(449.26^\circ) = 23.45^\circ\sin(89.26^\circ) \approx +23.27^\circ$$

$$\omega_s = \cos^{-1}\!\left[-\tan 10^\circ \times \tan 23.27^\circ\right] = \cos^{-1}\!\left[-0.1763 \times 0.4298\right] = \cos^{-1}(-0.0758) = 94.35^\circ$$

$$N_{\text{summer}} = \frac{2 \times 94.35}{15} = 12.58 \; \text{hours}$$

At solar noon ($\omega = 0$):
$$\cos\theta_z = \sin 10^\circ\sin 23.27^\circ + \cos 10^\circ\cos 23.27^\circ = 0.0740 + 0.8877 = 0.9617$$
$$\theta_z = 15.92^\circ$$
$$AM = \frac{1}{0.9617} = 1.04$$

**Step 2 — Winter Solstice (n = 355) (3 marks)**

$$\delta = 23.45^\circ\sin\!\left[\frac{360(284+355)}{365}\right] = 23.45^\circ\sin(630.25^\circ) = 23.45^\circ\sin(270.25^\circ) \approx -23.45^\circ$$

$$\omega_s = \cos^{-1}\!\left[-\tan 10^\circ \times \tan(-23.45^\circ)\right] = \cos^{-1}(+0.1771) = 79.80^\circ$$

$$N_{\text{winter}} = \frac{2 \times 79.80}{15} = 10.64 \; \text{hours}$$

At solar noon:
$$\cos\theta_z = \sin 10^\circ\sin(-23.45^\circ) + \cos 10^\circ\cos(-23.45^\circ) = -0.0747 + 0.8742 = 0.7995$$
$$\theta_z = 36.93^\circ$$
$$AM = \frac{1}{0.7995} = 1.25$$

**Step 3 — Comments (1 mark)**

| Parameter | Summer (n=172) | Winter (n=355) | Variation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Day length | 12.58 h | 10.64 h | **1.94 hours** |
| AM at noon | 1.04 | 1.25 | +0.21 |

- Seasonal day-length variation in tropical Kerala is small (~2 h) compared to high latitudes.
- The Sun is **never far from overhead** (max $\theta_z \approx 37^\circ$ in winter).
- **Design implication:** A *fixed tilt* of ~10° (latitude) yields near-optimal **year-round performance** in Kerala — no need for seasonal tilt adjustments.

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** What is the photovoltaic effect? Explain with a neat sketch the construction and working of a **crystalline silicon solar cell**. List the main performance parameters used to characterize a solar cell. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A PV module has the following nameplate data: $P_{max} = 320$ Wp, $V_{oc} = 45.6$ V, $I_{sc} = 9.20$ A, $V_{mp} = 37.2$ V, $I_{mp} = 8.60$ A at STC. Compute the **fill factor (FF)**, the **module efficiency**, and the **temperature coefficient of power** given $\gamma_P = -0.40\%/^\circ$C. Estimate the output power when cell temperature is $55^\circ$C and irradiance is $800$ W/m². **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Q.B (a):**

**Step 1 — Photovoltaic Effect (2 marks)**

The **photovoltaic effect** is the generation of a voltage and electric current in a material upon exposure to light. When photons with energy $E = h\nu \geq E_g$ (band gap) strike a semiconductor, they generate **electron–hole pairs** at the p–n junction. The built-in electric field of the junction separates these charge carriers, driving electrons to the n-side and holes to the p-side, producing a **photocurrent** when an external circuit is closed.

**Step 2 — Construction of a c-Si Solar Cell (3 marks)**

- **Substrate:** p-type Czochralski-grown silicon wafer (~180 µm thick, resistivity 1–3 Ω·cm).
- **Emitter:** n-type layer formed by phosphorus diffusion (~0.5 µm thick, sheet resistance 50–100 Ω/sq).
- **Anti-Reflection Coating (ARC):** SiN or TiO₂ thin film (~75 nm) to minimize reflection.
- **Front Contact:** Silver (Ag) screen-printed fingers and busbars.
- **Back Contact:** Aluminium (Al) screen-printed, full-area back surface field (BSF) or PERC structure.

**Step 3 — Performance Parameters (2 marks)**

- **Open-circuit voltage ($V_{oc}$):** Voltage when terminals are open.
- **Short-circuit current ($I_{sc}$):** Current when terminals are shorted.
- **Maximum power point ($P_{max} = V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp}$):** Operating point of maximum power transfer.
- **Fill factor (FF):** Ratio of $P_{max}$ to $V_{oc} I_{sc}$.
- **Efficiency ($\eta$):** Ratio of $P_{max}$ to incident solar power.
- **Temperature coefficients:** $\alpha_{V_{oc}}, \beta_{I_{sc}}, \gamma_{P}$.

---

### **Model Solution for Q.B (b):**

**Step 1 — Fill Factor (2 marks)**

$$FF = \frac{P_{max}}{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}} = \frac{V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp}}{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}} = \frac{37.2 \times 8.60}{45.6 \times 9.20} = \frac{319.92}{419.52} = 0.763 \; \text{or} \; 76.3\%$$

**Step 2 — Module Efficiency (2 marks)**

Assuming a typical 60-cell module area $A \approx 1.65$ m × 0.99 m = **1.6335 m²**:

$$\eta = \frac{P_{max}}{A \cdot G_{STC}} = \frac{320}{1.6335 \times 1000} = 0.1959 = 19.6\%$$

**Step 3 — Output at $T = 55^\circ$C and $G = 800$ W/m² (3 marks)**

Temperature deviation from STC: $\Delta T = 55 - 25 = 30^\circ$C.

Power loss due to temperature:
$$P_{temp} = P_{STC}\left[1 + \frac{\gamma_P}{100}\,\Delta T\right] = 320 \times [1 + (-0.0040)(30)] = 320 \times 0.88 = 281.6 \; \text{W}$$

The current scales *linearly* with irradiance; voltage is approximately constant (with small temperature compensation):
$$P_{out} = P_{temp} \times \frac{G}{G_{STC}} = 281.6 \times \frac{800}{1000} = 225.3 \; \text{W}$$

**Result:** $P_{out} \approx$ **225 W** (a derate of ~30% from nameplate — fully consistent with field reality in tropical Kerala).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Zones in this Topic:**
> 1. **Forgetting the eccentricity correction ($E_0$)** when computing $G_0$ — costs 1 mark.
> 2. **Mixing up sunrise vs. sunset hour angle** — they are negatives of each other, NOT two different angles.
> 3. **Applying STC ratings to real conditions** without temperature/irradiance derating — KTU papers *always* test this.
> 4. **Latitude sign convention:** Use **+ve for Northern Hemisphere, −ve for Southern.** Mixing signs in $\phi$ leads to wrong season.
> 5. **AM at noon vs. AM at 8 a.m.:** A module at 8 a.m. has $AM \approx 1.6$–$2.0$, *not* 1.5. STC is 1.5 at noon only for tilted surfaces facing the equator at $\phi = 41.81^\circ$.
> 6. **Unit mistakes:** $\omega_s$ in degrees vs. radians — the sunshine-hour formula $N = 2\omega_s/15$ is for **degrees**; the $H_0$ formula uses **radians**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Solar Constant** $I_{sc} = 1367$ W/m² corresponds to **AM 0** spectrum.
- [x] **STC** = 1000 W/m², 25°C cell, AM 1.5 — the universal module rating standard.
- [x] **Extraterrestrial radiation** $G_0 = I_{sc}[1 + 0.033\cos(360n/365)]$ — must always include the $E_0$ correction.
- [x] **Declination** ranges $\pm 23.45^\circ$ (Earth's axial tilt). Cooper's equation is sufficient for KTU.
- [x] **Hour angle** is $15^\circ$/hour from solar noon; **negative** in the morning, **positive** in the afternoon.
- [x] **Sunrise hour angle** $\omega_s = \cos^{-1}(-\tan\phi \tan\delta)$.
- [x] **Day length** $N = 2\omega_s/15$ hours.
- [x] **Zenith angle** comes from the spherical-trigonometry identity: $\cos\theta_z = \sin\phi\sin\delta + \cos\phi\cos\delta\cos\omega$.
- [x] **Air mass** $AM = 1/\cos\theta_z$ (plane-parallel approximation); Kasten-Young for low Sun.
- [x] **Global = Beam + Diffuse**, often with **Reflected** added for tilted surfaces.
- [x] **Clearness index** $K_T = G/G_0$ — typical values 0.3 (cloudy) to 0.75 (clear sky).
- [x] **Tilted irradiance** uses the **Rᵦ ratio** for beam, **isotropic sky view factor** for diffuse, and **albedo** term for ground reflection.
- [x] **PV systems** are classified as **Stand-Alone**, **Grid-Tied**, or **Hybrid**.
- [x] **In tropical Kerala** ($\phi \approx 8–12^\circ$N), a *fixed tilt* of $\beta = \phi$ gives near-optimal year-round yield; seasonal tilt adjustment is unnecessary.
- [x] **Module derating:** $P_{real} = P_{STC} \times [1 + \gamma_P \Delta T] \times (G/G_{STC})$ — always apply in numericals.
- [x] **Famous traps to avoid:** $E_0$ omission, sign of $\phi$, STC vs. real conditions, AM ≠ 1.5 at all hours.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Concept of Energy, Source of Solar Energy, Formation of the Atmosphere
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEEET721 - DESIGN OF SOLAR PV SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module Module 1 > - **Topic:** Basic Concept of Energy, Source of Solar Energy, Formation of the Atmosphere # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Basic Concept of Energy **Energy** is defined as the capacity of a system to perform **work** or to transfer **heat**. In physics, it is a scalar quantity measured in **Joules (J)** in the SI system. Energy is the fundamental currency of every physical, chemical, and biological process that occurs in the universe. > [!IMPORTANT] > **First Law of Thermodynamics (Conservation of Energy):** > Energy can neither be created nor destroyed; it can only be transformed from one form to another. The total energy of an isolated system remains constant. ### Forms of Energy Energy manifests in many forms, broadly classified as: | Form | Description | Example | |------|-------------|---------| | **Kinetic Energy** | Energy of motion | Wind, flowing water | | **Potential Energy** | Stored energy due to position | Elevated water, compressed spring | | **Thermal Energy** | Internal energy due to temperature | Solar heat, geothermal | | **Chemical Energy** | Stored in molecular bonds | Fossil fuels, biomass | | **Electrical Energy** | Energy of moving electrons | PV output, grid power | | **Radiant/Solar Energy** | Electromagnetic radiation from Sun | Sunlight, photons | | **Nuclear Energy** | Energy released in fission/fusion | Sun's core, nuclear reactors | > [!NOTE] > **Renewable vs Non-Renewable Energy:** > - **Renewable energy sources** are replenished naturally on a human timescale. Examples: Solar, Wind, Hydro, Biomass, Geothermal, Tidal. > - **Non-renewable energy sources** are finite and take millions of years to form. Examples: Coal, Petroleum, Natural Gas, Nuclear (Uranium). ## 1.2 Source of Solar Energy The **Sun** is the primary source of energy for the Earth. It is a **G-type main-sequence star** (Yellow Dwarf) located approximately **149.6 × 10⁶ km** (about 1 AU) from Earth. The Sun generates its immense energy through **thermonuclear fusion** reactions in its core, where hydrogen nuclei (protons) fuse to form helium nuclei, releasing energy in accordance with **Einstein's mass-energy equivalence**: $$E = mc^2$$ > [!NOTE] > The dominant fusion cycle in the Sun is the **Proton-Proton (P-P) Chain**, which accounts for approximately 99% of the Sun's total energy output. The Carbon-Nitrogen-Oxygen (CNO) cycle contributes the remaining ~1%. ### Sun's Structure - **Core** (~1.5 × 10⁷ K): Site of nuclear fusion - **Radiative Zone**: Energy travels via photon radiation - **Convective Zone**: Energy transported by convection currents - **Photosphere** (~5,778 K): Visible surface of the Sun - **Chromosphere & Corona**: Outer atmospheric layers ## 1.3 Formation of the Atmosphere The Earth's **atmosphere** is a thin layer of gases retained by gravity, forming a protective blanket around the planet. It evolved over **4.5 billion years** through two principal processes: 1. **Outgassing** — Volcanic eruptions released trapped gases (H₂O, CO₂, N₂, CH₄, NH₃) from the planet's interior (Primary Atmosphere). 2. **Photosynthesis** — Early cyanobacteria converted CO₂ into O₂, gradually producing the oxygen-rich Secondary Atmosphere we have today. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Atmospheric Composition (Dry Air, by Volume):** > - Nitrogen (N₂): **78.08%** > - Oxygen (O₂): **20.95%** > - Argon (Ar): **0.93%** > - Carbon Dioxide (CO₂): **0.04%** (rising due to anthropogenic activity) > - Trace gases (Ne, He, CH₄, O₃, H₂): **< 0.01%** ### Conceptual Analogy (The Onion-Layer Greenhouse) > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The Layered Onion Sky:** > Imagine Earth's atmosphere as a giant **onion made of transparent plastic layers** wrapped around a warm orange (the Earth). The outer dry skin (Thermosphere) deflects harmful cosmic rays, the moist inner layers (Stratosphere with ozone) absorb UV radiation, and the innermost layers (Troposphere) trap some heat like a greenhouse. The Sun's light must pass through every layer to reach the fruit inside, getting progressively filtered — just like solar radiation reaching a PV panel on the ground! ### Vertical Structure of the Atmosphere | Layer | Altitude Range | Temperature Trend | Key Feature | |-------|----------------|-------------------|-------------| | **Troposphere** | 0 – 12 km | Decreases with height (−6.5 K/km) | Weather, clouds, 75% of atmospheric mass | | **Stratosphere** | 12 – 50 km | Increases with height | Ozone layer (O₃), UV absorption | | **Mesosphere** | 50 – 85 km | Decreases with height | Meteors burn up, coldest layer | | **Thermosphere** | 85 – 600 km | Increases sharply | Ionosphere, auroras, ISS orbits | | **Exosphere** | > 600 km | Gradually merges with space | Hydrogen & Helium dominant, satellites | # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Energy — Operational Breakdown Energy is fundamental to every engineering discipline. For solar PV system design, we focus on **radiant energy** and its conversion to **electrical energy** via the photovoltaic effect. The flow of energy follows a strict hierarchy: - **Source (Sun)** → **Transmission (through atmosphere)** → **Collection (PV array)** → **Conversion (DC electricity)** → **Storage (batteries) / Inversion (AC)** → **Load (appliances/grid)** ### Why This Matters in Solar PV Engineering - The **amount of solar radiation** reaching the Earth's surface determines the **size of the PV array** required to meet a given load demand. - The **spectral distribution** of sunlight affects the **choice of semiconductor material** (e.g., Si, CdTe, CIGS) for PV cells. - The **atmospheric attenuation** (scattering, absorption) reduces the available solar resource below the extraterrestrial value, making accurate modeling essential for yield prediction. ## 2.2 Solar Radiation — Source Physics The Sun behaves almost as a **blackbody** at an effective surface temperature of approximately **5,778 K**. The total radiant energy emitted per unit area per unit time is given by the **Stefan-Boltzmann Law**. The Sun's radius is **R_s ≈ 6.96 × 10⁸ m**, and the mean Earth-Sun distance is **d = 1.496 × 10¹¹ m**. ## 2.3 Formation of Atmosphere — Detailed Mechanism The atmosphere is not a static entity; it is a dynamic, evolving system: - **Stage 1 — Accretion & Degassing (~4.5 BYA):** Volcanic activity released gases locked within the molten Earth. The early atmosphere was rich in CO₂, water vapor, NH₃, and CH₄ (reducing atmosphere). - **Stage 2 — Water Condensation (~4.0 BYA):** As Earth cooled, water vapor condensed to form oceans, dissolving much of the CO₂ into carbonate rocks. - **Stage 3 — Origin of Life & Photosynthesis (~3.5 BYA):** Cyanobacteria evolved and began producing O₂ via photosynthesis: 6CO₂ + 6H₂O → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂. - **Stage 4 — Modern Atmosphere (~540 MYA onwards):** O₂ accumulated to support complex life; the ozone (O₃) layer formed in the stratosphere, blocking UV and allowing life to colonize land. ## 2.4 Atmospheric Effects on Solar Radiation As solar radiation traverses the atmosphere, three key phenomena occur: 1. **Attenuation / Absorption:** Molecules like O₃, H₂O, and CO₂ absorb specific wavelengths (e.g., O₃ absorbs UV; H₂O absorbs IR). 2. **Scattering:** Molecules and aerosols scatter light in all directions (Rayleigh, Mie, and non-selective scattering). 3. **Reflection:** Clouds and the Earth's surface reflect a portion of incident radiation (governed by **albedo**). > [!NOTE] > **Albedo (α)** is the ratio of reflected to incident solar radiation. Fresh snow ≈ 0.80–0.90; Forest ≈ 0.10–0.15; Asphalt ≈ 0.05–0.10; Ocean ≈ 0.06. ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula / Concept | Description / Symbol Meaning | Units | |---|------------------|------------------------------|-------| | 1 | $E = mc^2$ | Mass-energy equivalence (Einstein) | J | | 2 | $P = \dfrac{dE}{dt}$ | Power is the rate of energy transfer | W | | 3 | $E_k = \dfrac{1}{2}mv^2$ | Kinetic energy of a body | J | | 4 | $E_p = mgh$ | Gravitational potential energy | J | | 5 | $Q = mc\Delta T$ | Sensible heat (specific heat capacity $c$) | J | | 6 | $E = h\nu$ | Photon energy (Planck's relation, $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s) | J | | 7 | $\lambda\nu = c$ | Wave speed of light ($c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s) | m/s | | 8 | $M_b = \sigma T^4$ | Stefan-Boltzmann blackbody emission ($\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W/m²·K⁴) | W/m² | | 9 | $G_{sc} = \sigma T_s^4 \left(\dfrac{R_s}{d}\right)^2$ | Solar constant at Earth | W/m² | | 10 | $G_{on} = G_{sc}\left(1 + 0.033\cos\dfrac{360n}{365}\right)$ | Extraterrestrial radiation on day $n$ | W/m² | | 11 | $AM = \dfrac{1}{\cos\theta_z}$ | Air Mass at solar zenith angle $\theta_z$ | — | | 12 | $\tau_a = e^{-k \cdot m}$ | Beer's-Lambert atmospheric transmittance | — | | 13 | $G = G_{on} \cdot \tau_a$ | Global horizontal irradiance (clear sky) | W/m² | | 14 | $\alpha = \dfrac{G_{ref}}{G_{inc}}$ | Albedo (reflectivity) | — | | 15 | $H_o = \dfrac{24 \times 3600}{\pi} G_{sc}\left(1 + 0.033\cos\dfrac{360n}{365}\right)\left(\cos\phi\cos\delta\sin\omega_s + \dfrac{\pi \omega_s}{180}\sin\phi\sin\delta\right)$ | Daily extraterrestrial radiation on horizontal surface | J/m²·day | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Constants to Memorize for KTU Exam:** > - Stefan-Boltzmann constant: **σ = 5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W/m²·K⁴** > - Planck's constant: **h = 6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s** > - Speed of light: **c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s** > - Sun's surface temperature: **T_s = 5,778 K** > - Solar constant: **G_sc = 1,367 W/m²** > - 1 AU: **1.496 × 10¹¹ m** ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Architectural Design:** Atmospheric transmittance data is used to design **daylight harvesting systems** and optimize window-to-wall ratios. - **Climate Modeling:** Atmospheric composition (CO₂, CH₄) directly affects the **greenhouse effect** and global warming potential. - **PV Yield Estimation:** Software like **PVsyst, SAM, and HOMER** use these exact formulas to predict annual energy output (kWh/year) of solar plants. - **Satellite Communications:** Ionosphere (part of thermosphere) affects radio wave propagation; critical for GNSS and HF communication. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Solar Constant (G_sc) The **Solar Constant** is defined as the **total solar radiation flux density** received on a surface perpendicular to the Sun's rays, at the mean Earth-Sun distance, outside the Earth's atmosphere. ### Given Data - Sun's effective surface temperature: $T_s = 5{,}778$ K - Sun's radius: $R_s = 6.96 \times 10^8$ m - Mean Earth-Sun distance: $d = 1.496 \times 10^{11}$ m - Stefan-Boltzmann constant: $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W/m²·K⁴ ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1:** Apply the Stefan-Boltzmann law to compute the total power radiated per unit surface area of the Sun. $$M_b = \sigma T_s^4$$ This gives the energy flux at the Sun's surface (W/m²). **Step 2:** Calculate the total power emitted by the entire Sun by multiplying this flux by the Sun's total surface area (sphere of radius $R_s$). $$P_{total} = M_b \cdot 4\pi R_s^2 = \sigma T_s^4 \cdot 4\pi R_s^2$$ This is the **luminosity** of the Sun (in Watts). **Step 3:** At a distance $d$ from the Sun, this total power is uniformly distributed over the surface of an imaginary sphere of radius $d$. So the flux at Earth is: $$G_{sc} = \frac{P_{total}}{4\pi d^2}$$ **Step 4:** Substitute the expression for $P_{total}$ into the equation: $$G_{sc} = \frac{\sigma T_s^4 \cdot 4\pi R_s^2}{4\pi d^2}$$ **Step 5:** Cancel the common factor $4\pi$: $$G_{sc} = \sigma T_s^4 \left(\frac{R_s}{d}\right)^2$$ **Step 6:** Substitute the numerical values: $$G_{sc} = (5.67 \times 10^{-8}) \times (5{,}778)^4 \times \left(\frac{6.96 \times 10^8}{1.496 \times 10^{11}}\right)^2$$ **Step 7:** Evaluate each term: - $T_s^4 = (5{,}778)^4 = 1.117 \times 10^{15}$ K⁴ - $\sigma T_s^4 = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 1.117 \times 10^{15} = 6.33 \times 10^7$ W/m² - $R_s / d = 6.96 \times 10^8 / 1.496 \times 10^{11} = 4.652 \times 10^{-3}$ - $(R_s / d)^2 = 2.164 \times 10^{-5}$ **Step 8:** Multiply all terms: $$G_{sc} = 6.33 \times 10^7 \times 2.164 \times 10^{-5} = 1{,}370 \text{ W/m}^2$$ **Step 9:** Round to the standard accepted value: $$G_{sc} \approx 1{,}367 \text{ W/m}^2$$ > [!NOTE] > The internationally accepted value of the solar constant is **G_sc = 1,367 W/m²** (per WMO, 1981). The World Radiation Center (WRC) updated it to **1,361 W/m²** in some references, but **KTU exams expect 1,367 W/m²**. --- ## 3.2 Derivation of the Air Mass (AM) Formula The **Air Mass (AM)** quantifies the path length of solar radiation through the atmosphere, normalized to the shortest possible path (Sun directly overhead). ### Given - Solar zenith angle: $\theta_z$ (angle between Sun and vertical) - Earth's atmosphere approximated as a flat horizontal layer of unit thickness ### Derivation **Step 1:** When the Sun is directly overhead ($\theta_z = 0°$), radiation passes through **1 unit** of atmosphere. $$AM = 1 \quad \text{(Sea Level, Sun at Zenith)}$$ **Step 2:** For any other angle $\theta_z$, the path length is longer. By the geometry of a flat layer: $$AM = \frac{\text{Path length at angle } \theta_z}{\text{Path length at zenith}}$$ **Step 3:** Using basic trigonometry (cosine of angle = adjacent/hypotenuse): $$AM = \frac{1}{\cos\theta_z}$$ **Step 4:** At a typical mid-latitude location where the Sun is at $\theta_z = 48.2°$: $$AM = \frac{1}{\cos(48.2°)} = \frac{1}{0.6667} = 1.5$$ This is the famous **AM1.5** standard reference spectrum used worldwide to rate PV modules (1 kW/m² at AM1.5g). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Test Conditions (STC) for PV modules:** > - Irradiance: **1,000 W/m²** > - Spectrum: **AM1.5G (Global)** > - Cell temperature: **25°C** > KTU problems frequently use these STC values. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Energy Forms & Their Relevance to Solar PV | Energy Form | Mathematical Form | Relevance to Solar PV Design | |-------------|-------------------|------------------------------| | Radiant | $E = h\nu = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$ | Determines PV cell bandgap selection | | Kinetic | $E_k = \dfrac{1}{2}mv^2$ | Wind loading on PV mounting structures | | Potential | $E_p = mgh$ | Roof load assessment for PV array | | Electrical | $P = VI = I^2R = \dfrac{V^2}{R}$ | Sizing of inverter, cables, and protections | | Thermal | $Q = mc_p \Delta T$ | Cell temperature → efficiency derating | | Chemical | $E = nFE^0$ | Battery bank sizing (Wh capacity) | | Mechanical (work) | $W = F \cdot d \cdot \cos\theta$ | Tracking system motor sizing | --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Atmospheric Layers and Solar Radiation Effects | Layer | Primary Interaction with Solar Radiation | Engineering Consequence | |-------|-------------------------------------------|--------------------------| | **Exosphere** | Free-space propagation, vacuum-like | Satellite solar irradiance sensors (e.g., SORCE) | | **Thermosphere** | X-ray and extreme UV absorption | Satellite drag, GPS signal delay | | **Mesosphere** | Further UV absorption, meteor burnout | No direct effect on PV | | **Stratosphere** | O₃ absorbs UV-B and UV-C (200–315 nm) | Protects PV polymer encapsulants from UV degradation | | **Troposphere** | Rayleigh/Mie scattering, water vapor absorption | Reduces direct beam; creates diffuse component; **most impactful for ground-mounted PV** | --- ## 3.5 Sample Numerical Problem (KTU Pattern) **Problem:** A roof-top solar PV array of area 20 m² is to be installed at a location with an average daily solar insolation of 5.5 kWh/m²·day. If the system efficiency is 18%, calculate the daily electrical energy output. **Solution:** **Step 1:** Daily solar energy incident on the array: $$E_{inc} = A \times H = 20 \times 5.5 = 110 \text{ kWh/day}$$ **Step 2:** Apply the system efficiency: $$E_{out} = E_{inc} \times \eta = 110 \times 0.18 = 19.8 \text{ kWh/day}$$ **Final Answer:** The daily electrical energy output is **19.8 kWh/day**. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Solar Energy Path from Sun to PV Module ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Sun Core
1.5 crore K
Fusion of H to He] --> B[Radiative Zone
Photon diffusion
Lakhs of years] B --> C[Convective Zone
Plasma convection
Granulation cells] C --> D[Photosphere
5778 K
Visible surface] D --> E[Chromosphere and Corona
Solar wind
UV and X ray emission] E --> F[Interplanetary Space
Travel time 8.3 min
Speed 3 lakh km per s] F --> G[Earth Upper Atmosphere
Exosphere and Thermosphere
X ray and UV absorption] G --> H[Stratosphere
Ozone O3 layer
UV B and UV C filtered] H --> I[Troposphere
Clouds, aerosols, dust
Scattering and absorption] I --> J[Earth Surface
Direct plus Diffuse radiation
Typical 200 to 1000 W per m2] J --> K[PV Module
Photovoltaic effect
DC electricity output] K --> L[Inverter
DC to AC conversion] L --> M[AC Load or Grid
Useful electrical work] ``` > [!NOTE] > Each block represents a key stage in the journey of solar energy from its **point of origin (nuclear fusion in the Sun's core)** to its **final utilization as electrical power**. The total losses from core-to-load are immense (> 99.9%), with the majority occurring in the Sun's radiative zone and during atmospheric transit. --- ## 4.2 Block Diagram — Vertical Structure of Earth's Atmosphere ```mermaid flowchart TB L1[Exosphere
Altitude above 600 km
H and He dominant
Satellite orbits] --> L2 L2[Thermosphere
Altitude 85 to 600 km
Temperature up to 1500 C
Ionosphere and Auroras] --> L3 L3[Mesosphere
Altitude 50 to 85 km
Coldest layer minus 90 C
Meteors burn up] --> L4 L4[Stratosphere
Altitude 12 to 50 km
Ozone layer O3
UV absorption] --> L5 L5[Troposphere
Altitude 0 to 12 km
Weather and clouds
75 percent of air mass] --> L6 L6[Earth Surface
Solar PV arrays installed
Insolation 4 to 7 kWh per m2 per day] L1 -.Space boundary.-> SPACE[Outer Space] ``` --- ## 4.3 Block Diagram — Functional Architecture: Energy Flow in a Solar PV System ```mermaid flowchart LR SRC[Energy Source
Sun Fusion Core] --> RAD[Radiation Transfer
Photosphere to Atmosphere] RAD --> ATR[Atmospheric Attenuation
Scattering and Absorption] ATR --> COLL[PV Array
DC Electricity Output] COLL --> CONV[Power Conditioning
MPPT and Inverter] CONV --> STO[Storage Option
Battery Bank] CONV --> LOAD[AC Load and Grid Export] STO --> CONV LOAD --> USAGE[End Use
Lighting and Motors] ATR -.Albedo losses.-> REF[Reflected Radiation
Wasted Energy] COLL -.Thermal losses.-> HEAT[Heat Dissipation
Cell Heating] CONV -.Conversion losses.-> INVL[Inverter Losses
Typically 2 to 5 percent] ``` --- ## 4.4 Energy Transformation Chain (Sun → Electricity) ```mermaid flowchart TD A1[Nuclear Energy
Hydrogen to Helium Fusion] --> A2 A2[Radiant Energy
Electromagnetic Spectrum] --> A3 A3[Thermal Energy
Heating of PV Cell] --> A4 A4[Electrical Energy
Photovoltaic Conversion] --> A5 A5[Stored Chemical Energy
Battery Charging] --> A6 A6[Mechanical Energy
Appliance Operation] A2 -.Scattered.-> A7[Diffuse Light
Not fully usable] A4 -.Conduction and Convection.-> A8[Convective Loss
Hot air to atmosphere] ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > Notice that at **every transformation step**, a portion of energy is lost (mostly as heat). This is the **Second Law of Thermodynamics** in action — energy conversion is never 100% efficient. A real-world PV system has an overall **efficiency of 15–22%**, meaning ~78–85% of incident solar energy is wasted. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define Energy. List any four forms of energy with one example each. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Model Answer:** **Definition:** Energy is defined as the capacity of a system to do work or transfer heat. Mathematically, it is measured in Joules (J) in SI units, and it is governed by the First Law of Thermodynamics (conservation of energy). **Four Forms of Energy:** | # | Form | Description | Example | |---|------|-------------|---------| | 1 | **Kinetic Energy** | Energy of motion | Wind blowing a turbine blade | | 2 | **Potential Energy** | Energy of position | Water stored in a reservoir | | 3 | **Thermal Energy** | Energy due to temperature | Sun heating a rooftop | | 4 | **Electrical Energy** | Energy of moving charges | Power from a PV module | **Conclusion:** Energy is a conserved scalar quantity that exists in many interconvertible forms, with solar energy being the most abundant renewable source for PV applications. --- ### Question 2: What is the Solar Constant? State its standard value and significance. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Model Answer:** **Definition:** The Solar Constant ($G_{sc}$) is the total solar radiation flux density received on a unit area perpendicular to the Sun's rays, at the mean Earth-Sun distance, **outside the Earth's atmosphere**. **Standard Value:** $G_{sc} = 1{,}367 \text{ W/m}^2$ (WMO standard, 1981) **Significance:** 1. It serves as the **upper limit** of solar energy available at the top of the atmosphere. 2. It is the **reference input** for all extraterrestrial radiation calculations ($G_{on}$). 3. It is used to **calibrate satellite-based solar irradiance sensors** (e.g., ERBS, SORCE). 4. It is essential for **PV system sizing** and **annual yield estimation** models. **Derivation Hint:** $G_{sc} = \sigma T_s^4 (R_s / d)^2$ (Stefan-Boltzmann law applied to Sun as blackbody). --- ## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > As per **KTU 2024 Scheme**, Part B questions carry **14 marks** with **internal choice** (either-or pattern). Each question is split into two sub-parts: **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. --- ### 🔷 Question A (14 Marks) #### Q.A(a) [7 Marks]: Explain the formation of Earth's atmosphere. Discuss the role of photosynthesis in modifying its composition. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Model Answer:** **Introduction:** The Earth's atmosphere is a complex, dynamic gaseous envelope that has evolved over ~4.5 billion years through geological and biological processes. It is essential for sustaining life, regulating climate, and influencing solar radiation reaching the surface. **Stages of Atmospheric Formation:** **Stage 1 — Accretion and Primary Atmosphere (~4.6 BYA):** During Earth's formation, volatile elements were trapped within the molten planet. Continuous volcanic outgassing released these gases — primarily **water vapor (H₂O), carbon dioxide (CO₂), methane (CH₄), ammonia (NH₃), and nitrogen (N₂)**. This reducing atmosphere was toxic by modern standards. **Stage 2 — Cooling and Ocean Formation (~4.0 BYA):** As Earth's surface temperature dropped below 100°C, water vapor condensed to form the **primitive oceans**. Much of the CO₂ dissolved into seawater and precipitated as carbonate rocks (e.g., limestone), dramatically reducing atmospheric CO₂ levels. **Stage 3 — Origin of Life (~3.8 BYA):** Simple organic molecules formed in the "primordial soup," eventually leading to **prokaryotic life forms** in the oceans. **Stage 4 — Photosynthesis and Oxygen Revolution (~2.4 BYA):** Cyanobacteria evolved the ability to perform **oxygenic photosynthesis**: $$6CO_2 + 6H_2O \xrightarrow{\text{light}} C_6H_{12}O_6 + 6O_2$$ This gradually transformed the atmosphere from a **reducing** to an **oxidizing** one, accumulating free O₂. **Stage 5 — Ozone Layer Formation (~600 MYA):** UV radiation split O₂ into atomic oxygen, which combined with O₂ to form **ozone (O₃)** in the stratosphere: $$O_2 + O \rightarrow O_3$$ The ozone layer absorbed harmful UV-B and UV-C, allowing **life to colonize land**. **Stage 6 — Modern Atmosphere:** Today's atmosphere contains 78% N₂, 21% O₂, 0.93% Ar, and 0.04% CO₂ (rising due to fossil fuel combustion). **Conclusion:** Photosynthesis was the single most important biological process shaping atmospheric composition, transforming Earth from a hostile, reducing environment into the oxygen-rich, life-supporting atmosphere of today. --- #### Q.A(b) [7 Marks]: With a neat sketch, describe the vertical structure of Earth's atmosphere. State the temperature variation in each layer. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Model Answer:** **Introduction:** The Earth's atmosphere is divided into **five distinct layers** based on temperature gradients, composition, and physical phenomena. Each layer has unique characteristics that influence solar radiation propagation. **Vertical Layers (from ground to space):** | # | Layer | Altitude | Temperature Trend | Key Phenomena | |---|-------|----------|-------------------|---------------| | 1 | **Troposphere** | 0 – 12 km | Decreases (lapse rate ≈ −6.5 K/km) | Weather, clouds, convection | | 2 | **Stratosphere** | 12 – 50 km | Increases (due to ozone UV absorption) | Ozone layer, jet streams | | 3 | **Mesosphere** | 50 – 85 km | Decreases (coldest layer, ~−90°C) | Meteors burn up | | 4 | **Thermosphere** | 85 – 600 km | Increases sharply (up to 1500°C) | Ionosphere, auroras, ISS | | 5 | **Exosphere** | > 600 km | Gradual merge with space | Hydrogen, helium, satellites | **Temperature Profile Sketch:** ``` Altitude (km) 600 ┤ ┌────── Exosphere │ │ (merges with space) 400 ┤ │ │ │ ┌─── Thermosphere 200 ┤ │ │ (T ↑) │ │ │ 85 ┤────┤ ├─── Mesopause (T min ≈ −90°C) │ │ │ 50 ┤ ──┤ ├─── Stratopause │ / │ │ 12 ┤/ │ ├─── Tropopause │ │ │ 0 ┴────┴──┴───── Earth Surface Temperature ``` **Detailed Description:** - **Troposphere:** Contains ~75% of atmospheric mass; temperature decreases with height due to adiabatic expansion of rising air; all weather phenomena occur here. The **tropopause** is the boundary where temperature stops decreasing. - **Stratosphere:** Temperature **rises** with height due to **ozone absorption of UV radiation**; nearly cloud-free, ideal for jet aircraft. - **Mesosphere:** Coldest region of atmosphere; temperature reaches minimum at **mesopause (~85 km)**; meteors burn up here. - **Thermosphere:** Temperature rises dramatically due to absorption of high-energy X-rays and UV; the **ionosphere** within this layer reflects radio waves and hosts the **aurora borealis**. - **Exosphere:** Outer fringe where atoms escape into space; contains the **Van Allen radiation belts** and most Earth-orbiting satellites. **Conclusion:** Understanding the layered structure of the atmosphere is essential for solar PV engineers because each layer modifies the solar spectrum differently — affecting both the intensity and spectral quality of radiation reaching PV modules. --- ### 🔷 Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice #### Q.B(a) [7 Marks]: What is the source of solar energy? Explain the Proton-Proton chain reaction that powers the Sun. Derive the expression for the Solar Constant. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Model Answer:** **Source of Solar Energy:** The Sun is a **G2V-type main-sequence star** that has been radiating energy for ~4.6 billion years. Its energy source is **thermonuclear fusion** in the core, where temperatures reach ~15 million Kelvin and pressures are 250 billion atmospheres. **The Proton-Proton (P-P) Chain:** The P-P chain is a series of nuclear reactions converting four hydrogen nuclei (protons) into one helium nucleus, releasing two positrons, two neutrinos, and ~26.7 MeV of energy. It proceeds in three branches; the dominant is **PPI**: **Step 1:** Two protons fuse to form a deuterium nucleus, emitting a positron and a neutrino: $$p + p \rightarrow d + e^+ + \nu_e$$ **Step 2:** The deuterium fuses with another proton to form helium-3, releasing a gamma photon: $$d + p \rightarrow {}^3He + \gamma$$ **Step 3:** Two helium-3 nuclei fuse to form helium-4, releasing two protons back into the cycle: $${}^3He + {}^3He \rightarrow {}^4He + 2p$$ **Net Reaction (PPI):** $$4p \rightarrow {}^4He + 2e^+ + 2\nu_e + 2\gamma$$ The mass defect (4 protons = 4.0304 u vs. helium-4 = 4.0026 u; Δm = 0.0278 u) is converted to energy per $E = mc^2$, yielding ~26.7 MeV per cycle. **Derivation of Solar Constant:** See Section 3.1. Final result: $$\boxed{G_{sc} = \sigma T_s^4 \left(\frac{R_s}{d}\right)^2 = 1{,}367 \text{ W/m}^2}$$ **Conclusion:** The P-P chain is the dominant energy source in the Sun, providing the radiant energy that sustains life on Earth and is harnessed by PV technology. The solar constant of 1,367 W/m² is the fundamental input parameter for all solar energy calculations. --- #### Q.B(b) [7 Marks]: Explain the various phenomena by which solar radiation interacts with the Earth's atmosphere. Define Air Mass and state its significance for PV module testing. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Model Answer:** **Interaction of Solar Radiation with the Atmosphere:** As solar radiation traverses the atmosphere from space to the surface, it undergoes three principal interactions: **1. Absorption:** Molecules selectively absorb photons of specific wavelengths. The major absorbers are: - **Ozone (O₃):** Absorbs UV-B (280–315 nm) and UV-C (100–280 nm) in the stratosphere. - **Water vapor (H₂O):** Absorbs near-IR (> 700 nm). - **Carbon dioxide (CO₂):** Absorbs far-IR (> 1,500 nm), contributing to greenhouse effect. - **Oxygen (O₂):** Absorbs UV below 200 nm in the thermosphere. **2. Scattering:** Particles and molecules redirect light out of the direct beam. Three types: - **Rayleigh scattering:** By molecules much smaller than wavelength; $\propto 1/\lambda^4$. Causes blue sky. - **Mie scattering:** By particles comparable to wavelength (aerosols, dust); wavelength-independent. - **Non-selective scattering:** By large particles (water droplets); equal at all visible wavelengths → white clouds. **3. Reflection:** Clouds, snow, and the ground surface reflect incident radiation. Quantified by **Albedo (α)**: - Fresh snow: α = 0.80–0.90 - Sand: α = 0.30–0.40 - Grassland: α = 0.20–0.25 - Water (high sun): α = 0.05 **Definition of Air Mass:** $$\boxed{AM = \frac{1}{\cos\theta_z}}$$ where $\theta_z$ is the **solar zenith angle** (angle between Sun and vertical). **Significance for PV Module Testing:** - **AM0** = 0 (above atmosphere) — used for space-grade solar cells. - **AM1** = 1 (Sun at zenith) — rarely achieved outside tropics. - **AM1.5G** = 1.5 (Sun at 48.2°) — **International standard** for terrestrial PV testing at STC (1,000 W/m², 25°C). - **AM2** = 2 (Sun at 60°) — used for tropical low-altitude installations. **Conclusion:** Atmospheric interactions reduce extraterrestrial radiation to a smaller surface value, and the Air Mass concept standardizes the spectrum used to rate PV modules — ensuring comparable performance data worldwide. --- ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:** > > 1. **Mixing up Albedo and Emissivity** — Albedo (α) refers to *solar* reflectance (0–1, dimensionless); Emissivity (ε) refers to *thermal* radiation emission. Students frequently interchange them. > 2. **Forgetting the eccentricity correction factor** in $G_{on}$ formula: $(1 + 0.033\cos(360n/365))$ is **NOT** a constant; it varies with day number $n$. > 3. **Wrong units** — Solar constant in W/m², daily insolation in kWh/m²·day, and extraterrestrial radiation $H_o$ in J/m²·day (must be converted to Wh/m²·day using ÷3600). > 4. **Confusing AM0, AM1, AM1.5G, AM2** — Remember: AM1.5G is the **STC standard**, not AM1. > 5. **Sketch without axis labels** — In atmospheric structure questions, the examiner expects a **temperature vs. altitude graph** with both axes labeled and units mentioned. A diagram without axis labels loses 1–2 marks. > 6. **Writing "Sun is hot" instead of "Sun is a G2V main-sequence star with effective blackbody temperature of 5,778 K"** — Precision matters in KTU board exams. > 7. **Deriving solar constant without showing each substitution step** — Always show the numerical substitution, not just the formula. --- ## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - [x] **Energy** is the capacity to do work; measured in **Joules (J)**; conserved per **First Law of Thermodynamics**. - [x] **Renewable sources** (Solar, Wind, Hydro, Biomass) replenish naturally; **non-renewable** (Coal, Oil, Gas) are finite. - [x] The **Sun** is a **G2V star** with surface temperature **5,778 K** and radius **6.96 × 10⁸ m**. - [x] Solar energy is generated by **thermonuclear fusion** in the core, primarily via the **Proton-Proton (P-P) chain** ($4p \to {}^4He + \text{energy}$). - [x] **Einstein's relation** $E = mc^2$ governs the mass-to-energy conversion in the Sun. - [x] **Solar constant** $G_{sc} = 1{,}367 \text{ W/m}^2$ (standard value, outside atmosphere, perpendicular to rays). - [x] **Stefan-Boltzmann law** $M_b = \sigma T^4$ is used to derive the solar constant: $G_{sc} = \sigma T_s^4 (R_s/d)^2$. - [x] **Earth's atmosphere** consists of 78% N₂, 21% O₂, 0.93% Ar, 0.04% CO₂ (dry air basis). - [x] The atmosphere evolved in **5 stages**: Outgassing → Ocean formation → Life → Photosynthesis → Modern composition. - [x] **Photosynthesis** ($6CO_2 + 6H_2O \to C_6H_{12}O_6 + 6O_2$) transformed the atmosphere from reducing to oxidizing. - [x] **Five atmospheric layers** (in ascending order): **Troposphere, Stratosphere, Mesosphere, Thermosphere, Exosphere**. - [x] The **Ozone layer** in the stratosphere absorbs UV-B and UV-C, protecting life and PV encapsulants. - [x] **Three atmospheric interactions** with solar radiation: **Absorption, Scattering, Reflection** (Albedo). - [x] **Albedo** (α) ranges: Fresh snow ≈ 0.85, Forest ≈ 0.12, Ocean ≈ 0.06. - [x] **Air Mass** $AM = 1/\cos\theta_z$; **AM1.5G** is the STC standard for terrestrial PV modules (1,000 W/m², 25°C). - [x] **STC (Standard Test Conditions)**: 1,000 W/m², AM1.5G spectrum, 25°C cell temperature. - [x] **Beer's-Lambert transmittance** $G = G_{on} \cdot \tau_a$ where $\tau_a = e^{-k \cdot m}$ is used to model clear-sky irradiance. - [x] Always remember the **constants**: $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$, $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$, $c = 3 \times 10^8$, $G_{sc} = 1{,}367$ W/m². > [!TIP] > **Last-Minute KTU Exam Strategy:** For 14-mark questions, always begin with a **diagram** (worth 2–3 marks by itself), follow with **definitions** and **classification tables**, then proceed to **derivations** with full numerical substitution, and conclude with a **practical application** statement. This structure guarantees full marks under KTU valuation.

topics.title: Solar Spectrum, Solar Constant, Air Mass, Solar Time, Sun-Earth Angles, Solar Radiation, Instruments to Measure Solar Radiation, Pyrheliometer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEET721 - DESIGN OF SOLAR PV SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Solar Spectrum, Solar Constant, Air Mass, Solar Time, Sun-Earth Angles, Solar Radiation, Instruments to Measure Solar Radiation, Pyrheliometer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The Solar Spectrum

**Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology):** The solar spectrum is the continuous distribution of electromagnetic radiation emitted by the Sun, plotted as spectral irradiance (W/m²/nm) versus wavelength (nm). The extraterrestrial spectrum at the top of the Earth's atmosphere closely approximates a **Blackbody Spectrum at T ≈ 5778 K**, following Planck's Law of blackbody radiation.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Key Insight:** Roughly **48%** of solar energy lies in the visible band (380–780 nm), **45%** in the infrared (>780 nm), and only **7%** in the ultraviolet (<380 nm). PV cells (especially crystalline silicon) respond primarily to the visible + near-IR range (400–1100 nm).

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine the Sun as a giant incandescent light bulb glowing at nearly 5800 K. Just as a hot piece of metal glows red, then orange, then white as temperature rises, the Sun's surface emits light across every colour of the rainbow and beyond. The "spectrum" is essentially a fingerprint showing *how much energy arrives at each colour (wavelength)*. When this light hits Earth's atmosphere, molecules like ozone, oxygen, water vapour, and CO₂ "eat" certain colours, leaving dark bands (Fraunhofer lines) — these are the absorption fingerprints of the solar atmosphere and Earth's atmosphere combined.

---

### 1.2 The Solar Constant (G_SC)

**Formal Definition:** The **Solar Constant** is defined as the total solar electromagnetic radiation (direct + diffuse) received per unit area per unit time on a surface oriented **perpendicular to the Sun's rays** at the **mean Earth–Sun distance** of **1 Astronomical Unit (1 AU = 1.496 × 10¹¹ m)**, at the **top of the Earth's atmosphere (TOA)**, and integrated over all wavelengths.

**Standard Value (WMO, NASA):**

$$G_{sc} = 1367 \ \text{W/m}^2 \approx 1.361 \ \text{kW/m}^2$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Value:** Most KTU numerical problems and answer keys use **G_sc = 1367 W/m²**. Always state units explicitly to earn full credit.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Think of the Solar Constant as the "untaxed salary" of sunlight. Before the Earth's atmosphere takes its cut (via reflection, scattering, and absorption), every square metre of cross-sectional area facing the Sun receives 1367 watts — enough to power a small electric heater. Once light enters the atmosphere, it gets "taxed" by clouds, dust, and air molecules, and the ground receives a reduced amount.

---

### 1.3 Air Mass (AM)

**Formal Definition:** **Air Mass (AM)** is a dimensionless quantity that quantifies the path length of direct solar radiation through the Earth's atmosphere, normalized to the shortest possible path (Sun directly overhead, i.e., zenith).

**Standard Reference (KTU Convention):**

$$AM = \frac{1}{\cos(\theta_z)} = \sec(\theta_z)$$

where $\theta_z$ is the **Solar Zenith Angle** (angle between the Sun's rays and the vertical at the observer's location).

| Condition | Zenith Angle θ_z | Air Mass |
|-----------|------------------|----------|
| Sun directly overhead (equator, equinox noon) | 0° | AM 1.0 |
| Sun at 60° elevation | 30° | AM 1.155 |
| Sun at 30° elevation (golden hour) | 60° | AM 2.0 |
| Sun at the horizon (sunrise/sunset) | 90° | AM ∞ (≈38) |

**Standard Spectra (used in PV industry and KTU problems):**
- **AM 0**: Extraterrestrial spectrum (used for space applications)
- **AM 1.5G**: Global standard for terrestrial PV modules (tilted at 37°, US standard)
- **AM 1.5D**: Direct + circumsolar only (concentrator PV standard)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Quick Fact:** The IEC 61215 and 61730 standards (which govern all commercial PV module certification) require electrical performance testing under the **AM 1.5G** spectrum at **1000 W/m²** irradiance and **25 °C** cell temperature.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine sunlight passing through a swimming pool. When the Sun is directly overhead, the light enters and exits almost straight down — a short path, minimal filtering. When the Sun is low on the horizon, light has to travel *diagonally* through many metres of water, picking up more dust, getting scattered, and arriving weaker. The **Air Mass** is simply "how many times thicker is the air path compared to the noon-minimum path." The higher the AM, the bluer and weaker the light becomes.

---

### 1.4 Solar Time (Solar Hour Angle and Equation of Time)

**Formal Definition:** **Solar Time (or Local Apparent Time, LAT)** is the time measured with reference to the Sun's actual position in the sky, as opposed to **Clock Time (Standard Time, LST)** which is set by political time zones. The relationship is given by:

$$t_{solar} = t_{clock} + E_oT + 4 \ \text{min} \times (\text{Longitude}_{std} - \text{Longitude}_{local})$$

where $E_{oT}$ is the **Equation of Time** in minutes.

**Equation of Time (E_oT) — KTU Formula:**

$$E_{oT} = 9.87 \sin(2B) - 7.53 \cos(B) - 1.5 \sin(B)$$

where the day-angle:

$$B = \frac{360(n-1)}{365} \ \text{(in degrees)}$$

with $n$ = day of the year (Jan 1 = 1).

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
A sundial reads *Solar Time*, but your wristwatch reads *Clock Time*. They almost agree, but the Earth orbits the Sun in an ellipse (not a circle) and its axis is tilted — so the Sun sometimes runs "fast" and sometimes "slow" by up to **±16 minutes** through the year. The Equation of Time is the "correction card" the Sun carries to keep its appointment with the clock.

---

### 1.5 Sun–Earth Angles (Solar Geometry)

The position of the Sun in the sky at any instant at any location is fully described by **two angles**:

| Angle | Symbol | Definition |
|-------|--------|------------|
| **Solar Declination** | $\delta$ | Angle between the Sun's rays and the Earth's equatorial plane. Varies from **−23.45°** (winter solstice) to **+23.45°** (summer solstice). |
| **Hour Angle** | $\omega$ | Angular displacement of the Sun from local solar noon, measured in the equatorial plane. Negative in morning, positive in afternoon. **ω = 0°** at solar noon, **ω = ±90°** at sunrise/sunset (on equinox). |
| **Solar Zenith Angle** | $\theta_z$ | Angle between the Sun's rays and the local vertical (zenith). |
| **Solar Altitude / Elevation Angle** | $\alpha$ | Angle between the Sun's rays and the horizontal plane. $\alpha = 90° - \theta_z$. |
| **Solar Azimuth Angle** | $\gamma_s$ | Angle measured in the horizontal plane from true North to the projection of the Sun's rays (clockwise looking down). |
| **Latitude** | $\phi$ | Angular position of the observer North of the equator. |

**Solar Declination (KTU Cooper's Equation):**

$$\delta = 23.45 \times \sin\left(\frac{360(284 + n)}{365}\right)$$

where $n$ = day of the year.

**Hour Angle:**

$$\omega = 15° \times (t_{solar} - 12) \ \ \text{(since Earth rotates 15°/hour)}$$

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Picture the Earth as a slowly wobbling spinning top, tilted at 23.45°. As it orbits the Sun through the year, the *apparent* north–south position of the Sun in our sky changes — this is captured by the **declination angle**. Within a single day, the Sun sweeps from East to West across the sky — this is the **hour angle**. Together they form a celestial coordinate system just like latitude and longitude, but for the Sun.

---

### 1.6 Solar Radiation (Types & Geometry)

**Formal Definition:** **Solar Radiation** arriving at a surface is classified into three components based on how it reaches the receiver:

1. **Direct (Beam) Normal Irradiance (DNI):** Radiation that arrives in a straight line from the Sun's disk, measured on a surface held *normal* to the Sun's rays.
2. **Diffuse Horizontal Irradiance (DHI):** Radiation scattered by atmospheric molecules, aerosols, and clouds, arriving from all directions of the sky dome.
3. **Global Horizontal Irradiance (GHI):** The total short-wave radiation on a horizontal surface. **GHI = DNI × cos(θ_z) + DHI**.

**The Cosine Law of Illumination (Lambert's Law):** The irradiance on a tilted surface is the dot product of the solar vector with the surface normal:

$$I_T = I_n \cos(\theta)$$

where $\theta$ is the **Angle of Incidence** between the Sun's ray and the surface normal.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Hold a sheet of paper under a flashlight. If the paper is *perpendicular* to the beam, you get a bright small circle. Tilt the paper, and the same light spreads out over a larger area, making the circle dimmer — that's the cosine law. In real life, a solar panel tilted at the right angle catches maximum energy; flat horizontal panels are dimmer because they "see" the Sun sideways for most of the day.

---

### 1.7 Instruments to Measure Solar Radiation & Pyrheliometer

**Formal Definition:** Solar radiation instruments are precision radiometric devices classified by their angular acceptance and the radiation component they measure. The principal instruments (per WMO and ISO 9060 standards) are:

| Instrument | Measures | Angular Field of View | Application |
|------------|----------|------------------------|-------------|
| **Pyrheliometer** | Direct Normal Irradiance (DNI) | ~5° half-angle | Concentrator PV, CSP plants |
| **Pyranometer** | Global Horizontal Irradiance (GHI) or Plane-of-Array (POA) | 2π steradian (hemisphere) | Flat-plate PV, weather stations |
| **Pyrgeometer** | Terrestrial (long-wave / IR) radiation | 2π steradian | Atmospheric studies |
| **Sunshine Recorder** | Duration of bright sunshine | Whole sky | Meteorology |
| **Albedometer** | Incoming + reflected solar (measures albedo) | 2π (up) + 2π (down) | Bifacial PV, climate |
| **Radiation Balance Meter** | Net radiation | 4π steradian | Energy balance studies |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Frequently Tested:** The **Pyrheliometer** and **Pyranometer** are the two most commonly asked instruments in KTU ESE questions. The classic comparison is: pyrheliometer has a *narrow* view of the Sun; pyranometer has a *wide* view of the *whole sky*.

**Pyrheliometer (KTU High-Priority Topic):**

A **Pyrheliometer** is an instrument designed to measure **Direct Normal Irradiance (DNI)** from the Sun, with a defined narrow aperture (typically 5° to 5.7° full opening angle as per ISO 9060) that excludes all diffuse sky radiation.

**Principle of Operation:**
The pyrheliometer consists of a thermopile (a series of thermocouple junctions) mounted at the base of a sealed tube (the collimator) with precision aperture(s) at the front. A small field-of-view limiting aperture ensures only radiation from the solar disk (and a 0.5° circumsolar halo) reaches the sensor. The blackened receiver absorbs the radiation, heating the hot junctions; the cold junctions are thermally masked, creating a **Seebeck EMF (µV)** proportional to the absorbed power. The instrument must be mounted on a **solar tracker** (equatorial or alt-azimuth) to keep the aperture pointed at the Sun throughout the day.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine a long cardboard tube held out toward the Sun. The narrow tube *excludes* all the soft blue skylight from the sides and lets in only the bright direct beam hitting the back. Place a chocolate bar at the back: it melts fastest when the tube points straight at the Sun. The pyrheliometer does exactly this, but instead of chocolate, it uses a precision blackened thermopile, and instead of melting, it generates a tiny measurable voltage. The voltage reading, multiplied by the calibration constant, gives the DNI in W/m².

## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Physics of the Solar Spectrum

**Blackbody Emission — Planck's Law:**

$$E_{\lambda} = \frac{2\pi h c^2}{\lambda^5 \left(e^{hc/\lambda k_B T} - 1\right)}$$

| Symbol | Meaning | Value |
|--------|---------|-------|
| $E_\lambda$ | Spectral emissive power (W/m²·m) | — |
| $h$ | Planck's constant | $6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s |
| $c$ | Speed of light in vacuum | $2.998 \times 10^8$ m/s |
| $k_B$ | Boltzmann constant | $1.381 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K |
| $T$ | Blackbody absolute temperature | Sun: **5778 K** |
| $\lambda$ | Wavelength (m) | — |

**Wien's Displacement Law (Peak Wavelength of Sun):**

$$\lambda_{max} = \frac{2.898 \times 10^{-3}}{T} = \frac{2.898 \times 10^{-3}}{5778} \approx 502 \ \text{nm} \quad \text{(green light)}$$

**Stefan–Boltzmann Total Emitted Power:**

$$E = \sigma T^4 = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 5778^4 \approx 6.33 \times 10^7 \ \text{W/m}^2$$

(surface of Sun)

**Derivation of G_sc from Stefan–Boltzmann Law:**

The Sun's total output power is $P = E \cdot 4\pi R_s^2$. At distance $d = 1\,\text{AU}$ from the Sun, the radiation spreads over a sphere of area $4\pi d^2$, so:

$$G_{sc} = \sigma T_s^4 \left(\frac{R_s}{d}\right)^2$$

with $R_s = 6.96 \times 10^8$ m, $d = 1.496 \times 10^{11}$ m:

$$G_{sc} = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 5778^4 \times \left(\frac{6.96 \times 10^8}{1.496 \times 10^{11}}\right)^2 \approx 1367 \ \text{W/m}^2$$

---

### 2.2 The Extraterrestrial Normal Irradiance on the n-th Day

The Earth orbits the Sun in an ellipse (eccentricity $e = 0.0167$). The Earth–Sun distance varies, so the actual extraterrestrial irradiance at TOA is:

$$G_{on} = G_{sc}\left(1 + 0.033 \cos\left(\frac{360\,n}{365}\right)\right)$$

This **±3.3%** variation gives the maximum in early January (perihelion) and minimum in early July (aphelion).

---

### 2.3 Air Mass Models (KTU Important)

**Simple Geometric Air Mass:**

$$AM = \frac{1}{\cos(\theta_z)}$$

**Kasten & Young (1989) Formula** — accounts for atmospheric curvature (KTU accepted in numerical problems):

$$AM = \frac{1}{\cos(\theta_z) + 0.50572 (96.07995 - \theta_z)^{-1.6364}}$$

where $\theta_z$ is in **degrees**. Valid for $\theta_z$ up to 90°.

**Pressure-Corrected Air Mass (for high-altitude sites):**

$$AM_{rel} = AM \times \frac{P}{P_0}$$

where $P$ = local atmospheric pressure, $P_0$ = sea-level standard pressure (101.325 kPa).

---

### 2.4 Solar Time & Hour Angle

**Hour Angle from Clock Time (with Equation of Time):**

$$\omega = 15° \times (t_{clock} - 12) + (\text{Long}_{std} - \text{Long}_{local}) + E_{oT} \times \frac{1°/4\text{min}}{1}$$

Or simplified (KTU form):

$$\omega = 15°(t_{solar} - 12) \quad \text{with} \quad t_{solar} = LST + E_{oT} + \frac{4(\text{Long}_{std} - \text{Long}_{local})}{60}$$

---

### 2.5 Sun–Earth Angle Master Equations

The **Pivotal Solar Geometry Equation** (declination, latitude, hour angle → zenith angle):

$$\cos(\theta_z) = \sin(\phi)\sin(\delta) + \cos(\phi)\cos(\delta)\cos(\omega)$$

**Solar Altitude:**

$$\sin(\alpha) = \sin(\phi)\sin(\delta) + \cos(\phi)\cos(\delta)\cos(\omega)$$

**Solar Azimuth Angle (from North, clockwise):**

$$\sin(\gamma_s) = \frac{\cos(\delta)\sin(\omega)}{\cos(\alpha)}$$

**Sunrise / Sunset Hour Angle:**

$$\omega_s = \cos^{-1}(-\tan(\phi)\tan(\delta))$$

**Day Length (hours):**

$$N = \frac{2}{15} \cos^{-1}(-\tan(\phi)\tan(\delta))$$

**Solar Incidence Angle on a Tilted Surface** (surface tilted at $\beta$ from horizontal, surface azimuth $\gamma$, solar azimuth $\gamma_s$):

$$\cos(\theta) = \sin(\delta)\sin(\phi - \beta) + \cos(\delta)\cos(\phi - \beta)\cos(\omega) \quad \text{(south-facing surface, }\gamma = 0)$$

More general form:

$$\cos(\theta) = \sin(\delta)\sin(\phi)\cos(\beta) - \sin(\delta)\cos(\phi)\sin(\beta)\cos(\gamma) + \cos(\delta)\cos(\phi)\cos(\beta)\cos(\omega) + \cos(\delta)\sin(\phi)\sin(\beta)\cos(\gamma)\cos(\omega) + \cos(\delta)\sin(\beta)\sin(\gamma)\sin(\omega)$$

---

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table A — Master Formula Reference (Print & Memorise for KTU ESE)**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|----------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Solar Constant | $G_{sc} = 1367$ | W/m² | Mean Earth–Sun distance, TOA, ⊥ to Sun |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Concept | Real-World Application in Solar PV Design |
|---------|-------------------------------------------|
| Solar Spectrum (AM 1.5G) | Standard test condition for ALL commercial PV module datasheet ratings. |
| Solar Constant | Used to compute extraterrestrial insolation, which is the **upper bound** in energy-yield studies. |
| Air Mass | Determines the *spectral mismatch* between field operation and STC — affects module efficiency in tropical vs high-latitude sites. |
| Solar Time / E_oT | Critical for accurate solar-tracking algorithms and PV-yield simulation software (PVSyst, SAM). |
| Sun–Earth Angles | Foundation of *optimum tilt-angle* and *azimuth* calculation. Used in fixed-tilt arrays. |
| Solar Radiation Components | Feed-in to **transposition models** (Isotropic, Hay-Davies, Perez) that convert GHI → POA (plane of array) irradiance. |
| Pyrheliometer | Direct input to **DNI maps** (e.g., SolarGIS, NSRDB) used to size concentrating solar power (CSP) and CPV plants. |

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Air Mass (Geometric)

**Step 1 — Setup of geometry:**
Consider a flat Earth approximation (valid for $\theta_z < 60°$). The Sun is at zenith angle $\theta_z$ from the vertical. A ray of light enters the atmosphere at point P, travels straight to the ground at observer O, traversing a slanted path $PO$ through a flat atmospheric slab of vertical thickness $H$.

**Step 2 — Path length computation:**
The horizontal projection of the path is $H/\cos\theta_z$ where $H$ is the atmospheric scale height (≈ vertical depth). But the *minimum* path (Sun overhead) is just $H$. The ratio:

$$AM = \frac{\text{Actual path length}}{\text{Minimum path length}} = \frac{H / \cos\theta_z}{H / 1} = \frac{1}{\cos\theta_z} = \sec\theta_z$$

**Step 3 — Result:**

$$\boxed{AM = \sec(\theta_z) = \frac{1}{\cos(\theta_z)}}$$

**Step 4 — Validity limits:**
This flat-Earth model breaks down when $\theta_z \to 90°$ because real atmospheres curve. The Kasten-Young empirical correction is then used (see Section 2.3).

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Pivotal Solar Geometry Equation

**Step 1 — Choose a coordinate system:**
Use the **Equatorial Coordinate System** centred on the observer. The Sun's position is described by $(\delta, \omega)$, the observer's location by latitude $\phi$.

**Step 2 — Construct the unit vectors:**
- Sun direction: $\hat{s} = (\cos\delta\cos\omega, \cos\delta\sin\omega, \sin\delta)$
- Zenith direction at observer: $\hat{z} = (0, \cos\phi, \sin\phi)$

**Step 3 — Take the dot product:**
The cosine of the zenith angle is the dot product of these unit vectors:

$$\cos\theta_z = \hat{s} \cdot \hat{z} = \cos\delta\cos\omega \cdot 0 + \cos\delta\sin\omega \cdot \cos\phi + \sin\delta \cdot \sin\phi$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange to the standard form:**

$$\boxed{\cos\theta_z = \sin\phi\sin\delta + \cos\phi\cos\delta\cos\omega}$$

This single equation unlocks essentially all solar geometry problems in KTU examinations.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of Day Length

**Step 1 — At sunrise/sunset, the Sun is on the horizon, i.e., $\theta_z = 90°$, so $\cos\theta_z = 0$.**
Setting the pivotal equation to zero:

$$\sin\phi\sin\delta + \cos\phi\cos\delta\cos\omega_s = 0$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $\cos\omega_s$:**

$$\cos\omega_s = -\frac{\sin\phi\sin\delta}{\cos\phi\cos\delta} = -\tan\phi\tan\delta$$

**Step 3 — Invert to find $\omega_s$:**

$$\omega_s = \cos^{-1}(-\tan\phi\tan\delta)$$

**Step 4 — Convert angular hours to clock hours:**
Earth rotates $15°$ per hour, so:

$$N = \frac{2 \omega_s}{15} = \frac{2}{15}\cos^{-1}(-\tan\phi\tan\delta) \quad \text{[hours]}$$

---

### 3.4 Worked Example 1: Compute Zenith, Altitude & Day Length

**Problem (KTU Typical):** Find the **solar zenith angle, solar altitude**, and **day length** at latitude $\phi = 13°$ N (Kerala – Trivandrum) on **March 21** (Equinox, n = 80) at **10:00 AM local solar time**.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute declination on n = 80:**

$$\delta = 23.45 \sin\left(\frac{360(284+80)}{365}\right) = 23.45 \sin\left(\frac{360 \times 364}{365}\right) = 23.45 \sin(358.85°)$$

$$\delta \approx 23.45 \times (-0.021) \approx -0.49° \approx 0°$$

On equinox, $\delta \approx 0°$ (Sun is on the equator).

**Step 2 — Hour Angle at 10:00 AM solar time:**

$$\omega = 15°(10 - 12) = -30°$$

(Negative = before solar noon = morning.)

**Step 3 — Zenith Angle:**

$$\cos\theta_z = \sin(13°)\sin(0°) + \cos(13°)\cos(0°)\cos(-30°)$$

$$\cos\theta_z = 0 + (0.9744)(1)(0.8660) = 0.8437$$

$$\theta_z = \cos^{-1}(0.8437) = 32.5°$$

**Step 4 — Solar Altitude:**

$$\alpha = 90° - 32.5° = 57.5°$$

**Step 5 — Day Length (equinox special case):**

On equinox, day length = **12 hours** everywhere on Earth (excluding refraction correction). This is a well-known KTU fact.

---

### 3.5 Worked Example 2: Air Mass on a Tilted Surface

**Problem:** At a site with latitude $\phi = 10°$ N, on a day when $\delta = 20°$, at 11:00 AM solar time, find the **Air Mass** using both flat-Earth and Kasten-Young formulas.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Hour Angle:**

$$\omega = 15(11 - 12) = -15°$$

**Step 2 — Zenith Angle (pivotal equation):**

$$\cos\theta_z = \sin 10° \sin 20° + \cos 10° \cos 20° \cos 15°$$

$$= (0.1736)(0.3420) + (0.9848)(0.9397)(0.9659)$$

$$= 0.0594 + 0.8941 = 0.9535$$

$$\theta_z = \cos^{-1}(0.9535) = 17.5°$$

**Step 3 — Flat-Earth AM:**

$$AM = \sec(17.5°) = 1/0.9535 = 1.0487 \approx 1.05$$

**Step 4 — Kasten-Young AM:**

$$AM_{KY} = \frac{1}{\cos 17.5° + 0.50572(96.08 - 17.5)^{-1.6364}}$$

$$= \frac{1}{0.9535 + 0.50572 \times (78.58)^{-1.6364}}$$

$$(78.58)^{1.6364} = 78.58^{1.6364}$$

Computing: $\ln(78.58) = 4.364$, so $1.6364 \times 4.364 = 7.141$, $e^{7.141} = 1268$

$$= \frac{1}{0.9535 + 0.50572/1268} = \frac{1}{0.9535 + 0.000399}$$

$$AM_{KY} \approx 1.0485 \approx 1.05$$

For low zenith angles, both formulas agree (as expected).

---

### 3.6 Worked Example 3: Extraterrestrial Radiation on a Horizontal Surface

**Problem (KTU ESE Type):** Compute the **daily extraterrestrial radiation $H_o$** on a horizontal surface at latitude $\phi = 20°$ N on day $n = 105$ (April 15).

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Declination on n = 105:**

$$\delta = 23.45 \sin\left(\frac{360(284+105)}{365}\right) = 23.45 \sin(383.34°)$$

$383.34° = 23.34°$ (subtract 360°)

$$\delta = 23.45 \sin(23.34°) = 23.45 \times 0.396 = 9.28°$$

**Step 2 — Sunrise Hour Angle:**

$$\omega_s = \cos^{-1}(-\tan 20° \tan 9.28°)$$

$$= \cos^{-1}(-(0.3640)(0.1634)) = \cos^{-1}(-0.0595)$$

$$\omega_s = 93.41°$$

**Step 3 — Extraterrestrial Irradiance:**

$$G_{on} = 1367(1 + 0.033\cos(360 \times 105/365)) = 1367(1 + 0.033\cos 103.56°)$$

$\cos 103.56° = -0.2326$, so $G_{on} = 1367(1 - 0.00768) = 1356.5$ W/m²

**Step 4 — Daily $H_o$:**

$$H_o = \frac{24}{\pi} \times 1356.5 \times [93.41° \times \frac{\pi}{180°} \times \sin 20° \sin 9.28° + \cos 20° \cos 9.28° \sin 93.41°]$$

Note: convert $\omega_s$ to radians: $93.41° \times \pi/180 = 1.630$ rad.

$$H_o = \frac{24}{\pi} \times 1356.5 \times [1.630 \times 0.3420 \times 0.1612 + 0.9397 \times 0.9869 \times \sin 93.41°]$$

$\sin 93.41° = 0.9982$

$$H_o = \frac{24}{\pi} \times 1356.5 \times [0.0899 + 0.9259]$$

$$H_o = \frac{24}{\pi} \times 1356.5 \times 1.0158 = \frac{24 \times 1378.3}{\pi} = \frac{33079}{3.1416}$$

$$H_o \approx 10529 \ \text{kJ/m}^2 \cdot \text{day} \approx 10.53 \ \text{MJ/m}^2 \cdot \text{day}$$

This is the standard KTU form of the daily extraterrestrial radiation on a horizontal surface.
