## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Q1]
**Define the term *Solar Constant*. What is its accepted standard value, and why is it called a "constant" despite the Earth-Sun distance varying through the year?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Definition (1.5 marks):** The Solar Constant is the total solar electromagnetic radiation received per unit area per unit time on a surface oriented **perpendicular** to the Sun's rays at the **mean Earth-Sun distance** of 1 AU, at the **top of Earth's atmosphere**, integrated over all wavelengths.
- **Value (0.5 mark):** $G_{sc} = 1367$ W/m² (per WMO, NASA standard).
- **Why "constant" (1 mark):** It is defined at the *mean* Earth-Sun distance. In reality, the actual extraterrestrial irradiance varies by ±3.3% due to the Earth's elliptical orbit; the *average* of these extremes gives the constant.

---

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Q2]
**Differentiate between a *Pyrheliometer* and a *Pyranometer* based on: (a) radiation component measured, (b) field of view, (c) need for a solar tracker.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Pyrheliometer | Pyranometer |
|---------|---------------|-------------|
| (a) Component | Direct Normal Irradiance (DNI) | Global Horizontal Irradiance (GHI) or Plane-of-Array (POA) |
| (b) Field of view | Narrow (5° half-angle, per ISO 9060) | Full hemisphere (2π steradian) |
| (c) Solar tracker | **Mandatory** — must point at Sun all day | **Not required** — has a glass dome covering 180° |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks, KTU Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Forgetting to convert **degrees ↔ radians** in the $\sin$ and $\cos$ arguments is the **#1 reason for losing marks** in solar geometry numericals. **All calculator-friendly formulas in KTU are written in degrees.**
> - Mixing up the **sign convention** for Hour Angle (morning = negative, afternoon = positive, noon = zero) is the **#2 reason**.
> - For declination, **Cooper's formula** uses day $n$ (1–365), with $n = 81$ (or 80) for spring equinox. **Always quote units (°) in final answers.**
> - In pyrheliometer descriptions, students often **omit the thermopile** and the **solar tracker** — both are mandatory to score full credit.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — Solar Geometry and Air Mass

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**

**(a)** *Derive the expression for the **solar declination angle** $\delta$ as a function of the day of the year. Briefly explain its physical significance. Mention the range of values. **(7 marks)***

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Definition (2 marks):**
The solar declination $\delta$ is the angle between the Sun's rays and the Earth's equatorial plane. It is caused by the **23.45° axial tilt** of the Earth. Physically, it represents the *north-south* position of the Sun in the sky as Earth orbits the Sun.

**Step 2 — Range (1 mark):** $\delta$ varies sinusoidally between **−23.45°** (Winter Solstice, ~Dec 21) and **+23.45°** (Summer Solstice, ~June 21). It is **0°** at the Equinoxes (March 21, September 23).

**Step 3 — Derivation of Cooper's Formula (4 marks):**
Cooper (1969) proposed a simple empirical sinusoidal fit to the exact astronomical formula:

$$\delta = 23.45 \sin\left(\frac{360(284 + n)}{365}\right)$$

This comes from the fact that the declination is approximately sinusoidal in time, with period 365.25 days, amplitude 23.45°, and a phase shift of 284 days (this makes $n = 81$ give $\delta = 0$, i.e., spring equinox).

- $n$ = day of the year (Jan 1 = 1)
- The argument $(284 + n)$ ensures that on March 21 ($n = 80$ or 81), the sine argument is $\approx 360° + \text{small}$, giving $\delta \approx 0°$.

**Validation (1 mark):** On $n = 172$ (June 21), the argument is $360 \times 456/365 = 449.75° = 89.75°$, $\sin 89.75° = 0.9999$, so $\delta \approx 23.45°$ ✓.

---

**(b)** *For a location at latitude **$\phi = 28.6°$ N** (Delhi) on day **$n = 172$** at **2:00 PM solar time**, compute the following: (i) declination $\delta$, (ii) hour angle $\omega$, (iii) zenith angle $\theta_z$, (iv) solar altitude $\alpha$, (v) Air Mass (flat-Earth). **(7 marks)***

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Declination (1.5 marks):**

$$\delta = 23.45 \sin\left(\frac{360(284+172)}{365}\right) = 23.45\sin\left(\frac{360 \times 456}{365}\right)$$

$$= 23.45 \sin(449.75°) = 23.45 \sin(89.75°) \approx 23.45 \times 0.99996 \approx 23.45°$$

**Step 2 — Hour Angle (1 mark):**

$$\omega = 15°(14 - 12) = 30°$$

(2:00 PM is 2 hours after solar noon; positive in the afternoon.)

**Step 3 — Zenith Angle (2 marks):**

$$\cos\theta_z = \sin(28.6°)\sin(23.45°) + \cos(28.6°)\cos(23.45°)\cos(30°)$$

$$= (0.4788)(0.3979) + (0.8779)(0.9174)(0.8660)$$

$$= 0.1905 + 0.6973 = 0.8878$$

$$\theta_z = \cos^{-1}(0.8878) = 27.38°$$

**Step 4 — Solar Altitude (1 mark):**

$$\alpha = 90° - 27.38° = 62.62°$$

**Step 5 — Air Mass (1.5 marks):**

$$AM = \frac{1}{\cos(27.38°)} = \frac{1}{0.8878} = 1.126 \approx \text{AM } 1.13$$

This is close to the standard **AM 1.5G** test spectrum used for PV modules.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — Pyrheliometer, Pyranometer & Radiation Components

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model — Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** *With a neat block diagram, explain the **construction and working principle of a Pyrheliometer**. What is the role of the solar tracker? Discuss the ISO 9060 classification. **(7 marks)***

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram (1 mark):** Refer to the Pyrheliometer Block Diagram in SECTION 4.3 above.

**Construction (3 marks):**

1. **Collimator tube:** A long precision-bored metal tube (typically 200–400 mm long) with a narrow aperture at the front, designed to admit only direct solar radiation within a 5° half-angle (per WMO/ISO 9060).
2. **Aperture(s):** One or two precision slits (front + secondary) that geometrically define the FOV and exclude sky/circumsolar radiation.
3. **Sensor:** A **blackened thermopile** at the base — a series of dissimilar-metal thermocouple junctions connected in series, with hot junctions coated in highly absorptive black paint (e.g., Parsons' optical black lacquer) and cold junctions in thermal contact with a heat sink.
4. **Sealed window (optional):** A sealed protective window of Schott glass (or quartz for extended UV) sometimes with a heater to prevent dew/ice.
5. **Body:** Weatherproof housing with electrical terminals.
6. **Solar tracker:** An **equatorial mount** (parallactic) or alt-azimuth two-axis tracker that keeps the tube pointed at the Sun with sub-0.5° accuracy throughout the day.

**Working Principle (2 marks):**
Direct solar radiation entering the collimator tube is absorbed by the blackened thermopile receiver, raising the temperature of the hot junctions. The cold junctions remain at ambient (or reference) temperature, hidden from the Sun. The temperature difference $\Delta T$ creates a **Seebeck EMF** at the thermopile terminals:

$$V = N \cdot S \cdot \Delta T$$

where $N$ = number of thermocouples, $S$ = Seebeck coefficient. The output voltage (in µV) is directly proportional to the absorbed power. Using the known **calibration constant** $C$ (µV per W/m², traceable to WRR — World Radiometric Reference at PMOD Davos, Switzerland):

$$E = \frac{V}{C} \quad [\text{W/m}^2]$$

**Role of Solar Tracker (0.5 mark):**
A pyrheliometer only "sees" the Sun within its 5° FOV. Without a tracker, the Sun's apparent motion (~15°/hour) would move it out of the FOV within minutes. The tracker compensates for Earth's rotation (and optionally for declination) to keep the instrument pointed at the Sun.

**ISO 9060 Classification (0.5 mark):**
- **Class A (Secondary Standard):** Best — uncertainties ±2% for hourly sums.
- **Class B (First Class):** ±5% uncertainty.
- **Class C (Second Class):** ±10% uncertainty.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Students often confuse **Class A = best, Class C = worst** — remember "A is Apex".

---

**(b)** *Explain the **three components of solar radiation** (DNI, DHI, GHI). With a diagram, show how the **global horizontal irradiance (GHI)** is related to the other two, and derive the expression. Also state the **cosine law of illumination** and its importance in PV design. **(7 marks)***

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Definitions (2 marks):**

- **DNI (Direct Normal Irradiance):** Radiation received from the *Sun's disk only* on a surface held **perpendicular** to the solar beam. Measured with a pyrheliometer. Units: W/m².
- **DHI (Diffuse Horizontal Irradiance):** Radiation *scattered* by atmospheric molecules, aerosols, and clouds, arriving from all directions of the sky dome. Measured by a pyranometer **shaded** from the Sun's direct beam. Units: W/m².
- **GHI (Global Horizontal Irradiance):** Total short-wave radiation on a *horizontal* plane. Measured by an unshaded pyranometer. Units: W/m².

**Step 2 — Geometric Relationship (2 marks):**
A horizontal surface receives:
- The **direct** component only the *vertical projection* of the beam: $DNI \times \cos\theta_z$ (the cosine law).
- The **diffuse** component in full: $DHI$ (the sky dome is already integrated over the horizontal).
- The **reflected** (albedo) component: $R \times GHI$, but for a horizontal black surface, this is zero.

**Step 3 — Equation (1 mark):**

$$\boxed{GHI = DNI \cdot \cos(\theta_z) + DHI}$$

**Step 4 — Cosine Law of Illumination (Lambert's Law) (1 mark):**

$$I_T = I_n \cos(\theta)$$

where $I_n$ = normal-incidence irradiance, $\theta$ = angle between ray and surface normal.

**Step 5 — Importance in PV Design (1 mark):**
- Determines the **incident power** on a tilted PV module at any time.
- A surface tilted at $\beta$ from horizontal receives $G_{T} = GHI \cdot R_b + DHI \cdot R_d + GHI \cdot \rho \cdot R_r$ (where $R_b$, $R_d$, $R_r$ are tilt factors and $\rho$ is albedo).
- A surface *perpendicular* to the Sun ($\theta = 0°$, $\cos\theta = 1$) receives maximum power — basis of **solar tracking** in CPV and CSP plants.
- For fixed flat-plate arrays, the cosine law dictates the **optimum tilt angle** ≈ $\phi - \delta$ (rule of thumb: latitude minus declination, with seasonal variation).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (High-Density Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🎯 KTU 2024 — Module 1 Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- ✅ **Solar Constant = 1367 W/m²** (top of atmosphere, ⊥ to Sun, mean Earth–Sun distance).
- ✅ **G_on = 1367 × (1 + 0.033 cos(360n/365))** — varies ±3.3% over the year.
- ✅ **Spectrum of Sun ≈ Blackbody at 5778 K**, peaks at ~502 nm (green). Visible = 48%, IR = 45%, UV = 7%.
- ✅ **Standard test spectrum for PV: AM 1.5G, 1000 W/m², 25 °C cell temp** (IEC 61215).
- ✅ **Air Mass AM = 1/cos(θ_z)**; for θ_z → 90° use Kasten-Young.
- ✅ **Declination δ = 23.45 sin(360(284+n)/365)** (Cooper's equation).
- ✅ **Hour Angle ω = 15(t_solar − 12)** (in degrees), negative AM, positive PM.
- ✅ **Pivotal solar geometry equation:** $\cos\theta_z = \sin\phi\sin\delta + \cos\phi\cos\delta\cos\omega$.
- ✅ **Day length N = (2/15) cos⁻¹(−tan φ tan δ)** hours.
- ✅ **GHI = DNI cos θ_z + DHI**; **Cosine Law: I_T = I_n cos θ**.
- ✅ **Pyrheliometer** measures DNI (narrow 5° FOV, needs solar tracker, has thermopile).
- ✅ **Pyranometer** measures GHI (hemisphere 180° FOV, no tracker needed, has glass dome).
- ✅ **Pyrheliometer ISO 9060 classes:** A (best, ±2%), B (±5%), C (±10%).
- ✅ **Equation of Time:** $E_{oT} = 9.87 \sin(2B) - 7.53 \cos B - 1.5 \sin B$, where $B = 360(n-1)/365$.
- ✅ **Solar Time = Clock + EoT + 4 min × (Long_std − Long_local)**.
- ✅ **Fraunhofer lines** = dark absorption lines in solar spectrum (e.g., H-α at 656.3 nm).
- ✅ **Solar altitude α = 90° − θ_z**; always compute $\cos\theta_z$ first, then take $\cos^{-1}$ in degrees.
- ✅ **Threshold of "AM 1.5G"** is at θ_z ≈ 48.2° (the official IEC standard).
- ✅ **Kerala latitudes:** 8.18° N (Trivandrum) to 12.79° N (Kasaragod) — all sub-equatorial.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pyranometer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEET721 - DESIGN OF SOLAR PV SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Pyranometer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Pyranometer** is a precision radiometric instrument used to measure the **total solar irradiance (W/m²)** falling on a planar surface, integrated over the entire solar spectrum (typically **300 nm to 2800 nm**, per ISO 9060:2018). It captures both the **direct beam radiation** and the **diffuse sky radiation**, providing what is known as the **Global Horizontal Irradiance (GHI)** when mounted horizontally, or the **Global Tilted Irradiance (GTI)** when mounted on an inclined surface.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> The pyranometer is a cornerstone of **Module 1** because accurate solar resource assessment is the *first non-negotiable step* in the design of any grid-tied or off-grid solar PV system. Without irradiance data, no module sizing, inverter sizing, or energy-yield estimation is possible.

Mathematically, the global irradiance measured by a pyranometer is expressed as:

$$G_{global} = G_{direct} + G_{diffuse}$$

where $G_{direct}$ is the Direct Normal Irradiance (DNI) projected on the horizontal plane and $G_{diffuse}$ is the Diffuse Horizontal Irradiance (DHI).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Solar Thermometer"

Imagine a **highly precise, ultra-dark black painting** left out under the sun. The black surface absorbs almost all incoming sunlight and converts it into heat. A pyranometer is essentially a **scientific version of this black absorber**, but instead of letting that heat raise its temperature, it uses an array of microscopic thermocouples (a *thermopile*) to convert the temperature difference between the hot absorber and a reference body into a tiny electrical voltage.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Intuition:** Think of a pyranometer as a **"smart sun-thermometer"** — it doesn't just feel the heat; it *quantifies* the energy flux of sunlight striking a surface, with a level of accuracy traceable to international standards (WMO, ISO 9060).

## 1.3 Working Principle — At a Glance

- **Absorption:** A black-painted absorber surface absorbs incoming shortwave solar radiation.
- **Temperature Difference:** The absorbed energy heats the absorber above the temperature of the instrument body.
- **Thermoelectric Conversion:** A **thermopile** (series of thermocouples) generates a **Seebeck EMF** proportional to the temperature gradient.
- **Signal Conditioning:** The millivolt-level signal is conditioned and converted to W/m² using a **calibration factor**.

## 1.4 The Solar Constant — A Reference Anchor

The **Total Solar Irradiance (TSI)** at the top of the Earth's atmosphere, measured on a plane perpendicular to the sun's rays, is the **Solar Constant**:

$$G_{sc} = 1367 \text{ W/m}^2 \quad (\text{standard value, WMO recommended: } 1361 \text{ W/m}^2)$$

This is the *upper theoretical bound* of what a pyranometer placed outside the atmosphere could read. At ground level, due to atmospheric attenuation (Rayleigh scattering, Mie scattering, absorption by ozone, water vapor, CO₂), the value drops to roughly **1000 W/m²** at solar noon on a clear day (AM 1.5 reference spectrum).

## 1.5 Why a Pyranometer Matters in Solar PV Design

> [!NOTE]
> In **solar PV design**, the pyranometer is the **"ground truth" sensor**. PV system performance simulations (using PVSyst, SAM, PVLib, etc.) require measured irradiance data, not just satellite-derived estimates, for:
> - Performance Ratio (PR) calculation
> - Bankable energy yield assessment
> - Module and inverter sizing validation
> - Soiling loss quantification
> - Long-term degradation monitoring
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Types of Pyranometers (KTU 2024 High-Yield)

There are **two principal families** of pyranometers:

### A. Thermal (Thermopile) Pyranometers — The "True" Pyranometer

- Use a **blackened thermopile** to measure absorbed radiation.
- Conform to **ISO 9060:2018** classification.
- Spectral response: **300 nm to 2800 nm** (covers ~97% of solar spectrum).
- High accuracy and cosine response.
- Used in **Class A** meteorological stations, research, and utility-scale PV plants.

### B. Photoelectric (Silicon) Pyranometers — The "Smart" Pyranometer

- Use a **silicon photodiode** (similar to PV cell technology) to estimate irradiance.
- Spectral response: **400 nm to 1100 nm** (narrow band only).
- Lower cost, lower accuracy, **not ISO 9060 compliant**.
- Used in **rooftop and small PV systems** where cost dominates.

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical KTU Pitfall:** Silicon pyranometers are **NOT true pyranometers**. They only estimate the broadband solar irradiance by measuring a narrow spectral band and applying a model-based correction. For bankable projects and KTU numerical problems, always assume a **thermopile pyranometer** unless stated otherwise.

## 2.2 ISO 9060:2018 Classification

The ISO 9060 standard classifies pyranometers into three classes based on accuracy, response time, cosine response, and temperature dependence:

| ISO 9060 Class | Old Terminology | Accuracy (Daily Totals) | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Class A** | Secondary Standard | ± 5% | Research, primary meteorological stations |
| **Class B** | First Class | ± 8% | High-quality PV plant monitoring |
| **Class C** | Second Class | ± 10% | Budget installations, secondary monitoring |

## 2.3 Construction of a Thermopile Pyranometer

A precision thermopile pyranometer consists of the following key components:

1. **Outer Glass Dome(s):** One or two concentric hemispherical domes made of Schott BK7 or similar optical glass. The dome(s) protect the sensor and create a **greenhouse effect** that minimizes convective heat loss while transmitting the solar spectrum.
2. **Black Absorber (Detector):** A circular black-painted (typically **3M Nextel Velvet Black 101-C10** or similar) target surface that absorbs broadband solar radiation uniformly.
3. **Thermopile Sensor:** A series of **hot junctions** beneath the absorber connected to **cold junctions** in the instrument body, generating a **Seebeck EMF**.
4. **Reference Body / Heatsink:** Maintains the cold junction at instrument body temperature.
5. **Desiccant Cartridge:** Prevents internal condensation (humidity damages thermopile).
6. **Leveling Base & Bubble Level:** Ensures horizontal mounting for GHI measurement.
7. **Cable & Signal Output:** Typically 0–20 mV DC for thermopile; 4–20 mA or Modbus for digital variants.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they are the core of KTU Module 1 numericals.**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $G_{global} = G_{beam} \cos(\theta_z) + G_{diffuse}$ | Decomposition of GHI into beam and diffuse on horizontal surface | W/m² |
| 2 | $G_{sc} = 1367 \text{ W/m}^2$ | Solar constant at top of atmosphere (TOA) | W/m² |
| 3 | $G_{on} = G_{sc} \cdot (1 + 0.033 \cos(360n/365))$ | Extraterrestrial irradiance on day $n$ | W/m² |
| 4 | $AM = 1/\cos(\theta_z)$ | Air Mass ratio (approximation for $\theta_z < 60°$) | dimensionless |
| 5 | $V_{out} = C_f \cdot G$ | Pyranometer output: voltage proportional to irradiance | V or mV |
| 6 | $C_f = G_{ref} / V_{ref}$ | Calibration factor (per calibration certificate) | W/m² per mV |
| 7 | $q = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$ | Stefan–Boltzmann radiative heat loss from absorber | W/m² |
| 8 | $E = S \cdot \Delta T$ | Seebeck EMF produced by thermopile | V |
| 9 | $\tau = RC$ | Thermal time constant of pyranometer | seconds |
| 10 | $GTI = G_{beam} \cos(\theta) + G_{diffuse} \cdot R_d + G_{ground} \cdot R_r$ | Tilted plane irradiance (isotropic Liu & Jordan model) | W/m² |
| 11 | $PR = \frac{E_{actual}}{P_{STC} \cdot G_{POA} / G_{STC}}$ | Performance Ratio (uses pyranometer-measured $G_{POA}$) | dimensionless |
| 12 | $\theta_z = \cos^{-1}(\sin\phi \sin\delta + \cos\phi \cos\delta \cos\omega)$ | Solar zenith angle | degrees or radians |

> Where: $n$ = day of year; $\theta_z$ = zenith angle; $\phi$ = latitude; $\delta$ = declination; $\omega$ = hour angle; $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W/m²·K⁴ (Stefan–Boltzmann constant); $S$ = Seebeck coefficient (~40 µV/°C for chromel-constantan); $\varepsilon$ = emissivity of absorber (~0.95 for black paint).

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The pyranometer is the **primary sensor** in any **SCADA-based PV plant monitoring system**. The data it produces feeds:

- **Performance Ratio (PR) calculation** — the single most important metric for plant health.
- **Inverter MPPT efficiency validation** — comparing DC power output against predicted values from measured $G_{POA}$.
- **Soiling loss analysis** — daily comparison of $G_{POA}$ vs. $E_{AC}/P_{STC}$.
- **Satellite-to-ground correlation** — calibrating satellite irradiance datasets (SolarGIS, NSRDB) using ground pyranometer stations.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Thermopile EMF to Solar Irradiance

The thermopile inside a pyranometer consists of $N$ thermocouples in series. When the black absorber receives solar irradiance $G$, it heats up to a temperature $T_h$, while the cold junctions remain at the body temperature $T_c$. The **Seebeck effect** generates an EMF.

**Step 1 — Radiative heat balance on the absorber:**

The absorber is in quasi-steady state, so absorbed solar power equals the sum of conductive loss, convective loss, and radiative loss.

$$G \cdot A \cdot \alpha = k_c (T_h - T_c) + h A (T_h - T_{air}) + \varepsilon \sigma A (T_h^4 - T_c^4)$$

Here, $A$ is the absorber area, $\alpha$ is the absorptivity (≈ 0.95 for black paint), $k_c$ is the conductive coupling to thermopile, $h$ is the convective heat transfer coefficient, $\varepsilon$ is the emissivity, and $\sigma$ is the Stefan–Boltzmann constant.

**Step 2 — Under well-designed conditions:**

The glass dome suppresses convection ($h \to 0$ via still air gap), and the conductive path through the thermopile is dominant. Radiative loss is small compared to the conductive term. So we can approximate:

$$G \cdot A \cdot \alpha \approx k_c (T_h - T_c)$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the temperature difference:**

$$\Delta T = T_h - T_c = \frac{G \cdot A \cdot \alpha}{k_c}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Seebeck effect for $N$ thermocouples:**

The total EMF generated by the thermopile is:

$$V_{out} = N \cdot S \cdot \Delta T$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $\Delta T$:**

$$V_{out} = N \cdot S \cdot \frac{G \cdot A \cdot \alpha}{k_c}$$

**Step 6 — Define the calibration factor $C_f$:**

Combining all sensor-specific constants ($N$, $S$, $A$, $\alpha$, $k_c$) into a single calibration constant:

$$C_f = \frac{k_c}{N \cdot S \cdot A \cdot \alpha}$$

Then the fundamental pyranometer measurement equation becomes:

$$G = C_f \cdot V_{out}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **single most important equation in this module**. The calibration factor $C_f$ is determined during factory calibration against a **reference pyranometer traceable to WRR (World Radiometric Reference)** at PMOD/WRC, Davos, Switzerland.

## 3.2 Analytical Worked Example (KTU Numerical Style)

**Problem:** A Class A thermopile pyranometer has a calibration factor of $C_f = 8.13 \, \mu\text{V}/(\text{W/m}^2)$. During a field test at solar noon, the instrument outputs $V_{out} = 16.4$ mV. Calculate the incident global horizontal irradiance.

**Step 1 — Convert units to be consistent:**

$$C_f = 8.13 \times 10^{-6} \text{ V}/(\text{W/m}^2)$$

$$V_{out} = 16.4 \times 10^{-3} \text{ V}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the pyranometer measurement equation:**

$$G = \frac{V_{out}}{C_f}$$

$$G = \frac{16.4 \times 10^{-3}}{8.13 \times 10^{-6}}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the quotient:**

$$G = \frac{1.64 \times 10^{-2}}{8.13 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{1.64}{8.13} \times 10^{4}$$

$$\frac{1.64}{8.13} \approx 0.2017$$

$$G \approx 0.2017 \times 10^{4} = 2017.2 \text{ W/m}^2$$

**Step 4 — Sanity check:**

This exceeds the solar constant $G_{sc} = 1367$ W/m², which is **physically impossible at ground level**. The result indicates either a calibration error or a faulty instrument. In a real KTU exam, this would be the conclusion students must state.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Pyranometer vs. Pyrheliometer vs. Sunshine Recorder

| Parameter | Pyranometer | Pyrheliometer | Sunshine Recorder |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Quantity Measured** | Global (beam + diffuse) irradiance | Direct Normal Irradiance (DNI) | Sunshine duration (hours) |
| **Mounting** | Horizontal or tilted | On solar tracker, normal to beam | Horizontal |
| **Sensor** | Thermopile / Si-photodiode | Thermopile (with narrow aperture) | Paper card / photoelectric |
| **Spectral Range** | 300–2800 nm | 300–2800 nm | Visible only |
| **Output** | W/m² | W/m² | Hours of bright sunshine |
| **Standard** | ISO 9060:2018 | ISO 9060:2018 | WMO Campbell-Stokes |
| **Typical Cost** | Moderate to high | Very high (tracker required) | Low |

## 3.4 Cosine Response — The Geometric Error

The **cosine response** quantifies how well the pyranometer follows Lambert's cosine law. Theoretically, the irradiance on a surface is:

$$G_{surface} = G_{incident} \cos(\theta)$$

A perfect pyranometer would reproduce this exactly. In practice, deviations occur, especially at high zenith angles ($> 80°$) due to:

- **Dome geometry** (shading at low sun angles)
- **Reflective losses** at oblique angles
- **Non-uniform absorber sensitivity**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU students should remember: **cosine response error** is a dominant systematic error source in early morning and late afternoon measurements, and the **ISO 9060 Class A** limit is **± 10 W/m²** for $0° \leq \theta_z \leq 60°$ and **± 20 W/m²** for $60° \leq \theta_z \leq 80°$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Thermopile Pyranometer

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **signal flow and energy conversion** inside a precision thermopile pyranometer, from solar radiation capture to conditioned electrical output.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Incoming Solar Radiation 300 to 2800 nm] --> B[Outer Glass Dome BK7 Optical Glass]
    B --> C[Inner Glass Dome Thermal Insulation]
    C --> D[Black Painted Absorber 3M Nextel Velvet Coating]
    D --> E[Temperature Rise Thot greater than Tcold]
    E --> F[Thermopile Series of Hot and Cold Junctions]
    F --> G[Seebeck EMF Generation Microvolt to Millivolt Range]
    G --> H[Output Terminals 0 to 20 mV DC]
    H --> I[External Data Logger or SCADA System]
    I --> J[Calibration Factor Application Cf]
    J --> K[Solar Irradiance Value in W per m2]
    L[Desiccant Cartridge Silica Gel] --> C
    M[Bubble Level and Mounting Base] --> D
    N[Cable with Shielded Twisted Pair] --> I
    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style D fill:#1A1A1A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
    style F fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
    style K fill:#32CD32,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.2 System-Level Integration in a Solar PV Plant

The diagram below shows where the pyranometer fits into a **utility-scale PV plant's measurement chain**.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
```

## 4.3 Decision Matrix — Selecting a Pyranometer for PV Application


<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Define a pyranometer. State the standard spectral range it measures as per ISO 9060:2018.*

**Model Answer:**
A pyranometer is a radiometric instrument used to measure the **total solar irradiance** (direct + diffuse) incident on a planar surface, expressed in **W/m²**. As per **ISO 9060:2018**, the standard spectral range for a thermopile pyranometer is **300 nm to 2800 nm**, which covers approximately 97% of the total extraterrestrial solar spectrum. The instrument typically outputs a millivolt-level DC signal proportional to the incident irradiance, which is converted to W/m² via a calibration factor.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Distinguish between a thermopile pyranometer and a silicon photodiode-based pyranometer.*

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Thermopile Pyranometer | Silicon Pyranometer |
|---|---|---|
| **Sensor** | Black absorber + thermocouple array | Silicon photodiode |
| **Spectral Range** | 300–2800 nm (broadband) | 400–1100 nm (narrow) |
| **Accuracy** | High (ISO 9060 Class A/B/C) | Lower (not ISO compliant) |
| **Cost** | High | Low |
| **Application** | Research, utility-scale PV | Rooftop, low-cost monitoring |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**Q A. [KTU University Exam — June 2023, Module 1, 14 Marks]**

*With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **construction and working principle of a thermopile pyranometer**. State the relevant ISO standard and explain the three classes of pyranometers under it.*

**Model Solution Structure:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:** Construction and Working

1. **Construction:**
   - Outer double glass dome (BK7 or quartz), protects sensor, filters far-IR and creates greenhouse effect.
   - Blackened absorber (Nextel Velvet coating), absorptivity α ≈ 0.95.
   - Thermopile: series of 50–100 chromel-constantan thermocouples.
   - Reference body (anodized aluminum) acts as heat sink for cold junctions.
   - Desiccant cartridge (silica gel) to prevent condensation damage.
   - Bubble level and adjustable leveling feet for horizontal mounting.
   - Cable gland with shielded cable to minimize EMI.

2. **Working Principle:**
   - Incoming solar radiation passes through the glass dome(s).
   - The black absorber absorbs nearly all incident radiation uniformly across the spectrum.
   - Absorber temperature rises above the body temperature.
   - Temperature difference drives a **Seebeck EMF** in the thermopile.
   - The millivolt output is linear with incident irradiance:

$$V_{out} = C_f^{-1} \cdot G$$

   - The signal is logged and the calibration factor is applied to obtain W/m².

3. **Standard:** **ISO 9060:2018** — "Solar energy — Specification and classification of instruments for measuring hemispherical solar and direct solar radiation."

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:** Three Classes

1. **Class A (Secondary Standard):** Highest accuracy. Used in research and primary meteorological networks. Daily totals within ± 5%.
2. **Class B (First Class):** Good accuracy. Used in high-quality PV plant monitoring. Daily totals within ± 8%.
3. **Class C (Second Class):** Moderate accuracy. Used in budget installations. Daily totals within ± 10%.

Each class has specific tolerances for: response time, zero offset (a & b), non-linearity, directional response (cosine), temperature dependence, and spectral selectivity.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks

**Q B. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Module 1, 14 Marks]**

*(a)* A thermopile pyranometer has a calibration sensitivity of **7.5 µV per W/m²**. During a field measurement, the output voltage recorded is **12.3 mV**. Determine the global solar irradiance at that instant. *(7 Marks)*

*(b)* Explain the concept of **cosine response** in a pyranometer. Why is it important for accurate solar resource assessment, and how does ISO 9060:2018 quantify it? *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify given data:**

$$C_f^{-1} = 7.5 \, \mu\text{V}/(\text{W/m}^2) = 7.5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ V}/(\text{W/m}^2)$$

$$V_{out} = 12.3 \text{ mV} = 12.3 \times 10^{-3} \text{ V}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the fundamental pyranometer equation:**

$$G = \frac{V_{out}}{C_f^{-1}}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute and evaluate:**

$$G = \frac{12.3 \times 10^{-3}}{7.5 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{12.3}{7.5} \times 10^{3}$$

$$G = 1.64 \times 10^{3} = 1640 \text{ W/m}^2$$

**Step 4 — Sanity check:**

The solar constant is 1367 W/m², so 1640 W/m² is **physically impossible at ground level**. This implies:
- Either the calibration factor is incorrect,
- Or the instrument is faulty (e.g., damaged dome causing greenhouse over-amplification),
- Or the measurement is contaminated by reflected radiation from a nearby surface.

A **realistic KTU exam answer** should point this out as a discussion point.

**Part (b) — Solution:**

1. **Definition of Cosine Response:**
The cosine response describes how closely the pyranometer's output follows the theoretical **Lambert's cosine law**:

$$G_{measured} = G_{actual} \cos(\theta_z)$$

where $\theta_z$ is the angle between the sun's rays and the normal to the pyranometer's detector surface.

2. **Physical Origin of Errors:**
   - At high solar zenith angles (sun near horizon), the long path through the glass dome attenuates radiation non-uniformly.
   - The dome's curvature casts a partial shadow on the absorber at oblique angles.
   - The absorber coating has slightly non-Lambertian response.

3. **Importance in Solar Assessment:**
   - Daily and annual energy yield estimates depend critically on accurate morning and evening irradiance measurements.
   - Cosine errors can cause **5–10% underestimation** of total daily solar energy in mid-latitude locations.
   - For high-latitude sites (e.g., Northern Europe, Northern Canada), cosine errors dominate the total measurement uncertainty.

4. **ISO 9060:2018 Quantification:**
   - The standard specifies **directional response** as the maximum deviation from ideal cosine law at specified zenith angles.
   - **Class A:** ± 10 W/m² for $0° \leq \theta_z \leq 60°$ and ± 20 W/m² for $60° \leq \theta_z \leq 80°$.
   - **Class B:** ± 20 W/m² for $0° \leq \theta_z \leq 60°$ and ± 40 W/m² for $60° \leq \theta_z \leq 80°$.
   - **Class C:** Even more relaxed tolerances.

5. **Correction Method:**
   - Most modern pyranometers are characterized in the factory and a **directional correction polynomial** is applied in the data logger to correct raw outputs.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read Carefully!**
> 1. **Unit consistency:** Always convert mV to V and µV to V before dividing. Losing 1 mark here is the most common KTU error.
> 2. **Calibration factor notation:** Some manufacturers specify it as $\mu\text{V}/(\text{W/m}^2)$ (sensitivity) and others as $(\text{W/m}^2)/\mu\text{V}$ (calibration constant). Confirm the convention from the problem statement. Do **not** invert blindly.
> 3. **Spectral range:** Do not write "300 nm to 1100 nm" for a thermopile pyranometer — that is the silicon photodiode range. Thermopile goes up to **2800 nm**.
> 4. **Cosine response vs. azimuth error:** These are two **distinct** error categories in ISO 9060. Mixing them up costs 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Glassing vs. non-glassing:** Pyranometers with **double domes** have better thermal stability; single-dome instruments have better cosine response. Always mention the dome configuration in construction questions.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Pyranometer (Module 1)**

- [ ] Pyranometer = **measures total (global) solar irradiance on a plane** in **W/m²**.
- [ ] Spectral range: **300 nm to 2800 nm** (ISO 9060:2018, thermopile type).
- [ ] Two main types: **Thermopile (true, ISO 9060 compliant)** vs. **Silicon photodiode (cheap, narrow band, non-ISO)**.
- [ ] **Working principle:** Black absorber → heat → Seebeck EMF in thermopile → voltage proportional to $G$.
- [ ] **Output equation:** $G = C_f \cdot V_{out}$ (or $V_{out}/C_f$ depending on convention).
- [ ] **ISO 9060:2018 classes:** A (Secondary Standard) → B (First Class) → C (Second Class).
- [ ] **Solar constant:** $G_{sc} = 1367$ W/m² (WMO updated: 1361 W/m²).
- [ ] **Cosine response** quantifies deviation from Lambert's law; dominant error at low sun angles.
- [ ] Pyranometer is essential for **Performance Ratio (PR)** calculation in PV plants.
- [ ] Typical output signal: **0 to 20 mV DC** (thermopile); 4–20 mA or Modbus (digital versions).
- [ ] **Dome material:** BK7 or quartz glass; **absorber coating:** 3M Nextel Velvet Black.
- [ ] **Calibration traceability:** WRR (World Radiometric Reference) at PMOD/WRC, Davos.
- [ ] Common confusion: **Pyrheliometer** = measures **DNI only** (with tracker); **Pyranometer** = measures **GHI/GTI** (fixed).
- [ ] **Air mass approximation:** $AM \approx 1/\cos(\theta_z)$ for $\theta_z < 60°$ (valid for $AM \leq 2$).
- [ ] **Thermal time constant** ($\tau = RC$): Class A < 10 s; Class B < 15 s; Class C < 30 s.

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Aid for KTU Viva:** When asked "What is a pyranometer?", your answer must include — (1) **what it measures** (global irradiance, W/m²), (2) **how it works** (thermopile + black absorber), (3) **the standard** (ISO 9060:2018), and (4) **why it matters for PV** (performance ratio). This 4-point structure fetches full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sunshine Recorder, Solar Radiation on a Horizontal Surface
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEET721 - DESIGN OF SOLAR PV SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Sunshine Recorder, Solar Radiation on a Horizontal Surface

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Sunshine Recorder — Formal Definition

A **Sunshine Recorder** is a meteorological instrument used to continuously record the **duration of bright sunshine** (i.e., the number of hours per day during which the solar beam intensity is high enough to cast distinct shadows). The threshold used by the World Meteorological Organization (WMO) is the energy level at which the direct solar beam exceeds **120 W/m²** (equivalent to the intensity that just produces a shadow). The most widely used instrument is the **Campbell-Stokes Sunshine Recorder**, which uses a solid glass sphere as a burning lens to focus sunlight onto a chemically treated calibrated paper card. The length of the burn trace on the card is directly proportional to the duration of bright sunshine.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — OEEET721 Module 1):**
> Students must know (i) the working principle of the Campbell-Stokes recorder, (ii) the geometry of solar radiation reaching a horizontal surface on Earth, and (iii) the mathematical relationships required to compute hourly, daily, and monthly solar radiation on a horizontal plane for PV system sizing.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Magnifying Glass Sun-Clock"

Imagine a child using a magnifying glass on a bright summer day to focus sunlight onto a piece of paper until it smokes and burns. If you fixed that magnifying glass on a stand, slowly rotated it with the sun, and replaced the paper with a **specially treated cardboard strip marked with hours**, you would get a **sunshine recorder**. The sun acts as the "match striker", the glass sphere acts as the "magnifying lens", and the burnt paper card acts as the "diary" of sunshine. Wherever the sun shines brightly, the paper burns — wherever clouds block the sun, the paper remains unburnt. The total length of the burnt trace directly tells the engineer how many hours of "useful" solar energy were available for PV power generation that day.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Constants Used Throughout This Module:**
> - Solar Constant **Gsc = 1367 W/m²** (≈ 1.361 kW/m²)
> - Mean Earth-Sun distance **= 1.496 × 10⁸ km (1 AU)**
> - Earth's axial tilt (obliquity) **ε = 23.45°**
> - Conversion factor **1 cal/cm²/day = 0.041868 MJ/m²/day**

### 1.3 Solar Radiation on a Horizontal Surface — Formal Definition

The **solar radiation incident on a horizontal surface** is the total radiant energy per unit area received from the sun by a flat plate lying parallel to the ground (tilt angle β = 0°). It consists of three components:

1. **Direct (or Beam) Radiation — Gb:** Radiation received directly from the sun's disk without being scattered by the atmosphere.
2. **Diffuse Radiation — Gd:** Solar radiation scattered by air molecules, dust, water vapor, and clouds, reaching the surface from all directions of the sky.
3. **Reflected (or Albedo) Radiation — Gr:** Radiation reflected from the ground and surrounding objects onto the tilted/horizontal surface.

The total radiation on a horizontal surface is therefore:

$$G_T = G_b + G_d + G_r$$

For a truly horizontal surface, the reflected component is generally **neglected** because ground reflection has a very small projected view from a horizontal plane. Hence:

$$G_{T,\text{horizontal}} \approx G_b + G_d$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Relevance for PV System Design:**
> PV modules mounted on flat rooftops, ground-mount arrays in plains, and building-integrated horizontal surfaces receive the radiation calculated by the formulas in this module. The **Performance Ratio (PR)** and **Capacity Utilisation Factor (CUF)** of a PV plant — both used in KTU PV design problems — depend directly on accurately computing the daily/monthly radiation on the horizontal plane before applying tilt-angle corrections.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Working Principle of the Campbell-Stokes Sunshine Recorder

The Campbell-Stokes sunshine recorder consists of the following components:

- A **solid glass sphere** (~10 cm diameter) mounted on a metal frame.
- A **calibrated paper card** held in a spherical groove below the sphere.
- The sphere is positioned so that the **focal point of the sun's image** falls on the paper card.
- The card is divided into **hourly time intervals** and is treated with a chemical (e.g., potassium iodide or sodium thiosulphate) that darkens upon exposure to focused sunlight.

**Logical Operation Sequence:**

- Step 1: Sunlight passes through the glass sphere.
- Step 2: The sphere acts as a convex lens and converges the parallel solar rays to a focal point.
- Step 3: The concentrated solar energy at the focal point **ignites a char line** on the chemically treated paper card.
- Step 4: As the sun moves across the sky (due to Earth's rotation), the focal point traces a path along the curved card.
- Step 5: The **length of the burn trace**, when read against the hourly markings, gives the duration of bright sunshine.
- Step 6: When the sun is obscured by clouds, the intensity falls below the burning threshold and the trace is interrupted.

**Three types of cards are used depending on the season:**

| Card Type | Used During | Sun's Path |
|---|---|---|
| **Long curved card** | Summer (long days, high sun) | Steep and long |
| **Short curved card** | Winter (short days, low sun) | Shallow and short |
| **Straight card** | Equinox (Spring/Autumn) | Intermediate |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the Glass Sphere Works:** A perfect glass sphere refracts parallel rays to a focal point at a distance of approximately **half the sphere's radius (R/2)**. This focal length is independent of the sun's position, which is why the device can track the sun automatically without any mechanical movement.

### 2.2 Solar Geometry — Key Angles and Parameters

To calculate radiation on a horizontal surface, we must first understand the geometry between the sun's position and an observer on Earth.

| Symbol | Parameter | Definition |
|---|---|---|
| **φ** | Latitude | Angular position North (+) or South (−) of the equator |
| **δ** | Solar declination | Angular position of the sun north/south of the equator |
| **β** | Surface tilt angle | Angle of the surface from the horizontal |
| **γ** | Surface azimuth angle | Deviation of surface normal from local meridian (East negative) |
| **θz** | Zenith angle | Angle between the sun and the vertical (local zenith) |
| **θ** | Angle of incidence | Angle between the sun's rays and the normal to the surface |
| **αs** | Solar altitude angle | Angle between the sun and the horizontal (αs = 90° − θz) |
| **γs** | Solar azimuth angle | Horizontal projection of sun's position, measured from South |
| **ω** | Hour angle | Angular displacement of sun east/west of local meridian (15°/hour) |
| **n** | Day of year | 1 = Jan 1, 365 = Dec 31 |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — Solar Geometry & Radiation on Horizontal Surface

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\delta = 23.45^\circ \sin\!\left[\dfrac{360}{365}(284+n)\right]$ | Solar declination (Cooper's equation) | degrees |
| 2 | $B = \dfrac{360}{365}(n-81)$ | Day-angle helper (intermediate) | degrees |
| 3 | $EoT = 9.87\sin(2B) - 7.53\cos(B) - 1.5\sin(B)$ | Equation of Time | minutes |
| 4 | $LST = LMT + \dfrac{\text{Long}_{\text{std}}-\text{Long}_{\text{local}}}{15} + \dfrac{EoT}{60}$ | Local Solar Time | hours |
| 5 | $\omega = 15^\circ (LST - 12)$ | Hour angle | degrees |
| 6 | $\cos\theta_z = \sin\phi\sin\delta + \cos\phi\cos\delta\cos\omega$ | Cosine of zenith angle | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\omega_s = \cos^{-1}(-\tan\phi\tan\delta)$ | Sunset hour angle | degrees |
| 8 | $AM = \dfrac{1}{\cos\theta_z}$ (for $\theta_z < 90^\circ$) | Air mass (Kasten-Young refined) | dimensionless |
| 9 | $G_{on} = G_{sc}\!\left[1+0.033\cos\!\left(\dfrac{360n}{365}\right)\right]$ | Extraterrestrial normal incidence | W/m² |
| 10 | $G_o = G_{on}\cos\theta_z$ | Extraterrestrial on horizontal surface | W/m² |
| 11 | $H_o = \dfrac{24 \times 3600 \times G_{sc}}{\pi}\!\left[1+0.033\cos\!\left(\dfrac{360n}{365}\right)\right]\!\left[\cos\phi\cos\delta\sin\omega_s + \dfrac{2\pi\omega_s}{360}\sin\phi\sin\delta\right]$ | Daily extraterrestrial on horizontal | J/m²/day |
| 12 | $I_o = \dfrac{12 \times 3600 \times G_{sc}}{\pi}\!\left[1+0.033\cos\!\left(\dfrac{360n}{365}\right)\right]\!\left[\cos\phi\cos\delta(\sin\omega_2-\sin\omega_1) + \dfrac{2\pi(\omega_2-\omega_1)}{360}\sin\phi\sin\delta\right]$ | Hourly extraterrestrial on horizontal | J/m²/h |
| 13 | $G_T = G_b + G_d + G_r$ | Total radiation (general surface) | W/m² |
| 14 | $G_{T,\text{horiz}} = G_b\cos\theta_z + G_d$ | Total on horizontal surface (horizon-tilted) | W/m² |
| 15 | $G_{sc} = 1367 \text{ W/m}^2$ | Solar constant (WMO standard) | W/m² |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Conditions to Remember for KTU Exam:**
> - Sunrise/sunset occurs when **ω = ±ωs**, where **ωs = cos⁻¹(−tan φ tan δ)**.
> - Polar day (sun never sets) when **ωs ≥ 180°**, i.e., **tan φ tan δ ≤ −1**.
> - Polar night (sun never rises) when **tan φ tan δ ≥ +1**.
> - At **ω = 0**, the sun is on the local meridian (solar noon).
> - For a horizontal surface, **θ = θz**, since the surface normal points to the zenith.

### 2.4 Components of Solar Radiation on a Horizontal Surface — Engineering Interpretation

For a PV engineer in Kerala (latitude ~8° N to ~12° N), the radiation falling on a **horizontal surface** is the most natural input to a PV array mounted flat on a roof. Understanding each component helps the designer:

- **Beam radiation (Gb)** drives the **maximum power point (MPP)** during clear-sky noon hours.
- **Diffuse radiation (Gd)** is what powers the PV modules during cloudy/rainy monsoon days (critical for Kerala climate).
- **Total daily radiation (kWh/m²/day)** is converted via the PV module's efficiency and area to compute the **daily energy yield (kWh)** of the system.

The KTU syllabus emphasises the **extraterrestrial radiation (Ho)** first, then the **clearness index (Kt = H/Ho)**, and finally the **ground-measured (terrestrial) radiation (H)** on the horizontal surface — the most direct input to PV sizing.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Zenith Angle Formula (cos θz)

The zenith angle is the angle between the sun's rays and the **local vertical**. It is the most fundamental angle in horizontal-surface radiation analysis, because for a horizontal surface the **angle of incidence equals the zenith angle**.

**Starting from the spherical trigonometric law of cosines:**

For any observer at latitude φ, the sun is at declination δ and hour angle ω. Apply the spherical cosine law to the astronomical triangle formed by the zenith, the celestial pole, and the sun:

$$\cos\theta_z = \sin\phi\sin\delta + \cos\phi\cos\delta\cos\omega$$

**Step-by-step explanation:**

- Step 1: The angular distance from the celestial pole to the sun is **90° − δ**.
- Step 2: The angular distance from the celestial pole to the zenith is **90° − φ**.
- Step 3: The angle between these two great-circle arcs is the hour angle **ω**.
- Step 4: Apply the cosine rule for sides: **cos(a) = cos(b)cos(c) + sin(b)sin(c)cos(A)**.
- Step 5: Substituting a = θz, b = 90° − δ, c = 90° − φ, A = ω gives the result.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Sunset Hour Angle (ωs)

The sunset hour angle is the value of the hour angle at which the sun is exactly on the horizon, i.e., the zenith angle θz = 90° (or cos θz = 0).

**Step 1: Set the zenith-angle condition for sunrise/sunset:**

$$\cos\theta_z = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sin\phi\sin\delta + \cos\phi\cos\delta\cos\omega_s = 0$$

**Step 2: Isolate the cosine term:**

$$\cos\phi\cos\delta\cos\omega_s = -\sin\phi\sin\delta$$

**Step 3: Divide both sides by cos φ cos δ:**

$$\cos\omega_s = -\tan\phi\tan\delta$$

**Step 4: Take the inverse cosine to obtain the sunset hour angle:**

$$\omega_s = \cos^{-1}(-\tan\phi\tan\delta)$$

> [!TIP]
> **Interpretation:** The daylight period in hours is given by **N = (2 ωs)/15**, where ωs is expressed in degrees. For Kerala (φ ≈ 10° N) on the equinox (δ ≈ 0°), ωs = 90° and N = 12 hours — the tropical location has nearly equal day and night year-round.

### 3.3 Derivation of Daily Extraterrestrial Radiation on a Horizontal Surface

This is the **most important KTU derivation** for Module 1. The Earth's surface outside the atmosphere (extraterrestrial) receives radiation that depends only on the **inverse-square law** of sun-Earth distance and the **solar zenith angle**.

**Step 1: Define the instantaneous extraterrestrial radiation on a horizontal surface:**

$$G_o = G_{on}\cos\theta_z$$

**Step 2: Insert the expression for cos θz:**

$$G_o = G_{on}\left[\sin\phi\sin\delta + \cos\phi\cos\delta\cos\omega\right]$$

**Step 3: Account for the variation of Earth-Sun distance using the eccentricity factor Eo:**

$$G_{on} = G_{sc}\left[1 + 0.033\cos\!\left(\frac{360n}{365}\right)\right] = G_{sc}\,E_o$$

**Step 4: To get daily radiation, integrate Go over the daylight period from sunrise (ω = −ωs) to sunset (ω = +ωs):**

$$H_o = \int_{-t_s}^{+t_s} G_o\,dt = \int_{-\omega_s}^{+\omega_s} G_{on}\left[\sin\phi\sin\delta + \cos\phi\cos\delta\cos\omega\right]\,\frac{d\omega}{15^\circ/\text{hr}}$$

(The factor dω/dt = 15°/hr because the Earth rotates 360° in 24 hours.)

**Step 5: Convert degrees to radians using 1° = π/180 rad, and integrate each term separately.**

The integral becomes (in degrees, but converted to seconds using 1 hr = 3600 s):

$$H_o = \frac{24 \times 3600 \times G_{sc}}{\pi}\, E_o \left[\cos\phi\cos\delta\sin\omega_s + \frac{2\pi\omega_s}{360}\sin\phi\sin\delta\right]$$

$$H_o = \frac{86400\,G_{sc}}{\pi}\, E_o \left[\cos\phi\cos\delta\sin\omega_s + \frac{\pi\omega_s}{180}\sin\phi\sin\delta\right]$$

This is the final **KTU-required expression** for daily extraterrestrial radiation on a horizontal surface.

**Step 6: Numerical verification with the constants:**

Substitute Gsc = 1367 W/m², the constant simplifies to:

$$\frac{24 \times 3600 \times 1367}{\pi} = 37.586 \times 10^6 \text{ J/m}^2\text{/day} \approx 37.6 \text{ MJ/m}^2\text{/day}$$

So in practice:

$$H_o = 37.6\, E_o \left[\cos\phi\cos\delta\sin\omega_s + \frac{\pi\omega_s}{180}\sin\phi\sin\delta\right] \quad [\text{MJ/m}^2\text{/day}]$$

### 3.4 Hourly Extraterrestrial Radiation on a Horizontal Surface

For hourly (rather than daily) integration, we integrate Go between two hour angles ω1 (start of the hour) and ω2 (end of the hour), with ω2 − ω1 = 15° (= 1 hour):

$$I_o = \int_{\omega_1}^{\omega_2} G_{on}\left[\sin\phi\sin\delta + \cos\phi\cos\delta\cos\omega\right]\,\frac{d\omega}{15}$$

Performing the integration:

$$I_o = \frac{12 \times 3600 \times G_{sc}}{\pi}\,E_o\!\left[\cos\phi\cos\delta(\sin\omega_2 - \sin\omega_1) + \frac{2\pi(\omega_2-\omega_1)}{360}\sin\phi\sin\delta\right]$$

$$I_o = 4.9171\,E_o\left[\cos\phi\cos\delta(\sin\omega_2 - \sin\omega_1) + \frac{\pi(\omega_2-\omega_1)}{180}\sin\phi\sin\delta\right] \quad [\text{MJ/m}^2]$$

### 3.5 Worked Example — KTU Typical Numerical Problem

**Problem Statement (Modelled on KTU Board Style):**

Calculate the daily extraterrestrial radiation on a horizontal surface at a location with latitude **φ = 13° N** (Trivandrum, Kerala) on **n = 172** (Summer Solstice, June 21).

**Solution — Step-by-Step:**

**Step 1: Compute the declination angle δ:**

$$\delta = 23.45^\circ \sin\!\left[\frac{360}{365}(284 + 172)\right] = 23.45^\circ \sin\!\left[\frac{360 \times 456}{365}\right]$$

$$\delta = 23.45^\circ \sin(449.75^\circ) = 23.45^\circ \sin(449.75^\circ - 360^\circ) = 23.45^\circ \sin(89.75^\circ)$$

$$\delta = 23.45^\circ \times 0.99999 \approx 23.45^\circ$$

This matches the expected value for the **summer solstice**.

**Step 2: Compute the eccentricity factor Eo:**

$$E_o = 1 + 0.033\cos\!\left(\frac{360 \times 172}{365}\right) = 1 + 0.033\cos(169.64^\circ)$$

$$E_o = 1 + 0.033 \times (-0.9843) = 1 - 0.0325 = 0.9675$$

**Step 3: Compute the sunset hour angle ωs:**

$$\omega_s = \cos^{-1}(-\tan\phi\tan\delta) = \cos^{-1}[-\tan(13^\circ)\tan(23.45^\circ)]$$

$$\tan(13^\circ) = 0.2309, \quad \tan(23.45^\circ) = 0.4335$$

$$\omega_s = \cos^{-1}(-0.2309 \times 0.4335) = \cos^{-1}(-0.1001)$$

$$\omega_s = \cos^{-1}(-0.1001) = 95.75^\circ$$

**Step 4: Compute daylight hours N:**

$$N = \frac{2\omega_s}{15} = \frac{2 \times 95.75}{15} = 12.77 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 5: Substitute all values in the daily extraterrestrial radiation formula:**

$$H_o = 37.6 \times 0.9675 \left[\cos(13^\circ)\cos(23.45^\circ)\sin(95.75^\circ) + \frac{\pi \times 95.75}{180}\sin(13^\circ)\sin(23.45^\circ)\right]$$

Compute each trigonometric term:

- $\cos(13^\circ) = 0.9744$
- $\cos(23.45^\circ) = 0.9178$
- $\sin(95.75^\circ) = 0.9950$
- $\sin(13^\circ) = 0.2250$
- $\sin(23.45^\circ) = 0.3979$

**Term 1:** $\cos\phi\cos\delta\sin\omega_s = 0.9744 \times 0.9178 \times 0.9950 = 0.8896$

**Term 2:** $\dfrac{\pi\omega_s}{180}\sin\phi\sin\delta = \dfrac{\pi \times 95.75}{180} \times 0.2250 \times 0.3979$

$$\frac{\pi \times 95.75}{180} = 1.6712 \text{ rad}$$

$$\text{Term 2} = 1.6712 \times 0.2250 \times 0.3979 = 0.1496$$

**Step 6: Final calculation:**

$$H_o = 37.6 \times 0.9675 \times (0.8896 + 0.1496) = 37.6 \times 0.9675 \times 1.0392$$

$$H_o = 37.6 \times 1.0054 = 37.80 \text{ MJ/m}^2/\text{day}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result Interpretation:** On the summer solstice in Trivandrum, the horizontal surface receives about **37.8 MJ/m²/day** (= 10.5 kWh/m²/day) of extraterrestrial radiation. After atmospheric attenuation, the **terrestrial radiation** is typically **60–70%** of this value, giving ~6.3–7.4 kWh/m²/day, which is consistent with measured data for Trivandrum.

### 3.6 Worked Example — Hourly Radiation on Horizontal Surface

**Problem:** For the same location (φ = 13° N, n = 172), compute the extraterrestrial radiation received between **9:00 AM and 10:00 AM solar time**.

**Step 1: Compute hour angles:**

- At 9 AM solar time: $\omega_1 = 15 \times (9 - 12) = -45^\circ$
- At 10 AM solar time: $\omega_2 = 15 \times (10 - 12) = -30^\circ$

**Step 2: Use the hourly radiation formula:**

$$I_o = 4.9171 \times 0.9675 \left[\cos(13^\circ)\cos(23.45^\circ)(\sin(-30^\circ) - \sin(-45^\circ)) + \frac{\pi \times 15}{180}\sin(13^\circ)\sin(23.45^\circ)\right]$$

**Step 3: Compute term-by-term:**

- $\sin(-30^\circ) = -0.5000$
- $\sin(-45^\circ) = -0.7071$
- $\sin(-30^\circ) - \sin(-45^\circ) = -0.5000 - (-0.7071) = 0.2071$
- $\dfrac{\pi \times 15}{180} = 0.2618 \text{ rad}$

**Term 1:** $0.9744 \times 0.9178 \times 0.2071 = 0.1852$

**Term 2:** $0.2618 \times 0.2250 \times 0.3979 = 0.02344$

**Step 4: Final calculation:**

$$I_o = 4.9171 \times 0.9675 \times (0.1852 + 0.02344) = 4.7576 \times 0.2086 = 0.993 \text{ MJ/m}^2$$

$$I_o = 0.993 \text{ MJ/m}^2 = 0.2758 \text{ kWh/m}^2 \text{ for the hour 9–10 AM.}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Campbell-Stokes Sunshine Recorder Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sunlight Parallel Rays] --> B[Solid Glass Sphere 10cm dia]
    B --> C[Refraction through Sphere]
    C --> D[Focal Point on Paper Card]
    D --> E[Chemical Reaction on Card]
    E --> F[Burn Trace Marked on Card]
    F --> G[Hourly Time Markings]
    G --> H[Total Sunshine Duration in Hours]
    H --> I[Daily / Monthly Sunshine Records]
    I --> J[Input to PV System Sizing and CUF Calculation]
    K[Card Type Selector] --> D
    K --> L[Long Curved Card - Summer]
    K --> M[Short Curved Card - Winter]
    K --> N[Straight Card - Equinox]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Solar Radiation Components on a Horizontal Surface

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A[Atmosphere Effects]
        SC[Scattering by Air Molecules] 
        AB[Absorption by Ozone Water Vapor CO2]
        CL[Cloud Reflection and Absorption]
    end
    subgraph B[Ground Level Horizontal Surface]
        GB[Direct Beam Radiation Gb]
        GD[Diffuse Sky Radiation Gd]
        GR[Reflected Ground Radiation Gr]
        GT[Total Radiation on Horizontal GT]
    end
    SRC[Sun 1367 W per m2] --> AMOD[Extraterrestrial Radiation Gon]
    AMOD --> A
    A --> GB
    A --> GD
    A --> GR
    GB --> GT
    GD --> GT
    GR --> GT
    GT --> PV[PV Array Mounted Horizontally]
```

### 4.3 Process Flow — Solar Time and Hour-Angle Calculation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Input Day of Year n Local Mean Time LMT Latitude phi Longitude] --> A1[Compute Declination delta using Cooper equation]
    A1 --> A2[Compute Day Angle B]
    A2 --> A3[Compute Equation of Time EoT]
    A3 --> A4[Compute Local Solar Time LST]
    A4 --> A5[Compute Hour Angle omega 15 degrees per hour]
    A5 --> A6[Compute Zenith Angle theta z]
    A6 --> A7[Compute Sunset Hour Angle omega s]
    A7 --> DECISION{Is omega within range}
    DECISION -->|Yes| A8[Compute Cos theta z and Air Mass]
    DECISION -->|No| A9[Sun Below Horizon Set Go 0]
    A8 --> A10[Compute Hourly or Daily Extraterrestrial Radiation on Horizontal]
    A9 --> A11[Output Zero]
    A10 --> OUT[Ho or Io for PV Sizing]
    A11 --> OUT
```

### 4.4 Schematic — Solar Geometry Parameters Around an Observer

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Sky[Sky Dome Above Observer]
        SUN[Sun Position]
        ZEN[Zenith Directly Overhead]
        HOR[Horizon Plane]
    end
    OBS[Observer on Earth Surface phi latitude]
    OBS --> ZEN
    OBS --> SUN
    OBS --> HOR
    ZEN ---|theta z Zenith Angle| SUN
    SUN ---|alpha s Altitude Angle| HOR
    SUN ---|gamma s Azimuth Angle| SOUTH[Local Meridian South]
    ZEN ---|phi Latitude| OBS
    SUN ---|90 minus delta Polar Distance| POLE[Celestial North Pole]
    POLE ---|Hour Angle omega| MERID[Observer Meridian Plane]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**

Explain the working principle of the **Campbell-Stokes sunshine recorder** with a neat sketch. Mention the function of the glass sphere and the role of the chemically treated paper card.

**Model Answer:**

The Campbell-Stokes sunshine recorder is a meteorological instrument used to record the **duration of bright sunshine** in hours per day. It consists of a **solid glass sphere** of about 10 cm diameter mounted on a metallic stand with a calibrated paper card held in a spherical groove below the sphere. When the sun shines, the glass sphere acts as a **convex lens** and converges the parallel solar rays to a focal point on the chemically treated paper card. The concentrated solar energy is sufficient to **char or burn a trace** on the card. As the sun moves across the sky due to Earth's rotation, the focal point traces a path along the curved card. Wherever the direct solar beam intensity exceeds **120 W/m²** (the WMO threshold for bright sunshine), a continuous burn mark is produced. Cloud cover interrupts the burn, leaving gaps. The total length of the burn trace, when read against the **hourly time markings** printed on the card, gives the daily bright sunshine hours. Three types of cards are used: **long curved (summer), short curved (winter), and straight (equinox)** — chosen to match the seasonal variation in the sun's path. *(3 marks: 1 for principle, 1 for components, 1 for card types.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**

Define the following solar geometry terms and state their typical units:
**(a) Solar declination angle (δ)**
**(b) Hour angle (ω)**
**(c) Zenith angle (θz)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Solar Declination Angle (δ):** It is the angular position of the sun's rays with respect to the **plane of the Earth's equator**. It varies between **−23.45°** (winter solstice) and **+23.45°** (summer solstice), and is zero at the equinoxes. It is calculated using Cooper's equation:

$$\delta = 23.45^\circ \sin\!\left[\frac{360}{365}(284 + n)\right]$$

Unit: **degrees**. (1 mark)

**(b) Hour Angle (ω):** It is the angular displacement of the sun **east or west of the local meridian**, caused by Earth's rotation of 15° per hour. At **solar noon**, ω = 0°; negative values are for the morning, positive for the afternoon. Unit: **degrees**. (1 mark)

**(c) Zenith Angle (θz):** It is the angle between the **sun's rays and the local vertical** (zenith direction). When the sun is directly overhead, θz = 0°; at sunrise/sunset, θz = 90°. For a horizontal surface, the angle of incidence equals the zenith angle. Unit: **degrees**. (1 mark)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Choice-Based)

> Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, Module 1 has an internal choice. Two alternative questions are provided below.

---

**Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper Style]*

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of a **Campbell-Stokes sunshine recorder**. Discuss the different types of cards used and the **threshold intensity** for recording bright sunshine. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A horizontal surface receives **direct beam radiation of 850 W/m²** when the zenith angle is **35°** and **diffuse radiation of 180 W/m²**. The ground reflectance (albedo) is **0.2**. Calculate the **total solar radiation incident on the horizontal surface**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution to Part (a):**

- **(i) Construction (3 marks):** The Campbell-Stokes recorder consists of a solid glass sphere (~10 cm dia) mounted on a metal stand. A spherical metal groove below the sphere holds a chemically treated paper card (treated with potassium iodide or sodium thiosulphate). The card has **hourly time markings** printed along its length. The whole assembly is placed in a **standard meteorological enclosure** with the sphere axis aligned parallel to the Earth's polar axis.

- **(ii) Working Principle (2 marks):** Sunlight passes through the glass sphere and is refracted to a **focal point** at a distance equal to approximately **half the sphere's radius (R/2)** below the sphere. The concentrated solar energy is sufficient to char the paper at the focal point. As the sun moves due to Earth's rotation, the focal point moves along the card, **drawing a burn trace**. The length of the burn trace, measured against the hourly markings, gives the bright sunshine hours.

- **(iii) Card types and threshold (2 marks):** Three card types — **Long curved (summer), short curved (winter), straight (equinox)** — are used to accommodate the seasonal variation in the sun's altitude. The **WMO threshold** for bright sunshine is **120 W/m²** of direct beam intensity.

**Model Solution to Part (b):**

Given: Gb = 850 W/m², θz = 35°, Gd = 180 W/m², ρg = 0.2.

**Step 1: Compute the beam radiation on the horizontal surface:**

$$G_{b,\text{horiz}} = G_b \cos\theta_z = 850 \times \cos(35^\circ) = 850 \times 0.8192 = 696.3 \text{ W/m}^2$$

**Step 2: Compute the diffuse radiation on the horizontal surface** (already on horizontal):

$$G_{d,\text{horiz}} = G_d = 180 \text{ W/m}^2$$

**Step 3: Compute the reflected radiation on the horizontal surface** (using the standard formulation for the reflected component contribution, even on horizontal):

For a truly horizontal surface, the **view factor of the ground is zero** because the surface looks only at the sky. Hence:

$$G_{r,\text{horiz}} \approx 0 \text{ W/m}^2$$

(Strictly, $G_r = G_T \rho_g (1 - \cos\beta)/2$; for β = 0°, $G_r = 0$.)

**Step 4: Total radiation on horizontal surface:**

$$G_{T,\text{horiz}} = G_{b,\text{horiz}} + G_{d,\text{horiz}} + G_{r,\text{horiz}} = 696.3 + 180 + 0$$

$$\boxed{G_{T,\text{horiz}} = 876.3 \text{ W/m}^2}$$

**Verification:** This is well below the extraterrestrial value (~1367 W/m²), confirming the answer is physically realistic.

---

**Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper Style]*

**(a)** Derive the expression for **daily extraterrestrial radiation Ho on a horizontal surface**, clearly explaining each step. State the value of the **solar constant** used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a location at **latitude 10° N**, calculate the **declination angle**, **sunset hour angle**, **daylight hours**, and **daily extraterrestrial radiation on a horizontal surface** for **n = 81** (vernal equinox). Use Gsc = 1367 W/m². **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution to Part (a):**

The derivation is as outlined in **Section 3.3** of these notes. The examiner-relevant steps are:

- **Step 1:** Instantaneous extraterrestrial radiation on a horizontal surface: $G_o = G_{on}\cos\theta_z$
- **Step 2:** Insert $\cos\theta_z = \sin\phi\sin\delta + \cos\phi\cos\delta\cos\omega$
- **Step 3:** Account for Earth-Sun distance variation: $G_{on} = G_{sc}E_o = 1367\left[1 + 0.033\cos(360n/365)\right]$ W/m²
- **Step 4:** Integrate from sunrise to sunset: $H_o = \int G_o\,dt$, with $dt = d\omega/15°$
- **Step 5:** Carry out the integration to obtain:

$$H_o = \frac{24 \times 3600 \times 1367}{\pi}\,E_o\left[\cos\phi\cos\delta\sin\omega_s + \frac{2\pi\omega_s}{360}\sin\phi\sin\delta\right]$$

$$H_o = 37.586 \times 10^6 \cdot E_o\left[\cos\phi\cos\delta\sin\omega_s + \frac{\pi\omega_s}{180}\sin\phi\sin\delta\right] \text{ J/m}^2\text{/day}$$

**Solar constant: Gsc = 1367 W/m² (WMO standard).** (7 marks: 2 for setup, 3 for integration, 2 for final expression.)

**Model Solution to Part (b):**

Given: φ = 10° N, n = 81.

**Step 1: Declination angle δ:**

$$\delta = 23.45^\circ \sin\!\left[\frac{360}{365}(284 + 81)\right] = 23.45^\circ \sin\!\left(\frac{360 \times 365}{365}\right) = 23.45^\circ \sin(360^\circ) = 0^\circ$$

This confirms the **vernal equinox** has δ ≈ 0°.

**Step 2: Eccentricity factor Eo:**

$$E_o = 1 + 0.033\cos\!\left(\frac{360 \times 81}{365}\right) = 1 + 0.033\cos(79.89^\circ) = 1 + 0.033 \times 0.1754 = 1.00579$$

**Step 3: Sunset hour angle ωs:**

Since δ = 0°, $\tan\delta = 0$:

$$\omega_s = \cos^{-1}(-\tan\phi \cdot 0) = \cos^{-1}(0) = 90^\circ$$

**Step 4: Daylight hours N:**

$$N = \frac{2\omega_s}{15} = \frac{2 \times 90}{15} = 12 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 5: Daily extraterrestrial radiation Ho:**

$$H_o = 37.6 \times 1.00579 \left[\cos(10^\circ)\cos(0^\circ)\sin(90^\circ) + \frac{\pi \times 90}{180}\sin(10^\circ)\sin(0^\circ)\right]$$

- $\cos(10^\circ) = 0.9848$
- $\sin(90^\circ) = 1.000$
- $\sin(0^\circ) = 0$ → second term vanishes

$$H_o = 37.6 \times 1.00579 \times [0.9848 \times 1 \times 1 + 0] = 37.6 \times 1.00579 \times 0.9848$$

$$H_o = 37.6 \times 0.9905 = 37.24 \text{ MJ/m}^2/\text{day}$$

Converting to kWh/m²/day: $37.24 / 3.6 = 10.34 \text{ kWh/m}^2/\text{day}$

$$\boxed{H_o \approx 37.24 \text{ MJ/m}^2/\text{day} \approx 10.34 \text{ kWh/m}^2/\text{day}}$$

**Physical interpretation:** At the equator (φ ≈ 0° to 10° N), even on the equinox, the daily extraterrestrial radiation is approximately constant at ~37 MJ/m²/day because the sun is overhead at noon and the day length is exactly 12 hours.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls to Avoid:**
> - **Pitfall 1 (most common):** Confusing **declination angle δ** with **latitude φ**. Declination varies with the day of year; latitude is fixed for a given location. Many students write "δ = 10°" for a location at 10° N — this is WRONG.
> - **Pitfall 2:** Using **degrees** in the final radiation formula without converting to **radians** where required (e.g., the term $\pi\omega_s/180$ correctly converts degrees to radians; do not replace it with just $\omega_s$).
> - **Pitfall 3:** Forgetting the eccentricity factor $E_o = 1 + 0.033\cos(360n/365)$. The KTU answer key usually deducts 1 mark for this omission.
> - **Pitfall 4:** Computing $\cos\omega_s$ as $\cos^{-1}(-\tan\phi\tan\delta)$ and then trying to take the inverse *square* root — remember the operation is **arccos**, not square root.
> - **Pitfall 5:** Not checking whether the sun is **above the horizon** (i.e., $\cos\theta_z > 0$). If $\cos\theta_z \leq 0$, the radiation is **zero** for that hour, not negative. Always report **Ho = 0** when the sun is below the horizon.
> - **Pitfall 6:** In the Campbell-Stokes question, students often write "the glass sphere burns the paper" — the correct explanation is that the **sphere focuses light** to a focal point, and **the focused light burns the paper**. The sphere itself does not burn.
> - **Pitfall 7:** Mixing up **local mean time (LMT)** and **local solar time (LST)**. The KTU question bank has 2-mark questions specifically testing the equation of time correction — don't lose these easy marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU Module 1 Exam:**

- **Sunshine Recorder:**
  - Campbell-Stokes uses a **solid glass sphere (~10 cm)** with focal length ≈ R/2.
  - Three card types: **long curved (summer), short curved (winter), straight (equinox)**.
  - WMO bright-sunshine threshold = **120 W/m²** direct beam intensity.
  - The burn trace on the **chemically treated card** gives sunshine duration.

- **Solar Geometry Key Formulas:**
  - Declination: $\delta = 23.45^\circ \sin\!\left[\dfrac{360}{365}(284+n)\right]$
  - Equation of Time: $EoT = 9.87\sin(2B) - 7.53\cos(B) - 1.5\sin(B)$ minutes
  - Hour angle: $\omega = 15^\circ(LST - 12)$
  - Zenith: $\cos\theta_z = \sin\phi\sin\delta + \cos\phi\cos\delta\cos\omega$
  - Sunset hour angle: $\omega_s = \cos^{-1}(-\tan\phi\tan\delta)$
  - Day length: $N = 2\omega_s/15$ hours

- **Extraterrestrial Radiation on Horizontal Surface:**
  - Instantaneous: $G_o = G_{on}\cos\theta_z$ with $G_{on} = 1367\,E_o$ W/m²
  - Hourly: $I_o = 4.9171\,E_o\!\left[\cos\phi\cos\delta(\sin\omega_2-\sin\omega_1) + \dfrac{\pi(\omega_2-\omega_1)}{180}\sin\phi\sin\delta\right]$ MJ/m²
  - Daily: $H_o = 37.6\,E_o\!\left[\cos\phi\cos\delta\sin\omega_s + \dfrac{\pi\omega_s}{180}\sin\phi\sin\delta\right]$ MJ/m²/day
  - Eccentricity factor: $E_o = 1 + 0.033\cos(360n/365)$

- **Components of Radiation on Horizontal Surface:**
  - $G_{T,\text{horiz}} = G_b\cos\theta_z + G_d + 0$ (reflected term = 0 for horizontal).
  - For PV, the **clearness index** $K_T = H/H_o$ ranges from 0.25 (very cloudy, monsoon) to 0.75 (clear, dry season) in Kerala.

- **Constants to Memorise:**
  - $G_{sc} = 1367$ W/m²
  - Earth's axial tilt = 23.45°
  - Earth-Sun distance variation: ±3.3% (captured by $E_o$)
  - Solar declination range: −23.45° to +23.45°

- **Exam-Boost Strategy:**
  - Always draw a **neat block diagram** for the Campbell-Stokes question.
  - Always **state the threshold** (120 W/m²) and **solar constant** (1367 W/m²) in any numerical.
  - For derivation questions, **show the integration step** with limits ±ωs — this is the most important KTU board check.
  - **Convert units** at the end (MJ/m²/day ↔ kWh/m²/day: divide by 3.6).
  - For Kerala-context problems, remember latitude φ ≈ **8° N to 12° N** — close to the equator.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEET721 - DESIGN OF SOLAR PV SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Extra-Terrestrial Region, Terrestrial Region, Solar Radiation on an Inclined Surface, Conversion Factors, Total Solar Radiation on an Inclined/Tilted Surface, Monthly Average Daily Solar Radiation on Inclined Surfaces

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Extra-Terrestrial Radiation (ETR)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
> Extra-terrestrial radiation is the solar radiation received at the top of the Earth's atmosphere, *before* any attenuation by the atmosphere, clouds, dust, or aerosols. It represents the solar energy flux available on a surface perpendicular to the Sun's rays outside the atmosphere.

**The Solar Constant (I_sc):**
The World Radiation Reference (WRR) defines the solar constant as:
- **I_sc = 1367 W/m²** (standard value)
- The actual value varies by ±3.4% throughout the year due to the Earth's elliptical orbit.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Think of extra-terrestrial radiation as the *gross salary* offered in a job offer letter — it is the total energy the Sun "pays out" before any "deductions" (atmospheric absorption, scattering, cloud reflection) reduce the amount that actually reaches the ground (the *take-home salary* = terrestrial radiation).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Extra-terrestrial radiation forms the *upper bound* of any solar energy calculation. Every terrestrial solar measurement is expressed as a *percentage* of this value. KTU questions frequently ask students to compute I_o for a specific day (n) and latitude (φ).

### Variation of Extra-Terrestrial Radiation with Day of the Year

The Earth-Sun distance varies because Earth's orbit is elliptical (eccentricity e = 0.0167). This causes the extraterrestrial irradiance on a surface normal to the solar beam to fluctuate:

$$I_{on} = I_{sc} \left(1 + 0.033 \cos \frac{360 n}{365}\right)$$

where:
- **I_on** = extra-terrestrial irradiance on day *n* (W/m²)
- **I_sc** = solar constant = **1367 W/m²**
- **n** = day of the year (1 = Jan 1, 365 = Dec 31)

## 1.2 Terrestrial Radiation

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
> Terrestrial radiation is the global solar radiation actually received on a horizontal surface at ground level, after attenuation by the Earth's atmosphere. It is the sum of three components:
> 1. **Direct (Beam) Radiation (I_b)** — arrives in a collimated straight line from the Sun.
> 2. **Diffuse Radiation (I_d)** — scattered by molecules, aerosols, and clouds; arrives from all sky directions.
> 3. **Reflected (Albedo) Radiation (I_r)** — reflected from the ground and surrounding surfaces.

The total terrestrial radiation on a horizontal surface:

$$I_T = I_b + I_d + I_r$$

However, since the surface is horizontal, the reflected component is normally considered zero for a horizontal plane, so:

$$I_T \approx I_b + I_d$$

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Imagine pouring clean water (extra-terrestrial radiation) through a dirty filter (atmosphere):
- The **first straight stream** that comes through = Direct beam
- The **scattered droplets** that come out at all angles = Diffuse radiation
- The **puddle splash** from the table surface = Reflected radiation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Fact:** On a clear day, direct beam accounts for ~85–90% of total radiation. On a heavily overcast day, diffuse radiation may account for nearly 100% of the total.

## 1.3 Solar Geometry — The Foundation of Inclined Surface Calculations

Before discussing inclined surfaces, students MUST master the following angles:

| Symbol | Angle Name | Definition |
|---|---|---|
| **φ** | Latitude | Angular position North (+) or South (−) of the equator |
| **δ** | Solar Declination | Angular position of Sun north (+) or south (−) of equator |
| **ω** | Hour Angle | 0° at solar noon, −15° per hour before noon, +15° per hour after |
| **θ_z** | Zenith Angle | Angle between the Sun and the vertical |
| **θ** | Altitude (Elevation) Angle | Angle between the Sun and the horizontal |
| **γ** | Solar Azimuth Angle | Horizontal angle measured from North to the Sun's projection |
| **β** | Tilt (Surface) Angle | Angle between the inclined surface and the horizontal |
| **γ_s** | Surface Azimuth Angle | Horizontal angle of the inclined surface from North |

**Key Relationship:** θ_z + θ = 90° (complementary angles)

## 1.4 Solar Radiation on an Inclined Surface — The Big Picture

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
> A solar collector or PV module is rarely installed horizontally — it is almost always tilted to maximize annual energy yield. The "radiation on an inclined surface" refers to the total solar flux incident on a tilted plane at angle β to the horizontal, facing a specific azimuth γ_s.

The three components on an inclined surface are:
- **Beam on tilted surface (I_bT)** — requires the *incidence angle* θ_i
- **Diffuse on tilted surface (I_dT)** — modeled by isotropic (Liu & Jordan) or anisotropic sky models
- **Reflected on tilted surface (I_rT)** — depends on ground reflectivity (albedo ρ_g)

$$I_T(\beta) = I_{bT} + I_{dT} + I_{rT}$$

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Picture a satellite dish (inclined surface). To receive the strongest signal from a satellite (the Sun), you must *aim* it correctly. The "aim" depends on three things:
1. **Where the Sun is in the sky** (solar geometry)
2. **How steeply you tilt the dish** (β)
3. **Which compass direction you face it** (γ_s)

## 1.5 Conversion Factors (Geometric Ratios)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (Critical for KTU Numericals):**
> Conversion factors are dimensionless ratios that convert radiation data measured on a *horizontal* surface into equivalent values on an *inclined* surface. They are also called **geometric factors** or **tilt factors**.

The three key conversion factors are:
- **R_b** — Beam (direct) conversion factor
- **R_d** — Diffuse conversion factor
- **R_r** — Reflected (ground) conversion factor

**Intuitive Analogy:**
Think of a *currency exchange rate*. The horizontal surface is Indian Rupees, the tilted surface is US Dollars. The conversion factor is the exchange rate. We need different rates (R_b, R_d, R_r) because direct, diffuse, and reflected radiation behave differently on a tilted surface.
