<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Extra-Terrestrial Radiation — Detailed Theoretical Analysis

### 2.1.1 The Earth's Orbital Geometry

The Earth revolves around the Sun in an elliptical orbit with:
- **Mean distance** = 1 AU = 1.496 × 10⁸ km
- **Perihelion** (closest, ~Jan 3): d = 0.983 AU → I_on ≈ 1413 W/m²
- **Aphehelion** (farthest, ~July 4): d = 1.017 AU → I_on ≈ 1321 W/m²

### 2.1.2 The Solar Declination Angle (δ)

The declination represents the season — it ranges from +23.45° (Summer Solstice, June 21) to −23.45° (Winter Solstice, Dec 21).

$$ \delta = 23.45^\circ \sin\left[\frac{360}{365}(284 + n)\right] $$

### 2.1.3 Extra-Terrestrial Daily Radiation on a Horizontal Surface (H_o)

The **hourly** extraterrestrial irradiance on a horizontal surface is:

$$ I_o = I_{on} \cos\theta_z $$

The **daily** (daily-integrated) extraterrestrial radiation on a horizontal surface is:

$$ H_o = \frac{24 \times 3600}{\pi} \, I_{sc} \left(1 + 0.033 \cos\frac{360n}{365}\right) \left[\cos\varphi \cos\delta \sin\omega_s + \frac{2\pi \omega_s}{360} \sin\varphi \sin\delta \right] $$

where the **sunset hour angle** is:

$$ \omega_s = \cos^{-1}(-\tan\varphi \tan\delta) $$

## 2.2 Solar Geometry — Why Each Angle Matters

**Why declination matters:** δ determines whether the Sun is north or south of the equator, affecting which hemisphere gets more sunlight on a given day.

**Why hour angle matters:** ω tracks the Sun's east-to-west motion (15°/hour). The sunrise/sunset hour angle ω_s defines the day length.

**Why incidence angle matters:** θ_i is THE critical angle for inclined surfaces — it tells us how perpendicular the Sun's rays are to the collector.

## 2.3 The Incidence Angle on a Tilted Surface

The angle between the Sun's beam and the *normal* to the inclined surface is:

$$ \cos\theta_i = \sin\delta \sin\varphi \cos\beta - \sin\delta \cos\varphi \sin\beta \cos\gamma_s + \cos\delta \cos\varphi \cos\omega \cos\beta + \cos\delta \sin\varphi \sin\omega \sin\gamma_s + \cos\delta \sin\beta \cos\omega \cos\varphi \sin\gamma_s + \cos\delta \sin\beta \sin\omega \cos\gamma_s $$

The simplified compact trigonometric identity (the most-used KTU form):

$$ \cos\theta_i = \cos\theta_z \cos\beta + \sin\theta_z \sin\beta \cos(\gamma_s - \gamma) $$

## 2.4 Terrestrial Radiation — Components and Atmospheric Effects

### 2.4.1 Direct Normal Irradiance (DNI) vs. Global Horizontal Irradiance (GHI)

- **GHI (I)** = total radiation on horizontal = I_b·cosθ_z + I_d
- **DNI (I_bn)** = direct beam on a surface normal to the Sun
- **DHI (I_d)** = diffuse on horizontal

$$ I = I_{bn} \cos\theta_z + I_d $$

### 2.4.2 Air Mass (AM)

The air mass quantifies atmospheric path length:

$$ AM = \frac{1}{\cos\theta_z} $$

For θ_z = 48.2°, AM = 1.5 (the standard test condition for PV modules: AM1.5).

### 2.4.3 Clearness Index (K_T)

The clearness index compares terrestrial to extraterrestrial radiation:

$$ K_T = \frac{H}{H_o} $$

- K_T > 0.65 → clear sky
- K_T = 0.35–0.65 → partly cloudy
- K_T < 0.35 → overcast

## 2.5 Conversion Factors — Complete Formulation

### 2.5.1 Beam Conversion Factor R_b

For an inclined surface facing the equator (γ_s = 0), R_b is:

$$ R_b = \frac{\cos(\varphi - \beta)\cos\delta \sin\omega_s' + \frac{\pi}{180} \omega_s' \sin(\varphi - \beta)\sin\delta}{\cos\varphi \cos\delta \sin\omega_s + \frac{\pi}{180} \omega_s \sin\varphi \sin\delta} $$

where ω_s' is the sunset hour angle on the tilted surface:

$$ \omega_s' = \min\left[\omega_s, \cos^{-1}(-\tan(\varphi - \beta)\tan\delta)\right] $$

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Special Case" simplification (KTU most-tested):**
> For a surface **facing the equator (γ_s = 0)** AND assuming the same day length on tilted and horizontal surfaces, the elegant closed form is:
>
> $$ R_b = \frac{\cos(\varphi - \beta)\cos\delta \sin\omega_s + \sin(\varphi - \beta)\sin\delta}{\cos\varphi \cos\delta \sin\omega_s + \sin\varphi \sin\delta} $$
>
> This is the most frequently tested formula in KTU Module 1.

### 2.5.2 Diffuse Conversion Factor R_d (Isotropic Sky Model — Liu & Jordan)

$$ R_d = \frac{1 + \cos\beta}{2} $$

This is the **view-factor to the sky** — it represents the fraction of the isotropic sky "seen" by the tilted surface.

### 2.5.3 Reflected Conversion Factor R_r

$$ R_r = \rho_g \frac{1 - \cos\beta}{2} $$

where ρ_g is the ground albedo (typically 0.2 for grass, 0.7 for fresh snow).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Reality:** The (1 − cos β)/2 term is the view-factor from the tilted surface to the ground. A vertical surface (β = 90°) sees 50% ground, while a horizontal surface (β = 0°) sees 0% ground.

## 2.6 Total Radiation on an Inclined Surface — Master Equation

Combining all three components using conversion factors:

$$ I_T(\beta) = I_b R_b + I_d R_d + (I_b + I_d) R_r $$

$$ \boxed{I_T(\beta) = I_b R_b + I_d \frac{(1 + \cos\beta)}{2} + \rho_g (I_b + I_d) \frac{(1 - \cos\beta)}{2}} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This single equation is the heart of KTU Module 1.** Master it, and you can solve nearly every Module 1 problem.

## 2.7 Monthly Average Daily Radiation on Inclined Surfaces

For monthly design (typical for PV system sizing), the mean daily extraterrestrial radiation on a horizontal surface is:

$$ \bar{H}_o = \frac{24}{\pi} I_{sc} \left(1 + 0.033 \cos\frac{360 \bar{n}}{365}\right) \left[\cos\varphi \cos\bar{\delta} \sin\bar{\omega}_s + \frac{\pi \bar{\omega}_s}{180} \sin\varphi \sin\bar{\delta} \right] $$

where overbars denote monthly mean values:
- **n̄** = mean day of the month (e.g., 17 for Jan, 47 for Feb, …, 344 for Dec)
- **δ̄** = mean declination for the month
- **ω̄_s** = mean monthly sunset hour angle

The **monthly mean clearness index**:

$$ \bar{K}_T = \frac{\bar{H}}{\bar{H}_o} $$

The **monthly average diffuse fraction** (Erbs correlation, or Collares-Pereira & Rabl):

$$ \frac{\bar{H}_d}{\bar{H}} = \begin{cases} 0.9511 - 0.1604 \bar{K}_T + 4.388 \bar{K}_T^2 - 16.638 \bar{K}_T^3 + 12.336 \bar{K}_T^4 & \text{for } \bar{K}_T < 0.715 \\ 0.165 & \text{for } \bar{K}_T \ge 0.715 \end{cases} $$

**The Liu and Jordan method for monthly average daily radiation on tilted surface:**

$$ \bar{H}_T = \bar{H}_b \bar{R}_b + \bar{H}_d \bar{R}_d + (\bar{H}_b + \bar{H}_d) \bar{R}_r $$

where:
- $\bar{H}_b = \bar{H} - \bar{H}_d$
- $\bar{H}_d$ is obtained from the Erbs correlation above
- $\bar{R}_b$ is the monthly mean beam conversion factor (with mean δ̄, mean ω̄_s)
- $\bar{R}_d = (1 + \cos\beta)/2$
- $\bar{R}_r = \rho_g (1 - \cos\beta)/2$

## 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **#** | **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Units** | **Remarks** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Solar constant | $I_{sc} = 1367$ | W/m² | WRMC standard |
| 2 | ETR on day *n* (normal) | $I_{on} = I_{sc}(1+0.033\cos(360n/365))$ | W/m² | Earth-Sun distance correction |
| 3 | Declination | $\delta = 23.45^\circ \sin[(360/365)(284+n)]$ | degrees | Spencer's formula |
| 4 | Hour angle | $\omega = 15^\circ(t_{solar} - 12)$ | degrees | −ve morning, +ve afternoon |
| 5 | Sunset hour angle | $\omega_s = \cos^{-1}(-\tan\varphi\tan\delta)$ | degrees | Day length = 2ω_s/15 hours |
| 6 | Zenith angle | $\cos\theta_z = \sin\varphi\sin\delta + \cos\varphi\cos\delta\cos\omega$ | degrees | 0 at noon |
| 7 | Altitude angle | $\sin\alpha = \cos\theta_z$ | degrees | α + θ_z = 90° |
| 8 | Air mass | $AM = 1/\cos\theta_z$ | dimensionless | AM1.5 = standard |
| 9 | Incidence on tilt | $\cos\theta_i = \cos\theta_z\cos\beta + \sin\theta_z\sin\beta\cos(\gamma_s - \gamma)$ | dimensionless | The master equation |
| 10 | Beam conversion | $R_b = \cos(\varphi-\beta)\cos\delta\sin\omega_s + (\pi\omega_s/180)\sin(\varphi-\beta)\sin\delta \over \cos\varphi\cos\delta\sin\omega_s + (\pi\omega_s/180)\sin\varphi\sin\delta$ | dimensionless | Equator-facing, simple form |
| 11 | Diffuse conversion | $R_d = (1+\cos\beta)/2$ | dimensionless | Isotropic sky |
| 12 | Reflected conversion | $R_r = \rho_g(1-\cos\beta)/2$ | dimensionless | View-factor to ground |
| 13 | Total on tilt | $H_T = H_b R_b + H_d R_d + \rho_g H(1-\cos\beta)/2$ | MJ/m²/day | Liu & Jordan |
| 14 | Clearness index | $K_T = H/H_o$ | dimensionless | 0 ≤ K_T ≤ 0.75 |
| 15 | Diffuse fraction (Erbs) | $H_d/H$ from polynomial in $K_T$ | dimensionless | Valid for $K_T \le 0.715$ |
| 16 | Optimum tilt (annual) | $\beta_{opt} \approx \vert\varphi\vert$ | degrees | Tilts 5°–15° from latitude |
| 17 | Optimum tilt (summer) | $\beta_{opt} = \vert\varphi\vert - 15^\circ$ | degrees | Maximum summer gain |
| 18 | Optimum tilt (winter) | $\beta_{opt} = \vert\varphi\vert + 15^\circ$ | degrees | Maximum winter gain |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Always check units in KTU exams:** W/m² for instantaneous, MJ/m²/day or kWh/m²/day for daily/monthly integrated.

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: The Beam Conversion Factor R_b for an Equator-Facing Surface

**Starting point:** The instantaneous beam radiation on a horizontal surface is $I_b = I_{bn}\cos\theta_z$, and on a tilted surface is $I_{bT} = I_{bn}\cos\theta_i$.

**Step 1 — Take the ratio of the two instantaneous values:**

$$ R_b^{inst} = \frac{I_{bn}\cos\theta_i}{I_{bn}\cos\theta_z} = \frac{\cos\theta_i}{\cos\theta_z} $$

**Step 2 — Substitute the cosines using solar geometry:**

For a surface facing the equator (γ_s = 0, γ = 0 for an equatorial location) at solar noon, the incidence angle reduces to θ_i = θ_z − β. The relevant terms in the cosine expressions become:

$$ \cos\theta_z = \sin\varphi\sin\delta + \cos\varphi\cos\delta\cos\omega $$

$$ \cos\theta_i = \sin(\varphi - \beta)\sin\delta + \cos(\varphi - \beta)\cos\delta\cos\omega $$

**Step 3 — Integrate over the day from sunrise to sunset:**

The daily beam radiation on a horizontal surface is:

$$ H_b = I_{bn} \int_{-\omega_s}^{\omega_s} \cos\theta_z \, d\omega $$

The daily beam radiation on a tilted surface (assuming same day length) is:

$$ H_{bT} = I_{bn} \int_{-\omega_s'}^{\omega_s'} \cos\theta_i \, d\omega $$

**Step 4 — Perform the integration:**

For a generic expression of the form $\int(A + B\cos\omega)d\omega = A\omega + B\sin\omega$:

$$ H_b = I_{bn} \int_{-\omega_s}^{\omega_s} (\sin\varphi\sin\delta + \cos\varphi\cos\delta\cos\omega)\,d\omega $$

$$ H_b = I_{bn} \left[ 2\omega_s \sin\varphi\sin\delta + 2\cos\varphi\cos\delta\sin\omega_s \right] $$

$$ H_b = 2 I_{bn} \left[\frac{\pi \omega_s}{180}\sin\varphi\sin\delta + \cos\varphi\cos\delta\sin\omega_s\right] \cdot \frac{180}{180} $$

Converting ω_s from degrees to radians (multiplied by π/180), we obtain the standard form:

$$ H_b = \frac{2 \times 180}{\pi} I_{bn} \left[\cos\varphi\cos\delta\sin\omega_s + \frac{\pi\omega_s}{180}\sin\varphi\sin\delta \right] $$

**Step 5 — Similarly compute H_bT (with φ replaced by φ − β):**

$$ H_{bT} = \frac{2 \times 180}{\pi} I_{bn} \left[\cos(\varphi-\beta)\cos\delta\sin\omega_s' + \frac{\pi\omega_s'}{180}\sin(\varphi-\beta)\sin\delta \right] $$

**Step 6 — Take the ratio H_bT / H_b:**

For the case where ω_s' = ω_s (i.e., the Sun is above the inclined surface for the full day — true when δ and β have the same sign, i.e., summer in the hemisphere):

$$ \boxed{R_b = \frac{H_{bT}}{H_b} = \frac{\cos(\varphi-\beta)\cos\delta\sin\omega_s + \frac{\pi\omega_s}{180}\sin(\varphi-\beta)\sin\delta}{\cos\varphi\cos\delta\sin\omega_s + \frac{\pi\omega_s}{180}\sin\varphi\sin\delta}} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** This is the formula KTU 2024 Module 1 Module 1 examiners *expect* students to write directly in Part B numericals.

## 3.2 Derivation: Daily Extra-Terrestrial Radiation on a Horizontal Surface H_o

**Starting Point:**

The instantaneous ETR on a horizontal surface is:

$$ I_o = I_{on}\cos\theta_z $$

The daily H_o is the time-integral from sunrise to sunset:

$$ H_o = \int_{-t_s}^{t_s} I_{on}\cos\theta_z \, dt $$

**Step 1 — Express dω = (2π/24) dt = (π/12) dt, so dt = (12/π) dω:**

$$ H_o = \int_{-\omega_s}^{\omega_s} I_{on} \cos\theta_z \frac{12}{\pi} d\omega $$

**Step 2 — Substitute the expression for cos θ_z:**

$$ H_o = \frac{12 I_{on}}{\pi} \int_{-\omega_s}^{\omega_s} \left[\sin\varphi\sin\delta + \cos\varphi\cos\delta\cos\omega\right] d\omega $$

**Step 3 — Perform the integration term-by-term:**

$$ \int_{-\omega_s}^{\omega_s} \sin\varphi\sin\delta \, d\omega = 2\omega_s \sin\varphi\sin\delta $$

$$ \int_{-\omega_s}^{\omega_s} \cos\varphi\cos\delta\cos\omega \, d\omega = 2\cos\varphi\cos\delta\sin\omega_s $$

**Step 4 — Combine and convert units to seconds:**

Since H_o must be in J/m²/day (and ω_s is in radians), we convert by multiplying by 3600 sec/hr:

$$ \boxed{H_o = \frac{24 \times 3600}{\pi} I_{sc}\left(1+0.033\cos\frac{360n}{365}\right)\left[\cos\varphi\cos\delta\sin\omega_s + \frac{2\pi\omega_s}{360}\sin\varphi\sin\delta\right]} $$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Typical 14-Mark Style)

**Problem:** A solar PV module is installed at a location with latitude φ = 28.6°N, longitude 77.2°E, tilted at β = 30° from horizontal, facing due south (γ_s = 0°). The day is March 21 (n = 80). Ground albedo ρ_g = 0.2. Given:
- Daily global horizontal radiation H = 18.5 MJ/m²/day
- Diffuse fraction H_d/H = 0.30

Compute:
**(a)** Hourly beam radiation on the tilted surface at 10:00 AM local solar time.
**(b)** Total daily radiation on the inclined surface using the Liu and Jordan method.

### Solution (a) — Hourly Beam at 10:00 AM

**Step A1 — Compute declination δ:**

$$ \delta = 23.45^\circ \sin\left[\frac{360}{365}(284+80)\right] = 23.45^\circ\sin(359.0^\circ) $$

$$ = 23.45^\circ \times (-0.01745) \approx -0.41^\circ $$

**Step A2 — Compute hour angle ω (10 AM = 2 hours before noon):**

$$ \omega = -15 \times 2 = -30^\circ $$

**Step A3 — Compute cos θ_z:**

$$ \cos\theta_z = \sin(28.6^\circ)\sin(-0.41^\circ) + \cos(28.6^\circ)\cos(-0.41^\circ)\cos(-30^\circ) $$

$$ = (0.479)(−0.0072) + (0.878)(0.99997)(0.866) $$

$$ = -0.00345 + 0.7603 = 0.7569 $$

**Step A4 — Compute cos θ_i using the master equation:**

$$ \cos\theta_i = \cos\theta_z\cos\beta + \sin\theta_z\sin\beta\cos(\gamma_s - \gamma) $$

For a south-facing surface in the Northern Hemisphere, the azimuth difference cos(γ_s − γ) ≈ cos(0) = 1 (sun is roughly south at 10 AM in March at this latitude).

$$ \cos\theta_i = 0.7569 \times 0.866 + 0.6535 \times 0.5 \times 1.0 $$

$$ = 0.6555 + 0.3267 = 0.9822 $$

**Step A5 — Compute the ratio I_bT / I_b (instantaneous R_b):**

$$ R_b^{inst} = \frac{\cos\theta_i}{\cos\theta_z} = \frac{0.9822}{0.7569} = 1.298 $$

**Step A6 — Compute hourly H_b from daily H:**

For an average day, hourly H_b ≈ H_b,daily/8 (approximate):

$$ H_b = 0.70 \times 18.5 = 12.95 \text{ MJ/m²/day} $$

$$ H_{b,hourly} \approx 12.95 / 8 = 1.619 \text{ MJ/m²/hr} $$

$$ H_{bT,hourly} = R_b \times H_{b,hourly} = 1.298 \times 1.619 = 2.10 \text{ MJ/m²/hr} $$

### Solution (b) — Total Daily Radiation on Inclined Surface

**Step B1 — Compute daily R_b (equator-facing simplified form):**

First compute sunset hour angle:

$$ \omega_s = \cos^{-1}(-\tan(28.6^\circ)\tan(-0.41^\circ)) = \cos^{-1}(-0.00104) = 90.06^\circ $$

Convert to radians: ω_s = 1.572 rad

$$ R_b = \frac{\cos(28.6^\circ - 30^\circ)\cos(-0.41^\circ)\sin(90.06^\circ) + \frac{\pi \times 1.572}{180}\sin(-1.4^\circ)\sin(-0.41^\circ)}{\cos(28.6^\circ)\cos(-0.41^\circ)\sin(90.06^\circ) + \frac{\pi \times 1.572}{180}\sin(28.6^\circ)\sin(-0.41^\circ)} $$

Numerator:
$$ = (0.9997)(0.99997)(1.0000) + (0.0274)(-0.0244)(-0.0072) $$

$$ = 0.9997 + 0.0000048 = 0.9997 $$

Denominator:
$$ = (0.878)(0.99997)(1.0000) + (0.0274)(0.479)(-0.0072) $$

$$ = 0.8780 - 0.0000945 = 0.8779 $$

$$ R_b = 0.9997 / 0.8779 = 1.139 $$

**Step B2 — Compute R_d and R_r:**

$$ R_d = \frac{1 + \cos 30^\circ}{2} = \frac{1 + 0.866}{2} = 0.933 $$

$$ R_r = 0.2 \times \frac{1 - \cos 30^\circ}{2} = 0.2 \times \frac{1 - 0.866}{2} = 0.2 \times 0.067 = 0.0134 $$

**Step B3 — Compute H_d, H_b:**

$$ H_d = 0.30 \times 18.5 = 5.55 \text{ MJ/m²/day} $$

$$ H_b = 18.5 - 5.55 = 12.95 \text{ MJ/m²/day} $$

**Step B4 — Apply Liu & Jordan equation:**

$$ H_T = H_b \cdot R_b + H_d \cdot R_d + (H_b + H_d) \cdot R_r $$

$$ H_T = 12.95 \times 1.139 + 5.55 \times 0.933 + 18.5 \times 0.0134 $$

$$ H_T = 14.75 + 5.18 + 0.248 $$

$$ \boxed{H_T = 20.18 \text{ MJ/m²/day}} $$

This represents a **9.1% gain** over the horizontal surface — clearly justifying the tilt.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** PV module manufacturers provide STC ratings in W. By converting H_T from MJ/m²/day to equivalent peak sun hours (1 PSH = 1 kWh/m²/day = 3.6 MJ/m²/day), designers can compute daily energy yield.

```mermaid
    B2 --> G
    B3 --> G
    H[Horizontal H] --> I[Split Hb and Hd]
    I --> G
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Tip (KTU Exam):** The architecture flow diagrams above are designed to help students visually trace the data flow when solving a numerical problem. Always start at the top (input data) and follow the branches downward to reach the required answer.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A: 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define extra-terrestrial radiation. Explain the variation of solar constant with the day of the year.**

**Model Answer:**
Extra-terrestrial radiation (ETR) is the solar radiation incident on a horizontal surface at the top of the Earth's atmosphere, before any atmospheric attenuation. The solar constant I_sc is defined as 1367 W/m² and represents the ETR on a surface normal to the Sun's rays at mean Earth-Sun distance. However, due to the elliptical orbit (eccentricity e = 0.0167), the actual ETR on a normal surface varies throughout the year as:

$$I_{on} = I_{sc}\left(1 + 0.033\cos\frac{360n}{365}\right)$$

Maximum I_on ≈ 1413 W/m² occurs near perihelion (n ≈ 3, early January), and minimum I_on ≈ 1321 W/m² near aphelion (n ≈ 185, early July).

---

### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**List the three components of terrestrial radiation. Explain the role of the clearness index K_T.**

**Model Answer:**
Terrestrial radiation consists of:
1. **Direct (Beam) radiation (I_b)** — collimated radiation from the Sun's disk
2. **Diffuse radiation (I_d)** — scattered radiation from the sky dome
3. **Reflected (Albedo) radiation (I_r)** — radiation reflected from the ground

Total: I_T = I_b + I_d + I_r

The **clearness index K_T = H/H_o** is the ratio of terrestrial to extraterrestrial daily radiation. It is a dimensionless indicator of atmospheric clarity. Typical values: 0.6–0.7 (clear), 0.4–0.6 (partly cloudy), < 0.3 (overcast). It is essential for estimating the diffuse fraction via empirical correlations (Erbs, Orgill-Hollands).

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2023]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the daily extra-terrestrial radiation H_o on a horizontal surface. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** At a location with latitude φ = 13.08°N, on the equinox day (March 21, n = 80), calculate:
- (i) Solar declination
- (ii) Sunset hour angle
- (iii) Day length
- (iv) Daily extra-terrestrial radiation H_o

**\[7 Marks\]**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Derivation:**

Starting from the instantaneous ETR on a horizontal surface:

$$I_o = I_{on}\cos\theta_z = I_{on}\left(\sin\varphi\sin\delta + \cos\varphi\cos\delta\cos\omega\right)$$

The daily H_o is the integral from −ω_s to +ω_s, using dω = (π/12) dt, dt in hours, with the final conversion to seconds (×3600):

$$H_o = \int_{-\omega_s}^{\omega_s}I_o\frac{12 \times 3600}{\pi}d\omega$$

Substituting and integrating term by term:

$$H_o = \frac{24 \times 3600}{\pi} I_{on}\left[\cos\varphi\cos\delta\sin\omega_s + \frac{2\pi\omega_s}{360}\sin\varphi\sin\delta\right]$$

where I_on = I_sc(1 + 0.033 cos(360n/365)) and ω_s = cos⁻¹(−tan φ tan δ).

**Part (b) — Numerical:**

**(i) Declination:**

$$\delta = 23.45^\circ\sin\left[\frac{360}{365}(284+80)\right] = 23.45^\circ\sin(359.0^\circ) \approx -0.41^\circ \approx 0^\circ$$

**(ii) Sunset hour angle:**

$$\omega_s = \cos^{-1}(-\tan(13.08^\circ)\tan(-0.41^\circ))$$

$$= \cos^{-1}(-(-0.2324)(-0.0072)) = \cos^{-1}(0.00167) = 89.90^\circ$$

**(iii) Day length:**

$$N = \frac{2\omega_s}{15} = \frac{2 \times 89.90}{15} = 11.99 \text{ hours} \approx 12 \text{ hours}$$

(As expected at the equinox.)

**(iv) Daily ETR:**

$$I_{on} = 1367\left(1 + 0.033\cos\frac{360 \times 80}{365}\right) = 1367(1 + 0.033\cos(78.9^\circ))$$

$$= 1367(1 + 0.033 \times 0.192) = 1367(1.00634) = 1375.7 \text{ W/m²}$$

Converting ω_s to radians: 89.90° × π/180 = 1.569 rad

$$H_o = \frac{24 \times 3600}{\pi}(1375.7)\left[(0.9745)(1.0)\sin(89.90^\circ) + (1.569/2)\sin(13.08^\circ)(0)\right]$$

$$= \frac{86400}{\pi}(1375.7)\left[0.9745 \times 0.99999 + 0\right]$$

$$= 27502.9 \times 1375.7 \times 0.9744 = 36.86 \times 10^6 \text{ J/m²/day}$$

$$H_o \approx 36.86 \text{ MJ/m²/day}$$

(Equivalent to ~10.24 kWh/m²/day — note this is at the top of atmosphere, so terrestrial values will be lower.)

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**

**(a)** What are conversion factors? Derive expressions for R_b, R_d, and R_r for a tilted surface. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** A south-facing PV array is installed at latitude φ = 30°N, tilted at β = 30°, with ground albedo ρ_g = 0.2. On a clear day with H = 22 MJ/m²/day and diffuse fraction = 0.25, compute the total daily radiation on the inclined surface assuming isotropic sky. Use mean day n̄ = 105 (mid-April) and assume δ = +9.4°, ω_s = 95°. **\[7 Marks\]**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Concept and Derivation:**

Conversion factors are dimensionless ratios that convert radiation data from a horizontal surface to an inclined surface. Three factors exist:

**Beam conversion factor (equator-facing, simplified):**

$$R_b = \frac{\cos(\varphi-\beta)\cos\delta\sin\omega_s + \frac{\pi\omega_s}{180}\sin(\varphi-\beta)\sin\delta}{\cos\varphi\cos\delta\sin\omega_s + \frac{\pi\omega_s}{180}\sin\varphi\sin\delta}$$

**Diffuse conversion factor (Liu & Jordan isotropic sky):**

$$R_d = \frac{1 + \cos\beta}{2}$$

**Reflected conversion factor:**

$$R_r = \rho_g\frac{1 - \cos\beta}{2}$$

The physical interpretation: R_d and R_r are view-factors from the tilted surface to the sky and ground hemispheres respectively.

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation:**

**Step 1 — Compute R_b:**

Given φ = 30°, β = 30°, δ = 9.4°, ω_s = 95° = 1.658 rad.

$$R_b = \frac{\cos(0)\cos(9.4^\circ)\sin(95^\circ) + (1.658/2)\sin(0)\sin(9.4^\circ)}{\cos(30^\circ)\cos(9.4^\circ)\sin(95^\circ) + (1.658/2)\sin(30^\circ)\sin(9.4^\circ)}$$

Numerator:
$$ = 1 \times 0.9863 \times 0.9962 + 0.829 \times 0 \times 0.1634 = 0.9826 $$

Denominator:
$$ = 0.866 \times 0.9863 \times 0.9962 + 0.829 \times 0.5 \times 0.1634 $$

$$ = 0.8510 + 0.0677 = 0.9187 $$

$$R_b = 0.9826 / 0.9187 = 1.070$$

**Step 2 — Compute R_d:**

$$R_d = \frac{1 + \cos 30^\circ}{2} = \frac{1 + 0.866}{2} = 0.933$$

**Step 3 — Compute R_r:**

$$R_r = 0.2 \times \frac{1 - \cos 30^\circ}{2} = 0.2 \times \frac{1 - 0.866}{2} = 0.2 \times 0.067 = 0.0134$$

**Step 4 — Compute H_d, H_b:**

$$H_d = 0.25 \times 22 = 5.5 \text{ MJ/m²/day}$$

$$H_b = 22 - 5.5 = 16.5 \text{ MJ/m²/day}$$

**Step 5 — Apply Liu & Jordan equation:**

$$H_T = H_b R_b + H_d R_d + (H_b + H_d) R_r$$

$$= 16.5 \times 1.070 + 5.5 \times 0.933 + 22 \times 0.0134$$

$$= 17.655 + 5.1315 + 0.2948$$

$$\boxed{H_T = 23.08 \text{ MJ/m²/day}}$$

This is a **4.9% improvement** over the horizontal — typical for a near-vertical sun in April at low latitudes.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** Students often mix degrees and radians in the (πω_s/180) term. Always confirm: if ω_s is in degrees in the numerator's sin, then multiply by π/180 to get radians for the second term.
> 2. **Declination Sign Error:** Mixing up + and − for δ. Remember: positive in Northern Hemisphere summer, negative in winter.
> 3. **Forgetting to Convert H_o Units:** H_o is in J/m²/day if you don't divide by 10⁶. Always express final answers in MJ/m²/day or kWh/m²/day.
> 4. **R_b Formula Confusion:** KTU examiners expect the *equator-facing simplified form* unless otherwise stated. Writing the full generic γ_s form for a south-facing surface will lose marks for not using the "default case."
> 5. **Sign of (φ − β) in R_b:** If β > φ (tilt steeper than latitude), the term sin(φ − β) becomes negative — this is correct, not an error. It accounts for the sun being behind the surface for part of the day in winter.
> 6. **Omission of (H_b + H_d) in R_r Term:** A classic blunder. The reflected term uses *total* horizontal radiation multiplied by R_r, not just H_b or H_d alone.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Solar constant I_sc = 1367 W/m²** is the foundational value — never write 1353 or 1361.
- [x] The ETR correction factor (1 + 0.033 cos(360n/365)) accounts for Earth-Sun distance variation (±3.4%).
- [x] **Solar declination δ** ranges from +23.45° to −23.45° and follows a sinusoidal annual cycle.
- [x] **Sunset hour angle ω_s** defines day length: N = 2ω_s/15 hours.
- [x] The **incidence angle θ_i** is the master angle for inclined surfaces — memorize the compact identity.
- [x] The **three conversion factors** are: R_b (geometry), R_d = (1+cos β)/2 (sky view), R_r = ρ_g(1−cos β)/2 (ground view).
- [x] The **Liu & Jordan equation** H_T = H_b R_b + H_d R_d + H R_r is the heart of Module 1 — practice writing it 10 times.
- [x] The **Clearness index K_T** quantifies atmospheric transparency and drives the Erbs diffuse correlation.
- [x] **Optimum annual tilt** ≈ latitude (β_opt = φ), with ±15° seasonal adjustments.
- [x] **Monthly mean day numbers** (n̄): Jan=17, Feb=47, Mar=75, Apr=105, May=135, Jun=172, Jul=198, Aug=228, Sep=258, Oct=288, Nov=318, Dec=344.
- [x] For an **equator-facing surface**, R_b simplifies to the closed form using (φ − β). KTU numericals are almost always this case.
- [x] **Units check:** W/m² (instantaneous) vs. MJ/m²/day or kWh/m²/day (daily integrated). 1 kWh/m²/day = 3.6 MJ/m²/day = 1 Peak Sun Hour.
- [x] **Albedo values:** grass ≈ 0.20, dry soil ≈ 0.30, sand ≈ 0.40, fresh snow ≈ 0.70, water ≈ 0.07.
- [x] The **isotropic sky assumption** (Liu & Jordan) underestimates diffuse on tilted surfaces. **Anisotropic models** (Hay-Davies, Perez) are more accurate but are not Module 1 syllabus.
- [x] The **beam gain** (R_b > 1) is the main reason for tilting; the diffuse and reflected terms partially offset this gain when the tilt is steep.
- [x] **Examine the KTU Formula Sheet (Section 2.8)** as a final revision checklist before any Module 1 exam.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Solar Thermal System, Principle of Conversion of Solar Radiation into Heat
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEET721 - DESIGN OF SOLAR PV SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Solar Thermal System, Principle of Conversion of Solar Radiation into Heat

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of a Solar Thermal System

A **Solar Thermal System** is an engineered assembly of components designed to intercept incoming **solar electromagnetic radiation**, convert this radiant energy into **thermal (heat) energy** through the photothermal effect, and deliver the captured heat for direct end-use (water/air heating, space heating, industrial process heat) or for indirect conversion to **mechanical work** and subsequently **electrical power** via thermodynamic cycles.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The conversion of solar radiation to heat is the *upstream* phenomenon for ALL solar energy applications. Even Solar PV systems must first absorb photons — the difference is the *terminal* conversion pathway (electrical versus thermal).

The governing energy cascade in a generic solar thermal system is:

$$\text{Solar Radiation} \rightarrow \text{Absorbed Radiant Energy} \rightarrow \text{Sensible/Latent Heat} \rightarrow \text{End Use or Work}$$

---

## 1.2 The Principle of Conversion of Solar Radiation into Heat

### 1.2.1 Physical Principle

Solar radiation, as a stream of **photons**, carries discrete quanta of energy given by the Planck–Einstein relation:

$$E_{photon} = h\nu = \frac{hc}{\lambda}$$

where $h$ is **Planck's constant** $\mathbf{(6.626 \times 10^{-34}\ J \cdot s)}$ and $c$ is the speed of light in vacuum. When these photons strike a material surface, three fundamental interactions occur:

1. **Absorption (coefficient $\alpha$):** Photon energy is transferred to the material's electrons/lattice, exciting vibrational and rotational modes — manifesting macroscopically as a **rise in temperature**.
2. **Reflection (coefficient $\rho$):** Photons bounce off without energy transfer.
3. **Transmission (coefficient $\tau$):** Photons pass through the material entirely.

The energy conservation identity holds for any surface:

$$\alpha + \rho + \tau = 1$$

For an **opaque surface** (such as the absorber plate of a solar collector), $\tau = 0$, which simplifies to:

$$\alpha + \rho = 1$$

### 1.2.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Black Bucket vs The White Bucket

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Check:** Imagine placing two identical buckets of water in the noon sun — one painted **matte black**, the other painted **glossy white**. After one hour, the water in the black bucket is noticeably warmer.

The reason: The black surface has a high **solar absorptivity ($\alpha \approx 0.95$)** and a low reflectivity ($\rho \approx 0.05$), so almost all incident photons are trapped and converted to molecular kinetic energy (heat). The white surface reflects most photons back to the sky, so its temperature rise is minimal. **This is the entire principle of solar thermal energy capture in a nutshell.**

A more sophisticated analogy is the **greenhouse effect** used in flat-plate collectors: short-wave solar radiation passes freely through the glass cover, gets absorbed by the dark absorber plate, and re-radiates as long-wave infrared radiation which the glass is **opaque** to. The heat is thus trapped — exactly as a horticultural greenhouse keeps plants warm.

---

## 1.3 The Solar Resource — Spectral Character

The sun approximates a **blackbody at 5778 K**, but the terrestrial solar spectrum is modified by atmospheric scattering and absorption (water vapour, CO₂, O₃). The standard reference is **AM 1.5 (Air Mass 1.5) Global Tilt**, with integrated intensity:

$$G_{sc} \approx 1000\ W/m^2\ (\text{peak solar irradiance at Earth's surface})$$

The solar spectrum is concentrated in the **0.3 to 2.5 μm** band, whereas a solar collector operating at, say, 350 K re-emits thermal radiation primarily in the **2.5 to 50 μm** band. This spectral mismatch is the foundation for **selective surfaces** discussed in Section 2.

---

## 1.4 Classification of Solar Thermal Systems

Solar thermal systems are conventionally classified by the **temperature regime** of the working fluid:

| Temperature Regime | Typical Range | Representative Technology | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Low-Temperature | $\lt 100^\circ C$ | Flat-plate collector, evacuated tube | Domestic water heating, space heating, swimming pools |
| Medium-Temperature | $100^\circ C - 400^\circ C$ | Parabolic trough, compound parabolic | Industrial process heat, air conditioning, refrigeration |
| High-Temperature | $\gt 400^\circ C$ | Solar power tower, parabolic dish, solar furnace | Electricity generation, materials processing, hydrogen production |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Examiners often award marks for *correctly placing a given collector type within the temperature regime* and stating the *physical reason* (concentration ratio) for the classification.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three-Layer Energy Interaction Model

Every solar thermal conversion process can be decomposed into three sequential energy interactions, each governed by a measurable radiative property:

### Layer 1 — Atmospheric Attenuation

The extraterrestrial flux $G_{o}$ is reduced by atmospheric transmittance $\tau_{atm}$ to yield terrestrial flux $G_T$:

$$G_T = G_o \cdot \tau_{atm}$$

### Layer 2 — Collector Cover Transmission

If the collector has a transparent cover (e.g., glass), only a fraction $\tau_{cover}$ of the incident flux reaches the absorber:

$$G_{abs} = G_T \cdot \tau_{cover} \cdot \alpha_{absorber}$$

The combined product $\mathbf{(\tau\alpha)}$ is called the **effective absorptance** and is a critical design parameter — a KTU favourite.

### Layer 3 — Useful Heat Extraction

The absorbed flux minus thermal losses equals the useful heat flux:

$$q_u = G_{abs} - q_{loss}$$

---

## 2.2 Blackbody Radiation Laws (Foundational to Solar Thermal Design)

A blackbody is an idealized surface that absorbs ALL incident radiation ($\alpha = 1$). The following laws govern the radiative emission from such a surface and form the theoretical bedrock of solar collector analysis.

### 2.2.1 Planck's Law of Blackbody Spectral Emittance

$$E_{\lambda, b}(T) = \frac{c_1}{\lambda^5 \left[ \exp\left(\dfrac{c_2}{\lambda T}\right) - 1 \right]}\ \left[\frac{W}{m^2 \cdot m}\right]$$

with $c_1 = 2\pi h c^2 = 3.742 \times 10^8\ W \cdot m^2 / m^2$ and $c_2 = hc/k_B = 1.439 \times 10^4\ \mu m \cdot K$.

### 2.2.2 Stefan–Boltzmann Law

The total hemispherical emission integrated over all wavelengths:

$$E_b = \sigma T^4\ \left[\frac{W}{m^2}\right]$$

where the Stefan–Boltzmann constant $\mathbf{\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}\ W/m^2 K^4}$.

### 2.2.3 Wien's Displacement Law

The wavelength of peak spectral emittance is inversely proportional to temperature:

$$\lambda_{max} \cdot T = 2.898 \times 10^{-3}\ m \cdot K$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why This Matters for KTU:** Wien's law explains *why* a hot collector (say 1000 K) emits radiation in the near-IR to visible, whereas a warm absorber (350 K) emits in the mid- to far-IR. This is the *spectral mismatch* that selective coatings exploit.

---

## 2.3 Kirchhoff's Law of Thermal Radiation

A body in **thermal equilibrium** with its surroundings must emit exactly as much energy at each wavelength as it absorbs at that wavelength. Mathematically:

$$\alpha_{\lambda}(T) = \varepsilon_{\lambda}(T)$$

This means a surface that is a *good solar absorber* is also a *good thermal emitter* — a paradox that motivated the invention of **selective surfaces** (Section 2.4).

---

## 2.4 Selective Surfaces — The Workhorse of Modern Collectors

A **selective surface** is engineered to have a **spectrally selective** radiative response:

- **High $\alpha$** in the solar spectrum (0.3 – 2.5 μm) — captures as much sunlight as possible.
- **Low $\varepsilon$** in the thermal IR spectrum (2.5 – 50 μm) — minimizes radiative re-emission losses at operating temperature.

The **performance metric** of a selective coating is the ratio $\alpha / \varepsilon$. A high-quality selective surface satisfies:

$$\alpha \ge 0.90,\ \ \varepsilon \le 0.10,\ \ \text{yielding}\ \ \alpha / \varepsilon \ge 9$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favourite:** Expect a 3-mark short question asking *"Why are selective surfaces used in solar collectors?"* Answer by invoking Kirchhoff's law, the spectral mismatch between sun and absorber, and the resulting minimization of thermal re-radiation losses.

### Common Selective Coating Chemistries

- **Black Chrome** ($\alpha/\varepsilon \approx 0.95/0.10$)
- **Black Nickel** ($\alpha/\varepsilon \approx 0.92/0.12$)
- **Cermet Composites** (e.g., Mo–Al₂O₃, $\alpha/\varepsilon \approx 0.95/0.05$)
- **Intrinsic Absorbers** (e.g., HfC, ZrB₂ — used in high-temperature applications)

---

## 2.5 Thermal Loss Mechanisms in a Solar Collector

Once the absorber is heated, three loss pathways compete with useful heat extraction:

| Loss Mechanism | Governing Equation | Typical Magnitude |
|---|---|---|
| **Conduction–Convection** (top, edges, bottom) | $q_{cond-conv} = U_L (T_p - T_a)$ | Dominant at low–mid temperatures |
| **Thermal Radiation** (from hot plate to sky) | $q_{rad} = \varepsilon \sigma (T_p^4 - T_{sky}^4)$ | Grows as $T_p^4$; critical at high $T$ |
| **Reflection from cover** | $q_{ref} = (1 - \tau_{cover}) G_T$ | Mitigated by anti-reflective coatings |

The lumped parameter $\mathbf{U_L}$ is the **overall heat loss coefficient** with units $\mathbf{W/m^2 K}$.

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize this table — it covers roughly 80% of numerical problems in KTU Solar Energy modules.

| Symbol | Quantity | Formula / Definition | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\alpha$ | Solar absorptivity | $\alpha = \dfrac{Q_{abs}}{Q_{inc}}$ | dimensionless |
| $\rho$ | Solar reflectivity | $\rho = \dfrac{Q_{ref}}{Q_{inc}}$ | dimensionless |
| $\tau$ | Solar transmissivity | $\tau = \dfrac{Q_{trans}}{Q_{inc}}$ | dimensionless |
| $\varepsilon$ | Thermal emissivity | $\varepsilon_{\lambda} = \alpha_{\lambda}\ (\text{Kirchhoff})$ | dimensionless |
| $E_b$ | Blackbody emissive power | $E_b = \sigma T^4$ | $W/m^2$ |
| $\lambda_{max}$ | Peak-emission wavelength | $\lambda_{max} T = 2.898 \times 10^{-3}$ | $m \cdot K$ |
| $(\tau\alpha)$ | Effective absorptance | product of cover transmittance and plate absorptivity | dimensionless |
| $U_L$ | Overall heat loss coefficient | $U_L = U_T + U_B + U_E$ (top + bottom + edge) | $W/m^2 K$ |
| $F_R$ | Heat removal factor | $F_R = \dfrac{\dot{m}c_p(T_{fo} - T_{fi})}{A \cdot G_T \cdot (\tau\alpha)}$ | dimensionless |
| $\eta$ | Instantaneous thermal efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{Q_u}{A \cdot G_T}$ | dimensionless |
| $K_{\tau\alpha}$ | Incidence angle modifier | corrects $(\tau\alpha)$ for non-normal incidence | dimensionless |
| $G_T$ | Total incident solar irradiance | direct + diffuse on tilted surface | $W/m^2$ |
| $G_{sc}$ | Solar constant | $\mathbf{1367\ W/m^2}$ (TOA value) | $W/m^2$ |
| $\sigma$ | Stefan–Boltzmann constant | $5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ | $W/m^2 K^4$ |
| $h$ | Planck's constant | $6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ | $J \cdot s$ |
| $k_B$ | Boltzmann constant | $1.381 \times 10^{-23}$ | $J/K$ |

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

Solar thermal conversion underpins the following high-impact applications:

- **Domestic Hot Water (DHW)** systems using flat-plate collectors — the largest deployed solar thermal technology worldwide.
- **Solar Industrial Process Heat (SIPH)** for textiles, food, dairy, and chemical industries up to 350°C.
- **Concentrated Solar Power (CSP)** plants (parabolic trough, tower, dish-Stirling) for grid-scale renewable electricity.
- **Solar Desalination** via multi-effect distillation and humidification–dehumidification.
- **Solar Cooling** through absorption/adsorption chillers (driven by 80–200°C heat).
- **Building Integrated Solar Thermal (BIST)** in façade and roof-integrated collectors.
- **Indirect PV Synergy** — solar thermal preheating improves the electrical efficiency of hybrid PV-T (photovoltaic-thermal) modules.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Energy Conservation on a Solar Absorber (Opaque Surface)

**Statement:** For a flat, opaque absorber plate under steady-state solar irradiation, derive the relationship between absorptivity, reflectivity, and the absence of transmission.

### Step 1 — Define the Incident, Reflected, Absorbed, and Transmitted Energy Streams

Let $Q_i$ be the total incident energy per unit area per unit time. This incident energy is partitioned into three disjoint fates: $Q_r$ reflected, $Q_a$ absorbed, and $Q_t$ transmitted.

$$Q_i = Q_a + Q_r + Q_t$$

### Step 2 — Introduce the Material Coefficients

By definition, each coefficient is the ratio of its respective energy stream to the incident stream:

$$\alpha = \frac{Q_a}{Q_i}, \quad \rho = \frac{Q_r}{Q_i}, \quad \tau = \frac{Q_t}{Q_i}$$

### Step 3 — Substitute the Coefficients into the Conservation Equation

$$Q_i = \alpha Q_i + \rho Q_i + \tau Q_i$$

Dividing both sides by $Q_i$ (which is strictly positive):

$$1 = \alpha + \rho + \tau$$

### Step 4 — Apply the Opaque-Surface Constraint

For a typical absorber plate (metallic or cermet), the thickness is many orders of magnitude greater than the skin depth of the solar spectrum, so no photons pass through. Therefore $\tau = 0$:

$$\boxed{\alpha + \rho = 1}$$

This implies that a *good solar absorber* must be a *poor solar reflector*, which intuitively validates the use of **dark, matte, micro-roughened** surfaces in collector design.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Spectral Distribution Shift (Wien's Displacement Law from Planck's Law)

**Statement:** Show that the wavelength of maximum spectral emittance is inversely proportional to absolute temperature.

### Step 1 — Write Planck's Spectral Emittance Function

$$E_{\lambda,b}(T) = \frac{c_1}{\lambda^5 \left[ \exp\left(\dfrac{c_2}{\lambda T}\right) - 1 \right]}$$

### Step 2 — Differentiate with Respect to $\lambda$ and Equate to Zero

At the peak, $\dfrac{dE_{\lambda,b}}{d\lambda} = 0$. Performing the calculus:

$$\frac{dE_{\lambda,b}}{d\lambda} = 0 \implies -5\lambda^{-6}\left[\exp\left(\frac{c_2}{\lambda T}\right) - 1\right] - \lambda^{-5} \exp\left(\frac{c_2}{\lambda T}\right)\left(-\frac{c_2}{\lambda^2 T}\right) = 0$$

### Step 3 — Simplify by Defining the Variable $x = \dfrac{c_2}{\lambda_{max} T}$

Substituting $x$ into the derivative-zero equation and simplifying yields the transcendental root:

$$5(1 - e^x) = x$$

### Step 4 — Solve Numerically for $x$

The non-trivial solution of this equation is:

$$x = 4.9651142317 \approx 4.9651$$

### Step 5 — Invert to Obtain Wien's Displacement Law

$$\lambda_{max} T = \frac{c_2}{x} = \frac{1.4388 \times 10^4\ \mu m \cdot K}{4.9651} = 2898\ \mu m \cdot K$$

$$\boxed{\lambda_{max} T = 2.898 \times 10^{-3}\ m \cdot K = 2898\ \mu m \cdot K}$$

**Physical Interpretation:** As an absorber heats up, its peak emission wavelength **shifts to shorter values** (Wien's displacement). A blackbody at 5800 K (the Sun) peaks near 0.5 μm (green-yellow), while a collector absorber at 400 K peaks near 7.2 μm (mid-IR). This separation is the *spectral gap* that selective surfaces exploit.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Useful Heat Gain Equation (Hottel–Whillier–Bliss Equation)

**Statement:** Derive the fundamental thermal efficiency equation for a flat-plate solar collector under steady-state conditions.

### Step 1 — Perform a Thermal Energy Balance on the Absorber Plate

At steady state, the rate of useful energy removal by the working fluid equals the absorbed solar flux minus all thermal losses:

$$Q_u = A \cdot S - Q_{loss}$$

where $S$ is the absorbed flux per unit area and $A$ is the collector aperture area.

### Step 2 — Express the Absorbed Solar Flux

The flux absorbed by the plate after passing through the cover(s) is:

$$S = G_T \cdot (\tau\alpha)$$

So:

$$Q_u = A \cdot G_T \cdot (\tau\alpha) - Q_{loss}$$

### Step 3 — Express Heat Losses Using the Lumped-Parameter Model

The total heat loss from the top, bottom, and edges of the collector is lumped into a single overall heat loss coefficient $U_L$ and the temperature difference between the **plate** and the **ambient**:

$$Q_{loss} = A \cdot U_L \cdot (T_p - T_a)$$

Substituting:

$$Q_u = A \cdot G_T \cdot (\tau\alpha) - A \cdot U_L \cdot (T_p - T_a)$$

### Step 4 — Relate Plate Temperature to Fluid Inlet Temperature via the Heat Removal Factor

Because the plate is not isothermal (it is hotter than the fluid), we define the **heat removal factor** $F_R$ that relates the fluid inlet temperature $T_{fi}$ to the plate temperature $T_p$:

$$F_R = \frac{\dot{m} c_p (T_{fo} - T_{fi})}{A \cdot G_T \cdot (\tau\alpha)}$$

Using this definition, one can show (through a small heat-transfer analysis on the fluid channel) that:

$$Q_u = A \cdot F_R \cdot \left[ G_T \cdot (\tau\alpha) - U_L \cdot (T_{fi} - T_a) \right]$$

This is the celebrated **Hottel–Whillier–Bliss Equation**.

### Step 5 — Derive the Instantaneous Thermal Efficiency

The instantaneous thermal efficiency of the collector is the useful heat delivered per unit incident solar power:

$$\eta_{th} = \frac{Q_u}{A \cdot G_T}$$

Dividing the HWB equation by $A \cdot G_T$:

$$\boxed{\eta_{th} = F_R \left[ (\tau\alpha) - \frac{U_L (T_{fi} - T_a)}{G_T} \right]}$$

### Step 6 — Discuss the Physical Meaning of Each Term

- $\mathbf{F_R (\tau\alpha)}$ is the **maximum optical efficiency** (achieved when fluid inlet = ambient).
- $\dfrac{U_L (T_{fi} - T_a)}{G_T}$ is the **fractional loss** — proportional to how hot the fluid is relative to ambient.
- The collector stops delivering useful heat when $\eta_{th} = 0$, defining the **stagnation temperature** $T_{stag}$:

$$T_{stag} = T_a + \frac{G_T \cdot (\tau\alpha)}{U_L}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Derivation Pitfall:** Students often forget the $F_R$ factor and write $\eta = (\tau\alpha) - U_L(T_{fi} - T_a)/G_T$. This yields inflated efficiency. The $F_R$ correction is **mandatory** for full marks.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Effective Transmittance–Absorptance Product Under Oblique Incidence

**Statement:** For a glass cover at incidence angle $\theta$, derive the modified $(\tau\alpha)$ product using Fresnel's equations.

### Step 1 — Component-Wise Transmittance

For a single glass cover, the transmittance is reduced from its normal-incidence value $\tau_n$ by the incidence angle modifier $K_{\tau\alpha}$:

$$(\tau\alpha)_\theta = (\tau\alpha)_n \cdot K_{\tau\alpha}(\theta)$$

### Step 2 — Fresnel Reflection Coefficients

The reflectance for a glass–air interface is polarization-dependent. For unpolarized sunlight, the average reflectance is:

$$r = \frac{1}{2}(r_s + r_p)$$

with $r_s = \left(\dfrac{n_1 \cos\theta_i - n_2 \cos\theta_t}{n_1 \cos\theta_i + n_2 \cos\theta_t}\right)^2$ and $r_p = \left(\dfrac{n_2 \cos\theta_i - n_1 \cos\theta_t}{n_2 \cos\theta_i + n_1 \cos\theta_t}\right)^2$

### Step 3 — Snell's Law Linking $\theta_t$ to $\theta_i$

$$n_1 \sin\theta_i = n_2 \sin\theta_t$$

### Step 4 — Final Form

For a single glass cover (n ≈ 1.526), the empirical correlation by Brandemuehl and Beckman is used in KTU textbooks:

$$K_{\tau\alpha} = 1 + b_0 \left(\frac{1}{\cos\theta_i} - 1\right)$$

with $b_0 = -0.223$ for a single cover. At $\theta = 60^\circ$, $K_{\tau\alpha} \approx 0.65$, reducing $(\tau\alpha)$ by about 35%.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why This Matters:** Real collectors are tilted; the optical losses at oblique incidence can be 10–40% and must be accounted for in KTU design problems.

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Generic Solar Thermal System

The following Mermaid block diagram traces the full path of solar energy from the sun to end use, identifying every component and energy stream. It is a "block-level functional architecture" representation — the requested alternative to physical drawings.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sun 5778 K Blackbody Source] --> B[Solar Irradiance G sub sc 1367 W per m squared at TOA]
    B --> C[Atmospheric Attenuation tau sub atm]
    C --> D[Terrestrial Irradiance G sub T 0 to 1100 W per m squared]
    D --> E[Collector Aperture Area A in m squared]
    E --> F[Transparent Cover tau sub cover 0.85 to 0.92]
    F --> G[Selective Absorber Plate alpha 0.90 to 0.95 and epsilon 0.05 to 0.15]
    G --> H[Absorbed Flux S equals tau alpha times G sub T]
    G --> I[Thermal Losses U sub L times T sub p minus T sub a]
    H --> J[Useful Heat Q sub u equals A F sub R times S minus U sub L T sub fi minus T sub a]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Heat Transfer Fluid Water or Glycol or Air or Thermic Oil]
    K --> L[Thermal Energy Storage Sensible or Latent or Thermochemical]
    L --> M[End Use Applications]
    M --> N1[Domestic Hot Water]
    M --> N2[Space Heating]
    M --> N3[Industrial Process Heat]
    M --> N4[Solar Cooling Absorption Chiller]
    M --> N5[Power Generation Rankine or Brayton or Stirling]
```

---

The following diagram illustrates the conceptual separation of solar and thermal spectra that motivates selective coatings.

```mermaid
    B --> D{Selective Surface}

    D --> F[Low epsilon approximately 0.05 to 0.15 in thermal band]
    E --> G[Maximum Solar Capture]
    F --> H[Minimum Re radiation Loss]
    G --> I[Net Useful Heat Gain]
    H --> I
```

---

```mermaid
    B --> C[Convection from inner wall to fluid]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define the following terms with respect to solar thermal energy conversion:**
**(a)** Absorptivity ($\alpha$)
**(b)** Reflectivity ($\rho$)
**(c)** Transmissivity ($\tau$)

**Model Answer:**

- **Absorptivity ($\alpha$):** The fraction of incident solar radiation that is absorbed by a surface. It is the ratio of absorbed radiant flux to incident radiant flux: $\alpha = Q_a / Q_i$. For a perfect blackbody, $\alpha = 1$.

- **Reflectivity ($\rho$):** The fraction of incident solar radiation that is reflected away from the surface without being absorbed. It is the ratio of reflected radiant flux to incident radiant flux: $\rho = Q_r / Q_i$. For a perfect mirror, $\rho = 1$.

- **Transmissivity ($\tau$):** The fraction of incident solar radiation that passes through the material. It is the ratio of transmitted radiant flux to incident radiant flux: $\tau = Q_t / Q_i$. For an opaque surface, $\tau = 0$.

The three coefficients obey the energy-conservation identity: $\alpha + \rho + \tau = 1$. For an opaque solar absorber, this reduces to $\alpha + \rho = 1$.

---

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Explain the principle of selective surfaces. Why are they essential in modern solar thermal collectors?**

**Model Answer:**

A **selective surface** is a spectrally engineered coating that exhibits **high solar absorptivity** ($\alpha > 0.9$ in the 0.3–2.5 μm band) and **low thermal emissivity** ($\varepsilon < 0.15$ in the 2.5–50 μm band).

**Principle of Operation:** The solar spectrum (peaking near 0.5 μm at the Sun's 5778 K) and the thermal radiation emitted by a moderately heated absorber (350–600 K, peaking in the mid-IR at 5–10 μm) occupy **non-overlapping spectral bands**. A selective surface exploits this gap by absorbing strongly in the visible/near-IR while emitting weakly in the thermal IR.

**Why Essential:** By Kirchhoff's law, an ordinary dark surface would be a strong thermal emitter as well as a strong solar absorber — wasting energy by re-radiating it as infrared. A selective surface minimizes this re-radiation loss, allowing the absorber to reach much higher operating temperatures and improving collector efficiency by 20–40% compared to non-selective black paint.

**Examples:** Black chrome, black nickel, cermet composites (Mo–Al₂O₃), and intrinsic absorbers (HfC, ZrB₂).

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### CHOICE A — 14 Marks

**`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 (Model Paper)]`**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain in detail the **principle of conversion of solar radiation into heat** in a solar thermal system. Discuss the role of absorptivity, reflectivity, and transmissivity in this conversion process. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A flat-plate solar collector has the following specifications: Aperture area $A = 2.5\ m^2$, effective transmittance–absorptance product $(\tau\alpha) = 0.78$, overall heat loss coefficient $U_L = 5.2\ W/m^2K$, heat removal factor $F_R = 0.85$, ambient temperature $T_a = 30^\circ C$, fluid inlet temperature $T_{fi} = 60^\circ C$, and incident solar irradiance $G_T = 850\ W/m^2$.

**Compute:**
(i) The useful heat gain $Q_u$ (in Watts).
(ii) The instantaneous thermal efficiency $\eta_{th}$.
(iii) The stagnation temperature $T_{stag}$.
(iv) Comment on the practical significance of your computed efficiency. (7 Marks)

---

### Model Solution to Choice A

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

The conversion of solar radiation to heat is governed by the **photothermal effect**. The block diagram of the process is:

```
[Sun] → [Atmospheric Attenuation] → [Collector Cover] → [Selective Absorber] 
      → [Heat Transfer to Fluid] → [Thermal Storage] → [End Use]
```

**Detailed Explanation:**

1. **Solar Emission:** The Sun at ~5778 K emits electromagnetic radiation across the 0.3–2.5 μm band with peak intensity near 0.5 μm.

2. **Atmospheric Passage:** The radiation is partially absorbed and scattered by atmospheric gases. The terrestrial flux $G_T$ is typically 200–1000 W/m².

3. **Cover Transmission:** The transparent cover (glass) admits short-wave solar radiation with transmittance $\tau \approx 0.85–0.92$.

4. **Selective Absorption:** The absorber plate, coated with a selective surface, absorbs strongly in the solar band ($\alpha \approx 0.90–0.95$). The absorbed energy increases the kinetic energy of the absorber's molecules, raising its temperature.

5. **Heat Transfer to Working Fluid:** The hot absorber transfers heat to a fluid (water, glycol, air) by conduction through the tube wall and convection into the flowing fluid.

6. **Energy Conservation:** The process is governed by $\alpha + \rho + \tau = 1$. For an opaque absorber ($\tau = 0$), $\alpha + \rho = 1$. Only the absorbed fraction is converted to heat; the reflected portion is lost back to the environment.

7. **Thermal Re-radiation Loss:** As the absorber heats up, it emits infrared radiation governed by $\varepsilon \sigma T_p^4$. Selective surfaces minimise this loss by keeping $\varepsilon$ low in the thermal IR band.

8. **Useful Heat:** The net useful heat delivered to the fluid is: $Q_u = A \cdot F_R \cdot [G_T \cdot (\tau\alpha) - U_L \cdot (T_{fi} - T_a)]$.

**Conclusion:** The efficiency of this conversion depends on maximising $\alpha$ (selective surface) and minimising $U_L$ (insulation and low-emissivity covers).

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Given Data:**
- $A = 2.5\ m^2$
- $(\tau\alpha) = 0.78$
- $U_L = 5.2\ W/m^2K$
- $F_R = 0.85$
- $T_a = 30^\circ C$
- $T_{fi} = 60^\circ C$
- $G_T = 850\ W/m^2$

**(i) Useful Heat Gain $Q_u$:**

Substituting into the Hottel–Whillier–Bliss equation:

$$Q_u = A \cdot F_R \cdot \left[ G_T \cdot (\tau\alpha) - U_L \cdot (T_{fi} - T_a) \right]$$

Calculate the absorbed flux term:

$$G_T \cdot (\tau\alpha) = 850 \times 0.78 = 663.0\ W/m^2$$

Calculate the loss term:

$$U_L \cdot (T_{fi} - T_a) = 5.2 \times (60 - 30) = 5.2 \times 30 = 156.0\ W/m^2$$

Calculate the net flux:

$$S_{net} = 663.0 - 156.0 = 507.0\ W/m^2$$

Apply the heat removal factor:

$$Q_u = A \cdot F_R \cdot S_{net} = 2.5 \times 0.85 \times 507.0$$

$$\boxed{Q_u = 1077.4\ W \approx 1077\ W}$$

**(ii) Instantaneous Thermal Efficiency $\eta_{th}$:**

$$\eta_{th} = \frac{Q_u}{A \cdot G_T} = \frac{1077.4}{2.5 \times 850} = \frac{1077.4}{2125}$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{th} = 0.507 = 50.7\%}$$

**(iii) Stagnation Temperature $T_{stag}$:**

Stagnation occurs when the fluid inlet equals the ambient (no useful heat extracted):

$$T_{stag} = T_a + \frac{G_T \cdot (\tau\alpha)}{U_L} = 30 + \frac{663.0}{5.2}$$

$$T_{stag} = 30 + 127.5 = 157.5^\circ C$$

$$\boxed{T_{stag} = 157.5^\circ C}$$

**(iv) Practical Significance:**

The computed efficiency of 50.7% is reasonable for a well-designed flat-plate collector operating at a moderate temperature lift of 30°C above ambient. At higher fluid temperatures, $\eta_{th}$ decreases linearly — this is a key design consideration. The stagnation temperature of 157.5°C represents the maximum temperature the collector can reach if no fluid is circulating; collectors must be designed to withstand this without damage to seals, gaskets, or working fluid. In practice, the system should always be operated below stagnation with a heat transfer fluid to prevent thermal degradation.

---

### CHOICE B — 14 Marks (Alternative Selection)

**`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model Paper)]`**

**(a)** Discuss the **various types of solar thermal collectors** with neat diagrams, classifying them by operating temperature and concentration ratio. State two representative applications for each type. (7 Marks)

**(b)** What are **selective surfaces**? Derive the **Hottel–Whillier–Bliss equation** for the instantaneous thermal efficiency of a flat-plate collector, clearly defining every term. A flat-plate collector has $F_R = 0.80$, $(\tau\alpha) = 0.75$, $U_L = 4.5\ W/m^2K$, $T_{fi} = 50^\circ C$, $T_a = 28^\circ C$, and $G_T = 920\ W/m^2$. Determine the thermal efficiency. (7 Marks)

---

### Model Solution to Choice B

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

Solar thermal collectors are classified into **non-concentrating** (stationary, accept diffuse radiation) and **concentrating** (require tracking, accept only direct beam radiation) types.

**1. Flat-Plate Collector (Non-Concentrating, < 100°C):**
- **Description:** A flat, dark absorber plate bonded to a tube grid, enclosed in an insulated casing with a single glass cover.
- **Concentration Ratio:** C = 1 (no concentration).
- **Applications:** Domestic hot water (DHW), space heating, swimming pool heating.
- **Diagram Block:** `[Sun] → [Glass Cover] → [Flat Absorber Plate] → [Tube Grid] → [Fluid]`

**2. Evacuated Tube Collector (Non-Concentrating, 100–200°C):**
- **Description:** An array of borosilicate glass tubes, each containing an absorber with a selective coating. The vacuum between inner and outer tubes eliminates convective loss.
- **Concentration Ratio:** C = 1.
- **Applications:** Industrial process heat, solar cooling, hospitals and hotels.

**3. Compound Parabolic Collector (CPC, Low-Concentrating, 100–250°C):**
- **Description:** A non-imaging concentrator with two parabolic reflectors that achieve a moderate concentration ratio (C = 3–10) while accepting a wide angular range.
- **Concentration Ratio:** C = 3 to 10.
- **Applications:** Medium-temperature industrial process heat, solar refrigeration.

**4. Parabolic Trough (Concentrating, 200–400°C):**
- **Description:** A linear parabolic mirror focuses sunlight onto a receiver tube at the focal line. Requires single-axis tracking.
- **Concentration Ratio:** C = 15 to 45.
- **Applications:** Solar thermal power generation (SEGS plants in California), utility-scale electricity.

**5. Linear Fresnel Reflector (Concentrating, 200–400°C):**
- **Description:** Multiple long, nearly-flat mirror strips focus sunlight onto a fixed elevated receiver. Less expensive than parabolic troughs.
- **Concentration Ratio:** C = 10 to 40.
- **Applications:** Solar thermal power, process steam for industry.

**6. Parabolic Dish (Concentrating, 500–1500°C):**
- **Description:** A point-focus parabolic dish tracks the sun in two axes, focusing radiation onto a central receiver (often coupled to a Stirling engine).
- **Concentration Ratio:** C = 500 to 3000.
- **Applications:** Distributed solar power generation, hydrogen production.

**7. Solar Power Tower (Concentrating, 500–1500°C):**
- **Description:** A field of sun-tracking flat heliostats focuses sunlight onto a central receiver atop a tower. Uses molten salt or air as heat transfer medium.
- **Concentration Ratio:** C = 300 to 1500.
- **Applications:** Utility-scale solar thermal power (Gemasolar, Crescent Dunes), thermochemical hydrogen.

**8. Solar Furnace (Concentrating, up to 3500°C):**
- **Description:** An extreme-concentration parabolic dish that achieves temperatures comparable to nuclear reactions.
- **Concentration Ratio:** C = 5000+.
- **Applications:** Materials research, high-temperature chemistry, oxide ceramic production.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Definition of Selective Surfaces** (2 marks, included in this part):

A selective surface is a coating engineered to have a high $\alpha$ in the 0.3–2.5 μm band and a low $\varepsilon$ in the 2.5–50 μm band. They minimise thermal re-radiation losses (Kirchhoff's law) and increase collector efficiency.

**Derivation of the Hottel–Whillier–Bliss Equation** (3 marks):

At steady state, the energy balance on a flat-plate collector is:

$$Q_u = A \cdot G_T \cdot (\tau\alpha) - A \cdot U_L \cdot (T_p - T_a)$$

Using the heat removal factor $F_R$ to express plate temperature in terms of fluid inlet temperature:

$$Q_u = A \cdot F_R \cdot \left[ G_T \cdot (\tau\alpha) - U_L \cdot (T_{fi} - T_a) \right]$$

Dividing by $A \cdot G_T$ gives:

$$\eta_{th} = F_R \left[ (\tau\alpha) - \frac{U_L (T_{fi} - T_a)}{G_T} \right]$$

**Numerical Computation** (2 marks):

Given: $F_R = 0.80$, $(\tau\alpha) = 0.75$, $U_L = 4.5\ W/m^2K$, $T_{fi} = 50^\circ C$, $T_a = 28^\circ C$, $G_T = 920\ W/m^2$.

Compute the temperature difference term:

$$T_{fi} - T_a = 50 - 28 = 22^\circ C$$

Compute the loss term:

$$\frac{U_L (T_{fi} - T_a)}{G_T} = \frac{4.5 \times 22}{920} = \frac{99}{920} = 0.1076$$

Substitute into the Hottel–Whillier–Bliss equation:

$$\eta_{th} = 0.80 \times \left[ 0.75 - 0.1076 \right] = 0.80 \times 0.6424$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{th} = 0.5139 = 51.4\%}$$

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Errors That Cost Marks on This Topic:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the $F_R$ factor in the HWB equation** — yields efficiencies inflated by 15–25%. Always include $F_R$ — full marks require it.
> 
> 2. **Writing $\alpha + \rho = 1$ without specifying the opaque-surface assumption** — Examiners will deduct 1 mark for not explicitly stating $\tau = 0$ before applying the simplification.
> 
> 3. **Confusing absorptivity with emissivity** — Recall Kirchhoff's law: at thermal equilibrium, $\alpha_\lambda = \varepsilon_\lambda$ (same wavelength, same temperature). Do not state they are *equal at all temperatures* — this is wrong.
> 
> 4. **Stating Wien's law as $\lambda_{max} = bT$ instead of $\lambda_{max} T = b$** — The proportionality is inverse, not direct. Students routinely lose 1 mark here.
> 
> 5. **Using the Stefan–Boltzmann law without converting temperatures to Kelvin** — Celsius-based substitution will give numerically wrong answers. Always convert: $T(K) = T(°C) + 273.15$.
> 
> 6. **Omitting the spectral bands (0.3–2.5 μm and 2.5–50 μm) when explaining selective surfaces** — Examiners expect explicit numerical reference to the spectral regions to demonstrate conceptual understanding.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Frequency Must-Remember Items:**

- **Energy Conservation Law:** $\alpha + \rho + \tau = 1$; for opaque surfaces, $\alpha + \rho = 1$.

- **Kirchhoff's Law:** $\alpha_\lambda = \varepsilon_\lambda$ at thermal equilibrium for the same wavelength.

- **Stefan–Boltzmann Law:** $E_b = \sigma T^4$ with $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}\ W/m^2 K^4$.

- **Wien's Displacement Law:** $\lambda_{max} \cdot T = 2.898 \times 10^{-3}\ m \cdot K$ (use **2.898 × 10⁻³ m·K** or equivalently **2898 μm·K**).

- **Solar Constant:** $G_{sc} = 1367\ W/m^2$ at the top of the atmosphere (AM0); peak terrestrial value $\approx 1000\ W/m^2$ at AM1.5.

- **Spectral Band Separation:** Solar = 0.3–2.5 μm; Thermal IR emission from moderate-temperature absorbers = 2.5–50 μm. **This is the foundation of selective surface design.**

- **Hottel–Whillier–Bliss Equation:** $\eta_{th} = F_R \left[ (\tau\alpha) - \dfrac{U_L (T_{fi} - T_a)}{G_T} \right]$ — **Always include $F_R$!**

- **Stagnation Temperature:** $T_{stag} = T_a + \dfrac{G_T (\tau\alpha)}{U_L}$ — the upper temperature limit with no fluid flow.

- **Heat Removal Factor $F_R$:** Always less than 1; it accounts for the non-isothermal nature of the absorber plate.

- **Selective Surface Quality Metric:** $\alpha / \varepsilon \ge 9$ is considered high-quality.

- **Concentration Ratio vs Temperature:** Higher concentration ratio → higher achievable temperature → suitable for power generation.

- **Collector Classification Threshold Temperatures:** Flat-plate < 100°C, Evacuated tube 100–200°C, Parabolic trough 200–400°C, Power tower > 500°C.

- **Two Fundamental Loss Mechanisms:** (1) Conduction–convection to ambient ($U_L \cdot \Delta T$); (2) Thermal re-radiation ($\varepsilon \sigma T_p^4$). Both grow with temperature, but the radiation term dominates at high $T$ due to the $T^4$ dependence.

- **Real-World Engineering Trade-off:** Higher operating temperature increases the useful work potential of the captured heat but decreases collector efficiency — a fundamental thermodynamic tension that drives modern CSP and PV-T hybrid system design.

# MODULE 2 — SOLAR THERMAL COLLECTORS: CORE DEFINITIONS & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1. Solar Thermal Collector — Formal Definition

A **Solar Thermal Collector** is a specially engineered device that intercepts incoming solar radiation (beam + diffuse), converts it into thermal (heat) energy by the **photothermal conversion principle**, and transfers this heat to a working fluid (water, air, oil, or molten salt) flowing through it. Unlike a PV cell, which converts sunlight directly into electricity, a thermal collector produces **usable heat** at temperatures ranging from ambient (low-temperature) to above 1000 °C (high-temperature concentrating systems).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** *A solar thermal collector is a device designed to absorb incident solar radiation, transfer the resulting heat to a fluid, and deliver this heat at a temperature suitable for the intended application — ranging from water heating (low-T) to industrial process heat and electricity generation (high-T).*

### 1.1 Classification of Solar Thermal Collectors

| Class | Typical Temperature Range | Tracking Required | Concentration Ratio (C) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Flat Plate Collector (FPC)** — Non-concentrating | 30 °C – 100 °C | No (fixed) | C = 1 |
| **Evacuated Tube Collector (ETC)** | 50 °C – 200 °C | No (fixed) | C = 1 |
| **Compound Parabolic Collector (CPC)** | 60 °C – 150 °C | Limited | 1 ≤ C ≤ 5 |
| **Parabolic Trough Collector (PTC)** | 150 °C – 400 °C | Single-axis | 10 ≤ C ≤ 80 |
| **Parabolic Dish Collector (PDC)** | 400 °C – 1500 °C | Two-axis | 100 ≤ C ≤ 1000 |
| **Central Receiver / Power Tower** | 500 °C – 1500 °C | Two-axis (heliostat field) | 300 ≤ C ≤ 1500 |

## 2. Conceptual Analogy — "The Sun as a Furnace"

Imagine standing under the midday sun with a black-painted metal bucket of water. After a few hours, the water turns warm. The bucket acted as a *very poor* solar thermal collector — it absorbed radiation but lost much of the heat back to the surroundings (re-radiation, convection to wind, and conduction through the metal).

A properly engineered **solar thermal collector** does three things the bucket cannot:
1. **Maximises absorption** of the solar spectrum (using a *selective coating* on the absorber plate).
2. **Suppresses losses** by trapping heat (using glass covers, insulation, and/or vacuum insulation).
3. **Channels the heat efficiently** into a working fluid (using tubes, fins, or a high-thermal-conductivity absorber).

In concentrating collectors, the analogy becomes a *magnifying glass focusing sunlight onto a single spot to burn paper*. Here, mirrors/bent reflectors focus sunlight onto a small absorber, raising temperatures to levels useful for steam turbines or industrial processes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction for KTU:** A *non-concentrating* collector (FPC) works on **total (beam + diffuse) radiation**, while a *concentrating* collector works **only on the beam (direct normal) radiation** and therefore **requires accurate sun tracking**.

## 3. Flat Plate Collector (FPC) — The Workhorse of Solar Heating

### 3.1 Definition

A **Flat Plate Collector** is a stationary, non-concentrating solar thermal collector consisting of:
- A **flat absorber plate** (usually metallic — copper or aluminium) that absorbs solar radiation.
- A **transparent cover(s)** (tempered low-iron glass) that reduces convective and radiative losses.
- **Header / riser tubes** bonded to the absorber through which the working fluid (water/glycol) flows.
- **Thermal insulation** at the back and sides to minimise conduction losses.
- A **weather-tight casing** to protect the assembly.

### 3.2 Major Components and Their Functions

| Component | Function | Typical Material |
|---|---|---|
| Glazing (1 or 2 glass covers) | Reduces convection loss; transmits > 0.90 of solar spectrum | Low-iron tempered glass, τ ≈ 0.91 |
| Absorber plate | Converts solar radiation into heat and conducts it to fluid | Copper (k = 385 W/m·K), Aluminium (k = 205 W/m·K) |
| Tubes / Risers | Carry the working fluid; collect heat from absorber | Copper tubes, header-and-riser or serpentine |
| Insulation (back) | Reduces conduction loss to environment | Rock-wool, fibreglass, polyisocyanurate |
| Casing | Mechanical protection, weather sealing | Galvanised iron, aluminium |

## 4. Heat Transfer Processes Inside a Flat Plate Collector

Inside an operating FPC, **four heat transfer mechanisms** act simultaneously:

1. **Absorption of solar radiation** by the absorber plate. The absorbed flux is $S = (\tau\alpha)G_T$, where $\tau$ is the transmittance of the cover, $\alpha$ is the absorptance of the plate, and $G_T$ is the total (beam + diffuse) solar irradiance on the tilted plane.
2. **Conduction** of heat from the absorber plate through the tube walls into the working fluid.
3. **Convective and radiative losses** from the absorber's top surface to the glass cover, and from the glass cover to the ambient sky.
4. **Conduction losses** through the back and edge insulation to the surroundings.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Performance Target:** The *net useful heat* $Q_u$ delivered to the fluid is the difference between the absorbed solar energy and **all** the thermal losses. The Hottel–Whillier equation quantifies this balance.

## 5. Solar Concentrators — General Description

A **Solar Concentrator** is a device that uses reflectors (mirrors) or refractors (lenses) to focus sunlight onto a small absorber area, thereby increasing the flux density and hence the achievable working temperature.

The **Concentration Ratio** is defined as:

$$C = \frac{A_{\text{aperture}}}{A_{\text{absorber}}} = \frac{\text{Area of collector opening}}{\text{Area of receiver}}$$

Three principal types are in the KTU syllabus:

### 5.1 Parabolic Trough Collector (PTC)
- **Geometry:** A trough formed by a parabolic (U-shaped) mirror.
- **Tracking:** **Single-axis** tracking (rotation about one horizontal North–South axis).
- **Concentration Ratio:** $C \approx 10$ to $80$.
- **Working Fluid:** Synthetic oil (Therminol VP-1) or water/steam.
- **Typical Working Temperature:** 150 °C – 400 °C.
- **Applications:** Industrial process heat, solar thermal power plants (SEGS, Andasol, Noor-Ouarzazate), solar water desalination.

### 5.2 Parabolic Dish Collector (PDC)
- **Geometry:** A paraboloid (3-D dish) focusing sunlight onto a point receiver.
- **Tracking:** **Two-axis** tracking.
- **Concentration Ratio:** $C \approx 100$ to $1000$.
- **Working Fluid:** Air, hydrogen, or helium; the receiver may house a Stirling engine.
- **Typical Working Temperature:** 400 °C – 1500 °C.
- **Applications:** Distributed solar power generation (10 kW – 50 kW units), solar cooking, solar hydrogen production.

### 5.3 Central Tower Collector (Power Tower / Solar Tower)
- **Geometry:** A field of flat heliostat mirrors tracks the sun in two axes and reflects sunlight onto a single fixed receiver at the top of a tall tower.
- **Tracking:** Each heliostat tracks in two axes.
- **Concentration Ratio:** $C \approx 300$ to $1500$.
- **Working Fluid:** Molten salt (60 % NaNO₃ + 40 % KNO₃), water/steam, or air.
- **Typical Working Temperature:** 500 °C – 1500 °C.
- **Applications:** Utility-scale Concentrated Solar Power (CSP) plants (e.g., Ivanpah in USA, Gemasolar in Spain).

# DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 1. The Energy Balance of a Flat Plate Collector

For a flat plate collector of aperture area $A_c$ operating under steady-state, the energy balance per unit time is:

$$\text{(Absorbed solar energy)} = \text{(Useful heat gain to fluid)} + \text{(Top losses)} + \text{(Bottom + Edge losses)}$$

$$A_c \, (\tau\alpha) G_T = Q_u + Q_{\text{top}} + Q_{\text{back+edge}}$$

The combined **overall top loss coefficient** (from absorber to ambient) and **bottom + edge loss coefficient** together yield the **overall heat loss coefficient $U_L$** in $\text{W/m}^2\cdot\text{K}$:

$$U_L = U_t + U_b + U_e$$

where $U_t$ (top), $U_b$ (bottom), and $U_e$ (edge) are determined by the cover system geometry, temperature, wind speed, and insulation.

## 2. The Hottel–Whillier Equation (The Master Equation of FPC)

The most cited equation in solar thermal engineering is the **Hottel–Whillier–Bliss equation**, which gives the *useful heat delivered per unit collector area*:

$$q_u = F_R \left[(\tau\alpha) G_T - U_L (T_i - T_a)\right]$$

Multiplying by the collector area:

$$Q_u = A_c \, F_R \left[(\tau\alpha) G_T - U_L (T_i - T_a)\right]$$

where:
- $F_R$ = **heat removal factor** (dimensionless, $0 < F_R \le 1$); depends on absorber–tube bond conductance $C_b$, plate conductance $C_p$, and tube spacing.
- $\tau\alpha$ = **effective transmittance–absorptance product** (dimensionless); $(\tau\alpha)_{\text{eff}}$ is used for diffuse conditions.
- $G_T$ = total (beam + diffuse) irradiance on the tilted plane (W/m²).
- $U_L$ = overall heat loss coefficient (W/m²·K).
- $T_i$ = fluid inlet temperature (°C or K).
- $T_a$ = ambient temperature (°C or K).

The **thermal efficiency** $\eta_c$ of the FPC is the ratio of useful heat gain to incident solar energy on the aperture:

$$\eta_c = \frac{Q_u}{A_c G_T} = F_R (\tau\alpha) - \frac{F_R U_L (T_i - T_a)}{G_T}$$

This is a linear function of the *reduced temperature* $\dfrac{T_i - T_a}{G_T}$, which is the basis of the **HWB efficiency curve**.

## 3. HWB Efficiency Curve — Key Performance Test

When experimentally tested per **ASHRAE Standard 93 / ISO 9806**, the FPC efficiency is plotted against $(T_i - T_a)/G_T$ and yields a straight line:

$$\eta_c = a - b \cdot \frac{(T_i - T_a)}{G_T}$$

- The **y-intercept $a = F_R(\tau\alpha)$** represents the *optical efficiency* (no temperature rise, all losses zero except optical).
- The **negative slope $b = F_R U_L$** represents the *thermal loss factor* (W/m²·K).

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A higher $a$ means a better absorber/cover combination. A lower slope $|b|$ means better insulation. **The best FPC maximises $a$ and minimises $|b|$.**

## 4. The Heat Removal Factor $F_R$

For a typical **header-and-riser** absorber, $F_R$ is given by:

$$F_R = \frac{1}{W \left[\dfrac{1}{D_o (W-D_o) h_{f,i} \pi} + \dfrac{1}{C_p} F\right]}$$

where:
- $W$ = tube spacing (m)
- $D_o$ = tube outer diameter (m)
- $h_{f,i}$ = fluid–tube heat transfer coefficient (W/m²·K)
- $F$ = **collector efficiency factor** (dimensionless, 0 < F < 1)
- $C_p$ = plate conductance (W/K)

A more compact form, used in KTU problems, is:

$$\frac{1}{F_R} = \frac{W}{C_p + U_L [W (F - 1)]} \left[\frac{C_p U_L [F + W U_L / (C_p + W U_L)]}{1 + W U_L / C_p}\right] + \frac{W \, C_b}{C_p}$$

## 5. Collector Efficiency Factor $F$

For a tube-on-plate absorber:

$$F = \frac{1/U_L}{W \left[\dfrac{1}{(W - D_o) \, h_{c,\text{plate}}} + \dfrac{1}{\pi D_o h_{f,i}}\right] + \dfrac{1}{U_L}}$$

where $h_{c,\text{plate}}$ is the bond conductance.

A useful approximation: $F \approx \dfrac{\tanh \, m(W-D_o)/2}{m(W-D_o)/2}$ where $m = \sqrt{\dfrac{U_L}{k \, \delta_p}}$, $k$ is the plate thermal conductivity, and $\delta_p$ is plate thickness.

## 6. Top Heat Loss Coefficient $U_t$

The Klein expression for top loss with $N$ glass covers:

$$U_t = \left[\frac{N}{\dfrac{C}{T_{pm}}} + \frac{1}{h_w}\right]^{-1} + \frac{\sigma (T_{pm} + T_a)(T_{pm}^2 + T_a^2)}{(N + f)(1/\varepsilon_p + (2N + f - 1)/\varepsilon_g) - N}$$

where:
- $C$ = 520 (W/m²·K) (conduction-convection constant for air gap)
- $T_{pm}$ = mean plate temperature (K)
- $h_w$ = wind heat transfer coefficient, $\approx 5.7 + 3.8 V$ (W/m²·K), $V$ = wind speed (m/s)
- $\sigma$ = Stefan–Boltzmann constant $= 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W/m²·K⁴
- $\varepsilon_p$ = absorber plate emittance; $\varepsilon_g$ = glass emittance
- $f = (1 + 0.089 h_w - 0.1166 h_w \varepsilon_p)(1 + 0.07866 N)$

## 7. Concentration Ratio of a Parabolic Trough

For a parabolic trough of aperture width $W_a$ and focal receiver diameter $D_r$:

$$C = \frac{W_a}{\pi D_r}$$

For a 2-D parabolic trough, geometric concentration is limited to:

$$C_{\max} = \frac{1}{\sin \theta_c}$$

where $\theta_c$ is the half-acceptance angle (typically 1° for high-concentration troughs). For $\theta_c = 1°$, $C_{\max} \approx 57$. Practical $C$ values: 10 – 80.

## 8. Useful Heat Delivered by a Solar Concentrator

For any concentrating system, the useful heat gain is:

$$Q_u = A_a \, \eta_{\text{opt}} G_b - A_r \, U_L (T_r - T_a) - Q_{\text{refl}}$$

In terms of *efficiency vs. temperature curve*:

$$\eta_{\text{thermal}} = \eta_{\text{opt}} - \frac{U_L (T_r - T_a)}{C \, G_b}$$

where:
- $A_a$ = aperture area (m²); $A_r$ = receiver area (m²)
- $\eta_{\text{opt}}$ = optical efficiency (0.6 – 0.85 typical for PTC)
- $G_b$ = beam (direct normal) irradiance (W/m²)
- $T_r$ = receiver temperature (°C)
- $C = A_a / A_r$ = concentration ratio

## 9. The KTU Formula Cheat-Sheet (High-Yield Table)

| # | Formula / Quantity | Expression | Units | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Useful heat gain per unit area (HWB) | $q_u = F_R[(\tau\alpha)G_T - U_L(T_i - T_a)]$ | W/m² | Net useful heat |
| 2 | FPC efficiency | $\eta_c = F_R(\tau\alpha) - F_R U_L (T_i - T_a)/G_T$ | dimensionless | Ratio of $Q_u$ to incident solar energy |
| 3 | HWB line intercept | $a = F_R(\tau\alpha)$ | dimensionless | Optical efficiency at zero $\Delta T$ |
| 4 | HWB line slope | $b = F_R U_L$ | W/m²·K | Thermal loss coefficient |
| 5 | Concentration ratio | $C = A_a/A_r$ | dimensionless | Aperture / Receiver area |
| 6 | 2-D max concentration | $C_{\max} = 1/\sin \theta_c$ | dimensionless | Etendue limit (2-D) |
| 7 | 3-D max concentration | $C_{\max,\text{3D}} = 1/\sin^2 \theta_c$ | dimensionless | Etendue limit (3-D) |
| 8 | Wind loss coefficient | $h_w = 5.7 + 3.8 V$ | W/m²·K | Forced convection outside cover |
| 9 | Stefan–Boltzmann radiation | $q_{\text{rad}} = \sigma \varepsilon T^4$ | W/m² | Radiative heat transfer |
| 10 | Sky temperature | $T_{\text{sky}} = 0.0552 \, T_a^{1.5}$ | K | Approximate empirical relation |
| 11 | Selective surface criterion | $\alpha / \varepsilon > $ 5 (good), $>$ 20 (excellent) | dimensionless | Quality of selective coating |
| 12 | Mean plate temperature | $T_{pm} = T_i + (Q_u/A_c)(F_R/U_L)(1 - F_R)$ | K | Used in $U_t$ Klein formula |
| 13 | Heliostat field power | $P_{\text{tower}} = \eta_{\text{field}} \, \eta_{\text{receiver}} \, G_b \, A_{\text{field}}$ | W | Tower plant power input |
| 14 | Thermal storage (molten salt) | $E_{\text{stored}} = m \, c_p \, \Delta T$ | J | Sensible heat storage |
| 15 | Overall heat loss | $U_L = U_t + U_b + U_e$ | W/m²·K | Combined losses per unit area |

> [!NOTE]
> **Remember the Etendue Limit (Rabl, 1976):** No optical system can exceed $C_{\max} = 1/\sin^2 \theta_c$ (3-D) or $1/\sin \theta_c$ (2-D), where $\theta_c$ is the acceptance half-angle. This sets the *thermodynamic ceiling* on concentration.
