# STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Hottel–Whillier Equation from First Principles

### Step 1: Set up the energy balance on the absorber plate

For a steady-state FPC, the absorbed solar flux on the absorber plate per unit area is $(\tau\alpha) G_T$. This absorbed energy is partially lost through the top, bottom and edges (combined coefficient $U_L$) and partially transferred to the fluid.

For a small element of the absorber of width $dx$ along the tube:

$$(\tau\alpha) G_T = U_L (T_p - T_a) + q_u(x)$$

where $T_p$ is the local plate temperature, $T_a$ is ambient, and $q_u(x)$ is the local useful heat flux.

### Step 2: Apply the thermal network from plate to fluid

A standard fin–tube network analysis gives the **collector efficiency factor** $F$ (Duffie & Beckman, *Solar Engineering of Thermal Processes*, classic):

$$q_u(x) = F \left[(\tau\alpha) G_T - U_L (T_f - T_a)\right]$$

where $T_f$ is the local fluid temperature. $F$ accounts for the plate's resistance between tubes.

### Step 3: Introduce the heat removal factor $F_R$ for the flow direction

The fluid temperature rises along the flow path. Integrating the differential energy balance along the fluid direction yields the **heat removal factor $F_R$**:

$$\frac{Q_u}{A_c} = F_R \left[(\tau\alpha) G_T - U_L (T_i - T_a)\right]$$

where the inlet temperature $T_i$ replaces the local $T_f$. This is the **Hottel–Whillier equation** in its most general form.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The difference $F_R \le F$ arises because the fluid enters below the mean plate temperature; $F_R$ is **always less than $F$** for a single-cover, single-pass FPC.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Linear Efficiency Curve (HWB Test)

Starting from the HWB equation, divide both sides by $G_T$:

$$\eta_c = \frac{Q_u}{A_c G_T} = \frac{F_R (\tau\alpha) G_T}{G_T} - \frac{F_R U_L (T_i - T_a)}{G_T}$$

$$\eta_c = F_R (\tau\alpha) - F_R U_L \cdot \frac{T_i - T_a}{G_T}$$

Define the *reduced temperature* $T^* = (T_i - T_a)/G_T$:

$$\eta_c = a - b \, T^*$$

where $a = F_R(\tau\alpha)$ and $b = F_R U_L$. This is the **HWB linear test equation**, the standard ASHRAE 93 / ISO 9806 test result.

### Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU Problem)

> A flat plate collector has $F_R (\tau\alpha) = 0.74$ and $F_R U_L = 4.5$ W/m²·K. The incident solar radiation is $G_T = 800$ W/m², ambient temperature is $T_a = 30$ °C, and fluid inlet temperature is $T_i = 70$ °C. Find (a) the efficiency $\eta_c$, and (b) the useful heat per m².

**Solution:**

Reduced temperature:

$$T^* = \frac{T_i - T_a}{G_T} = \frac{70 - 30}{800} = 0.05 \text{ K·m}^2/\text{W}$$

(a) Efficiency:

$$\eta_c = 0.74 - 4.5 \times 0.05 = 0.74 - 0.225 = 0.515 \approx 51.5\%$$

(b) Useful heat per m²:

$$q_u = \eta_c \cdot G_T = 0.515 \times 800 = 412 \text{ W/m}^2$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Concentration Ratio of a Parabolic Trough

Consider a parabolic mirror with focal length $f$ whose axis lies along the $x$-direction. The rim angle $\phi_r$ is the angle subtended at the focus by the rim.

The **aperture width** $W_a$ is:

$$W_a = 2 f \tan \phi_r$$

A cylindrical receiver of diameter $D_r$ sits at the focus. The **geometric concentration ratio** is:

$$C = \frac{W_a}{\pi D_r} = \frac{2 f \tan \phi_r}{\pi D_r}$$

The rim angle for a perfect focus requires all reflected rays to fall on the receiver. Geometric optics gives the limit for a 2-D trough:

$$C_{\max} = \frac{1}{\sin \theta_c} \approx 57 \quad (\text{for } \theta_c = 1°)$$

The **thermodynamic (étendue) limit** for a 3-D dish concentrator is:

$$C_{\max,3D} = \frac{1}{\sin^2 \theta_c} \approx 3300 \quad (\text{for } \theta_c = 1°)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Rabl's Etendue Theorem** sets a hard upper bound. For a *single* mirror and a *flat* receiver with sun's half-angle $\theta_s = 0.27°$, the absolute limit is $C_{\max} \approx 46{,}000$ (3-D). Real systems operate far below this because of optical aberrations, mirror slope errors, and tracking inaccuracy.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Useful Heat of a Parabolic Trough Collector

For a PTC of aperture area $A_a$ and beam normal irradiance $G_{b,n}$:

$$Q_u = A_a \left[ \eta_{\text{opt}} G_{b,n} - U_L (T_r - T_a) \right]$$

where $\eta_{\text{opt}} = \rho \, \tau \, \gamma \, \alpha$ is the product of mirror reflectance, glass envelope transmittance, intercept factor, and absorber absorptance.

The **receiver efficiency** is:

$$\eta_r = \eta_{\text{opt}} - \frac{U_L (T_r - T_a)}{G_{b,n}}$$

Multiplying by $C$ for receiver-area basis:

$$\eta_r = \eta_{\text{opt}} - \frac{U_L (T_r - T_a)}{C \, G_{b,n}}$$

This shows that **high concentration reduces thermal losses** and allows operation at much higher temperatures.

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Heliostat Field Power Delivered to a Central Tower

Consider a field of $N$ heliostats, each of mirror area $A_h$, tracking the sun in two axes and reflecting onto a fixed receiver of area $A_r$ on top of a tower.

Total intercepted solar power:

$$P_{\text{intercepted}} = N \, A_h \, \eta_{\cos} \, \eta_{\text{refl}} \, G_{b,n}$$

where $\eta_{\cos}$ is the cosine efficiency of the heliostat (depends on sun angle), and $\eta_{\text{refl}} \approx 0.88$–$0.92$ is the mirror reflectance.

The **field efficiency** $\eta_{\text{field}}$ aggregates cosine, shadowing, blocking, attenuation (atmospheric), and reflectance losses:

$$\eta_{\text{field}} = \eta_{\cos} \cdot \eta_{\text{shad}} \cdot \eta_{\text{blk}} \cdot \eta_{\text{atm}} \cdot \eta_{\text{refl}}$$

The power absorbed by the receiver:

$$P_{\text{rec,abs}} = P_{\text{intercepted}} \cdot \alpha_r = N A_h \eta_{\text{field}} G_{b,n} \alpha_r$$

Subtracting thermal losses from the receiver:

$$P_{\text{thermal}} = P_{\text{rec,abs}} - A_r U_L (T_r - T_a)$$

The **cycle efficiency** (Rankine/Brayton) converts thermal power to electrical:

$$P_{\text{el}} = \eta_{\text{cycle}} \cdot P_{\text{thermal}}$$

Typical $\eta_{\text{cycle}} = 0.30$ to $0.40$ for modern Rankine with supercritical steam.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Comparing the Three Concentrators

| Parameter | Parabolic Trough (PTC) | Parabolic Dish (PDC) | Central Tower (CRT) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Geometry | 2-D parabolic | 3-D paraboloid | 2-axis heliostat field |
| Tracking | Single-axis (E–W or N–S) | Two-axis (polar) | Two-axis (each heliostat) |
| $C$ | 10 – 80 | 100 – 1000 | 300 – 1500 |
| Receiver | Linear tube | Point / cavity | Cavity on tower |
| Working fluid | Oil, water/steam | Air, H₂, He | Molten salt, water/steam, air |
| $T_{\text{working}}$ | 150 – 400 °C | 400 – 1500 °C | 500 – 1500 °C |
| $\eta_{\text{thermal}}$ | 30 – 45 % | 25 – 35 % | 30 – 40 % |
| $\eta_{\text{el}}$ (with cycle) | 15 – 25 % | 25 – 30 % | 20 – 30 % |
| Storage capability | Limited (oil) | Limited | Excellent (molten salt, 8 – 15 h) |
| Land use per MW | ~ 2 ha | ~ 1 ha | ~ 1 – 2 ha |
| Commercial maturity | Mature (Andasol 150 MW) | Pilot (10 kW – 50 kW units) | Commercial (Ivanpah 392 MW, Gemasolar 24.8 MW) |
| Main drawback | High oil cost; $T < 400$ °C | Unit size limited; no storage | Tower shadowing; atmospheric attenuation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Central Tower advantage:** Only the tower can integrate **large-scale thermal storage** (molten salt, 565 °C) and dispatch power 24/7 (e.g., Gemasolar operated for 24 continuous hours in 2011).

# STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block Architecture — Flat Plate Collector Subsystem

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Sunlight Beam plus Diffuse] --> B[Tempered Glass Cover tau approx 0.91]
    B --> C[Selective Coated Absorber Plate alpha approx 0.95 epsilon approx 0.12]
    C --> D[Tube Risers Bonded to Plate]
    D --> E[Header Pipe Manifold]
    E --> F[Working Fluid Outlet to Load]
    G[Thermal Insulation Rockwool Back] -.-> C
    H[Galvanised Casing] -.-> B
    I[Frame Mounting Tilted] -.-> H
    J[Wind and Ambient Air] -. Heat Loss .- B
    K[Working Fluid Inlet Cold] --> E
```

## 4.2 Block Architecture — Parabolic Trough Collector System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Solar Beam DNI] --> B[Parabolic Trough Reflector Surface]
    B --> C[Glass Envelope around Receiver Tube]
    C --> D[Selective Coated Absorber Tube]
    D --> E[Heat Transfer Fluid Synthetic Oil]
    E --> F[Piping to Heat Exchanger]
    F --> G[Steam Generation Block]
    G --> H[Turbine Generator Set]
    H --> I[Electrical Grid Output]
    J[Single Axis Tracking Motor] -. Rotates .- B
    K[Cold Fluid Return] --> E
```

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Sun Beam Direct Normal Irradiance] --> B[Field of Heliostat Mirrors N equals 10000 plus]
    B --> C[Reflected Beam Concentrated onto Tower Top]
    C --> D[Cavity Receiver at Tower Top]
    D --> E[Working Fluid Molten Salt or Steam]
    E --> F[Cold Salt Storage Tank 290 degC]
    E --> G[Hot Salt Storage Tank 565 degC]
    G --> H[Steam Generator Heat Exchanger]
    H --> I[Supercritical Steam Turbine]
    I --> J[Electrical Generator]
    J --> K[Grid plus Condenser]
    L[Two Axis Tracking Controller per Heliostat] -. Commands .- B
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Sun Beam DNI] --> B[Paraboloidal Dish Mirror]

    C --> D[Stirling Engine Hot Cylinder]
    D --> E[Stirling Engine Cold Cylinder]
    E --> F[Alternator Generator]
    F --> G[Power Conditioning and Grid]

    I[Cooling Air or Hydrogen] -.-> E
```

## 4.5 Heat Transfer Path inside a Flat Plate Collector

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Incident Solar G_T] --> B[Cover Transmission tau]
    B --> C[Absorber Absorption alpha]
    C --> D[Plate to Tube Conduction F factor]
    D --> E[Tube to Fluid Convection h_f]
    E --> F[Useful Heat Q_u to Load]
    G[Plate to Cover Convection plus Radiation U_t] -. Loss .- C
    H[Cover to Ambient Convection plus Radiation h_w plus sigma] -. Loss .- G
    I[Plate to Back Insulation U_b] -. Loss .- C
    J[Edge Losses U_e] -. Loss .- D
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]**
**Define a solar thermal collector. Differentiate between a flat plate collector and a concentrating collector.**

**Model Answer:**
A *solar thermal collector* is a device that absorbs incident solar radiation, converts it into heat, and transfers this heat to a working fluid for useful applications.

**Flat Plate Collector (FPC):**
- Non-concentrating ($C = 1$)
- Stationary (no tracking)
- Collects both **beam + diffuse** radiation
- Operating temperature: 30 °C – 100 °C

**Concentrating Collector (e.g., PTC, PDC, Tower):**
- $C > 1$ (uses mirrors)
- Requires **sun tracking** (1-axis or 2-axis)
- Collects **beam radiation only** (diffuse lost)
- Operating temperature: 150 °C – 1500 °C

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Style]**
**State the Hottel–Whillier–Bliss (HWB) equation and explain the significance of the parameters $F_R$ and $U_L$.**

**Model Answer:**
The HWB equation is:

$$\eta_c = F_R(\tau\alpha) - \frac{F_R U_L (T_i - T_a)}{G_T}$$

- $F_R$ is the **heat removal factor**; it represents the ratio of actual useful heat to the maximum possible useful heat that would be transferred if the entire absorber were at fluid inlet temperature. $0 < F_R \le 1$.
- $U_L$ is the **overall heat loss coefficient** (W/m²·K), combining top, bottom, and edge losses.

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### CHOICE A — Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Mod 2 Past Paper Style, July 2024]**

(a) *Describe the construction and working of a flat plate solar collector with a neat block diagram. Explain the role of selective coating and glass cover. (7 marks)*

(b) *Derive the Hottel–Whillier–Bliss equation for a flat plate collector and explain how the HWB test curve is used to characterise the collector performance. (7 marks)*

#### Part (a) Model Answer

**Construction of a Flat Plate Collector:**

A flat plate collector consists of the following components, each playing a critical role:

1. **Glazing (1 or 2 glass covers):** Low-iron tempered glass of transmittance $\tau \approx 0.91$. It transmits most of the solar spectrum but blocks long-wave infrared radiation emitted by the absorber, thereby reducing radiative loss.

2. **Absorber plate:** A blackened metal plate (copper or aluminium) with a *selective coating* such as black nickel, black chrome, or TiNOX. The coating has high solar absorptance ($\alpha \approx 0.95$) and low thermal emittance ($\varepsilon \approx 0.12$), satisfying the criterion $\alpha / \varepsilon > 5$ (often 8 – 20).

3. **Tubes/Risers:** Copper tubes bonded to the absorber (by ultrasonic welding, brazing, or epoxy) through which the working fluid flows. Typical tube diameter: 12 – 25 mm; spacing: 100 – 150 mm.

4. **Header pipes:** Manifolds that distribute the fluid across all the riser tubes.

5. **Back insulation:** Rockwool or polyisocyanurate, 50 – 100 mm thick, with thermal conductivity $\approx 0.04$ W/m·K.

6. **Casing:** Galvanised iron or aluminium, weather-sealed.

**Working Principle:**
Solar radiation $G_T$ falls on the tilted glass. The glass transmits most of it; the absorber absorbs $(\tau\alpha)G_T$. Heat flows by conduction through the plate to the tubes, then by convection to the working fluid, which carries it to the storage/load. Heat losses occur through the top (convection + radiation) and bottom (conduction) — minimised by the cover and insulation.

**Role of Selective Coating:** Maximises absorption of solar spectrum ($\alpha > 0.90$) while minimising thermal re-radiation ($\varepsilon < 0.20$) per Kirchhoff's law.

**Role of Glass Cover:** Reduces top convection loss by creating a still-air gap, and reduces radiation loss (greenhouse effect — solar shortwave passes, IR longwave is absorbed and re-emitted in both directions).

**Neat Block Diagram:** (See SECTION_4, Diagram 4.1)

#### Part (b) Model Answer

**Derivation:** (See SECTION_3, Derivation 3.1 for the full derivation)

**Final Result — HWB Equation:**

$$\eta_c = F_R(\tau\alpha) - F_R U_L \cdot \frac{T_i - T_a}{G_T}$$

**HWB Test Procedure (per ASHRAE 93 / ISO 9806):**

1. The collector is tested outdoors under controlled steady-state conditions.
2. Measurements of $G_T$, $T_i$, $T_a$, $Q_u$ (via mass flow $\dot{m}$ and $T_{\text{out}}$) are recorded at many points during a sunny day.
3. For each point, $\eta_c$ and $T^* = (T_i - T_a)/G_T$ are computed.
4. A best-fit straight line is plotted: $\eta_c = a - b T^*$.
5. The intercept $a = F_R(\tau\alpha)$ is the *optical efficiency*; the slope $b = F_R U_L$ is the *total loss coefficient*.
6. These two parameters fully characterise the FPC at any operating condition.

A "good" FPC has $a > 0.75$ and $b < 5$ W/m²·K. Higher slope means higher heat loss and is undesirable.

### CHOICE B — Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Mod 2 Past Paper Style, May 2024]**

(a) *Explain the construction and working of a Parabolic Trough Collector (PTC). What is the role of single-axis tracking? (7 marks)*

(b) *A PTC has an aperture width of 5.0 m, a focal length of 1.5 m, and a rim angle of 50°. The receiver tube has an outer diameter of 70 mm. Calculate (i) the geometric concentration ratio, and (ii) the useful thermal power delivered if the direct normal irradiance is 900 W/m², optical efficiency is 0.75, receiver heat loss coefficient is 8 W/m²·K, ambient temperature is 30 °C, and the working fluid (oil) is to be heated to 350 °C. (7 marks)*

#### Part (a) Model Answer

**Construction of a Parabolic Trough Collector:**

A PTC consists of:
- A **parabolic (2-D) mirror surface** with a focal length $f$ and rim angle $\phi_r$, made of silvered glass or polished aluminium.
- A **receiver tube** (steel) at the focus, coated with a selective absorber coating and enclosed in a glass envelope to reduce convective loss.
- A **single-axis tracking mechanism** that rotates the trough about a horizontal axis (usually N–S) to follow the sun's daily motion in E–W direction.
- A **support structure** (steel pylon).
- **Piping and a pump** to circulate the heat transfer fluid (synthetic oil, e.g., Therminol VP-1, or water/steam).
- An optional **heat trace** and expansion tank.

**Working:**
Sunlight (beam only) is reflected by the parabolic mirror and concentrated onto the receiver tube. The absorber coating converts the concentrated flux into heat, which is transferred to the oil. The hot oil flows to a heat exchanger to generate steam for a Rankine cycle (electricity) or for industrial process heat.

**Role of Single-Axis Tracking:**
A parabolic trough can only concentrate **beam** radiation (not diffuse). The sun's beam direction changes by 15° per hour. If the trough is not aligned, the reflected beam misses the receiver. Continuous single-axis tracking keeps the beam focused on the receiver throughout the day, maximising intercept factor and useful heat.

#### Part (b) Model Solution

**Given Data:**
- Aperture width $W_a = 5.0$ m
- Rim angle $\phi_r = 50°$ (note: full rim-to-rim angle)
- Receiver tube outer diameter $D_r = 70$ mm $= 0.070$ m
- Beam normal irradiance $G_{b,n} = 900$ W/m²
- Optical efficiency $\eta_{\text{opt}} = 0.75$
- Heat loss coefficient $U_L = 8$ W/m²·K
- Ambient $T_a = 30$ °C, Receiver temperature $T_r = 350$ °C

**Step 1 — Geometric Concentration Ratio:**

$$C = \frac{W_a}{\pi D_r} = \frac{5.0}{\pi \times 0.070}$$

$$C = \frac{5.0}{0.2199} = 22.73$$

**Step 2 — Useful Thermal Power per unit aperture area:**

$$\eta_{\text{thermal}} = \eta_{\text{opt}} - \frac{U_L (T_r - T_a)}{G_{b,n}}$$

$$= 0.75 - \frac{8 \times (350 - 30)}{900}$$

$$= 0.75 - \frac{8 \times 320}{900} = 0.75 - 2.844 = \text{NEGATIVE}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **EXAMINER'S PITFALL CALL-OUT:** A common error is to compute $C$ first and then forget that *thermal loss per unit aperture area* is $U_L (T_r - T_a)$, not $U_L (T_r - T_a) / C$. When $T_r - T_a$ is large and $C$ is moderate, the loss can exceed the absorbed power, giving a **negative thermal efficiency** — i.e., the system cannot sustain that temperature. In such cases, a *higher-concentration* system (PDC or Tower) must be used.

**Interpretation:** With $C \approx 23$ and $U_L = 8$ W/m²·K, the *maximum* working temperature for the trough to be self-sustaining is:

$$T_{r,\max} = T_a + \frac{\eta_{\text{opt}} G_{b,n} C}{U_L \cdot 1} \cdot \frac{1}{C} = T_a + \frac{\eta_{\text{opt}} G_{b,n}}{U_L}$$

$$= 30 + \frac{0.75 \times 900}{8} = 30 + 84.4 = 114.4 \text{ °C}$$

So with this $U_L$ and concentration, the trough can only heat oil to **about 114 °C**, not 350 °C. Real troughs achieve 350 °C only with $U_L \approx 0.35$ W/m²·K (achieved by vacuum-jacketed glass envelopes around the receiver tube).

**Recompute with realistic $U_L = 0.35$ W/m²·K:**

$$\eta_{\text{thermal}} = 0.75 - \frac{0.35 \times 320}{900} = 0.75 - 0.124 = 0.626$$

Power per unit aperture area:

$$q_u = \eta_{\text{thermal}} \cdot G_{b,n} = 0.626 \times 900 = 563.4 \text{ W/m}^2$$

For a 1-metre length of trough, aperture area $= 1 \times 5.0 = 5.0$ m². So useful power per metre:

$$Q_u = 563.4 \times 5.0 = 2817 \text{ W/m} \approx 2.82 \text{ kW/m}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING:**
> 1. **Confusing $C = W_a/(\pi D_r)$ with $C = W_a/D_r$** — both are common; the correct one is the *circumference* of the receiver tube. Marks lost: **2 marks**.
> 2. **Forgetting the constant $\pi$ in concentration** — leads to a 3.14× error.
> 3. **Using $T_i$ in place of $T_r$** — $T_i$ is for FPC; for concentrators, the working temperature at the receiver is $T_r$.
> 4. **Not reporting negative efficiency** — if a numerical setup gives $\eta < 0$, students should *interpret* the result and state that the design is infeasible at that temperature.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- **Flat Plate Collector (FPC)** is a *non-concentrating, stationary* device that uses both beam and diffuse radiation; the Hottel–Whillier equation $\eta_c = F_R(\tau\alpha) - F_R U_L (T_i - T_a)/G_T$ governs its performance.
- **Heat Removal Factor $F_R$** is always $\le 1$ and decreases as the bond conductance between tube and plate worsens.
- **Selective coatings** achieve $\alpha \approx 0.95$ and $\varepsilon \approx 0.12$; the ratio $\alpha/\varepsilon$ is the key quality metric.
- **Two glass covers** reduce $U_t$ but also reduce $\tau$; usually 1 cover for tropical climates and 2 for colder ones.
- **Concentrators use only beam radiation (DNI)** and **must track the sun**; non-tracking concentrators waste most of the beam.
- **Concentration Ratio** is limited by *etendue*: $C_{\max} = 1/\sin \theta_c$ (2-D) and $1/\sin^2 \theta_c$ (3-D).
- **Parabolic Trough (PTC):** single-axis, $C \approx 10$–$80$, oil, $T \le 400$ °C.
- **Parabolic Dish (PDC):** two-axis, $C \approx 100$–$1000$, Stirling engine, $T \le 1500$ °C, highest efficiency.
- **Central Tower:** two-axis heliostat field, $C \approx 300$–$1500$, only system with cost-effective **large-scale thermal storage** (molten salt, 8–15 hours).
- **Heat losses** in FPC: top (convection + radiation through cover), bottom (conduction through insulation), edge (conduction through side walls).
- **Wind heat transfer coefficient** is $h_w \approx 5.7 + 3.8V$ (V in m/s) — wind strongly affects top losses.
- **HWB test plot** $\eta_c$ vs $(T_i - T_a)/G_T$ is a straight line: intercept = optical efficiency, slope = thermal loss coefficient.
- **The dimensionless reduced temperature** $T^* = (T_i - T_a)/G_T$ is the key independent variable in FPC characterisation.
- **Receiver thermal efficiency of a concentrator** is $\eta_r = \eta_{\text{opt}} - U_L(T_r - T_a)/(C \cdot G_{b,n})$ — higher $C$ means lower thermal loss penalty.
- **Diffuse radiation is wasted** in concentrators — a major reason FPCs and ETCs are preferred in cloudy/cloud-edge regions like Kerala.
- **Aperture area $A_a$** is the mirror/glass area that intercepts sunlight; **receiver area $A_r$** is the small region where concentrated flux is absorbed.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Air Conditioning and Refrigeration System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEET721 - DESIGN OF SOLAR PV SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Air Conditioning and Refrigeration System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Air Conditioning — Formal Definition

**Air Conditioning (AC)** is the process of simultaneously controlling four fundamental environmental parameters of indoor air to provide thermal comfort and acceptable indoor air quality (IAQ). As per **ASHRAE Standard 55**, these parameters are:

- **Temperature** (typically 23 °C – 26 °C dry-bulb for comfort)
- **Humidity** (relative humidity 40 % – 60 %)
- **Air Cleanliness** (filtration of particulates and gaseous contaminants)
- **Air Motion / Distribution** (velocity 0.05 m/s – 0.25 m/s for occupied zone)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** *Air conditioning* refers to the treatment of air to control its temperature, humidity, cleanliness, and distribution to meet the requirements of the conditioned space. A *refrigeration system* is a sub-system that supplies the cooling effect (heat removal) required by the air conditioner.

## 1.2 Refrigeration — Formal Definition

**Refrigeration** is the branch of engineering concerned with the production of temperatures lower than that of the surrounding atmosphere, by transferring heat from a low-temperature body (cold reservoir / evaporator) to a high-temperature body (hot reservoir / condenser) using a working fluid called a **refrigerant**.

Mathematically, refrigeration effect is the **net heat extracted from the cooled space per unit time**:

$$Q_{evap} \; = \; \dot{m}_{ref} \, (h_{1} - h_{4}) \;\; [\text{kW}]$$

where $h_1$ and $h_4$ are the enthalpies at the evaporator inlet and outlet of the refrigerant, and $\dot{m}_{ref}$ is the mass flow rate of the refrigerant (kg/s).

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Refrigerant is a Heat Porter"

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The "Heat Porter" Model**
>
> Imagine a **porter** (the refrigerant) standing inside a cold room. He picks up heat "boxes" from inside the room (evaporator) by **evaporating** (liquid → vapour absorbs latent heat), carries them outside the room through a "door" (compressor + condenser) and dumps them into the hot outside environment by **condensing** (vapour → liquid releases latent heat). The porter then quietly returns (through an expansion valve, losing pressure) to pick up another load.
>
> - **Cold room** = evaporator (low pressure, low temperature)
> - **Hot outside** = condenser (high pressure, high temperature)
> - **The door through which the porter exits hot** = compressor (adds work $W_c$)
> - **The narrow gate on return** = expansion/throttle valve

This simple mental picture makes the entire vapour-compression cycle immediately intuitive.

## 1.4 Standard Performance Metrics (Must Memorize)

| Metric | Symbol | Definition | Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Tonne of Refrigeration | **1 TR** | Heat removed to freeze 1 tonne (1000 kg) of water at 0 °C into ice at 0 °C in 24 hours | **3.517 kW** (≈ 12000 BTU/hr) |
| Coefficient of Performance (Cooling) | **COP** | $Q_{evap} / W_{input}$ | dimensionless |
| Energy Efficiency Ratio | **EER** | Cooling output (BTU/hr) / Power input (W) | BTU/W·h |
| Seasonal EER | **SEER** | Total cooling output / Total electrical input over a season | BTU/W·h |
| Indian Seasonal EER | **ISEER** | BEE (Bureau of Energy Efficiency) star rating metric | BTU/W·h |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **1 TR = 3.517 kW = 12000 BTU/hr** is a KTU favourite conversion. Always keep this on your cheat sheet — it appears in almost every numerical problem.

## 1.5 Classification of Air-Conditioning Systems

1. **By Application:**
   - Comfort AC (residential, commercial)
   - Industrial AC (process cooling, clean rooms)
2. **By Equipment Layout:**
   - **Unitary:** Window AC, Split AC, Packaged AC
   - **Central:** Chilled-Water Plant (CWP), AHU-based systems, VRF/VRV
3. **By Heat-Rejection Medium:**
   - **Air-cooled** (condenser uses ambient air)
   - **Water-cooled** (condenser uses cooling tower water)
4. **By Driving Energy Source (Solar PV context — important!):**
   - **Vapour Compression (VCRC)** — electrically driven
   - **Vapour Absorption (VARC)** — thermally driven (LiBr-water, Ammonia-water)
   - **Solar Solid-Adsorption** — silica-gel or activated-carbon + water
   - **Evaporative / Desiccant** — hybrid systems

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Vapour Compression Refrigeration Cycle (VCRC) — The Workhorse

The **Vapour Compression Refrigeration Cycle (VCRC)** is the dominant refrigeration cycle used in window, split, and centralized AC systems. It uses a mechanical compressor to circulate the refrigerant.

### 2.1.1 The Four Hardware Components

1. **Evaporator** — Low-pressure side. Refrigerant evaporates (boils) by absorbing heat $Q_{evap}$ from the conditioned space. Operates at $T_{evap} \approx 5$ °C – 8 °C.
2. **Compressor** — Suction-side (low-pressure) vapour is compressed to high-pressure, high-temperature superheated vapour. Work input $W_c$. Compressor types: reciprocating, scroll, screw, centrifugal.
3. **Condenser** — High-pressure, high-temperature refrigerant rejects heat $Q_{cond}$ to ambient (outdoor air or cooling-tower water). Operates at $T_{cond} \approx 40$ °C – 55 °C.
4. **Expansion Valve (Throttle)** — High-pressure liquid refrigerant is throttled to a low-pressure, low-temperature two-phase mixture. An **isenthalpic** process ($h_3 = h_4$).

### 2.1.2 The Four Thermodynamic Processes

| State | Location | Process | Property Constant |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 → 2 | Compressor | Compression | **Isentropic** ($s_1 = s_2$) for ideal |
| 2 → 3 | Condenser | Condensation (heat rejection) | Isobaric (at $P_{cond}$) |
| 3 → 4 | Throttle valve | Expansion | **Isenthalpic** ($h_3 = h_4$) |
| 4 → 1 | Evaporator | Evaporation (heat absorption) | Isobaric (at $P_{evap}$) |

### 2.1.3 Ideal VCRC on P-h Diagram

> [!NOTE]
> The **Pressure–Enthalpy (P-h) diagram** of the refrigerant is the single most important graphical tool for VCRC analysis. The four state points form a quadrilateral inside the **vapour dome**, where the right side is saturated vapour (S.V.) and the left side is saturated liquid (S.L.). State 1 lies on S.V. (saturated vapour at evaporator outlet), state 3 lies on S.L. (saturated liquid at condenser outlet), state 4 lies inside the dome (after throttling), and state 2 lies in the superheated region.

## 2.2 Coefficient of Performance (COP) — The Heart of VCRC

The **COP** is the single most important performance metric. For a cooling (refrigeration) system:

$$COP_{cooling} \; = \; \frac{\text{Desired Output (Cooling Effect)}}{\text{Required Input (Work or Heat)}} \; = \; \frac{Q_{evap}}{W_{input}}$$

### 2.2.1 COP of Ideal VCRC (Step-by-Step Logic)

The **refrigerating effect (RE)** per kg of refrigerant:

$$RE \; = \; h_1 - h_4 \quad [\text{kJ/kg}]$$

The **compressor work** per kg:

$$W_c \; = \; h_2 - h_1 \quad [\text{kJ/kg}]$$

The **heat rejected** at the condenser:

$$Q_{cond} \; = \; h_2 - h_3 \quad [\text{kJ/kg}]$$

**First-Law Energy Balance** (steady-state, single-component cycle):

$$Q_{cond} \; = \; Q_{evap} + W_c$$

Therefore:

$$COP_{ideal} \; = \; \frac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}$$

### 2.2.2 COP of Reversed Carnot Cycle (Theoretical Upper Bound)

For comparison, the COP of a reversed Carnot cycle operating between $T_L$ (K, evaporator) and $T_H$ (K, condenser) is:

$$COP_{Carnot} \; = \; \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **reversed Carnot COP is the theoretical maximum**. Real VCRC systems operate at 30 % – 60 % of this Carnot limit due to compressor inefficiencies, non-ideal heat exchange, and pressure drops. KTU frequently asks students to *compare* real COP with Carnot COP.

## 2.3 Vapour Absorption Refrigeration Cycle (VARC) — Solar Friendly

Unlike VCRC, the **Vapour Absorption Refrigeration Cycle (VARC)** uses **thermal energy (heat)** instead of mechanical work to drive the refrigerant. This makes it ideally suited for **solar thermal** applications.

### 2.3.1 Working Principle (LiBr–Water System)

- **Refrigerant:** Water (low boiling point 100 °C)
- **Absorbent:** Lithium Bromide (LiBr) — strongly hygroscopic
- **Generator:** Solar heat drives water out of the LiBr solution
- **Condenser:** Water vapour condenses → releases heat $Q_{cond}$
- **Evaporator:** Liquid water evaporates at low pressure → absorbs heat $Q_{evap}$
- **Absorber:** Water vapour is reabsorbed by the LiBr solution → releases heat $Q_{abs}$

The mass ratio of refrigerant to absorbent is called the **Circulation Ratio (CR)**:

$$CR \; = \; \frac{M_a + M_r}{M_r}$$

For LiBr–Water systems, **CR typically ranges from 4 to 12**.

### 2.3.2 COP of VARC

$$COP_{VARC} \; = \; \frac{Q_{evap}}{Q_{gen} + W_{pump}}$$

The pump work $W_{pump}$ is **negligible** (≈ 1 % of $Q_{gen}$), so:

$$COP_{VARC} \; \approx \; \frac{Q_{evap}}{Q_{gen}} \; \approx \; 0.6 \text{ – } 0.8$$

> [!NOTE]
> Solar absorption chillers are commercially available in capacities of **5 TR to 1500 TR**, using **CPC (Compound Parabolic Concentrator)** or **Fresnel** collectors to provide the 80 °C – 95 °C generator temperature.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This table is your one-stop KTU exam revision sheet for Module 2.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units | Typical KTU Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $1 \; TR \; = \; 3.517 \; kW$ | Tonne of Refrigeration | kW | Convert cooling load to TR |
| 2 | $1 \; TR \; = \; 12000 \; BTU/hr$ | Tonne of Refrigeration | BTU/hr | EER calculations |
| 3 | $Q_{evap} \; = \; \dot{m} \,(h_1 - h_4)$ | Cooling rate (mass form) | kW | Capacity of evaporator |
| 4 | $W_c \; = \; \dot{m} \,(h_2 - h_1)$ | Compressor power (mass form) | kW | Power input to AC |
| 5 | $Q_{cond} \; = \; \dot{m} \,(h_2 - h_3)$ | Heat rejected to ambient | kW | Sizing condenser / heat-reject |
| 6 | $COP \; = \; \dfrac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}$ | Coefficient of performance | — | VCRC performance |
| 7 | $COP_{Carnot} \; = \; \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$ | Carnot (theoretical) COP | — | Second-Law comparison |
| 8 | $EER \; = \; 3.516 \times COP$ | Energy Efficiency Ratio | — | Star rating calculations |
| 9 | $E_{daily} \; = \; Q_{evap} \times t_{run}$ | Daily energy consumption | kWh | Solar PV sizing |
| 10 | $N_{panel} \; = \; \dfrac{E_{daily}}{\eta_{sys} \times P_{panel} \times PSH}$ | Number of PV panels | panels | PV array design |
| 11 | $CR \; = \; \dfrac{M_a + M_r}{M_r}$ | Circulation ratio (VARC) | — | Absorption cycle design |
| 12 | $Q_{gen,thermal} \; = \; \dot{m}_{sol} \times C_p \times \Delta T$ | Solar heat to generator | kW | Solar collector sizing |
| 13 | $h_{4} \; = \; h_3$ | Throttling process isenthalpy | — | State-point calculation |
| 14 | $s_1 \; = \; s_2$ | Isentropic compression | — | State-point calculation |
| 15 | $P_{PV,AC} \; = \; \dfrac{Q_{evap}}{COP \times \eta_{inv}}$ | PV power to drive AC | kWp | Solar AC sizing |

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Solar PV Engineering

- **Off-grid solar AC**: PV panels drive inverter → drives VCRC compressor. Sizing must account for high **surge current** at compressor start-up (3× – 7× running current).
- **Solar thermal AC**: Concentrating solar collectors (CPC, parabolic trough) deliver 80 °C – 200 °C to VARC generator — zero electricity needed.
- **Hybrid systems**: PV + solar thermal combination gives the **highest solar fraction** (up to 95 %).
- **Net-Zero Buildings**: BEE-rated (Bureau of Energy Efficiency, India) 5-star AC paired with rooftop solar forms the **backbone of green-building certification (GRIHA, LEED-India)**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — COP of the Ideal Vapour Compression Refrigeration Cycle

> **Starting Premise:** Apply the **First Law of Thermodynamics** to a control volume enclosing each of the four components, assuming **steady-state, single-component, negligible KE/PE changes**.

### 3.1.1 Evaporator (Process 4 → 1)

**Working fluid** enters as a low-quality two-phase mixture at state 4 and leaves as **saturated vapour** at state 1.

**Heat absorbed per kg of refrigerant (refrigerating effect, RE):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Energy balance on evaporator:} \quad \dot{m} \, h_4 + \dot{Q}_{evap} & = \dot{m} \, h_1 \\
\text{Therefore:} \quad RE \; = \; \frac{\dot{Q}_{evap}}{\dot{m}} & = h_1 - h_4 \quad [\text{kJ/kg}]
\end{aligned}$$

**Why?**
The refrigerant picks up latent heat of vaporization as it boils at constant low pressure. The enthalpy jump $h_1 - h_4$ is the **useful cooling output** of the cycle.

### 3.1.2 Compressor (Process 1 → 2)

The compressor is treated as a **steady-flow adiabatic** device. For an **ideal (reversible adiabatic / isentropic) compressor**:

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{m} \, h_1 + \dot{W}_c & = \dot{m} \, h_2 \\
\text{Specific work input:} \quad w_c & = h_2 - h_1 \quad [\text{kJ/kg}] \\
\text{Isentropic condition:} \quad s_1 & = s_2
\end{aligned}$$

**Why?**
The compressor is the **only work-consuming** device in the cycle. The enthalpy rise $h_2 - h_1$ equals the mechanical shaft work per kg of refrigerant circulated.

### 3.1.3 Condenser (Process 2 → 3)

The superheated vapour at state 2 first **desuperheats** (2 → 2'), then **condenses** to saturated liquid (2' → 3). Total heat rejected per kg:

$$q_{cond} \; = \; h_2 - h_3 \quad [\text{kJ/kg}]$$

### 3.1.4 Expansion Valve (Process 3 → 4)

The expansion valve is modelled as a **throttling device** — adiabatic, no work, negligible KE change.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Steady-flow energy balance:} \quad h_3 & = h_4 \\
\text{Hence:} \quad h_4 & = h_3
\end{aligned}$$

This single line ($h_4 = h_3$) is the **most-remembered identity** of the VCRC.

### 3.1.5 Putting It Together — COP Derivation

**Step 1 — Definition of COP (cooling):**

$$COP \; = \; \frac{\text{Desired cooling output}}{\text{Required work input}} \; = \; \frac{q_{evap}}{w_c}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute per-kg quantities:**

$$COP \; = \; \frac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}$$

**Step 3 — Use isenthalpic throttling ($h_4 = h_3$):**

$$COP \; = \; \frac{h_1 - h_3}{h_2 - h_1}$$

**Step 4 — Cross-check with first-law cycle balance** $q_{cond} = q_{evap} + w_c$:

$$\begin{aligned}
h_2 - h_3 & = (h_1 - h_3) + (h_2 - h_1) \\
\text{LHS: } (h_2 - h_3) & = h_2 - h_1 + h_1 - h_3 = \text{RHS} \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}$$

The identity holds, confirming the derivation is **self-consistent**.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Standard Style)

> **Problem:** A refrigeration system uses R-134a as the refrigerant. The evaporator operates at $-10$ °C and the condenser at 40 °C. The isentropic compression yields: $h_1 = 392$ kJ/kg, $h_2 = 430$ kJ/kg, $h_3 = 256$ kJ/kg. Saturated vapour at $-10$ °C and saturated liquid at 40 °C. The refrigerant mass flow rate is 0.05 kg/s.
>
> **Find:** (i) Refrigerating effect per kg, (ii) Compressor work per kg, (iii) COP, (iv) Cooling capacity in kW and TR, (v) Carnot COP for comparison, (vi) Relative COP efficiency.

### Solution (Step-by-Step)

**Step (i) — Throttling enthalpy:**

$$h_4 \; = \; h_3 \; = \; 256 \; \text{kJ/kg}$$

**Step (ii) — Refrigerating effect per kg:**

$$RE \; = \; h_1 - h_4 \; = \; 392 - 256 \; = \; 136 \; \text{kJ/kg}$$

**Step (iii) — Compressor work per kg:**

$$w_c \; = \; h_2 - h_1 \; = \; 430 - 392 \; = \; 38 \; \text{kJ/kg}$$

**Step (iv) — COP:**

$$COP \; = \; \frac{RE}{w_c} \; = \; \frac{136}{38} \; = \; 3.579$$

**Step (v) — Cooling capacity:**

$$Q_{evap} \; = \; \dot{m} \times RE \; = \; 0.05 \times 136 \; = \; 6.8 \; \text{kW}$$

**Step (vi) — Convert to TR:**

$$\text{Capacity (TR)} \; = \; \frac{6.8}{3.517} \; = \; 1.933 \; \text{TR}$$

**Step (vii) — Carnot COP (T in Kelvin):**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_L & = -10 + 273.15 = 263.15 \; \text{K} \\
T_H & = 40 + 273.15 = 313.15 \; \text{K} \\
COP_{Carnot} & = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L} = \frac{263.15}{313.15 - 263.15} = \frac{263.15}{50.00} = 5.263
\end{aligned}$$

**Step (viii) — Relative COP efficiency (second-law efficiency):**

$$\eta_{II} \; = \; \frac{COP_{actual}}{COP_{Carnot}} \; = \; \frac{3.579}{5.263} \; = \; 0.680 \; = \; 68.0 \; \%$$

**Step (ix) — Compressor power input:**

$$P_{comp} \; = \; \dot{m} \times w_c \; = \; 0.05 \times 38 \; = \; 1.9 \; \text{kW (mechanical)}$$

**Step (x) — Daily energy consumption (assuming 10 hours runtime):**

$$E_{daily} \; = \; P_{comp} \times t_{run} \; = \; 1.9 \times 10 \; = \; 19 \; \text{kWh/day}$$

**Step (xi) — Number of 330 W PV panels needed (PSH = 4.5, $\eta_{sys} = 0.80$):**

$$N_{panel} \; = \; \frac{E_{daily}}{\eta_{sys} \times P_{panel} \times PSH} \; = \; \frac{19}{0.80 \times 0.330 \times 4.5} \; = \; \frac{19}{1.188} \; = \; 15.99 \; \approx \; 16 \; \text{panels}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This single numerical integrates Module 1 (PV sizing) with Module 2 (AC/Refrigeration).** It is the type of "multi-module integration" question that KTU increasingly asks in 14-mark ESE problems.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — VCRC vs VARC vs Solar-Thermal AC

| Parameter | Vapour Compression (VCRC) | Vapour Absorption (VARC) | Solar-Thermal (Concentrating) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Driving Energy | Mechanical work (electricity) | Thermal heat (Q) | Concentrated solar heat (Q) |
| Typical COP | 2.5 – 4.5 | 0.6 – 0.8 | 0.5 – 0.7 (generator limited) |
| Solar Compatibility | PV-driven | Direct thermal | Direct thermal |
| Equipment Cost (₹/TR) | Low (mass-produced) | High (specialized) | Highest (CPC + chiller) |
| Maintenance | Moderate (moving parts) | Low (no compressor) | Moderate (collectors + chiller) |
| Noise | High (compressor) | Very low (no moving parts) | Low |
| Capacity Range | 0.5 TR – 5000 TR | 5 TR – 1500 TR | 10 TR – 500 TR |
| Generator Temp. Required | N/A | 80 °C – 200 °C | 120 °C – 200 °C (Fresnel) |
| Refrigerants | R-22, R-134a, R-410A, R-290 | LiBr–H₂O, NH₃–H₂O | LiBr–H₂O (most common) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Vapour Compression Refrigeration Cycle (VCRC)

> [!NOTE]
> This block diagram represents the **functional architecture** of a VCRC. State points (1, 2, 3, 4) and process labels are deliberately kept as raw uppercase text per the engine's anti-formatting safeguard inside mermaid nodes.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    EVAP_A[EVAPORATOR\nState 4 to State 1\nLow Pressure Side] -->|Saturated Vapour h1| COMP_A
    COMP_A[COMPRESSOR\nState 1 to State 2\nIsentropic Compression] -->|Superheated Vapour h2| COND_A
    COND_A[CONDENSER\nState 2 to State 3\nHeat Rejection to Ambient] -->|Saturated Liquid h3| EXV_A
    EXV_A[EXPANSION VALVE\nState 3 to State 4\nIsenthalpic Throttling] -->|Two Phase Mixture h4| EVAP_A
    EVAP_A -. COOLING EFFECT QEVAP .-> SPACE_A[CONDITIONED SPACE\nHeat Absorbed from Room]
    COND_A -. HEAT REJECTION QCOND .-> AMB_A[OUTDOOR AMBIENT AIR\nHot Side]
    COMP_A -. WORK INPUT WC .-> GRID_A[ELECTRICAL SUPPLY\nPV Array or Grid]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Solar-Powered Air Conditioning System Architecture

> [!NOTE]
> This diagram maps how solar PV and solar thermal integration delivers air-conditioning. It is a **block-level functional architecture**, not a physical wiring diagram.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SUN_A[SOLAR RADIATION] --> PV_A
    SUN_A -->|Thermal Energy| COLL_A
    PV_A[ROOFTOP PV ARRAY] -->|DC Power| INV_A
    INV_A[GRID TIE INVERTER] -->|AC Power| AC_SYS
    AC_SYS[VCRC BASED AC UNIT\n1 TR to 5 TR] -->|Cool Air| ROOM_A[CONDITIONED ROOM]
    COLL_A[SOLAR THERMAL COLLECTORS\nFPC or ETC or CPC] -->|Hot Water 80C| ABS_GEN
    ABS_GEN[VAPOUR ABSORPTION CHILLER\nVARC Unit] -->|Chilled Water 7C| AHU_A
    AHU_A[AHU AIR HANDLING UNIT] -->|Cool Air| ROOM_A
    GRID_A[UTILITY GRID\nNet Meter Backup] -.->|Hybrid Net Zero| INV_A
    BATT_A[VRLA OR LITHIUM BATTERY BANK] -.->|DC Bus Backup| INV_A
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Schematic — Ideal VCRC States on P-h Diagram (Conceptual)

> [!NOTE]
> The P-h diagram is a **2-D thermodynamic chart** that mermaid cannot render natively. The diagram below represents the **logical flow of state transitions** in enthalpy space — a one-to-one conceptual mapping of the four VCRC processes.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["STATE 1\nSaturated Vapour Outlet of Evaporator\nP evap and h1 = 392 kJ per kg"] -->|Process 1 to 2\nIsentropic Compression| P2["STATE 2\nSuperheated Vapour Outlet of Compressor\nP cond and h2 = 430 kJ per kg"]
    P2 -->|Process 2 to 3\nIsobaric Condensation| P3["STATE 3\nSaturated Liquid Outlet of Condenser\nP cond and h3 = 256 kJ per kg"]
    P3 -->|Process 3 to 4\nIsenthalpic Throttling| P4["STATE 4\nTwo Phase Mixture Inlet of Evaporator\nP evap and h4 = h3 = 256 kJ per kg"]
    P4 -->|Process 4 to 1\nIsobaric Evaporation| P1
```

## 4.4 State-Transition Summary Table (Cross-Reference to Diagrams)

| Process | From → To | Component | Constant Property | Enthalpy Change | Diagram Marker |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Compression | 1 → 2 | Compressor | $s_1 = s_2$ | $h_2 - h_1$ (positive) | Steep upward arrow |
| Condensation | 2 → 3 | Condenser | $P = P_{cond}$ | $h_3 - h_2$ (negative) | Horizontal arrow left |
| Throttling | 3 → 4 | Expansion Valve | $h_3 = h_4$ | $h_4 - h_3 = 0$ | Vertical downward arrow |
| Evaporation | 4 → 1 | Evaporator | $P = P_{evap}$ | $h_1 - h_4$ (positive) | Horizontal arrow right |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU Part A questions test **recall, definition, and one-step calculation**. Keep answers to 3–4 lines with a key formula.

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Define Tonne of Refrigeration (TR). Express 1 TR in kW and BTU/hr.**

**Model Answer:**
Tonne of Refrigeration (TR) is the unit of refrigeration effect equal to the rate of heat removal required to freeze **1 metric tonne (1000 kg) of water at 0 °C into ice at 0 °C in 24 hours**.

The latent heat of fusion of water is **335 kJ/kg**. Therefore:

$$1 \; TR \; = \; \frac{1000 \times 335}{24 \times 3600} \; = \; 3.517 \; \text{kW} \; = \; 12000 \; \text{BTU/hr}$$

> **Examiner's Note:** Many students write 3.5 kW without units or forget the BTU conversion. Always quote **both** values for full marks.

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**What is the Coefficient of Performance (COP) of a refrigerator? Why is the COP of a refrigerator always greater than 1?**

**Model Answer:**
COP of a refrigerator is the ratio of the **desired cooling effect (heat extracted)** to the **work input**:

$$COP \; = \; \frac{Q_{evap}}{W_{input}} \; = \; \frac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}$$

COP > 1 because the heat $Q_{evap}$ is **extracted at a temperature lower than ambient** and rejected to ambient — the work input is only used to "pump" heat from low to high temperature, not to create the heat itself. Hence the heat moved (numerator) exceeds the work spent (denominator).

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Do not confuse COP with efficiency. COP is dimensionless but **can exceed unity** (often 2 – 5), whereas thermal efficiency is always < 1. Examiners deduct marks for confusing the two.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Module-Internal Choice (A or B)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 14-mark ESE questions typically have **two sub-parts of 7 marks each**. The choice (A or B) gives the student one of two parallel options. Each sub-part should carry a sub-question that tests **Apply/Analyse** cognitive level.

---

### ⭐ OPTION A — Question A `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**A vapour compression refrigeration cycle uses R-134a. The compressor takes in saturated vapour at $-15$ °C and compresses it isentropically to 1.0 MPa. At the condenser exit the refrigerant is saturated liquid at 36 °C. The refrigerant mass flow rate is 0.08 kg/s. From the refrigerant tables: $h_1 = 389$ kJ/kg, $h_2 = 425$ kJ/kg, $h_3 = 241$ kJ/kg. Calculate:**
**(i) The refrigerating effect per kg, (ii) Compressor work per kg, (iii) COP of the cycle, (iv) Cooling capacity in kW, (v) Cooling capacity in TR.**

##### Model Solution

**Step (i) — Throttling enthalpy** (expansion valve is isenthalpic):

$$h_4 \; = \; h_3 \; = \; 241 \; \text{kJ/kg}$$

**Step (ii) — Refrigerating Effect (RE) per kg:**

$$RE \; = \; h_1 - h_4 \; = \; 389 - 241 \; = \; 148 \; \text{kJ/kg}$$

**Step (iii) — Compressor work per kg:**

$$w_c \; = \; h_2 - h_1 \; = \; 425 - 389 \; = \; 36 \; \text{kJ/kg}$$

**Step (iv) — COP of the cycle:**

$$COP \; = \; \frac{RE}{w_c} \; = \; \frac{148}{36} \; = \; 4.111$$

**Step (v) — Cooling capacity in kW:**

$$Q_{evap} \; = \; \dot{m} \times RE \; = \; 0.08 \times 148 \; = \; 11.84 \; \text{kW}$$

**Step (vi) — Cooling capacity in TR:**

$$\text{Capacity} \; = \; \frac{Q_{evap}}{3.517} \; = \; \frac{11.84}{3.517} \; = \; 3.367 \; \text{TR}$$

> **Final Answers (boxed for valuation):**
> - RE = **148 kJ/kg** ✓
> - Compressor work = **36 kJ/kg** ✓
> - COP = **4.111** ✓
> - Capacity = **11.84 kW = 3.367 TR** ✓

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Compare the COP of an ideal Vapour Compression Refrigeration Cycle with that of a Reversed Carnot Cycle operating between the same temperature limits. For the VCRC in part (a), with evaporator at $-15$ °C and condenser at 36 °C, calculate the Carnot COP and the second-law efficiency. Comment on the result.**

##### Model Solution

**Step 1 — Convert temperatures to Kelvin:**

$$T_L \; = \; -15 + 273.15 \; = \; 258.15 \; \text{K}$$

$$T_H \; = \; 36 + 273.15 \; = \; 309.15 \; \text{K}$$

**Step 2 — Carnot COP:**

$$COP_{Carnot} \; = \; \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L} \; = \; \frac{258.15}{309.15 - 258.15} \; = \; \frac{258.15}{51.00} \; = \; 5.062$$

**Step 3 — Actual VCRC COP (from part a):**

$$COP_{VCRC} \; = \; 4.111$$

**Step 4 — Second-Law (Relative) Efficiency:**

$$\eta_{II} \; = \; \frac{COP_{VCRC}}{COP_{Carnot}} \; = \; \frac{4.111}{5.062} \; = \; 0.8122 \; = \; 81.22 \; \%$$

**Step 5 — Heat rejected at the condenser per kg:**

$$q_{cond} \; = \; h_2 - h_3 \; = \; 425 - 241 \; = \; 184 \; \text{kJ/kg}$$

**Step 6 — First-law cycle check:**

$$q_{cond} \; = \; RE + w_c \; = \; 148 + 36 \; = \; 184 \; \text{kJ/kg} \; \checkmark$$

**Step 7 — Commentary:**
The actual VCRC COP of 4.111 is about **81 % of the Carnot COP of 5.062**. The remaining 19 % is lost due to (i) non-isentropic (real) compression, (ii) pressure drops in suction/discharge lines, (iii) heat exchange irreversibilities, and (iv) superheat and subcooling beyond the ideal cycle. Modern scroll compressors with **EEV (Electronic Expansion Valves)** and **variable-speed drives (VSD)** can push real COP above 90 % of Carnot.

> **Final Answer:** Carnot COP = **5.062**, Second-Law Efficiency = **81.22 %**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall — Part B (14 marks):** Examiners allocate **2 marks for state-1 assumptions**, **3 marks for COP derivation with substitutions**, and **2 marks for unit conversions (kW ↔ TR)**. If you skip the throttling-enthalpy step ($h_4 = h_3$) and just substitute the given $h_4$ directly, you **lose 1 mark** because the logical chain is broken. Always show the throttling identity explicitly.

---

### ⭐ OPTION B — Question B `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the working of a Vapour Absorption Refrigeration System (VARS) using the LiBr–H₂O pair. State any four advantages of VARS over VCRS for solar air-conditioning applications.**

##### Model Solution

**Working Principle — LiBr–H₂O VARS:**

The LiBr–H₂O system uses **water as the refrigerant** (low boiling point 100 °C) and **Lithium Bromide (LiBr) solution as the absorbent**. The cycle has **four heat exchangers and a pump** (no compressor). The sequence is:

1. **Generator:** Concentrated LiBr solution is heated by **solar thermal energy** (80 °C – 95 °C from FPC/ETC/CPC collectors). Water vapour is driven out of the solution (refrigerant boils off).
2. **Condenser:** The high-pressure water vapour from the generator flows to the condenser, where it rejects heat to ambient (or cooling tower) and **condenses to liquid water**, releasing $Q_{cond}$.
3. **Evaporator:** The condensed water passes through an expansion valve to the **low-pressure evaporator** (3 – 8 °C, ≈ 8 mbar absolute), where it **evaporates by absorbing heat $Q_{evap}$** from the chilled-water loop serving the AHU.
4. **Absorber:** The low-pressure water vapour is **absorbed by the strong LiBr solution** from the generator's heat-exchanger, becoming weak/dilute solution. Heat $Q_{abs}$ is rejected to the cooling loop. The dilute solution is pumped back to the generator.

**The four key temperatures are:**
- $T_{gen} = 80$ °C – 95 °C (driven by solar heat)
- $T_{cond} = 30$ °C – 40 °C
- $T_{evap} = 5$ °C – 8 °C
- $T_{abs} = 30$ °C – 40 °C

**Four Advantages of VARS over VCRS for Solar AC:**

| # | Advantage | Explanation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Driven by heat, not electricity** | Can use low-grade solar thermal energy (80 °C – 95 °C) — no PV/ inverter needed |
| 2 | **No moving compressor** | Almost silent operation; no mechanical wear, long life (20+ years) |
| 3 | **No harmful CFCs/HFCs** | Water is the refrigerant — zero ODP and zero GWP; environment-friendly |
| 4 | **Low operating cost** | Solar heat is free after installation; running cost is only for the small solution pump (~ 1 % of total input) |

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**A 10 TR solar absorption chiller (LiBr–H₂O) has a COP of 0.70. It is integrated with a 100 m² rooftop solar thermal collector array (FPC type, $\eta_{coll} = 0.65$) receiving an average daily solar insolation of 5.0 kWh/m²/day. The auxiliary pump consumes 0.5 kW. Calculate:**
**(i) The cooling energy delivered per day if the chiller runs 8 hours, (ii) The thermal energy required by the generator per day, (iii) The number of FPC panels (each 2.0 m²) needed, and (iv) The solar fraction if a 5 kW PV array supplements the electrical pump load.**

##### Model Solution

**Step (i) — Cooling energy per day:**

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_{evap,day} & = \text{Capacity} \times \text{Time} \\
& = 10 \times 3.517 \times 8 \\
& = 281.36 \; \text{kWh/day}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step (ii) — Thermal energy required by the generator:**

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_{gen,day} & = \frac{Q_{evap,day}}{COP_{VARS}} \\
& = \frac{281.36}{0.70} \\
& = 401.94 \; \text{kWh/day}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step (iii) — Solar thermal energy available from the existing 100 m² array:**

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_{solar,day} & = A_{coll} \times I \times \eta_{coll} \\
& = 100 \times 5.0 \times 0.65 \\
& = 325 \; \text{kWh/day}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Verdict:** With the **existing 100 m² array, only 325 kWh/day is available**, but the chiller needs **401.94 kWh/day** — a **shortfall of 76.94 kWh/day** that must be made up by an auxiliary gas/electric boiler. Hence we proceed to compute the *required* array size for 100 % solar fraction.

**Step (iv) — Total collector area required for 100 % solar fraction:**

$$A_{req} \; = \; \frac{Q_{gen,day}}{I \times \eta_{coll}} \; = \; \frac{401.94}{5.0 \times 0.65} \; = \; 123.68 \; \text{m}^2$$

**Step (v) — Number of FPC panels (each 2.0 m²):**

$$N_{panels} \; = \; \frac{123.68}{2.0} \; = \; 61.84 \; \approx \; 62 \; \text{panels}$$

**Step (vi) — Auxiliary pump energy per day:**

$$E_{pump,day} \; = \; 0.5 \; \text{kW} \times 8 \; \text{h} \; = \; 4.0 \; \text{kWh/day}$$

**Step (vii) — Daily PV energy from 5 kW array (PSH = 4.5):**

$$E_{PV,day} \; = \; 5 \times 4.5 \; = \; 22.5 \; \text{kWh/day}$$

**Step (viii) — Solar fraction (pump only):**

$$f_{solar} \; = \; \frac{E_{PV,day}}{E_{pump,day}} \; = \; \frac{22.5}{4.0} \; = \; 5.625 \; \rightarrow \; 100 \; \% \; (\text{capped})$$

> **Final Answers (boxed):**
> - Cooling energy = **281.36 kWh/day** ✓
> - Generator thermal energy = **401.94 kWh/day** ✓
> - Number of FPC panels = **62** ✓
> - Solar fraction of pump = **100 %** (PV array is oversized for the pump load)

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall — Part B Option 2:** The COP of an absorption chiller is **less than 1** (typically 0.6 – 0.8). Many students mistakenly assume COP > 1 and divide incorrectly. **Absorption cycles have COP < 1** because the input is heat, not work. Always double-check whether the input is **work (W)** or **heat (Q)** before applying the COP formula.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Mark-Loss Areas in KTU AC/Refrigeration Questions (Module 2):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the throttling identity** $h_4 = h_3$ — this costs 1 mark in nearly every derivation.
> 2. **Mixing up units**: using BTU/hr when kW is asked, or using °C in Carnot COP (must be Kelvin).
> 3. **Wrong COP formula for VARC vs VCRC**: VARC COP is $Q_{evap}/Q_{gen}$, **not** $Q_{evap}/W$. The denominator is heat, not work.
> 4. **Ignoring compressor isentropic efficiency** when "actual" compression is specified (then $h_2 \neq h_{2s}$). Always read the problem for "isentropic" vs "actual".
> 5. **Sizing the PV array without surge factor**: compressor inrush current is **3× – 7× rated**. The inverter must be sized at **1.5× – 2× the running load**, and the PV array sized for the larger of (running, surge) demand. This is a Module-1 + Module-2 integration trap.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Read this 30 minutes before the exam):**

- 🔹 **1 TR = 3.517 kW = 12 000 BTU/hr** — non-negotiable conversion.
- 🔹 **COP of VCRC** $= (h_1 - h_4) / (h_2 - h_1)$ — draw the P-h diagram and label all four states.
- 🔹 **Throttling** is **isenthalpic** ($h_3 = h_4$); **compression** is **isentropic** ($s_1 = s_2$).
- 🔹 **Carnot COP** $= T_L / (T_H - T_L)$ — always use **Kelvin**.
- 🔹 **Second-law efficiency** $=$ (Actual COP) / (Carnot COP) $\times 100$ %.
- 🔹 **VCRC** is **work-driven**; **VARC** is **heat-driven** — used in solar thermal AC.
- 🔹 **LiBr–H₂O** system: water is the refrigerant; LiBr is the absorbent; generator needs 80 °C – 95 °C.
- 🔹 **Sizing PV for AC** must account for **surge current** (3× – 7×), **PSH (Peak Sun Hours)**, and **system efficiency** ($\eta_{sys} \approx 0.75$ – $0.85$).
- 🔹 **EER $= 3.516 \times$ COP**; **ISEER** is the Indian star-rating metric.
- 🔹 **Refrigerant hierarchy (eco-friendly):** R-22 → R-410A → R-134a → **R-290 (propane, GWP ≈ 3)**.
- 🔹 **FPC (Flat Plate Collector)** for 60 °C – 80 °C; **ETC (Evacuated Tube Collector)** for 80 °C – 120 °C; **CPC (Compound Parabolic Concentrator)** for 80 °C – 150 °C with diffuse-radiation gain.
- 🔹 **Net-Zero Building AC** = 5-star ISEER AC + Rooftop PV (BEE-rated) — this is the KTU 2024 design philosophy.

> **Closing Thought:** Air Conditioning and Refrigeration Systems form the **single largest residential and commercial electricity load** in tropical Kerala (30 °C – 38 °C ambient). Solar PV + efficient AC + absorption technology is the **only sustainable pathway** for India's cooling demand. Master these formulas — they will appear in **both Module-2 and the integrated Module-2-to-5 design project**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEEET721 - DESIGN OF SOLAR PV SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Pumping System ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Solar PV Pumping System (SPPS):** An integrated electromechanical system that converts solar photovoltaic (PV) electricity directly into hydraulic energy to lift and deliver water for irrigation, drinking, livestock, or industrial use, without grid dependence. The system comprises a **PV array**, a **power conditioning unit (PCU/MPPT controller)**, an **electric motor** (DC/AC), and a **hydraulic pump** (centrifugal, submersible, or positive displacement). A Solar PV Pumping System is classified as a **standalone (off-grid)**, **variable-speed** system in which the operating point of the pump tracks the available solar irradiance throughout the day, producing maximum water during peak sunshine hours (**10:00 – 14:00 hrs**). ### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Sun-Driven Heart" Imagine a human heart that pumps blood through the body using chemical energy. Now, replace the **chemical energy** with **sunlight**, the **heart muscle** with a **PV array + motor**, and the **blood vessels** with **pipes**. The result is a solar pumping system: the sun (acting like food for the heart) provides photons, the PV array converts them into electricity, the motor converts electricity into mechanical rotation, and the pump lifts water against gravity. The faster the sun shines, the faster the "heart" pumps. ### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics | Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value | Unit | | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | | Density of water | $\rho$ | **1000** | $\text{kg/m}^3$ | | Acceleration due to gravity | $g$ | **9.81** | $\text{m/s}^2$ | | Specific weight of water | $\gamma = \rho g$ | **9810** | $\text{N/m}^3$ | | Standard peak irradiance | $G_{STC}$ | **1000** | $\text{W/m}^2$ | | Standard test temperature | $T_{STC}$ | **25** | $^\circ\text{C}$ | > [!NOTE] > KTU examiners expect students to recall $\rho g = 9.81\ \text{kN/m}^3$ (or **9810 N/m³**) directly. Memorize this constant — it appears in **every** pump-design numerical. ### 1.4 Major Components — The "Four-Stage Energy Chain" The system delivers water through a four-stage energy conversion chain: $$ \text{Sunlight} \xrightarrow{\eta_{PV}} \text{DC Electricity} \xrightarrow{\eta_{MPPT}} \text{Conditioned Power} \xrightarrow{\eta_{motor}} \text{Mechanical Rotation} \xrightarrow{\eta_{pump}} \text{Hydraulic Energy} $$ 1. **PV Array** — converts solar irradiance into DC electricity (typical module efficiency: 15–22 %). 2. **MPPT Controller / VFD** — extracts maximum power at every irradiance level (efficiency $\approx$ 95–98 %). 3. **Electric Motor** — DC (PMDC/BLDC) or AC (3-phase induction with VFD); efficiency 80–92 %. 4. **Pump** — converts mechanical rotation into hydraulic head; efficiency 25–70 %. ### 1.5 Classification of SPPS **A. By Pump Type** - **Centrifugal Pumps** (Radial / Mixed / Axial flow) — suited for **low head (< 50 m), high discharge** applications. - **Submersible Pumps (Borewell)** — suited for **deep wells (50–250 m)**, low–medium discharge. - **Positive Displacement Pumps (Reciprocating / Diaphragm)** — suited for **high head, low discharge** (e.g., 100–300 m head). **B. By Motor Type** - **DC Motor Pumps** (PMDC / BLDC) — simple, high efficiency at variable speed, **no inverter required**. - **AC Motor Pumps (3-$\phi$ Induction + VFD)** — robust, used for high-power systems (≥ 5 HP). **C. By Tracking Configuration** - **Static / Fixed-tilt Array** — most common in KTU problems. - **Single-axis / Dual-axis Tracker** — boosts daily energy yield by **20–35 %**. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Hydraulic Power — The "Heartbeat" of Pumping The hydraulic power required to lift a volume flow rate $Q$ against a total head $H$ is: $$ P_h = \rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot H $$ Where: - $P_h$ = hydraulic power (W) - $Q$ = volumetric flow rate ($\text{m}^3/\text{s}$) - $H$ = total head (m) - $\rho g = 9810\ \text{N/m}^3$ **Why this formula works:** Power = Force × Velocity. Force needed to lift water = $\rho g H$ (per unit area, this is pressure). Multiplying by the volumetric flow $Q$ gives the rate of energy transfer (power). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Unit Conversion Trap (KTU Examiner's favourite):** If $Q$ is given in **L/s (litres per second)**, the formula simplifies to a "Kerala-board-friendly" form: > > $$P_h \, (\text{kW}) = \frac{9.81 \times Q \, (\text{L/s}) \times H \, (\text{m})}{1000}$$ For **L/day** water requirement, convert first: $$ Q \, (\text{L/s}) = \frac{V_{daily} \, (\text{L/day})}{3600 \times T_{sun} \, (\text{h})} $$ ### 2.2 Total Dynamic Head (TDH) — The "Vertical Climb" Concept Total Dynamic Head is the **equivalent vertical height** the water must be lifted, accounting for all real-world losses. $$ TDH = H_{static} + H_{friction} + H_{minor} + H_{velocity} $$ | Component | Symbol | Meaning | Typical Magnitude | | :--- | :---: | :--- | :--- | | Static Head | $H_s$ | Vertical lift from water source to delivery point | **70–80 % of TDH** | | Friction Head | $H_f$ | Loss due to pipe-wall friction | **10–25 % of TDH** | | Minor Losses | $H_m$ | Losses in bends, valves, foot valve | **2–5 % of TDH** | | Velocity Head | $H_v$ | Kinetic energy term $v^2 / 2g$ | usually negligible | > [!NOTE] > In KTU problems, minor and velocity heads are often **clubbed with friction head** unless explicitly mentioned. The simplified form is: > > $$TDH = H_{static} + H_{friction}$$ ### 2.3 Friction Head — Hazen-Williams Equation (Most Tested) $$ H_f = \frac{10.67 \times L \times Q^{1.852}}{C^{1.852} \times d^{4.87}} $$ Where: - $L$ = pipe length (m) - $Q$ = flow rate ($\text{m}^3/\text{s}$) - $d$ = internal pipe diameter (m) - $C$ = Hazen-Williams roughness coefficient (PVC: 150, GI: 120, CI: 100) **Intuition:** Friction head scales **strongly** with flow rate (power 1.852) and inversely with pipe diameter (power 4.87). Doubling the flow increases $H_f$ by **$2^{1.852} \approx 3.6$ times**. Doubling the pipe diameter reduces $H_f$ by **$2^{4.87} \approx 29$ times**. This is why **oversized pipes are critical** in SPPS design. ### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Application | Units | | :-- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | $P_h = \rho g Q H$ | Hydraulic power | W | | 2 | $E_{hyd} = \rho g V H$ | Daily hydraulic energy (V in m³) | J | | 3 | $TDH = H_s + H_f + H_m$ | Total head requirement | m | | 4 | $H_f = \dfrac{10.67 L Q^{1.852}}{C^{1.852} d^{4.87}}$ | Hazen-Williams friction | m | | 5 | $P_m = \dfrac{P_h}{\eta_{pump}}$ | Shaft power at motor | W | | 6 | $P_{in} = \dfrac{P_h}{\eta_{pump} \cdot \eta_{motor}}$ | Electrical input to motor | W | | 7 | $P_{PV} = \dfrac{P_h}{\eta_{pump} \cdot \eta_{motor} \cdot \eta_{MPPT}}$ | Required PV array power | Wp | | 8 | $N_{modules} = \dfrac{P_{PV}}{P_{module}}$ | Number of PV modules | integer | | 9 | $N_{series} = \dfrac{V_{DC,bus}}{V_{module}}$ | Modules in series string | integer | | 10 | $N_{parallel} = \dfrac{N_{modules}}{N_{series}}$ | Parallel strings | integer | | 11 | $Q_{avg} = \dfrac{V_{daily}}{3600 \times PSH}$ | Average flow from daily volume | L/s | | 12 | $\eta_{overall} = \eta_{PV} \cdot \eta_{MPPT} \cdot \eta_{motor} \cdot \eta_{pump}$ | System efficiency | fraction | | 13 | $A_{PV} = \dfrac{P_{PV}}{G \cdot \eta_{PV}}$ | PV array area | m² | | 14 | $CF = \dfrac{E_{actual}}{P_{PV,rated} \times 8760}$ | Capacity factor (annual) | fraction | ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Significance Solar PV pumping systems are the **backbone of PM-KUSUM** (Pradhan Mantri Kisan Urja Suraksha evam Utthaan Mahabhiyan), India's flagship programme to solarize agriculture. Over **3.5 lakh solar pumps** have been deployed in India as of 2024, each replacing a diesel pump that consumes **1500–2500 litres of diesel/year**. The engineering challenge: matching pump hydraulic curve to PV array's I-V curve so the operating point stays near MPP across the day. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Board Pattern Tip:** Numericals on "Design a solar PV pump to deliver X litres/day at Y metre head" account for **60–70 %** of Module 2 marks. Memorize the formula chain **$P_h \rightarrow P_m \rightarrow P_{in} \rightarrow P_{PV}$** with efficiency multipliers at every stage. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Daily Hydraulic Energy from Volume & Head **Starting point:** Hydraulic power $P_h = \rho g Q H$ **Step 1:** Express $Q$ in terms of daily volume $V$ and pumping hours $t$: $$ Q = \frac{V}{t} $$ **Step 2:** Substitute into the power equation: $$ P_h = \rho g \left(\frac{V}{t}\right) H $$ **Step 3:** Multiply both sides by pumping time $t$ to obtain the **daily hydraulic energy**: $$ E_{hyd} = P_h \times t = \rho g V H $$ **Step 4:** Substitute numerical constants ($\rho = 1000\ \text{kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^2$, V in m³, H in m, E in Joules): $$ E_{hyd} = 9810 \times V \times H \quad \text{(Joules)} $$ **Step 5:** Convert to **Watt-hours** (1 Wh = 3600 J): $$ E_{hyd} \, (\text{Wh}) = \frac{9810 \times V \times H}{3600} = 2.725 \times V \times H $$ **Step 6:** Convert to **kWh**: $$ E_{hyd} \, (\text{kWh}) = \frac{2.725 \times V \times H}{1000} = 0.002725 \times V \times H $$ **Final Compact Form:** $$ E_{hyd} \, (\text{kWh}) = 2.725 \times 10^{-3} \times V \times H $$ --- ### 3.2 Complete Numerical Worked Example (KTU Board Pattern) **Problem:** Design a solar PV pumping system to deliver **50,000 litres of water per day** at a total head of **30 m**. The site receives **5.0 peak sun hours/day**. Assume: $\eta_{pump} = 0.45$, $\eta_{motor} = 0.85$, $\eta_{MPPT} = 0.95$, $\eta_{wiring} = 0.98$. Available module: **330 Wp, 37.5 Vmp, 8.8 Imp**. **Step 1 — Compute average flow rate Q:** Convert daily volume to m³: $V = 50{,}000\ \text{L} = 50\ \text{m}^3$ $$ Q = \frac{V}{t \times 3600} = \frac{50{,}000}{5.0 \times 3600} = 2.778\ \text{L/s} $$ **Step 2 — Compute required hydraulic power $P_h$:** $$ P_h = \frac{9.81 \times Q \times H}{1000} = \frac{9.81 \times 2.778 \times 30}{1000} = 0.8175\ \text{kW} = 817.5\ \text{W} $$ **Step 3 — Compute required shaft (mechanical) power at pump coupling $P_m$:** $$ P_m = \frac{P_h}{\eta_{pump}} = \frac{817.5}{0.45} = 1816.7\ \text{W} $$ **Step 4 — Compute electrical input to motor $P_{in}$:** $$ P_{in} = \frac{P_m}{\eta_{motor}} = \frac{1816.7}{0.85} = 2137.3\ \text{W} $$ **Step 5 — Compute required PV array power $P_{PV}$ (peak watt):** $$ P_{PV} = \frac{P_{in}}{\eta_{MPPT} \times \eta_{wiring}} = \frac{2137.3}{0.95 \times 0.98} = 2295.0\ \text{Wp} $$ **Step 6 — Compute number of modules:** $$ N = \frac{P_{PV}}{P_{module}} = \frac{2295.0}{330} = 6.95 \approx 7\ \text{modules} $$ **Step 7 — String Sizing:** For a **48 V battery-less DC pump** (typical Vmp target = 60 V): - Modules in series: $N_s = 60 / 37.5 = 1.6 \rightarrow 2$ modules - Strings in parallel: $N_p = 7 / 2 = 3.5 \rightarrow 4$ strings - **Final array configuration: 2 series × 4 parallel = 8 modules (2640 Wp)** with $V_{mp,array} = 75$ V. **Step 8 — Verification of daily energy delivered:** $$ E_{daily} = P_{PV} \times PSH \times \eta_{overall} $$ $$ \eta_{overall} = 0.95 \times 0.98 \times 0.85 \times 0.45 = 0.357 $$ $$ E_{daily} = 2640 \times 5.0 \times 0.357 = 4712.4\ \text{Wh} = 4.71\ \text{kWh} $$ **Step 9 — Volume delivered per day:** $$ V = \frac{E_{daily}}{2.725 \times 10^{-3} \times H} = \frac{4712.4}{2.725 \times 10^{-3} \times 30} = 57{,}654\ \text{L/day} $$ **Conclusion:** The designed system delivers **57,654 L/day**, exceeding the demand of 50,000 L/day — design is **acceptable**. ✅ --- ### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Pump Type Selection | Site Condition | Recommended Pump | Head Range (m) | Flow (L/s) | Motor Type | | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | | Shallow pond / open well | Centrifugal (surface) | 5 – 30 | 5 – 50 | AC 1-$\phi$ / DC | | Medium borewell (60–100 m) | Submersible centrifugal | 50 – 150 | 1 – 10 | AC 3-$\phi$ + VFD | | Deep borewell (150–300 m) | Helical / Multi-stage submersible | 150 – 300 | 0.5 – 5 | AC 3-$\phi$ + VFD | | Drip irrigation, low discharge | Positive displacement (diaphragm) | 20 – 80 | 0.1 – 1 | DC PMDC | | Foul water / muddy water | Centrifugal with open impeller | 5 – 40 | 3 – 30 | AC / DC | --- ### 3.4 Derivation: Affinity Laws for Variable-Speed Pumping When a centrifugal pump is operated at variable speed (typical in SPPS), its performance scales by the **Affinity Laws**: $$ Q \propto N, \quad H \propto N^2, \quad P \propto N^3 $$ Where $N$ is the rotational speed. **Physical interpretation:** Doubling the pump speed doubles the flow, quadruples the head, and **octuples** the power. This cubic relationship is why MPPT control in solar pumps is so valuable — at half-rated speed, power consumption drops to 1/8, allowing the pump to operate for longer hours at lower irradiance. **Combined Affinity Law (for two speeds $N_1$, $N_2$):** $$ \frac{Q_2}{Q_1} = \frac{N_2}{N_1}, \quad \frac{H_2}{H_1} = \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^2, \quad \frac{P_2}{P_1} = \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^3 $$ ## 4. WATER DEMAND V LITRE PER DAY
AND HEAD H METRE GIVEN"]) s1["STEP 1
COMPUTE AVERAGE FLOW Q
Q EQUALS V DIVIDED BY 3600 INTO PSH"] s2["STEP 2
COMPUTE HYDRAULIC POWER Ph
Ph EQUALS 9.81 INTO Q INTO H DIVIDED BY 1000"] s3["STEP 3
COMPUTE SHAFT POWER Pm
Pm EQUALS Ph DIVIDED BY eta pump"] s4["STEP 4

Pin EQUALS Pm DIVIDED BY eta motor"] s5["STEP 5

Ppv EQUALS Pin DIVIDED BY eta MPPT eta wiring"] s6["STEP 6
