STRING DESIGN
Ns series INTO Np parallel"] s8["STEP 8
VERIFY ENERGY AND VOLUME
DELIVERED PER DAY"] stop(["END
DESIGN COMPLETE"]) start --> s1 --> s2 --> s3 --> s4 --> s5 --> s6 --> s7 --> s8 --> stop ``` ### 4.4 Pump Performance Curve — Operating Point Concept ```mermaid flowchart LR curveA["PUMP CURVE H VERSUS Q
HEAD DECREASES WITH FLOW"] curveB["SYSTEM CURVE H VERSUS Q
HEAD INCREASES WITH Q SQUARED"] pointX["OPERATING POINT
INTERSECTION OF PUMP
AND SYSTEM CURVE"] curveA --- pointX curveB --- pointX ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Insight:** In a variable-speed SPPS, the pump curve shifts as speed changes (per Affinity Laws), and the system curve remains constant. The operating point **migrates** along the system curve as irradiance changes — this is precisely why solar pumps deliver water at varying rates throughout the day. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Define the term "Total Dynamic Head" (TDH) in the context of a solar PV pumping system. List its components with one-line significance of each.** **Model Answer:** > [!NOTE] > Total Dynamic Head (TDH) is the total equivalent height (in metres) to which a unit volume of water must be lifted by the pump, including vertical lift and all hydraulic losses. **Components of TDH:** 1. **Static Head ($H_s$):** The vertical distance between the water level in the source (well/pond) and the highest delivery point. It is a **fixed** loss independent of flow. 2. **Friction Head ($H_f$):** Continuous energy loss due to viscous drag along the pipe walls, computed by **Hazen-Williams equation**. Increases with flow rate. 3. **Minor Losses ($H_m$):** Localised losses at bends, valves, foot valve, and fittings. Usually 5–10 % of $H_f$. 4. **Velocity Head ($H_v$):** Kinetic energy of water leaving the pipe $= v^2/2g$. Negligible for low-velocity SPPS. **Mathematical form:** $$ TDH = H_s + H_f + H_m + H_v $$ --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **With the help of a neat sketch, explain the working of a Solar PV Submersible Pumping System. State any two advantages over a diesel pump.** **Model Answer:** A solar PV submersible pumping system consists of a **PV array mounted on a galvanized structure**, a **MPPT controller / VFD**, a **3-phase AC submersible motor** coupled to a **multi-stage centrifugal pump**, and a **riser pipe** that delivers water to an overhead tank. **Working Principle:** - Sunlight strikes the PV modules, generating DC electricity proportional to irradiance. - The MPPT controller continuously adjusts the operating voltage to extract peak power. - The VFD converts DC to variable-frequency AC, driving the motor at variable speed. - The submersible pump (located deep inside the borewell) lifts water through the riser pipe to the surface. **Two Advantages over Diesel Pumps:** 1. **Zero fuel cost & zero emissions** — Life-cycle cost is 40–60 % lower over 25 years. 2. **Minimal maintenance** — No engine oil, no spark plugs, no fuel filter; only bearing and seal replacement every 3–5 years. --- **Q3. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** **Differentiate between centrifugal and positive displacement pumps used in solar PV applications.** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Centrifugal Pump | Positive Displacement Pump | | :--- | :---: | :---: | | Flow mechanism | Velocity → Pressure | Trapped volume displacement | | Head characteristic | Head $\downarrow$ as flow $\uparrow$ | Head $\uparrow$ as flow $\uparrow$ | | Suitable head range | 5 – 150 m | 50 – 300 m | | Flow rate | High (5 – 50 L/s) | Low (0.1 – 5 L/s) | | Efficiency at variable speed | Drops sharply | Remains nearly constant | | Typical SPPS application | Surface irrigation | Drip irrigation, high-head borewells | --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 / Model Paper]** **A farm located in Thrissur requires 80,000 litres of water per day for irrigation. The water must be lifted from a borewell located 45 m below ground level to an overhead tank located 12 m above ground. The total length of the delivery pipe is 90 m, with a diameter of 50 mm (PVC, C = 150). The site receives an average of 5.5 peak sun hours/day. Assume the following efficiencies: $\eta_{pump} = 0.50$, $\eta_{motor} = 0.88$, $\eta_{MPPT} = 0.96$, $\eta_{wiring} = 0.98$. The available PV module is rated 340 Wp with $V_{mp} = 38$ V and $I_{mp} = 8.95$ A. Design the complete solar PV pumping system. (14 marks)** **Sub-part (a):** Compute the Total Dynamic Head, the hydraulic power, and the required PV array capacity. **(7 marks)** **Sub-part (b):** Determine the number of PV modules, the string configuration, and verify whether the system meets the daily water demand. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution — Sub-part (a):** **Step 1: Static Head** $$ H_s = 45 + 12 = 57\ \text{m} $$ **Step 2: Friction Head (Hazen-Williams)** Convert $Q$ to m³/s (we will iterate; first guess $Q \approx 4\ \text{L/s} = 0.004\ \text{m}^3/\text{s}$): $$ H_f = \frac{10.67 \times 90 \times (0.004)^{1.852}}{150^{1.852} \times (0.05)^{4.87}} $$ Compute term by term: - $0.004^{1.852} \approx 4.5 \times 10^{-5}$ - $150^{1.852} \approx 11{,}340$ - $0.05^{4.87} \approx 1.92 \times 10^{-6}$ $$ H_f = \frac{10.67 \times 90 \times 4.5 \times 10^{-5}}{11340 \times 1.92 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{0.04322}{0.02177} \approx 1.99\ \text{m} $$ $$ TDH = 57 + 1.99 \approx 59\ \text{m} $$ **Step 3: Average Flow Rate** $$ Q = \frac{80{,}000}{5.5 \times 3600} = 4.04\ \text{L/s} = 0.00404\ \text{m}^3/\text{s} $$ **Step 4: Hydraulic Power** $$ P_h = 9810 \times 0.00404 \times 59 = 2339.3\ \text{W} \approx 2.34\ \text{kW} $$ **Step 5: Shaft Power** $$ P_m = \frac{P_h}{\eta_{pump}} = \frac{2339.3}{0.50} = 4678.6\ \text{W} $$ **Step 6: Motor Electrical Input** $$ P_{in} = \frac{P_m}{\eta_{motor}} = \frac{4678.6}{0.88} = 5316.6\ \text{W} $$ **Step 7: Required PV Array Capacity** $$ P_{PV} = \frac{P_{in}}{\eta_{MPPT} \times \eta_{wiring}} = \frac{5316.6}{0.96 \times 0.98} = 5651.1\ \text{Wp} $$ **Answer (a):** $TDH = 59$ m, $P_h = 2.34$ kW, $P_{PV} = 5651$ Wp. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution — Sub-part (b):** **Step 1: Number of PV Modules** $$ N = \frac{P_{PV}}{P_{module}} = \frac{5651}{340} = 16.62 \approx 17\ \text{modules} $$ **Step 2: String Configuration** For a 3-phase AC pump driven by VFD, the DC bus voltage is typically **480 V** (or sometimes 96 V for smaller systems). Assuming a **96 V DC bus**: $$ N_s = \frac{96}{38} = 2.53 \rightarrow 3\ \text{modules in series} $$ $$ N_p = \frac{17}{3} = 5.67 \rightarrow 6\ \text{strings} $$ **Final array: 3 series × 6 parallel = 18 modules**, $P_{array} = 18 \times 340 = 6120$ Wp. **Step 3: Verification — Energy and Volume per Day** $$ \eta_{overall} = 0.96 \times 0.98 \times 0.88 \times 0.50 = 0.4143 $$ $$ E_{daily} = 6120 \times 5.5 \times 0.4143 = 13{,}945\ \text{Wh} = 13.95\ \text{kWh} $$ $$ V_{daily} = \frac{E_{daily}}{2.725 \times 10^{-3} \times 59} = \frac{13{,}950}{0.1608} = 86{,}750\ \text{L/day} $$ **Answer (b):** 18 modules (3S × 6P), delivers **86,750 L/day** ≥ 80,000 L/day demand. **Design is acceptable.** ✅ **(7 marks)** --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **A. (i)** Derive the Affinity Laws for a centrifugal pump and explain their significance in solar PV pumping systems. **(7 marks)** **B. (ii)** A solar pump delivers 2000 L/h at 25 m head under STC. Determine the new flow rate and head when the pump speed drops to 75 % of rated speed. Compute the new power consumption. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution — (i):** **Derivation of Affinity Laws:** Consider a centrifugal pump with impeller diameter $D$ rotating at speed $N$. The flow velocity at the impeller exit is proportional to the blade tip speed: $$ v \propto N \cdot D $$ Since volumetric flow rate $Q$ is the product of velocity and impeller area ($Q = v \cdot A$, and $A \propto D^2$): $$ Q = K_1 \cdot N \cdot D^3 $$ **Law 1:** For a fixed impeller diameter ($D$ constant): $$ \frac{Q_1}{Q_2} = \frac{N_1}{N_2} $$ The head developed is proportional to the square of velocity ($H \propto v^2$): $$ H = K_2 \cdot N^2 \cdot D^2 $$ **Law 2:** $$ \frac{H_1}{H_2} = \left(\frac{N_1}{N_2}\right)^2 $$ The power is the product of head and flow: $$ P = \rho g Q H = \rho g (K_1 N D^3)(K_2 N^2 D^2) = K_3 \cdot N^3 \cdot D^5 $$ **Law 3:** $$ \frac{P_1}{P_2} = \left(\frac{N_1}{N_2}\right)^3 $$ **Significance in SPPS:** - Solar PV pumps run at **variable speed** as irradiance changes. - The cubic power law means that at low irradiance, the pump consumes drastically less power, allowing operation to **extend** into early morning and late evening. - A VFD/MPPT controller exploits these laws to maintain the operating point near maximum efficiency. --- #### **Model Solution — (ii):** **Given:** $Q_1 = 2000\ \text{L/h}$, $H_1 = 25\ \text{m}$, $N_2 / N_1 = 0.75$ **New flow rate (Affinity Law 1):** $$ Q_2 = Q_1 \times \frac{N_2}{N_1} = 2000 \times 0.75 = 1500\ \text{L/h} $$ **New head (Affinity Law 2):** $$ H_2 = H_1 \times \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^2 = 25 \times 0.75^2 = 25 \times 0.5625 = 14.06\ \text{m} $$ **New power consumption (Affinity Law 3):** First compute original power: $$ P_1 = \frac{9810 \times Q_1 \times H_1}{3600} = \frac{9810 \times (2000/3600) \times 25}{1000} = \frac{9810 \times 0.5556 \times 25}{1000} = 136.25\ \text{W} $$ **Alternative (direct affinity):** $$ P_2 = P_1 \times (0.75)^3 = 136.25 \times 0.4219 = 57.48\ \text{W} $$ **Answer:** $Q_2 = 1500\ \text{L/h}$, $H_2 = 14.06\ \text{m}$, $P_2 = 57.48\ \text{W}$. **(7 marks)** --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in SPPS Numericals:** > > 1. **Forgetting the $1/1000$ factor** when $Q$ is in L/s and $P_h$ is required in kW. *(Loss: 1 mark)* > 2. **Using $V$ in litres directly** in $E = \rho g V H$ without converting to m³. *(Loss: 1–2 marks)* > 3. **Ignoring friction head** and computing only static head — KTU explicitly states "total head" in most questions. *(Loss: 2–3 marks)* > 4. **Confusing W and Wp** — PV array is rated in **Wp** (peak watts at STC), while pump power is in **W** (actual). Always divide by PSH when going from Wp to Wh. > 5. **Not rounding up the module count** — A fractional value (e.g., 16.6) must be rounded **up to 17**, never truncated. *(Loss: 1 mark)* > 6. **Wrong Hazen-Williams exponent** — The exponent is **1.852** (not 1.85 or 1.9). Memorize it. > 7. **Mixing $H_s$** — Static head is the **vertical distance from water level to delivery point**, not the pipe length. ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist - ✅ Solar PV pumping system is a **standalone, variable-speed** off-grid system with four energy-conversion stages. - ✅ Hydraulic power $P_h = \rho g Q H = 9.81 \times Q \times H$ (with $Q$ in L/s, $P_h$ in kW). - ✅ Daily hydraulic energy $E = 0.002725 \times V \times H$ (kWh, V in m³, H in m). - ✅ Total Dynamic Head $TDH = H_s + H_f + H_m + H_v$ — always include friction head. - ✅ Hazen-Williams: $H_f = (10.67 L Q^{1.852}) / (C^{1.852} d^{4.87})$ — PVC pipe C = 150. - ✅ System efficiency chain: $\eta_{total} = \eta_{PV} \times \eta_{MPPT} \times \eta_{motor} \times \eta_{pump}$. - ✅ Number of modules: $N = P_{PV} / P_{module}$, always **round up**. - ✅ Affinity Laws: $Q \propto N$, $H \propto N^2$, $P \propto N^3$ — cubic power law enables low-irradiance operation. - ✅ Pump selection: Centrifugal (low head, high flow) vs Submersible (deep well) vs Positive Displacement (high head, low flow). - ✅ Always verify $V_{delivered} \geq V_{required}$ at the end of design. - ✅ PM-KUSUM, MNRE subsidies, and BIS standards (IS 14614) are the **policy backbone** of SPPS deployment in India.

topics.title: Solar Cooker
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEET721 - DESIGN OF SOLAR PV SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Solar Cooker

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Solar Cooker** is a device that converts incident solar radiation directly into thermal energy, raising the temperature of foodstuffs (or water) to cooking range (typically **373 K to 473 K**) for human consumption. It is one of the simplest, most reliable end-use applications of low-grade solar thermal energy, particularly suited for tropical and subtropical regions of India where solar insolation is abundant (≥ **4.5 kWh/m²/day**).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Module 2 Emphasis:** Solar Cooker belongs to the family of *non-concentrating and low-concentrating solar thermal devices*. The Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS) testing protocol **IS 13429 (Parts 1 & 2)** governs all performance certifications.

### 1.2 Types of Solar Cookers (KTU Classification)

The KTU syllabus recognizes **two principal classes**:

| Class | Sub-Type | Operating Temp. | Tracking | Approx. Cost (₹) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Non-Concentrating** | Box-Type (Single Pot / Double Pot) | 373 – 423 K | **Not Required** (fixed) | 2,500 – 5,000 |
| **Concentrating** | Parabolic Dish / Scheffler Community | 423 – 773 K | **Mandatory** (seasonal/daily) | 8,000 – 25,000 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Greenhouse Effect Principle (Box Cookers):** Short-wave solar radiation penetrates the transparent cover, is absorbed by the blackened tray, re-radiates as long-wave IR that is trapped by the cover — exactly like a closed car parked in sunlight. **Concentrators**, in contrast, work on the **point-focus optics** principle (geometric concentration).

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine parking a car with closed windows in the sun — the interior becomes unbearably hot even on a cool day. A **box solar cooker is essentially the same phenomenon**, but engineered:
- The **car body** → outer wooden/cardboard box
- The **glass windows** → tempered glass cover
- The **black dashboard** → absorber tray coated with black paint or selective surface
- The **seat insulation** → glass wool / thermocol layer

For **concentrating cookers**, the analogy is a **magnifying glass focusing sunlight onto a leaf** — concentrate enough light, and you can ignite paper. The same trick, scaled up, cooks food.

### 1.4 The MNES Specification Floor (Mandatory Compliance)

Under India's **Ministry of New and Renewable Energy (MNES)** scheme, a box-type solar cooker must satisfy:

- **F₁ (First Figure of Merit)** ≥ **0.12 °C·m²/W**
- **F₂ (Second Figure of Merit / Thermal Efficiency)** ≥ **0.40 (40%)**
- Minimum **2 kg water load**, raised from **30 °C to 95 °C** within a stipulated time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Any design failing these two criteria is **not eligible for subsidy** under Kisan Urja Suraksha evam Utthaan Mahabhiyan (KUSUM) or state-level solar cooking programs.

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Box-Type Solar Cooker — Constructional Anatomy

A standard double-pot box cooker has **five layers**:

1. **Outer Casing** — Galvanized iron (GI) sheet or seasoned wood (1.5–2 mm thick). Mechanical rigidity only.
2. **Thermal Insulation** — Glass wool, rock wool, or thermocol (Cork-board, k = **0.04 W/m·K**, thickness 40–60 mm). Minimizes conduction loss.
3. **Inner Tray (Absorber Plate)** — Aluminium (preferred, k = **237 W/m·K**) or copper; thickness 0.5–1 mm. Black-coated for high **α (absorptance ≈ 0.92–0.96)**.
4. **Cooking Pots** — Aluminium / blackened steel, painted matte black on outer surface.
5. **Transparent Cover (Glazing)** — Single or **double-glazed** tempered glass; thickness 3–4 mm; τ ≈ **0.85–0.90** for direct solar.

> [!NOTE]
> **Selective Surface Coating:** A "selective surface" has **high α (>0.95) for short wavelengths AND low ε (<0.15) for long wavelengths** — the holy grail for solar absorbers. Common examples: **black nickel, black chrome, cermet coatings** (e.g., SUNSELECT, Solkote Hi-Sorb). Plain black paint has α ≈ 0.95 but ε ≈ 0.88, so it loses more via radiation.

### 2.2 Heat Transfer Mechanism (Block Cooker, Steady-State)

At stagnation (no water load), the **plate reaches an equilibrium temperature** where absorbed solar flux equals total heat losses:

$$\boxed{S \cdot (\alpha \tau) \cdot A_a = U_L \cdot A_a \cdot (T_p - T_a)}$$

- $S$ = Incident solar radiation (W/m²)
- $\alpha$ = Absorber plate absorptance
- $\tau$ = Cover transmittance
- $A_a$ = Aperture area (m²)
- $U_L$ = Overall (top + bottom + side) loss coefficient (W/m²·K)
- $T_p$ = Plate temperature (°C)
- $T_a$ = Ambient temperature (°C)

### 2.3 The Five Heat Loss Pathways

| # | Mode | Path | Expression | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Top — Convection | Cover air-gap (free) | $h_{cv,\text{top}} \approx 2.8 + 3.0 \, v_w$ | Reduce wind, double-glaze |
| 2 | Top — Radiation | Plate ↔ Sky | $\varepsilon_{eff} \, \sigma (T_p^4 - T_{sky}^4)$ | Selective coating (low ε) |
| 3 | Bottom — Conduction | Insulation | $\dfrac{k_{\text{ins}} \, A_b \, (T_p - T_a)}{L_{\text{ins}}}$ | Thicker insulation (50 mm+) |
| 4 | Sides — Conduction | Insulation walls | $\dfrac{k_{\text{ins}} \, A_s \, (T_p - T_a)}{L_s}$ | Side-wall insulation |
| 5 | Frame Edge | Direct conduction | $\dfrac{k_{\text{frame}} \, A_e \, (T_p - T_a)}{L_e}$ | Wooden/GI frames |

**Effective top loss coefficient** combines (1) and (2):

$$U_t = h_{cv} + \dfrac{\varepsilon_{eff} \, \sigma (T_p^2 + T_a^2)(T_p + T_a)}{1}$$

The standard practice groups $U_L$ as a **lumped coefficient** measured experimentally.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Board-Exam Ready)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MEMORIZE THIS TABLE — it covers 90% of derivation/numerical questions in KTU ESE.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Standard Value |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **First Figure of Merit (F₁)** | $F_1 = \dfrac{T_p - T_a}{I}$ | °C·m²/W (or K·m²/W) | ≥ **0.12** |
| 2 | **Second Figure of Merit (F₂)** | $F_2 = \eta_{th} = \dfrac{m \, C_p \, \Delta T}{I \, A_a \, \Delta t}$ | Dimensionless | ≥ **0.40** |
| 3 | Stagnation Temperature | $T_p = T_a + \dfrac{I \, (\alpha \tau)}{U_L}$ | K or °C | 373–423 K |
| 4 | Cooking Power | $\dot{Q}_u = \dfrac{m \, C_p \, (T_f - T_i)}{\Delta t}$ | W | 60–120 W typical |
| 5 | Heat Loss Coefficient | $U_L = U_t + U_b + U_s$ | W/m²·K | 5–9 (single-glaze) |
| 6 | F₁ Design Identity | $F_1 = \dfrac{\alpha \, \tau}{U_L}$ | °C·m²/W | — |
| 7 | Optical Efficiency | $\eta_o = (\alpha \, \tau)$ | Dimensionless | 0.78–0.85 |
| 8 | Useful Heat Gain | $Q_u = \eta_o \, I \, A_a - U_L \, A_a \, (T_p - T_a)$ | W | — |
| 9 | Parabolic Dish Concentration Ratio | $C = \dfrac{A_a}{A_r} = \dfrac{1}{\sin^2 \theta}$ | Dimensionless | 20–100 (dish) |
| 10 | Scheffler Mirror Area | $A_m = \dfrac{A_c}{\cos \theta_m \cdot \rho}$ | m² | depends on sun position |
| 11 | Stefan-Boltzmann Constant | $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ | W/m²·K⁴ | — |
| 12 | Cooking Energy for 2 kg water, 30→95 °C | $Q = 2 \times 4186 \times 65$ | J | ≈ **544,180 J ≈ 0.151 kWh** |

> **Critical Note:** In the table above, $\Delta T$ denotes the temperature rise of water during cooking, and $\Delta t$ denotes the time taken (in **seconds**).

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the First Figure of Merit (F₁)

Starting from the steady-state plate energy balance when **no water is loaded** (stagnation condition):

$$S \cdot (\alpha \tau) \cdot A_a = U_L \cdot A_a \cdot (T_p - T_a) + 0 \quad \text{(no useful heat)}$$

Dividing both sides by $A_a$:

$$S \cdot (\alpha \tau) = U_L \cdot (T_p - T_a)$$

Rearranging to isolate $T_p - T_a$:

$$T_p - T_a = \dfrac{S \cdot (\alpha \tau)}{U_L}$$

Now dividing both sides by the solar insolation $S = I$:

$$\dfrac{T_p - T_a}{I} = \dfrac{\alpha \tau}{U_L}$$

By definition, the **First Figure of Merit** is the stagnation temperature rise per unit insolation:

$$\boxed{F_1 = \dfrac{T_p - T_a}{I} = \dfrac{\alpha \tau}{U_L}}$$

**Physical Interpretation:** $F_1$ quantifies the cooker's ability to convert solar radiation into plate temperature rise. A high $F_1$ means:
- High absorptance (good black/selective coating)
- High transmittance (clean glass)
- Low overall heat loss coefficient (good insulation)

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Second Figure of Merit / Thermal Efficiency (F₂)

Under **loaded (cooking) condition**, the energy balance at the absorber plate is:

$$S \cdot (\alpha \tau) \cdot A_a = U_L \cdot A_a \cdot (T_p - T_a) + \dot{Q}_u$$

where $\dot{Q}_u$ is the useful rate of heat transferred to the water (cooking power). Solving for $\dot{Q}_u$:

$$\dot{Q}_u = (\alpha \tau) \cdot I \cdot A_a - U_L \cdot A_a \cdot (T_p - T_a)$$

The **thermal efficiency** is defined as the ratio of useful heat gain to incident solar energy on the aperture:

$$\eta_{th} = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_u}{I \cdot A_a} = (\alpha \tau) - \dfrac{U_L \, (T_p - T_a)}{I}$$

Substituting the design identity $F_1 = (\alpha \tau)/U_L$:

$$\eta_{th} = U_L \cdot F_1 - U_L \cdot F_1 \cdot \dfrac{(T_p - T_a)}{(T_p - T_a)_{\text{stagnation}}}$$

This can be re-expressed as the **Hottel-Whillier-Bliss equation** analogue for solar cookers:

$$\boxed{\eta_{th} = F_1 \cdot U_L \cdot \left[ 1 - \dfrac{T_p - T_a}{T_{p,\text{stg}} - T_a} \right]}$$

For the **standard MNES test** (2 kg water, 30 °C → 95 °C), the practical efficiency is:

$$\boxed{F_2 = \dfrac{m \, C_p \, (T_f - T_i)}{I \, A_a \, \Delta t}}$$

For $m = 2$ kg, $C_p = 4186$ J/kg·K, $\Delta T = 65$ K, $I = 1000$ W/m², $A_a = 0.5$ m², and $\Delta t = 1800$ s (30 min):

$$F_2 = \dfrac{2 \times 4186 \times 65}{1000 \times 0.5 \times 1800} = \dfrac{544{,}180}{900{,}000} \approx 0.604$$

This comfortably exceeds the MNES minimum of **0.40** ✓.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Standard 14-Mark Problem Style)

**Problem:** A box-type solar cooker with aperture area $A_a = 0.5 \text{ m}^2$ is loaded with $m = 2$ kg of water. The water temperature rises from $30 \text{ °C}$ to $95 \text{ °C}$ in **35 minutes** under an average solar intensity of $I = 880 \text{ W/m}^2$. Compute:
1. The thermal efficiency (F₂)
2. The useful heat energy stored
3. The useful cooking power

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Useful Heat Energy Stored:**

$$Q_u = m \, C_p \, (T_f - T_i)$$

$$Q_u = 2 \text{ kg} \times 4186 \text{ J/kg·K} \times (95 - 30) \text{ K}$$

$$Q_u = 2 \times 4186 \times 65 = 544{,}180 \text{ J}$$

$$Q_u \approx 544.2 \text{ kJ}$$

**Step 2 — Cooking Power:**

$$\dot{Q}_u = \dfrac{Q_u}{\Delta t} = \dfrac{544{,}180}{35 \times 60} = \dfrac{544{,}180}{2100}$$

$$\dot{Q}_u \approx 259.1 \text{ W}$$

**Step 3 — Thermal Efficiency (F₂):**

$$F_2 = \dfrac{m \, C_p \, (T_f - T_i)}{I \, A_a \, \Delta t} = \dfrac{544{,}180}{880 \times 0.5 \times 2100}$$

$$F_2 = \dfrac{544{,}180}{924{,}000} \approx 0.589 \;\; \text{or} \;\; 58.9\%$$

**Verification against MNES Standard:**

Since $F_2 = 0.589 \geq 0.40$ ✓ → **Cooker passes the MNES test.**

---

### 3.4 Derivation of Concentration Ratio for a Parabolic Dish

A parabolic dish with rim angle $\theta_r$ concentrates sunlight onto a focal point. The geometric concentration ratio is:

$$C_g = \dfrac{A_a}{A_r}$$

For a circular dish of diameter $D$ and a focal spot of diameter $d_f$:

$$A_a = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}, \quad A_r = \dfrac{\pi d_f^2}{4}$$

$$C_g = \left(\dfrac{D}{d_f}\right)^2$$

The focal spot diameter is related to the rim angle by:

$$d_f = D \cdot \sin \theta_r$$

Hence:

$$\boxed{C_g = \dfrac{1}{\sin^2 \theta_r}}$$

For $\theta_r = 11.5°$ (typical Scheffler design): $C_g = 1/\sin^2(11.5°) = 1/0.0397 \approx 25.2$

For $\theta_r = 6°$ (deep dish): $C_g = 1/\sin^2(6°) = 1/0.0109 \approx 91.4$

> **Physical Note:** A rim angle of **90°** would mean a flat plate (no concentration, $C_g = 1$); a rim angle approaching **0°** would mean infinite concentration (impractical due to aberrations).

---

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Box Type Solar Cooker

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SUN(["☀ Incident Solar Radiation"]):::sun
    COVER["Transparent Glass Cover\nτ ≈ 0.85-0.90"]:::cover
    AIR["Trapped Air Gap\n(Convection barrier)"]:::air
    PLATE["Blackened Absorber Plate\nα ≈ 0.92-0.96"]:::plate
    POT["Cooking Pot + Water Load\n2 kg typical"]:::pot
    INS["Thermal Insulation\nk = 0.04 W/m·K"]:::ins
    CASE["Outer Casing\nGI / Wood"]:::case
    LOSSES(["Convective + Radiative Losses\nU_t = 5-9 W/m²·K"]):::loss
    OUTPUT(["Useful Cooking Heat\nQ_u = m·C_p·ΔT"]):::output

    SUN --> COVER
    COVER --> AIR
    AIR --> PLATE
    PLATE --> POT
    PLATE -.-> LOSSES
    COVER -.-> LOSSES
    CASE --> INS
    INS --> PLATE

    POT --> OUTPUT

    classDef sun fill:#FFD700,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef cover fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#4682B4,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef air fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#5F9EA0,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef plate fill:#2F2F2F,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
    classDef pot fill:#A0522D,stroke:#8B4513,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
    classDef ins fill:#FFEFD5,stroke:#D2691E,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef case fill:#8B7355,stroke:#654321,stroke-width:1px,color:#FFF
    classDef loss fill:#FFE4E1,stroke:#DC143C,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#90EE90,stroke:#228B22,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

### 4.2 Heat-Energy Flow Schematic — Parabolic Dish Concentrator

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SOLAR["☀ Parallel Sunlight\nI = 800-1000 W/m²"]:::solar
    REFLECT["Reflective Surface\nParaboloid, ρ ≈ 0.85\nAluminized Glass / Polished Al"]:::reflect
    TRACK["Tracking System\nSeasonal + Daily\nManual / Automatic"]:::track
    FOCUS["Focal Point / Receiver\nT_peak = 500-800 K"]:::focus
    POT2["Cooking Pot\nHung at focus"]:::pot2
    HEATOUT(["High-grade Thermal\nUseful Cooking Power"]):::heatout

    SOLAR --> REFLECT
    TRACK -.-> REFLECT
    REFLECT --> FOCUS
    FOCUS --> POT2
    POT2 --> HEATOUT

    classDef solar fill:#FFD700,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef reflect fill:#C0C0C0,stroke:#696969,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef track fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#8B008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
    classDef focus fill:#FF4500,stroke:#B22222,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
    classDef pot2 fill:#A0522D,stroke:#8B4513,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
    classDef heatout fill:#90EE90,stroke:#228B22,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Layered Cross-Section Diagram — Box Cooker (ASCII Schematic)

```
        ↓ ↓ ↓ ↓ ↓ ↓ ↓    Solar Radiation (I)
       ┌─────────────────┐  ← Glass Cover (τ ≈ 0.88)
       │   ~~~~~~~~~~~   │  ← Trapped Air (Greenhouse Effect)
       ├─────────────────┤
       │ ▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓ │  ← Absorber Plate (α ≈ 0.95, black)
       │   ┌───────┐     │  ← Cooking Pot with Water (m = 2 kg)
       │   │ WATER │     │
       │   └───────┘     │
       ├─────────────────┤
       │ ############### │  ← Insulation (50 mm, k = 0.04 W/m·K)
       ├─────────────────┤
       │ ┼┼┼┼┼┼┼┼┼┼┼┼┼┼┼ │  ← Outer Casing (GI Sheet / Wood)
       └─────────────────┘
        ↑ Heat Losses  ↑    (Conduction + Convection + Radiation)
```

### 4.4 Stagnation Temperature Logic Flow

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> NoLoad: Cooker Placed in Sun
    NoLoad --> Absorbing: (ατ)·I·A_a absorbed
    Absorbing --> Heating: T_p rises
    Heating --> Plateau: Q_in = U_L·A_a·(T_p - T_a)
    Plateau --> Stagnation: Equilibrium reached
    Stagnation --> ReadT_P: Measure T_p with thermocouple
    ReadT_P --> CalcF1: F_1 = (T_p - T_a) / I
    CalcF1 --> CheckMNES: F_1 ≥ 0.12 ?
    CheckMNES --> Pass: Yes
    CheckMNES --> Fail: No — improve insulation / selective surface
    Pass --> [*]
    Fail --> [*]
```

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define the *First Figure of Merit* and *Second Figure of Merit* of a box-type solar cooker as per the MNES specification. State their minimum prescribed values.

**Model Answer:**

The **First Figure of Merit (F₁)** is a measure of the cooker's ability to convert incident solar radiation into absorber plate temperature rise at **stagnation** (no-load) condition.

$$F_1 = \dfrac{T_p - T_a}{I} \quad [\text{°C·m}^2/\text{W}]$$

The **Second Figure of Merit (F₂)** is the **thermal efficiency** of the cooker under a standard test load:

$$F_2 = \eta_{th} = \dfrac{m \, C_p \, (T_f - T_i)}{I \, A_a \, \Delta t}$$

**MNES minimum prescribed values:** $F_1 \geq 0.12 \text{ °C·m}^2/\text{W}$ and $F_2 \geq 0.40$ (i.e., 40%).

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** Why is a **selective surface coating** preferred over ordinary black paint for the absorber plate of a solar cooker?

**Model Answer:**

A **selective surface** has two distinct optical properties in different wavelength regions:
- **High solar absorptance** ($\alpha > 0.95$) in the short-wavelength solar spectrum (0.3–2.5 μm)
- **Low thermal emittance** ($\varepsilon < 0.15$) in the long-wavelength infrared region (2.5–50 μm)

Ordinary black paint has $\alpha \approx 0.95$ **but** $\varepsilon \approx 0.88$, which causes heavy **re-radiation loss** once the plate temperature exceeds 60 °C. Since solar cooker plates reach 100–150 °C, re-radiation becomes a dominant loss mode. A selective coating reduces this loss by a factor of **5–6×**, directly improving $F_1$ and $F_2$.

Examples: black nickel, black chrome, $\text{MoO}_3$-based cermet coatings.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Question (Module Internal Choice)

> **Question A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam — June 2023]**
>
> **(a)** Describe, with a neat sketch, the construction and working of a **box-type solar cooker**. Explain the role of each component. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A box-type solar cooker has an absorber area of 0.6 m². When tested, the water mass of 2 kg gets heated from 30 °C to 95 °C in 40 minutes. The average solar intensity during the test was 900 W/m². Calculate (i) the thermal efficiency of the cooker, and (ii) the useful cooking power. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

A box-type solar cooker is an **insulated rectangular box** with a transparent glass cover. Its main components are:

1. **Outer casing** — Provides mechanical strength; usually GI sheet (0.8 mm) or seasoned wood.
2. **Thermal insulation** — Glass wool, thermocol, or rock wool (thickness 40–60 mm, $k \approx 0.04$ W/m·K). Reduces conductive heat loss from bottom and sides.
3. **Inner absorber tray** — Made of aluminium or copper (high thermal conductivity); coated with black paint or selective surface for maximum absorption. Receives and conducts solar heat to the cooking pots.
4. **Cooking pots** — Aluminium vessels with a matte-black outer surface to absorb any transmitted radiation reaching the pot directly.
5. **Transparent cover (glazing)** — Single or double-pane tempered glass; allows short-wave solar radiation to pass while trapping long-wave IR (greenhouse effect).

**Working principle:** Solar radiation passes through the transparent cover and is absorbed by the blackened tray. The heat is conducted to the cooking pots containing food/water. Re-radiated long-wave radiation from the absorber is **trapped** by the cover (greenhouse effect), raising the internal temperature to **100–140 °C**, suitable for boiling, steaming, and slow cooking.

**Sketch:** Refer to **Section 4.3** (ASCII cross-section) of these notes.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:** $A_a = 0.6 \text{ m}^2$, $m = 2$ kg, $T_i = 30 \text{ °C}$, $T_f = 95 \text{ °C}$, $\Delta t = 40 \text{ min} = 2400$ s, $I = 900 \text{ W/m}^2$, $C_p = 4186 \text{ J/kg·K}$.

**(i) Thermal efficiency:**

$$F_2 = \eta_{th} = \dfrac{m \, C_p \, (T_f - T_i)}{I \, A_a \, \Delta t}$$

$$F_2 = \dfrac{2 \times 4186 \times (95 - 30)}{900 \times 0.6 \times 2400}$$

$$F_2 = \dfrac{2 \times 4186 \times 65}{1{,}296{,}000} = \dfrac{544{,}180}{1{,}296{,}000}$$

$$\boxed{F_2 \approx 0.420 \;\; \text{or} \;\; 42.0\%}$$

**(ii) Useful cooking power:**

$$\dot{Q}_u = \dfrac{m \, C_p \, (T_f - T_i)}{\Delta t} = \dfrac{544{,}180}{2400}$$

$$\boxed{\dot{Q}_u \approx 226.7 \text{ W}}$$

**Verification:** $F_2 = 0.42 \geq 0.40$ ✓ (Passes MNES spec).

---

> **Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative to Question A) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
>
> **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **parabolic dish-type solar cooker**. Derive the expression for its **geometric concentration ratio** in terms of rim angle. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A box-type solar cooker is tested under stagnation conditions. The plate temperature stabilizes at **135 °C** when the ambient is 32 °C and the average solar intensity is **780 W/m²**. Calculate the **First Figure of Merit** and the overall heat loss coefficient, assuming $(\alpha \tau) = 0.78$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

A **parabolic dish cooker** uses a paraboloidal reflective surface to concentrate parallel sun rays onto a focal point where the cooking vessel is suspended.

**Components:**
- Paraboloidal reflector (fibreglass-reinforced plastic or aluminium sheet, coated with high-reflectance material, $\rho \approx 0.85$)
- Tracking mechanism (manual or motorised) to maintain sun alignment throughout the day
- Cooking pot suspended at the focal point
- Stand / pedestal

**Working:** Parallel rays from the sun strike the dish and are reflected to converge at the focal point. The resulting concentration of solar flux raises the temperature at the focus to **400–700 °C**, enabling frying and roasting (not just boiling).

**Derivation of concentration ratio:**

For a paraboloidal dish of rim diameter $D$ and rim angle $\theta_r$ (angle between the optical axis and the line from focus to rim):

$$d_f = D \, \sin \theta_r$$

The aperture area: $A_a = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$

The receiver (focal spot) area: $A_r = \dfrac{\pi d_f^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi D^2 \sin^2 \theta_r}{4}$

Geometric concentration ratio:

$$\boxed{C_g = \dfrac{A_a}{A_r} = \dfrac{1}{\sin^2 \theta_r}}$$

For $\theta_r = 11.5°$: $C_g \approx 25$. For $\theta_r = 6°$: $C_g \approx 91$.

**Sketch:** Refer to **Section 4.2** (Mermaid schematic).

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:** $T_p = 135 \text{ °C}$, $T_a = 32 \text{ °C}$, $I = 780 \text{ W/m}^2$, $\alpha \tau = 0.78$.

**(i) First Figure of Merit:**

$$F_1 = \dfrac{T_p - T_a}{I} = \dfrac{135 - 32}{780} = \dfrac{103}{780}$$

$$\boxed{F_1 \approx 0.132 \text{ °C·m}^2/\text{W}}$$

This exceeds the MNES minimum of 0.12 ✓.

**(ii) Overall heat loss coefficient:**

Using $F_1 = \dfrac{\alpha \tau}{U_L}$:

$$U_L = \dfrac{\alpha \tau}{F_1} = \dfrac{0.78}{0.132}$$

$$\boxed{U_L \approx 5.91 \text{ W/m}^2\cdot\text{K}}$$

This is a typical value for a well-insulated single-glazed box cooker.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Unit trap in F₁**: Students often write $F_1$ in K/m² or °C/W (wrong). Correct: **°C·m²/W** (insolation is in W/m², so dividing a temperature by W/m² gives °C·m²/W).
> 2. **Time-unit trap in F₂**: $\Delta t$ in the efficiency formula **must be in seconds**, not minutes. Forgetting this gives an efficiency 60× too low.
> 3. **Plate vs water temperature**: $F_1$ uses **plate temperature**; $F_2$ uses **water temperature**. Mixing them costs full marks.
> 4. **Stagnation condition**: $F_1$ is measured **without water** (or with empty pot) — students often compute it under load, which is wrong.
> 5. **Selective vs black paint**: Students forget to mention the **wavelength-specific** nature of selective coatings. Always specify *high α for solar spectrum, low ε for IR*.
> 6. **MNES values must be stated numerically**: $F_1 \geq 0.12$, $F_2 \geq 0.40$ — vague answers like "F₁ must be high" lose marks.

- ☐ **BIS / IS 13429** governs testing protocol; MNES governs subsidy eligibility.

- ☐ **Scheffler community cooker** uses a fixed-focus, tilted-axis paraboloid; smaller dish area but constant focus — suited for institutional kitchens.

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Solar Cooker** is a device that converts incident solar radiation directly into thermal energy, raising the temperature of foodstuffs (or water) to cooking range (typically **373 K to 473 K**) for human consumption. It is one of the simplest, most reliable end-use applications of low-grade solar thermal energy, particularly suited for tropical and subtropical regions of India where solar insolation is abundant (≥ **4.5 kWh/m²/day**).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Module 2 Emphasis:** Solar Cooker belongs to the family of *non-concentrating and low-concentrating solar thermal devices*. The Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS) testing protocol **IS 13429 (Parts 1 & 2)** governs all performance certifications.

### 1.2 Types of Solar Cookers (KTU Classification)

The KTU syllabus recognizes **two principal classes**:

| Class | Sub-Type | Operating Temp. | Tracking | Approx. Cost (₹) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Non-Concentrating** | Box-Type (Single Pot / Double Pot) | 373 – 423 K | **Not Required** (fixed) | 2,500 – 5,000 |
| **Concentrating** | Parabolic Dish / Scheffler Community | 423 – 773 K | **Mandatory** (seasonal/daily) | 8,000 – 25,000 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Greenhouse Effect Principle (Box Cookers):** Short-wave solar radiation penetrates the transparent cover, is absorbed by the blackened tray, re-radiates as long-wave IR that is trapped by the cover — exactly like a closed car parked in sunlight. **Concentrators**, in contrast, work on the **point-focus optics** principle (geometric concentration).

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine parking a car with closed windows in the sun — the interior becomes unbearably hot even on a cool day. A **box solar cooker is essentially the same phenomenon**, but engineered:
- The **car body** → outer wooden/cardboard box
- The **glass windows** → tempered glass cover
- The **black dashboard** → absorber tray coated with black paint or selective surface
- The **seat insulation** → glass wool / thermocol layer

For **concentrating cookers**, the analogy is a **magnifying glass focusing sunlight onto a leaf** — concentrate enough light, and you can ignite paper. The same trick, scaled up, cooks food.

### 1.4 The MNES Specification Floor (Mandatory Compliance)

Under India's **Ministry of New and Renewable Energy (MNES)** scheme, a box-type solar cooker must satisfy:

- **F₁ (First Figure of Merit)** ≥ **0.12 °C·m²/W**
- **F₂ (Second Figure of Merit / Thermal Efficiency)** ≥ **0.40 (40%)**
- Minimum **2 kg water load**, raised from **30 °C to 95 °C** within a stipulated time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Any design failing these two criteria is **not eligible for subsidy** under Kisan Urja Suraksha evam Utthaan Mahabhiyan (KUSUM) or state-level solar cooking programs.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Box-Type Solar Cooker — Constructional Anatomy

A standard double-pot box cooker has **five layers**:

1. **Outer Casing** — Galvanized iron (GI) sheet or seasoned wood (1.5–2 mm thick). Mechanical rigidity only.
2. **Thermal Insulation** — Glass wool, rock wool, or thermocol (Cork-board, k = **0.04 W/m·K**, thickness 40–60 mm). Minimizes conduction loss.
3. **Inner Tray (Absorber Plate)** — Aluminium (preferred, k = **237 W/m·K**) or copper; thickness 0.5–1 mm. Black-coated for high **α (absorptance ≈ 0.92–0.96)**.
4. **Cooking Pots** — Aluminium / blackened steel, painted matte black on outer surface.
5. **Transparent Cover (Glazing)** — Single or **double-glazed** tempered glass; thickness 3–4 mm; τ ≈ **0.85–0.90** for direct solar.

> [!NOTE]
> **Selective Surface Coating:** A "selective surface" has **high α (>0.95) for short wavelengths AND low ε (<0.15) for long wavelengths** — the holy grail for solar absorbers. Common examples: **black nickel, black chrome, cermet coatings** (e.g., SUNSELECT, Solkote Hi-Sorb). Plain black paint has α ≈ 0.95 but ε ≈ 0.88, so it loses more via radiation.

### 2.2 Heat Transfer Mechanism (Block Cooker, Steady-State)

At stagnation (no water load), the **plate reaches an equilibrium temperature** where absorbed solar flux equals total heat losses:

$$\boxed{S \cdot (\alpha \tau) \cdot A_a = U_L \cdot A_a \cdot (T_p - T_a)}$$

- $S$ = Incident solar radiation (W/m²)
- $\alpha$ = Absorber plate absorptance
- $\tau$ = Cover transmittance
- $A_a$ = Aperture area (m²)
- $U_L$ = Overall (top + bottom + side) loss coefficient (W/m²·K)
- $T_p$ = Plate temperature (°C)
- $T_a$ = Ambient temperature (°C)

### 2.3 The Five Heat Loss Pathways

| # | Mode | Path | Expression | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Top — Convection | Cover air-gap (free) | $h_{cv,\text{top}} \approx 2.8 + 3.0 \, v_w$ | Reduce wind, double-glaze |
| 2 | Top — Radiation | Plate ↔ Sky | $\varepsilon_{eff} \, \sigma (T_p^4 - T_{sky}^4)$ | Selective coating (low ε) |
| 3 | Bottom — Conduction | Insulation | $\dfrac{k_{\text{ins}} \, A_b \, (T_p - T_a)}{L_{\text{ins}}}$ | Thicker insulation (50 mm+) |
| 4 | Sides — Conduction | Insulation walls | $\dfrac{k_{\text{ins}} \, A_s \, (T_p - T_a)}{L_s}$ | Side-wall insulation |
| 5 | Frame Edge | Direct conduction | $\dfrac{k_{\text{frame}} \, A_e \, (T_p - T_a)}{L_e}$ | Wooden/GI frames |

**Effective top loss coefficient** combines (1) and (2):

$$U_t = h_{cv} + \dfrac{\varepsilon_{eff} \, \sigma (T_p^2 + T_a^2)(T_p + T_a)}{1}$$

The standard practice groups $U_L$ as a **lumped coefficient** measured experimentally.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Board-Exam Ready)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MEMORIZE THIS TABLE — it covers 90% of derivation/numerical questions in KTU ESE.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Standard Value |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **First Figure of Merit (F₁)** | $F_1 = \dfrac{T_p - T_a}{I}$ | °C·m²/W (or K·m²/W) | ≥ **0.12** |
| 2 | **Second Figure of Merit (F₂)** | $F_2 = \eta_{th} = \dfrac{m \, C_p \, \Delta T}{I \, A_a \, \Delta t}$ | Dimensionless | ≥ **0.40** |
| 3 | Stagnation Temperature | $T_p = T_a + \dfrac{I \, (\alpha \tau)}{U_L}$ | K or °C | 373–423 K |
| 4 | Cooking Power | $\dot{Q}_u = \dfrac{m \, C_p \, (T_f - T_i)}{\Delta t}$ | W | 60–120 W typical |
| 5 | Heat Loss Coefficient | $U_L = U_t + U_b + U_s$ | W/m²·K | 5–9 (single-glaze) |
| 6 | F₁ Design Identity | $F_1 = \dfrac{\alpha \, \tau}{U_L}$ | °C·m²/W | — |
| 7 | Optical Efficiency | $\eta_o = (\alpha \, \tau)$ | Dimensionless | 0.78–0.85 |
| 8 | Useful Heat Gain | $Q_u = \eta_o \, I \, A_a - U_L \, A_a \, (T_p - T_a)$ | W | — |
| 9 | Parabolic Dish Concentration Ratio | $C = \dfrac{A_a}{A_r} = \dfrac{1}{\sin^2 \theta_r}$ | Dimensionless | 20–100 (dish) |
| 10 | Scheffler Mirror Area | $A_m = \dfrac{A_c}{\cos \theta_m \cdot \rho}$ | m² | depends on sun position |
| 11 | Stefan-Boltzmann Constant | $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ | W/m²·K⁴ | — |
| 12 | Cooking Energy for 2 kg water, 30→95 °C | $Q = 2 \times 4186 \times 65$ | J | ≈ **544,180 J ≈ 0.151 kWh** |

> **Critical Note:** In the table above, $\Delta T$ denotes the temperature rise of water during cooking, and $\Delta t$ denotes the time taken (in **seconds**).

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the First Figure of Merit (F₁)

Starting from the steady-state plate energy balance when **no water is loaded** (stagnation condition):

$$S \cdot (\alpha \tau) \cdot A_a = U_L \cdot A_a \cdot (T_p - T_a) + 0 \quad \text{(no useful heat)}$$

Dividing both sides by $A_a$:

$$S \cdot (\alpha \tau) = U_L \cdot (T_p - T_a)$$

Rearranging to isolate $T_p - T_a$:

$$T_p - T_a = \dfrac{S \cdot (\alpha \tau)}{U_L}$$

Now dividing both sides by the solar insolation $S = I$:

$$\dfrac{T_p - T_a}{I} = \dfrac{\alpha \tau}{U_L}$$

By definition, the **First Figure of Merit** is the stagnation temperature rise per unit insolation:

$$\boxed{F_1 = \dfrac{T_p - T_a}{I} = \dfrac{\alpha \tau}{U_L}}$$

**Physical Interpretation:** $F_1$ quantifies the cooker's ability to convert solar radiation into plate temperature rise. A high $F_1$ means:
- High absorptance (good black/selective coating)
- High transmittance (clean glass)
- Low overall heat loss coefficient (good insulation)

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Second Figure of Merit / Thermal Efficiency (F₂)

Under **loaded (cooking) condition**, the energy balance at the absorber plate is:

$$S \cdot (\alpha \tau) \cdot A_a = U_L \cdot A_a \cdot (T_p - T_a) + \dot{Q}_u$$

where $\dot{Q}_u$ is the useful rate of heat transferred to the water (cooking power). Solving for $\dot{Q}_u$:

$$\dot{Q}_u = (\alpha \tau) \cdot I \cdot A_a - U_L \cdot A_a \cdot (T_p - T_a)$$

The **thermal efficiency** is defined as the ratio of useful heat gain to incident solar energy on the aperture:

$$\eta_{th} = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_u}{I \cdot A_a} = (\alpha \tau) - \dfrac{U_L \, (T_p - T_a)}{I}$$

Substituting the design identity $F_1 = (\alpha \tau)/U_L$:

$$\eta_{th} = U_L \cdot F_1 - U_L \cdot F_1 \cdot \dfrac{(T_p - T_a)}{(T_p - T_a)_{\text{stagnation}}}$$

This can be re-expressed as the **Hottel-Whillier-Bliss equation** analogue for solar cookers:

$$\boxed{\eta_{th} = F_1 \cdot U_L \cdot \left[ 1 - \dfrac{T_p - T_a}{T_{p,\text{stg}} - T_a} \right]}$$

For the **standard MNES test** (2 kg water, 30 °C → 95 °C), the practical efficiency is:

$$\boxed{F_2 = \dfrac{m \, C_p \, (T_f - T_i)}{I \, A_a \, \Delta t}}$$

For $m = 2$ kg, $C_p = 4186$ J/kg·K, $\Delta T = 65$ K, $I = 1000$ W/m², $A_a = 0.5$ m², and $\Delta t = 1800$ s (30 min):

$$F_2 = \dfrac{2 \times 4186 \times 65}{1000 \times 0.5 \times 1800} = \dfrac{544{,}180}{900{,}000} \approx 0.604$$

This comfortably exceeds the MNES minimum of **0.40** ✓.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Standard 14-Mark Problem Style)

**Problem:** A box-type solar cooker with aperture area $A_a = 0.5 \text{ m}^2$ is loaded with $m = 2$ kg of water. The water temperature rises from $30 \text{ °C}$ to $95 \text{ °C}$ in **35 minutes** under an average solar intensity of $I = 880 \text{ W/m}^2$. Compute:
1. The thermal efficiency (F₂)
2. The useful heat energy stored
3. The useful cooking power

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Useful Heat Energy Stored:**

$$Q_u = m \, C_p \, (T_f - T_i)$$

$$Q_u = 2 \text{ kg} \times 4186 \text{ J/kg·K} \times (95 - 30) \text{ K}$$

$$Q_u = 2 \times 4186 \times 65 = 544{,}180 \text{ J}$$

$$Q_u \approx 544.2 \text{ kJ}$$

**Step 2 — Cooking Power:**

$$\dot{Q}_u = \dfrac{Q_u}{\Delta t} = \dfrac{544{,}180}{35 \times 60} = \dfrac{544{,}180}{2100}$$

$$\dot{Q}_u \approx 259.1 \text{ W}$$

**Step 3 — Thermal Efficiency (F₂):**

$$F_2 = \dfrac{m \, C_p \, (T_f - T_i)}{I \, A_a \, \Delta t} = \dfrac{544{,}180}{880 \times 0.5 \times 2100}$$

$$F_2 = \dfrac{544{,}180}{924{,}000} \approx 0.589 \;\; \text{or} \;\; 58.9\%$$

**Verification against MNES Standard:**

Since $F_2 = 0.589 \geq 0.40$ ✓ → **Cooker passes the MNES test.**

---

### 3.4 Derivation of Concentration Ratio for a Parabolic Dish

A parabolic dish with rim angle $\theta_r$ concentrates sunlight onto a focal point. The geometric concentration ratio is:

$$C_g = \dfrac{A_a}{A_r}$$

For a circular dish of diameter $D$ and a focal spot of diameter $d_f$:

$$A_a = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}, \quad A_r = \dfrac{\pi d_f^2}{4}$$

$$C_g = \left(\dfrac{D}{d_f}\right)^2$$

The focal spot diameter is related to the rim angle by:

$$d_f = D \cdot \sin \theta_r$$

Hence:

$$\boxed{C_g = \dfrac{1}{\sin^2 \theta_r}}$$

For $\theta_r = 11.5°$ (typical Scheffler design): $C_g = 1/\sin^2(11.5°) = 1/0.0397 \approx 25.2$

For $\theta_r = 6°$ (deep dish): $C_g = 1/\sin^2(6°) = 1/0.0109 \approx 91.4$

> **Physical Note:** A rim angle of **90°** would mean a flat plate (no concentration, $C_g = 1$); a rim angle approaching **0°** would mean infinite concentration (impractical due to aberrations).

### 4.3 Layered Cross-Section Diagram — Box Cooker (ASCII Schematic)

```
        ↓ ↓ ↓ ↓ ↓ ↓ ↓    Solar Radiation (I)
       ┌─────────────────┐  ← Glass Cover (τ ≈ 0.88)
       │   ~~~~~~~~~~~   │  ← Trapped Air (Greenhouse Effect)
       ├─────────────────┤
       │ ▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓ │  ← Absorber Plate (α ≈ 0.95, black)
       │   ┌───────┐     │  ← Cooking Pot with Water (m = 2 kg)
       │   │ WATER │     │
       │   └───────┘     │
       ├─────────────────┤
       │ ############### │  ← Insulation (50 mm, k = 0.04 W/m·K)
       ├─────────────────┤
       │ ┼┼┼┼┼┼┼┼┼┼┼┼┼┼┼ │  ← Outer Casing (GI Sheet / Wood)
       └─────────────────┘
        ↑ Heat Losses  ↑    (Conduction + Convection + Radiation)
```

### 4.4 Stagnation Temperature Logic Flow

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> NoLoad: Cooker Placed in Sun
    NoLoad --> Absorbing: (ατ)·I·A_a absorbed
    Absorbing --> Heating: T_p rises
    Heating --> Plateau: Q_in = U_L·A_a·(T_p - T_a)
    Plateau --> Stagnation: Equilibrium reached
    Stagnation --> ReadT_P: Measure T_p with thermocouple
    ReadT_P --> CalcF1: F_1 = (T_p - T_a) / I
    CalcF1 --> CheckMNES: F_1 ≥ 0.12 ?
    CheckMNES --> Pass: Yes
    CheckMNES --> Fail: No — improve insulation / selective surface
    Pass --> [*]
    Fail --> [*]
```

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define the *First Figure of Merit* and *Second Figure of Merit* of a box-type solar cooker as per the MNES specification. State their minimum prescribed values.

**Model Answer:**

The **First Figure of Merit (F₁)** is a measure of the cooker's ability to convert incident solar radiation into absorber plate temperature rise at **stagnation** (no-load) condition.

$$F_1 = \dfrac{T_p - T_a}{I} \quad [\text{°C·m}^2/\text{W}]$$

The **Second Figure of Merit (F₂)** is the **thermal efficiency** of the cooker under a standard test load:

$$F_2 = \eta_{th} = \dfrac{m \, C_p \, (T_f - T_i)}{I \, A_a \, \Delta t}$$

**MNES minimum prescribed values:** $F_1 \geq 0.12 \text{ °C·m}^2/\text{W}$ and $F_2 \geq 0.40$ (i.e., 40%).

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** Why is a **selective surface coating** preferred over ordinary black paint for the absorber plate of a solar cooker?

**Model Answer:**

A **selective surface** has two distinct optical properties in different wavelength regions:
- **High solar absorptance** ($\alpha > 0.95$) in the short-wavelength solar spectrum (0.3–2.5 μm)
- **Low thermal emittance** ($\varepsilon < 0.15$) in the long-wavelength infrared region (2.5–50 μm)

Ordinary black paint has $\alpha \approx 0.95$ **but** $\varepsilon \approx 0.88$, which causes heavy **re-radiation loss** once the plate temperature exceeds 60 °C. Since solar cooker plates reach 100–150 °C, re-radiation becomes a dominant loss mode. A selective coating reduces this loss by a factor of **5–6×**, directly improving $F_1$ and $F_2$.

Examples: black nickel, black chrome, $\text{MoO}_3$-based cermet coatings.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Question (Module Internal Choice)

> **Question A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam — June 2023]**
>
> **(a)** Describe, with a neat sketch, the construction and working of a **box-type solar cooker**. Explain the role of each component. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A box-type solar cooker has an absorber area of 0.6 m². When tested, the water mass of 2 kg gets heated from 30 °C to 95 °C in 40 minutes. The average solar intensity during the test was 900 W/m². Calculate (i) the thermal efficiency of the cooker, and (ii) the useful cooking power. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

A box-type solar cooker is an **insulated rectangular box** with a transparent glass cover. Its main components are:

1. **Outer casing** — Provides mechanical strength; usually GI sheet (0.8 mm) or seasoned wood.
2. **Thermal insulation** — Glass wool, thermocol, or rock wool (thickness 40–60 mm, $k \approx 0.04$ W/m·K). Reduces conductive heat loss from bottom and sides.
3. **Inner absorber tray** — Made of aluminium or copper (high thermal conductivity); coated with black paint or selective surface for maximum absorption. Receives and conducts solar heat to the cooking pots.
4. **Cooking pots** — Aluminium vessels with a matte-black outer surface to absorb any transmitted radiation reaching the pot directly.
5. **Transparent cover (glazing)** — Single or double-pane tempered glass; allows short-wave solar radiation to pass while trapping long-wave IR (greenhouse effect).

**Working principle:** Solar radiation passes through the transparent cover and is absorbed by the blackened tray. The heat is conducted to the cooking pots containing food/water. Re-radiated long-wave radiation from the absorber is **trapped** by the cover (greenhouse effect), raising the internal temperature to **100–140 °C**, suitable for boiling, steaming, and slow cooking.

**Sketch:** Refer to **Section 4.3** (ASCII cross-section) of these notes.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:** $A_a = 0.6 \text{ m}^2$, $m = 2$ kg, $T_i = 30 \text{ °C}$, $T_f = 95 \text{ °C}$, $\Delta t = 40 \text{ min} = 2400$ s, $I = 900 \text{ W/m}^2$, $C_p = 4186 \text{ J/kg·K}$.

**(i) Thermal efficiency:**

$$F_2 = \eta_{th} = \dfrac{m \, C_p \, (T_f - T_i)}{I \, A_a \, \Delta t}$$

$$F_2 = \dfrac{2 \times 4186 \times (95 - 30)}{900 \times 0.6 \times 2400}$$

$$F_2 = \dfrac{2 \times 4186 \times 65}{1{,}296{,}000} = \dfrac{544{,}180}{1{,}296{,}000}$$

$$\boxed{F_2 \approx 0.420 \;\; \text{or} \;\; 42.0\%}$$

**(ii) Useful cooking power:**

$$\dot{Q}_u = \dfrac{m \, C_p \, (T_f - T_i)}{\Delta t} = \dfrac{544{,}180}{2400}$$

$$\boxed{\dot{Q}_u \approx 226.7 \text{ W}}$$

**Verification:** $F_2 = 0.42 \geq 0.40$ ✓ (Passes MNES spec).

---

> **Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative to Question A) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
>
> **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **parabolic dish-type solar cooker**. Derive the expression for its **geometric concentration ratio** in terms of rim angle. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A box-type solar cooker is tested under stagnation conditions. The plate temperature stabilizes at **135 °C** when the ambient is 32 °C and the average solar intensity is **780 W/m²**. Calculate the **First Figure of Merit** and the overall heat loss coefficient, assuming $(\alpha \tau) = 0.78$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

A **parabolic dish cooker** uses a paraboloidal reflective surface to concentrate parallel sun rays onto a focal point where the cooking vessel is suspended.

**Components:**
- Paraboloidal reflector (fibreglass-reinforced plastic or aluminium sheet, coated with high-reflectance material, $\rho \approx 0.85$)
- Tracking mechanism (manual or motorised) to maintain sun alignment throughout the day
- Cooking pot suspended at the focal point
- Stand / pedestal

**Working:** Parallel rays from the sun strike the dish and are reflected to converge at the focal point. The resulting concentration of solar flux raises the temperature at the focus to **400–700 °C**, enabling frying and roasting (not just boiling).

**Derivation of concentration ratio:**

For a paraboloidal dish of rim diameter $D$ and rim angle $\theta_r$ (angle between the optical axis and the line from focus to rim):

$$d_f = D \, \sin \theta_r$$

The aperture area: $A_a = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$

The receiver (focal spot) area: $A_r = \dfrac{\pi d_f^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi D^2 \sin^2 \theta_r}{4}$

Geometric concentration ratio:

$$\boxed{C_g = \dfrac{A_a}{A_r} = \dfrac{1}{\sin^2 \theta_r}}$$

For $\theta_r = 11.5°$: $C_g \approx 25$. For $\theta_r = 6°$: $C_g \approx 91$.

**Sketch:** Refer to **Section 4.2** (Mermaid schematic).

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:** $T_p = 135 \text{ °C}$, $T_a = 32 \text{ °C}$, $I = 780 \text{ W/m}^2$, $\alpha \tau = 0.78$.

**(i) First Figure of Merit:**

$$F_1 = \dfrac{T_p - T_a}{I} = \dfrac{135 - 32}{780} = \dfrac{103}{780}$$

$$\boxed{F_1 \approx 0.132 \text{ °C·m}^2/\text{W}}$$

This exceeds the MNES minimum of 0.12 ✓.

**(ii) Overall heat loss coefficient:**

Using $F_1 = \dfrac{\alpha \tau}{U_L}$:

$$U_L = \dfrac{\alpha \tau}{F_1} = \dfrac{0.78}{0.132}$$

$$\boxed{U_L \approx 5.91 \text{ W/m}^2\cdot\text{K}}$$

This is a typical value for a well-insulated single-glazed box cooker.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Unit trap in F₁**: Students often write $F_1$ in K/m² or °C/W (wrong). Correct: **°C·m²/W** (insolation is in W/m², so dividing a temperature by W/m² gives °C·m²/W).
> 2. **Time-unit trap in F₂**: $\Delta t$ in the efficiency formula **must be in seconds**, not minutes. Forgetting this gives an efficiency 60× too low.
> 3. **Plate vs water temperature**: $F_1$ uses **plate temperature**; $F_2$ uses **water temperature**. Mixing them costs full marks.
> 4. **Stagnation condition**: $F_1$ is measured **without water** (or with empty pot) — students often compute it under load, which is wrong.
> 5. **Selective vs black paint**: Students forget to mention the **wavelength-specific** nature of selective coatings. Always specify *high α for solar spectrum, low ε for IR*.
> 6. **MNES values must be stated numerically**: $F_1 \geq 0.12$, $F_2 \geq 0.40$ — vague answers like "F₁ must be high" lose marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & High-Yield Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this block for the night before the exam.**

- ☐ **Solar cookers** = solar thermal cooking devices; two families: **non-concentrating (box)** and **concentrating (dish, Scheffler)**.
- ☐ **Box cooker** operates on **greenhouse effect**; typical stagnation temperature = **100–140 °C**; no tracking needed.
- ☐ **Five components**: outer casing, insulation, absorber tray, cooking pots, transparent cover.
- ☐ **Selective surface** = high α (>0.95) for solar, low ε (<0.15) for IR — key to high F₁.
- ☐ **Insulation material**: glass wool / thermocol; thickness 40–60 mm; $k \approx 0.04$ W/m·K.
- ☐ **Glazing**: tempered glass, τ ≈ 0.85–0.90; single or double.
- ☐ **Stagnation energy balance**: $(\alpha \tau) \cdot I = U_L \cdot (T_p - T_a)$.
- ☐ **F₁ formula**: $F_1 = (T_p - T_a)/I = (\alpha \tau)/U_L \geq \mathbf{0.12}$ °C·m²/W (MNES).
- ☐ **F₂ formula**: $F_2 = m C_p \Delta T / (I A_a \Delta t) \geq \mathbf{0.40}$ (MNES).
- ☐ **Standard test**: 2 kg water, 30 °C → 95 °C, $\Delta t$ in seconds.
- ☐ **Useful heat for 2 kg water at 65 K rise**: ~544 kJ.
- ☐ **Heat loss coefficient** $U_L \approx 5$–$9$ W/m²·K for box cookers.
- ☐ **Parabolic dish** operates on **point-focus geometric concentration**; needs **daily tracking**.
- ☐ **Concentration ratio**: $C_g = 1/\sin^2 \theta_r$; typical range 20–100.
- ☐ **Stefan-Boltzmann constant**: $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W/m²·K⁴ (memorize).
- ☐ **BIS / IS 13429** governs testing protocol; MNES governs subsidy eligibility.
- ☐ **Energy conservation identity**: $\eta_{th} = U_L \cdot F_1 \cdot \left[1 - \dfrac{(T_p - T_a)}{(T_p - T_a)_{stg}}\right]$.
- ☐ **Scheffler community cooker** uses a fixed-focus, tilted-axis paraboloid; smaller dish area but constant focus — suited for institutional kitchens.
- ☐ **Typical cooking power** of a box cooker: 60–260 W.
- ☐ **No-load energy** of a 2 kg, 30 → 95 °C test ≈ 0.151 kWh.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Solar Furnace
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEEET721 - DESIGN OF SOLAR PV SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Solar Furnace # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Solar Furnace** is a high-concentration solar thermal device that harnesses and concentrates direct normal solar irradiance (DNI) onto a small focal area (receiver) using reflective optics, producing extremely high temperatures typically in the range of **1500 K to 4000 K**. It is classified as a *concentrating solar power (CSP)* technology under the family of *Point-Focus Solar Collectors*, and is primarily employed for high-temperature research, materials processing, metallurgy, ceramic synthesis, and thermochemical reactions. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A solar furnace is a non-imaging/imaging concentrator system that converts incident solar radiation into thermal energy at extremely high flux densities, characterized by a **concentration ratio (C)** substantially greater than unity (typically **C > 1000** for high-temperature solar furnaces). The fundamental governing equation that distinguishes a solar furnace from a flat-plate collector is the **geometric concentration ratio**: $$C = \frac{A_{aperture}}{A_{receiver}}$$ where $A_{aperture}$ is the effective light-collecting mirror area and $A_{receiver}$ is the focal/absorbing area. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine holding a magnifying glass on a bright sunny day and focusing its light onto a single dry leaf. Within seconds, the leaf begins to smoke and eventually catches fire. That tiny, intensely hot spot is the working principle of a solar furnace. The magnifying glass is the **concentrator**, the sunbeam is the **fuel**, and the leaf is the **receiver (target)**. A solar furnace is essentially a giant, precisely engineered version of that magnifying glass — but instead of a 5 cm lens, it uses a parabolic dish or an array of heliostats spanning tens of meters, capable of melting steel, vaporizing refractory oxides, and simulating re-entry conditions for spacecraft. > [!NOTE] > **Why does concentration produce heat?** The sun delivers a finite power per unit area (~**1361 W/m²** in space, ~**1000 W/m²** at the earth's surface on a clear day). By gathering sunlight over a *large* area and squeezing it into a *small* area, the power density (W/m²) at the focal spot is amplified by the concentration ratio, producing extreme local temperatures. ## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - **Solar Constant (I_sc):** 1361 W/m² - **Direct Normal Irradiance (DNI) at ground:** 800–1000 W/m² (clear sky) - **Sun's Blackbody Temperature (T_s):** 5778 K - **Stefan-Boltzmann Constant (σ):** 5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W/m²·K⁴ - **Concentration Ratio Range:** 100 (low-temp furnaces) to **50000+** (research-grade imaging furnaces like Odeillo, France) - **Peak Flux Density:** up to **16 MW/m²** at the focal spot of the largest furnaces > [!TIP] > **KTU Quick Recall Tip:** Always remember that a solar furnace is **NOT a solar PV system** — it produces **heat**, not electricity. However, the thermal energy it generates *can* drive a heat engine (Stirling, Rankine, Brayton) to produce electricity, in which case it becomes part of a **Solar Dish-Stirling** or **Solar Power Tower** system. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Physical Principle of Solar Concentration The operation of a solar furnace rests on three sequential physical processes: 1. **Collection:** Sunlight is intercepted over a large aperture using reflective (specular) surfaces — typically polished aluminum, silvered glass, or thin-film polymer mirrors. 2. **Concentration:** The reflected rays are converged toward a geometric focal point or a small focal zone using parabolic, ellipsoidal, or flat-heliostat optics. The optical path obeys the laws of reflection (angle of incidence = angle of reflection). 3. **Absorption & Conversion:** A receiver placed at the focal point absorbs the concentrated flux and converts it to thermal energy. For solar furnaces (as opposed to solar cookers), the receiver is usually a *cavity receiver* with a small aperture, minimizing convective and radiative re-losses. > [!IMPORTANT] > The **law of conservation of étendue (optical invariant)** states that the product of beam area and beam solid angle is invariant along an optical system. This is why *no optical system can produce a temperature at the receiver higher than the sun's surface temperature (5778 K)* — a hard physical limit. ## 2.2 Classification of Solar Furnaces Solar furnaces are categorized based on their concentrator geometry: | Type | Optics | Concentration Ratio (C) | Typical Use | |------|--------|--------------------------|-------------| | **Parabolic Dish Furnace** | Single paraboloid dish | 1000 – 10000 | Materials research, small-scale metallurgy | | **Heliostat-Field Furnace (Solar Tower)** | Flat heliostats + central tower receiver | 500 – 5000 | Industrial high-temp processes, hydrogen production | | **Imaging Lens Furnace** | Fresnel or imaging lens | 100 – 1000 | Laboratory-scale high-flux testing | | **Solar Furnace with Compound Parabolic Concentrator (CPC)** | Non-imaging CPC + secondary | 10 – 100 | Moderate-temperature applications | ## 2.3 Theoretical Maximum Temperature For an *ideal* solar furnace (perfect blackbody receiver, no convective losses, infinite solar surface as seen from the receiver), the maximum achievable absolute temperature at the receiver is given by: $$T_{max} = T_{sun} \cdot \sqrt[4]{C}$$ This equation emerges from equating the absorbed solar flux to the re-radiated blackbody flux from the receiver at thermal equilibrium. The fourth root arises because radiative power scales as T⁴ (Stefan-Boltzmann law). > [!NOTE] > In practice, T_max is much lower (~2000–3500 K) due to: (i) imperfect reflectivity of mirrors (ρ ≈ 0.85–0.95), (ii) cosine losses at the aperture, (iii) shading & blocking by structural elements, (iv) re-radiation and convection from the receiver, and (v) atmospheric attenuation between heliostats and tower. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---------|-------------|-------| | 1 | $C = A_a / A_r$ | Geometric concentration ratio | dimensionless | | 2 | $T_{max} = T_s \cdot C^{1/4}$ | Ideal max receiver temperature (blackbody) | K | | 3 | $Q_{abs} = I_{DNI} \cdot A_a \cdot \rho \cdot (\tau \alpha)$ | Useful absorbed heat flux | W | | 4 | $q'' = I_{DNI} \cdot C \cdot \eta_{opt}$ | Receiver flux density (W/m²) | W/m² | | 5 | $\eta_{opt} = \rho_{mirror} \cdot (1 - f_{shade}) \cdot (1 - f_{cos})$ | Optical efficiency | dimensionless | | 6 | $Q_{loss} = \sigma \cdot \varepsilon \cdot A_r \cdot T_r^4$ | Radiative re-loss from receiver | W | | 7 | $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{Q_{useful}}{Q_{abs}}$ | Thermal efficiency | dimensionless | | 8 | $\eta_{overall} = \eta_{opt} \cdot \eta_{th}$ | Overall furnace efficiency | dimensionless | | 9 | $P_{delivered} = I_{DNI} \cdot A_a \cdot C \cdot \eta_{overall}$ | Useful power at receiver | W | | 10 | $f_{blocking} = \dfrac{A_{blocked}}{A_{aperture}}$ | Fractional heliostat blocking loss | dimensionless | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Utility:** The concentration ratio C is the **single most important design parameter** for a solar furnace. Increasing C raises the maximum temperature but decreases the *tolerance* to optical aberrations, tracking errors, and mirror surface imperfections. The KTU examiner frequently tests the relationship $T_{max} \propto C^{1/4}$. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications - **Materials Research:** Phase-change studies of ultra-refractory ceramics (ZrO₂, Al₂O₃, SiC) at temperatures exceeding 2000 K. - **Metallurgy:** Solar reduction of metal oxides (e.g., ZnO → Zn, FeO → Fe) using carbothermic or electrolytic processes powered by solar heat. - **Aerospace Testing:** Simulation of re-entry heat flux on thermal protection systems (TPS). - **Hydrogen Production:** Solar thermochemical splitting of water (Cu-Cl cycle, ZnO/Zn cycle) at > 1800 K. - **Solar Updraft Tower & Dish-Stirling:** Integration of a solar furnace as the heat source for a closed-cycle gas engine. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Maximum Theoretical Receiver Temperature We start from the **first law of thermodynamics** applied to a control volume surrounding the receiver. At thermal steady-state equilibrium, the absorbed solar power equals the sum of useful power extracted plus all loss terms. **Step 1 — Define the absorbed solar flux at the receiver.** The sun behaves as a blackbody at $T_s = 5778$ K. The total radiant exitance from the sun's surface is: $$M_{sun} = \sigma \cdot T_s^4$$ **Step 2 — Express the solar flux intercepted by the concentrator aperture.** A concentrator of area $A_a$ intercepts the directional solar flux $I_{DNI}$ (W/m²). The total intercepted power is: $$Q_{in} = I_{DNI} \cdot A_a$$ **Step 3 — Apply the concentration ratio to find flux at the receiver.** If the concentrator focuses all intercepted light onto a receiver of area $A_r$, the geometric concentration ratio is: $$C = \frac{A_a}{A_r}$$ The flux density at the receiver is therefore: $$q''_{receiver} = \frac{Q_{in}}{A_r} = I_{DNI} \cdot \frac{A_a}{A_r} = I_{DNI} \cdot C$$ **Step 4 — Energy balance at the receiver at thermal equilibrium.** At the maximum equilibrium temperature, the receiver is assumed to be a perfect blackbody (emissivity $\varepsilon = 1$) and convection is neglected. The re-radiated flux is: $$q''_{re-rad} = \sigma \cdot T_r^4$$ Setting $q''_{receiver} = q''_{re-rad}$ (steady-state, no useful work extracted): $$I_{DNI} \cdot C = \sigma \cdot T_r^4$$ **Step 5 — Solve for the receiver temperature T_r.** $$T_r^4 = \frac{I_{DNI} \cdot C}{\sigma}$$ $$T_r = \left(\frac{I_{DNI} \cdot C}{\sigma}\right)^{1/4}$$ **Step 6 — Relate T_r to the sun's surface temperature.** Substitute $I_{DNI} = \sigma \cdot T_s^4 \cdot (\Omega_{sun}/\pi)$ (where $\Omega_{sun}$ is the solid angle subtended by the sun). After algebraic manipulation, this simplifies to the well-known result: $$T_{r,max} = T_s \cdot C^{1/4}$$ This is the **KTU-canonical formula** for the maximum theoretical temperature of a solar furnace. ## 3.2 Derivation: Thermal Efficiency of a Solar Furnace **Step 1 — Write the energy balance for the receiver in steady state.** $$Q_{absorbed} = Q_{useful} + Q_{re-radiated} + Q_{convective}$$ **Step 2 — Express each term.** $$I_{DNI} \cdot A_a \cdot \eta_{opt} = Q_{useful} + \sigma \cdot \varepsilon \cdot A_r \cdot T_r^4 + h_c \cdot A_r \cdot (T_r - T_{amb})$$ **Step 3 — Define thermal efficiency.** $$\eta_{th} = \frac{Q_{useful}}{Q_{absorbed}} = 1 - \frac{Q_{re-rad} + Q_{conv}}{Q_{absorbed}}$$ **Step 4 — Substitute $Q_{abs} = I_{DNI} \cdot A_a \cdot \eta_{opt}$ and $C = A_a / A_r$.** $$\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{\sigma \cdot \varepsilon \cdot T_r^4 + h_c (T_r - T_{amb})}{I_{DNI} \cdot C \cdot \eta_{opt}}$$ **Step 5 — Interpret physically.** As **C increases**, the denominator grows, so re-radiation losses become a smaller *fraction* of the absorbed energy — meaning **higher concentration ratios yield higher thermal efficiencies** for the same receiver temperature. ## 3.3 Numerical Example (KTU-Style) **Problem:** A parabolic dish solar furnace has an aperture diameter of **3 m** and a focal-spot receiver of diameter **30 mm**. The mirror reflectivity is **0.90**, the cosine factor is **0.95**, and the DNI is **950 W/m²**. Find: (a) The geometric concentration ratio. (b) The ideal maximum receiver temperature. (c) The absorbed heat flux at the receiver. (d) The thermal efficiency if the useful heat extracted is 4.5 kW. **Solution:** **(a) Concentration ratio:** $$A_a = \pi \cdot (1.5)^2 = 7.069 \text{ m}^2$$ $$A_r = \pi \cdot (0.015)^2 = 7.069 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$ $$C = \frac{A_a}{A_r} = \frac{7.069}{7.069 \times 10^{-4}} = 10000$$ **(b) Ideal maximum temperature:** $$T_{max} = T_s \cdot C^{1/4} = 5778 \cdot (10000)^{1/4} = 5778 \cdot 10 = 57780 \text{ K}$$ > [!WARNING] > This is an *ideal* value — physically impossible due to étendue conservation. Real furnaces at C = 10000 achieve ~3000–4000 K. **(c) Absorbed heat flux at the receiver:** $$\eta_{opt} = 0.90 \cdot 0.95 = 0.855$$ $$q''_{rec} = I_{DNI} \cdot C \cdot \eta_{opt} = 950 \cdot 10000 \cdot 0.855 = 8.12 \times 10^6 \text{ W/m}^2$$ **(d) Thermal efficiency:** $$Q_{abs} = I_{DNI} \cdot A_a \cdot \eta_{opt} = 950 \cdot 7.069 \cdot 0.855 = 5742 \text{ W}$$ $$\eta_{th} = \frac{4500}{5742} = 0.784 \text{ or } 78.4\%$$ # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of a Heliostat-Field Solar Furnace The diagram below maps the **functional architecture** of a typical solar tower furnace (the same class as the famous **Odeillo 1 MW solar furnace** in France and the **Weizmann Institute** furnace in Israel). ```mermaid flowchart TB SunNode["SUN
5778 K Blackbody Source"] --> DNIInput["Direct Normal Irradiance
DNI = 800-1000 W/m2"] DNIInput --> HelioField["Heliostat Field
Flat Tracking Mirrors
Area = 2000-10000 m2"] HelioField --> TrackingCtrl["Dual-Axis Tracking Control
Sun Sensor + PLC"] TrackingCtrl --> HelioField HelioField -->|Reflected Beams| TowerOptics["Tower-Top Secondary Optics
Compound Parabolic Concentrator"] TowerOptics -->|Converged Beam| CavityRecv["Cavity Receiver
Aperture = 0.1-0.5 m
Refractory Lined"] CavityRecv -->|Absorbed Flux| ThermalMass["Thermal Storage
SiC or ZrO2 Brick"] CavityRecv -->|Heat Loss| ReRadiate["Re-Radiation
Sigma * Epsilon * T^4"] CavityRecv -->|Convective Loss| ConvLoss["Natural Convection
h_c * A * Delta_T"] ThermalMass --> WorkBench["High-Temperature Workbench
Materials Testing / Metallurgy"] WorkBench --> EndUser["End Product
Refractory Ceramic / Pure Metal / H2 Gas"] ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Architecture of a Parabolic Dish Solar Furnace ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Paraboloidal Dish
Aperture = 3-7 m
Focal Length = 1-3 m"] -->|Concentrated Beam| B["Focal Point
Flux Density up to 16 MW/m2"] B --> C["Cavity Receiver
High-Temp Alloy or Ceramic"] C --> D["Heat Exchanger
Water or Gas"] D --> E["Thermal Storage
Phase Change Material"] E --> F["Stirling Engine
OR Process Heat Load"] F --> G["Electrical Output
OR Industrial Process"] A -.->|Tracking Actuator| H["Sun Tracking
Azimuth + Altitude"] H -.-> A C -.->|Losses| I["Convective + Radiative
Recycling via Cavity Geometry"] ``` ## 4.3 Concentration Ratio vs Application Mapping ```mermaid flowchart TB StartNode["Solar Furnace Design"] --> Ccalc["Calculate C = Aa / Ar"] Ccalc --> Cbranch{"C Value?"} Cbranch -->|C = 10 to 100| LowApp["Low-Temp Applications
Solar Cooking
Water Desalination
Process Steam"] Cbranch -->|C = 100 to 1000| MidApp["Mid-Temp Applications
Stirling Engine Drive
Air Heating
Thermochemical Low-Temp"] Cbranch -->|C = 1000 to 10000| HighApp["High-Temp Applications
Materials Research
Metallurgy
Ceramic Sintering"] Cbranch -->|C greater than 10000| ExtremeApp["Extreme-Temp Applications
Re-Entry Simulation
Solar Hydrogen Production
Plasma Generation"] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define a Solar Furnace. Mention any two practical applications. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 | Module 2]** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A solar furnace is a high-concentration solar thermal device that uses reflective optics (parabolic dish or heliostat field) to focus direct normal solar radiation onto a small receiver, achieving temperatures of 1500 K – 4000 K for high-temperature applications. *Applications (1 mark each, any two):* - High-temperature materials research and refractory ceramic processing. - Solar thermochemical hydrogen production via water-splitting cycles. ### Question 2: What is meant by geometric concentration ratio? State its significance. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 | Module 2]** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The geometric concentration ratio C is the ratio of the effective light-collecting aperture area to the area of the focal receiver: $$C = \frac{A_a}{A_r}$$ It is a dimensionless quantity that determines (i) the maximum achievable receiver temperature via $T_{max} = T_s \cdot C^{1/4}$, and (ii) the flux density amplification at the focal point, which in turn dictates the suitability of the furnace for specific high-temperature applications. --- ## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) ### Question A: Design Analysis of a Parabolic Dish Solar Furnace **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 | Module 2 | 14 Marks]** **(a) Derive the expression for the maximum theoretical temperature achievable at the receiver of an ideal solar furnace. State clearly the assumptions made. [7 marks]** **Model Solution:** *Assumptions:* 1. Receiver is a perfect blackbody ($\varepsilon = 1$). 2. No convective or conductive losses from the receiver. 3. The concentrator focuses all incident rays with no optical aberrations. 4. The sun is treated as a blackbody at $T_s = 5778$ K. 5. Steady-state thermal equilibrium at the receiver. *Derivation:* At thermal equilibrium, the absorbed solar power equals the re-radiated power from the receiver. $$I_{DNI} \cdot A_a = \sigma \cdot A_r \cdot T_r^4$$ Dividing both sides by $A_r$: $$I_{DNI} \cdot \frac{A_a}{A_r} = \sigma \cdot T_r^4$$ Substituting $C = A_a / A_r$: $$I_{DNI} \cdot C = \sigma \cdot T_r^4$$ Rearranging: $$T_r = \left(\frac{I_{DNI} \cdot C}{\sigma}\right)^{1/4}$$ Using the relation $I_{DNI} = \sigma T_s^4 \cdot (\Omega_{sun}/\pi)$, the expression reduces to: $$\boxed{T_{r,max} = T_s \cdot C^{1/4}}$$ This is the KTU-canonical fourth-root relationship. **(b) A solar furnace uses a parabolic dish of 5 m diameter. The receiver is a cavity of 50 mm diameter. The average DNI is 900 W/m², the mirror reflectivity is 0.88, and the cosine efficiency is 0.94. Calculate: (i) the concentration ratio, (ii) the maximum theoretical temperature, (iii) the absorbed heat flux at the receiver, and (iv) the optical efficiency. [7 marks]** **Model Solution:** **(i) Concentration ratio:** $$A_a = \pi \cdot (2.5)^2 = 19.635 \text{ m}^2$$ $$A_r = \pi \cdot (0.025)^2 = 1.963 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$ $$C = \frac{19.635}{1.963 \times 10^{-3}} = 10000$$ **(ii) Maximum theoretical temperature:** $$T_{max} = 5778 \cdot (10000)^{1/4} = 5778 \cdot 10 = 57780 \text{ K (ideal upper bound)}$$ Practical limit: ~3500 K. **(iii) Absorbed heat flux at the receiver:** $$\eta_{opt} = 0.88 \cdot 0.94 = 0.827$$ $$q''_{rec} = I_{DNI} \cdot C \cdot \eta_{opt} = 900 \cdot 10000 \cdot 0.827 = 7.44 \times 10^6 \text{ W/m}^2$$ **(iv) Optical efficiency:** $$\eta_{opt} = 0.827 \text{ or } 82.7\%$$ --- ### Question B: Optical Design & Efficiency of a Solar Furnace (ALTERNATIVE) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 | Module 2 | 14 Marks]** **(a) Explain with a neat block diagram the working of a heliostat-field solar furnace. List its main components. [7 marks]** **Model Solution:** A heliostat-field solar furnace consists of a large array of flat, computer-controlled mirrors (*heliostats*) that track the sun in two axes and reflect sunlight onto a fixed receiver mounted atop a tower. *Main Components:* - **Heliostat Field:** Array of flat, sun-tracking mirrors (typical total area: 2000–10000 m²). - **Dual-Axis Tracking System:** Each heliostat has azimuth and altitude actuators controlled by a sun-tracking algorithm (closed-loop with solar sensor). - **Central Tower:** A structural tower (20–60 m tall) supporting the secondary optics and receiver. - **Secondary Concentrator (optional):** A compound parabolic concentrator (CPC) at the tower-top to further boost concentration. - **Cavity Receiver:** A refractory-lined cavity with a small aperture to absorb the concentrated flux and minimize re-radiation. - **Cooling / Heat Extraction System:** For delivering process heat or driving a thermodynamic cycle. - **Control & Instrumentation:** PLC-based system for heliostat aiming, flux mapping, and safety interlocks. *Working Principle:* Each heliostat is aimed such that the *normal* to its mirror surface bisects the angle between the incoming sunray and the direction to the receiver. The reflected beam from each heliostat is thus directed at the receiver.
