The superposition of beams from all heliostats produces an intense concentrated flux at the focal point. **(b) A 50 m² solar furnace has a cavity receiver with a 0.1 m diameter aperture. The average absorbed flux is 8 MW/m². If the receiver radiates as a blackbody at 3000 K, determine: (i) the concentration ratio, (ii) the useful heat extracted (assume re-radiation is the only loss), and (iii) the thermal efficiency. [7 marks]** **Model Solution:** **(i) Concentration ratio:** $$A_r = \pi \cdot (0.05)^2 = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$ $$C = \frac{50}{7.854 \times 10^{-3}} = 6366$$ **(ii) Useful heat extracted:** $$Q_{abs} = q'' \cdot A_r = 8 \times 10^6 \cdot 7.854 \times 10^{-3} = 62832 \text{ W}$$ Re-radiated loss: $$Q_{loss} = \sigma \cdot A_r \cdot T_r^4 = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \cdot 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \cdot (3000)^4$$ $$Q_{loss} = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \cdot 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 8.1 \times 10^{13} = 36070 \text{ W}$$ Useful heat: $$Q_{useful} = Q_{abs} - Q_{loss} = 62832 - 36070 = 26762 \text{ W} \approx 26.76 \text{ kW}$$ **(iii) Thermal efficiency:** $$\eta_{th} = \frac{26762}{62832} = 0.426 \text{ or } 42.6\%$$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > - **Unit mismatch trap:** Students often forget to convert mm to m when computing areas. Always convert first. > - **Forgetting $\varepsilon$:** For a graybody receiver, multiply re-radiation by emissivity $\varepsilon$ (typically 0.8–0.9 for ceramics, 0.3–0.5 for polished metals). > - **Confusing C and T scaling:** The $T \propto C^{1/4}$ relationship is a *fourth root*, NOT a linear or square-root dependence. Doubling C only raises T_max by a factor of $2^{1/4} \approx 1.19$. > - **Ignoring optical losses:** Always subtract mirror reflectivity and cosine losses before computing the absorbed flux. > - **Sign convention:** Re-radiation is a *loss*; do not add it to $Q_{useful}$. The energy balance is $Q_{abs} = Q_{useful} + Q_{loss}$. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Solar Furnace** = High-concentration solar thermal device (C > 100) for ultra-high-temperature applications. - **Concentration Ratio:** $C = A_a / A_r$ — the single most important design parameter. - **Max Temperature (ideal):** $T_{max} = T_s \cdot C^{1/4}$ — fourth-root dependence. - **Optical Efficiency:** $\eta_{opt} = \rho \cdot (1 - f_{shade}) \cdot (1 - f_{cosine})$ - **Thermal Efficiency:** $\eta_{th} = Q_{useful} / Q_{abs}$ — depends on receiver design (cavity geometry, emissivity, insulation). - **Two Main Architectures:** Parabolic Dish (point focus) and Heliostat-Field Tower (distributed focus). - **Hard Physical Limit:** Receiver temperature cannot exceed the sun's surface temperature (5778 K) — étendue conservation. - **Realistic Operating Range:** 2000–3500 K for engineering-grade furnaces. - **Applications:** Materials research, metallurgy, ceramic processing, solar hydrogen, re-entry simulation. - **DNI Dependency:** Solar furnaces require *direct* (not diffuse) sunlight — hence unsuitable for overcast climates; best in arid, high-altitude regions (Rajasthan, Arizona, Atacama, Odeillo). - **Key Loss Mechanisms:** Mirror reflectivity, cosine loss, shading/blocking, re-radiation, convection, atmospheric attenuation.

topics.title: Solar Greenhouse
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEET721 - DESIGN OF SOLAR PV SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Solar Greenhouse

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Solar Greenhouse** (also called a *Solar-passive greenhouse* or *Sun-heated greenhouse*) is a specially designed enclosed structure that uses solar radiation as its primary energy input to create and maintain a microclimate suitable for plant growth, while integrating photovoltaic (PV) technology either as a power source or as a semi-transparent energy-generating roofing element.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A Solar Greenhouse is an energy-efficient structure that combines the **greenhouse effect** (trapping short-wave solar radiation and re-radiating long-wave infrared energy) with active/passive solar thermal collection and/or photovoltaic conversion, designed to optimize crop yield while minimizing auxiliary energy consumption.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**The Car-in-Sun Analogy:** Think about what happens when you park a closed car under direct sunlight. The inside of the car becomes significantly hotter than the outside air. This is exactly how a greenhouse works on a larger scale — the transparent roof/walls let sunlight in, but trap the heat that tries to escape.

**The Wallet Analogy for PV Integration:** Imagine a greenhouse whose glass roof is partially replaced with tinted, semi-transparent solar panels. The roof still lets some light through (for plants) but also "catches" a portion of sunlight and converts it into electricity — like a wallet that earns money while holding it. This is a **PV-integrated Greenhouse (PV-G)** or **Agrivoltaic Greenhouse**.

**The Sweater Analogy for Thermal Mass:** Just as a sweater keeps you warm by trapping body heat, a solar greenhouse uses **thermal mass** (water barrels, stone floors, concrete walls) to absorb solar heat during the day and release it at night, maintaining stable temperatures.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

- **Solar Constant (G_sc):** **1367 W/m²** (extraterrestrial radiation at top of atmosphere)
- **Peak Sun Hours (PSH):** **4–6 hours/day** in most of Kerala
- **Greenhouse Cover Transmissivity (τ):** **0.85–0.91** for standard glass; **0.60–0.80** for PE films
- **Thermal Transmittance (U-value):** **3.0–6.5 W/m²·K** for single-glazed greenhouses
- **Photosynthetically Active Radiation (PAR):** **400–700 nm** waveband
- **Optimal Day Temperature:** **21–27°C** for most crops
- **Optimal Night Temperature:** **16–18°C** for most crops
- **Optimal Relative Humidity (RH):** **60–80%**

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The term "Solar Greenhouse" in KTU 2024 Module 2 specifically covers the design of greenhouses that utilize **both** solar thermal energy (for heating/ventilation) **and** photovoltaic energy (for pumping, lighting, and automation).

## 1.4 Why Solar Greenhouses Matter in KTU Context

In Kerala's tropical climate:
- **High humidity** requires controlled ventilation
- **Monsoon shading** reduces natural light → PV with battery backup helps with supplemental grow-lighting
- **High solar insolation** (~5.0–5.5 kWh/m²/day) makes PV integration economically attractive
- **Coconut/areca/mixed cropping patterns** create demand for protected cultivation

---

## 1.5 Classification of Solar Greenhouses

| Type | Description | Energy Use |
|------|-------------|------------|
| **Passive Solar Greenhouse** | Uses only solar gain + thermal mass; no active heating | Minimal |
| **Active Solar Greenhouse** | Uses solar collectors + storage + backup heater | Moderate |
| **PV-Integrated Greenhouse** | Transparent or semi-transparent PV modules replace glass | Generates electricity |
| **Hybrid Solar Greenhouse** | Combines thermal + PV + biomass backup | Net-zero capable |

---

## 1.6 Architecture of a Typical Solar Greenhouse

A standard solar greenhouse consists of the following subsystems:

1. **Glazing/Cladding System** — Transparent cover (glass, polyethylene, polycarbonate, or PV modules)
2. **Structural Frame** — GI steel, aluminum, or treated wood
3. **Thermal Mass** — Water drums, rock beds, concrete north wall
4. **Ventilation System** — Roof vents, side vents, exhaust fans
5. **Heating System** — Solar air/water heaters + backup
6. **Shading System** — Internal/external shade nets
7. **PV Power System** — Panels, batteries, inverters, charge controllers
8. **Irrigation System** — Drip/sprinkler (often solar-pump driven)
9. **Control System** — Sensors + microcontroller (temperature, RH, soil moisture, light)

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When asked to "list the components of a solar greenhouse," present them in **functional groupings** (Energy capture, Storage, Control, Auxiliary) — examiners reward structured answers.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Greenhouse Effect — Physical Foundation

The greenhouse effect is the foundational physics of any solar greenhouse. The process occurs in three sequential steps:

### Step-by-Step Mechanism

1. **Short-wave solar radiation** (λ = 0.3–3 μm) passes through the transparent glazing with minimal absorption (transmissivity τ ≈ 0.85).
2. Inside surfaces (plants, soil, thermal mass) **absorb** this radiation and warm up.
3. These warm surfaces **re-radiate energy** as **long-wave infrared** (λ = 3–50 μm), for which the glazing is largely **opaque** (ε ≈ 0.05 emissivity for glass in LWIR range).
4. The trapped long-wave energy raises the internal air temperature.
5. Heat is lost only through **conduction** (frame, glazing), **convection** (ventilation), and **radiation** (re-emission).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Glass Traps Heat:** Glass is transparent to short-wave (visible) radiation but **opaque to long-wave (infrared) radiation** — this differential transmission is the entire physics of the greenhouse effect.

## 2.2 Energy Balance of a Solar Greenhouse

For a steady-state solar greenhouse, the **fundamental energy balance** is:

$$
Q_{solar} + Q_{heater} = Q_{conduction} + Q_{ventilation} + Q_{radiation} + Q_{evapotranspiration}
$$

Each term is derived as follows:

### 2.2.1 Solar Heat Gain (Q_solar)

$$
Q_{solar} = A_g \cdot I_t \cdot \tau_{glazing} \cdot \eta_{absorption}
$$

- $A_g$ = Floor area (m²)
- $I_t$ = Total solar insolation on tilted surface (W/m²)
- $\tau_{glazing}$ = Transmissivity of cover material
- $\eta_{absorption}$ = Fraction absorbed by interior surfaces (typically 0.6–0.7)

### 2.2.2 Conduction Heat Loss (Q_cond)

$$
Q_{cond} = \sum (U_i \cdot A_i) \cdot (T_{in} - T_{out})
$$

- $U_i$ = Overall heat transfer coefficient of element $i$ (W/m²·K)
- $A_i$ = Area of element $i$ (m²)
- $T_{in}, T_{out}$ = Inside and outside air temperatures (°C)

### 2.2.3 Ventilation Heat Loss (Q_vent)

$$
Q_{vent} = \dot{m}_{air} \cdot c_p \cdot (T_{in} - T_{out}) = \rho \cdot V \cdot n \cdot c_p \cdot (T_{in} - T_{out})
$$

- $\dot{m}_{air}$ = Mass flow rate of ventilation air (kg/s)
- $c_p$ = Specific heat of air = **1.005 kJ/kg·K**
- $\rho$ = Air density = **1.2 kg/m³**
- $V$ = Greenhouse volume (m³)
- $n$ = Air changes per hour (ACH) — typically **1–2 ACH** for winter, **20–30 ACH** for summer

### 2.2.4 Radiation Heat Loss (Q_rad)

$$
Q_{rad} = A_{surface} \cdot \varepsilon \cdot \sigma \cdot (T_{in}^4 - T_{sky}^4)
$$

- $\varepsilon$ = Emissivity of cover (≈ 0.9 for glass)
- $\sigma$ = Stefan-Boltzmann constant = **5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W/m²·K⁴**
- $T_{sky}$ = Effective sky temperature (K) — often taken as $T_{out} - 6°C$ in humid tropics

### 2.2.5 Evapotranspiration Loss (Q_ET)

$$
Q_{ET} = A_g \cdot ET \cdot \lambda_{water}
$$

- $ET$ = Evapotranspiration rate (kg/m²·s)
- $\lambda_{water}$ = Latent heat of vaporization = **2.45 MJ/kg** at 20°C

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $Q_{solar} = A_g \cdot I_t \cdot \tau \cdot \eta$ | Solar heat gain | W |
| 2 | $Q_{cond} = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$ | Conduction loss through envelope | W |
| 3 | $Q_{vent} = \rho \cdot V \cdot n \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Ventilation heat loss | W |
| 4 | $Q_{rad} = \varepsilon \cdot \sigma \cdot A \cdot (T_{in}^4 - T_{sky}^4)$ | Radiative loss to sky | W |
| 5 | $Q_{ET} = A_g \cdot ET \cdot \lambda_w$ | Evapotranspiration loss | W |
| 6 | $\text{COP}_{solar} = \frac{Q_{useful}}{Q_{auxiliary}}$ | Coefficient of Performance | – |
| 7 | $SHR = \frac{Q_{sensible}}{Q_{sensible} + Q_{latent}}$ | Sensible Heat Ratio | – |
| 8 | $P_{PV,required} = \frac{E_{daily}}{\eta_{inv} \cdot \eta_{bat} \cdot PSH}$ | PV sizing equation | W |
| 9 | $C_{storage} = E_{daily} \cdot \text{DOD}^{-1} \cdot \text{Autonomy}$ | Battery capacity | Wh or Ah |
| 10 | $\text{Performance Ratio} = \frac{Y_f}{Y_r}$ | PV system performance | – |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Pitfall:** In the energy balance equation, students often **forget the evapotranspiration term** in tropical greenhouses (Kerala). In humid climates, Q_ET can be **30–50% of total losses** due to high transpiration rates.

## 2.4 Heating Requirement Calculation

The peak heating load determines the size of the backup heater (and indirectly the PV system size):

$$
Q_{heat,max} = U_{total} \cdot A_g \cdot (T_{in,design} - T_{out,min})
$$

where $U_{total} \cdot A_g$ is the **overall heat loss factor** typically:
- **6.0–8.0 W/K per m²** of floor area for a single-glazed greenhouse
- **3.5–4.5 W/K per m²** for a double-glazed greenhouse

## 2.5 Ventilation Requirement Calculation

For temperature control, the required air change rate is:

$$
\dot{V}_{vent} = \frac{Q_{solar,excess}}{\rho \cdot c_p \cdot (T_{in,set} - T_{out})}
$$

where $Q_{solar,excess}$ is the solar heat gain in excess of plant requirements.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Protected cultivation** of high-value crops (capsicum, orchids, gerbera) in Kerala's humid tropics
- **Net-zero energy greenhouses** in R&D projects (IISR, KFRI, CPCRI Kasaragod)
- **Cold-chain agriculture** for export-grade produce
- **Rooftop greenhouses** in urban Kerala apartments integrating building-integrated PV (BIPV)

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked-Out Derivation: Sizing a Solar Greenhouse for Kerala

### Problem Statement (Typical KTU-style):

> Design a solar greenhouse of floor area 50 m² located in Thrissur, Kerala. The design outdoor minimum temperature is **18°C**, indoor set-point is **24°C**, and the average daily solar insolation on the horizontal is **5.2 kWh/m²/day**. The cover is single-glazed glass with τ = 0.85. Determine:
> (a) The peak heating load
> (b) The annual heating energy required (assume 60 heating days)
> (c) The PV array size to meet auxiliary loads (fans + pump + lights) totaling 1.5 kWh/day

---

### Part (a) — Peak Heating Load

**Given:**
- $A_g = 50 \text{ m}^2$
- $T_{in} = 24°C$, $T_{out,min} = 18°C$
- $U \cdot A_g$ factor (single glass) = 7.0 W/K·m² (assumed for Kerala)
- Therefore $U_{total} \cdot A_g = 7.0 \times 50 = 350$ W/K

**Calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{heat,max} &= U_{total} \cdot A_g \cdot (T_{in} - T_{out,min}) \\[6pt]
Q_{heat,max} &= 350 \times (24 - 18) \\[6pt]
Q_{heat,max} &= 350 \times 6 \\[6pt]
Q_{heat,max} &= 2100 \text{ W} = 2.1 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** Peak heating load = **2.1 kW**

---

### Part (b) — Annual Heating Energy

**Assumption:** Average ΔT during heating days = **4°C** (instead of peak 6°C).

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{avg} &= 350 \times 4 = 1400 \text{ W} = 1.4 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

Assuming 8 effective heating hours per day:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{heat,daily} &= 1.4 \text{ kW} \times 8 \text{ h} = 11.2 \text{ kWh/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

For 60 heating days:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{heat,annual} &= 11.2 \times 60 = 672 \text{ kWh/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** Annual heating energy = **672 kWh/year**

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic:** Multiplying average power (kW) by hours of operation (h) yields energy in kWh — this is the standard **power × time = energy** relationship used in all thermal system designs.

---

### Part (c) — PV Array Size

**Given:**
- Daily load: $E_{daily} = 1.5$ kWh/day
- Peak Sun Hours (PSH) in Thrissur ≈ **4.8 h/day**
- Inverter efficiency $\eta_{inv} = 0.95$
- Battery efficiency $\eta_{bat} = 0.85$
- Battery Depth-of-Discharge (DOD) = **0.8**
- Days of autonomy = **2 days**

**Step 1: PV Array Sizing**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{PV} &= \frac{E_{daily}}{\eta_{inv} \cdot \eta_{bat} \cdot PSH} \\[6pt]
P_{PV} &= \frac{1500}{0.95 \times 0.85 \times 4.8} \\[6pt]
P_{PV} &= \frac{1500}{3.876} \\[6pt]
P_{PV} &= 387.0 \text{ W} \approx 400 \text{ W}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Battery Capacity Sizing**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{battery} &= \frac{E_{daily} \times \text{Autonomy}}{DOD \times V_{system}} \\[6pt]
C_{battery} &= \frac{1500 \times 2}{0.8 \times 12} \\[6pt]
C_{battery} &= \frac{3000}{9.6} = 312.5 \text{ Ah} \approx 320 \text{ Ah}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** PV Array = **400 W** (e.g., 2 × 200 W panels), Battery = **12 V, 320 Ah** (or equivalent lithium bank)

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Trick:** Examiners love "round-up" rounding — always round PV size **up** to the nearest standard module size (e.g., 200 W, 250 W, 330 W, 400 W). A 387 W requirement translates to **2 × 200 W modules** (400 W), not 1.5 modules.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Cover Material Selection

| Property | Glass | Polycarbonate (Twin-wall) | Polyethylene Film | PV Module (Semi-transparent) |
|----------|-------|---------------------------|-------------------|-------------------------------|
| τ (visible) | 0.85–0.91 | 0.78–0.83 | 0.80–0.88 | 0.30–0.60 |
| τ (PAR) | 0.85 | 0.75 | 0.75 | 0.20–0.45 |
| U-value (W/m²·K) | 5.8 | 3.4 | 6.0 | 5.5 |
| Lifespan (years) | 25+ | 10–15 | 3–4 | 25+ |
| Cost (₹/m²) | 800–1500 | 600–1200 | 50–100 | 2500–5000 |
| Weight | Heavy | Light | Very Light | Heavy |
| Best Use | Premium | Premium-Mid | Budget | Power-Generating |

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: PV Greenhouse Configuration Options

| Configuration | Description | Pros | Cons |
|--------------|-------------|------|------|
| **Roof-integrated PV** | PV on south roof, glass on north | Maximum power | Uneven PAR distribution |
| **Spaced PV (Strip)** | PV strips alternate with transparent strips | Better light uniformity | Complex structure |
| **Tilted overhead PV** | PV tilted, light reaches plants via reflection | High efficiency | Complex optics |
| **Side-wall PV** | PV on west/east walls | Doesn't shade crops | Limited area |
| **Movable PV array** | Tracking PV that moves to follow sun | Maximum control | Expensive |

---

## 3.4 Mathematical Analysis: Optimum Tilt Angle for Kerala

For Kerala (latitude φ ≈ 10°N), the optimum tilt for **year-round solar collection** is:

$$
\beta_{opt} = \phi - 15° \approx -5° \text{ (slightly south-facing or flat)}
$$

For **winter collection** (Nov–Feb in Kerala = NE monsoon):

$$
\beta_{opt,winter} = \phi + 15° = 25° \text{ (steeper south tilt)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Kerala Climate Note:** Kerala's solar geometry differs from North India — the sun is closer to zenith year-round, so a **near-horizontal (or slightly south-tilted)** collector is often optimal for year-round operation.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Solar Greenhouse System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Solar Radiation 1367 W/m²] --> B[Glazing System]
    A --> C[PV Module Array]
    B --> D[Internal Air Space]
    C --> E[PV Charge Controller]
    D --> F[Thermal Mass Storage]
    F --> G[Heat Release at Night]
    D --> H[Plants and Crops]
    H --> I[Evapotranspiration Cycle]
    I --> J[Humidity Control Vent]
    D --> K[Ventilation System]
    K --> L[Exhaust Fan 75W]
    E --> M[Battery Bank 12V 320Ah]
    M --> N[Inverter 1kVA]
    N --> O[DC and AC Loads]
    L --> O
    P[Sensor Network Temp RH Soil] --> Q[Microcontroller]
    Q --> K
    Q --> R[Water Pump 50W Drip]
    R --> O
    N --> R
```

## 4.2 Process Flow: Energy Conversion Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Sunlight Photons] --> B[PV Conversion 18 percent]
    A --> C[Thermal Conversion 70 percent]
    B --> D[DC Electricity 12V 24V]
    D --> E[Battery Storage]
    E --> F[Inverter DC to AC]
    F --> G[AC Loads Fans Pumps Lights]
    C --> H[Heat Stored in Thermal Mass]
    H --> I[Nighttime Heat Release]
    I --> J[Maintained Plant Temperature]
    J --> K[Optimal Crop Growth]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Control System Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Level 1 Sensors] --> B[Microcontroller Arduino or Raspberry Pi]
    B --> C[Level 2 Decision Logic]
    C --> D[Ventilation Fan Relay]
    C --> E[Heater Relay]
    C --> F[Water Pump Relay]
    C --> G[Shade Curtain Motor]
    C --> H[Alert and Data Logging]
    H --> I[Cloud or Local Display]
    D --> J[Actuators Field Devices]
    E --> J
    F --> J
    G --> J
```

## 4.4 Schematic: PV-Greenhouse Power Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[PV Panel 200W x 2] --> CC[MPPT Charge Controller 30A]
    CC --> BAT[Battery 12V 320Ah]
    BAT --> INV[Pure Sine Inverter 1000W]
    INV --> L1[Ventilation Fan 75W]
    INV --> L2[Drip Pump 50W]
    INV --> L3[Grow Lights 100W LED]
    INV --> L4[Control Electronics 20W]
    BAT -.-> DC[Direct DC Loads]
    DC --> S1[Sensors 5W]
    DC --> S2[Microcontroller 3W]
```

## 4.5 Component Layout (Conceptual 2D Block)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph N[North Side]
        NW1[North Wall Opaque Insulated]
    end
    subgraph E[East Side]
        EW1[East Wall Glass Vent]
    end
    subgraph W[West Side]
        WW1[West Wall Glass Vent]
    end
    subgraph S[South Side]
        SW1[South Roof Sloped PV Modules]
        SW2[South Roof Transparent Strips]
    end
    subgraph C[Center]
        CW1[Cultivation Beds]
        CW2[Pathway]
        CW3[Water Drums Thermal Mass]
    end
    N --> C
    E --> C
    W --> C
    S --> C
```

## 4.6 Daily Operation Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[06:00 Sunrise] --> B[Ventilators Open]
    B --> C[09:00 PV Charges Battery]
    C --> D[12:00 Peak Heat Ventilation Max]
    D --> E[15:00 Thermal Mass Charges]
    E --> F[18:00 Sunset Vents Close]
    F --> G[21:00 Thermal Mass Releases Heat]
    G --> H[24:00 Heater Backup if Needed]
    H --> I[06:00 Next Day Cycle]
```

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Solar Greenhouse and list its major components. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

A **Solar Greenhouse** is a controlled-environment agricultural structure that uses solar energy (both thermal and photovoltaic) to maintain optimal growing conditions for plants, while minimizing auxiliary energy consumption.

**Major Components (5 minimum required for full marks):**

1. Transparent cover / glazing system (glass, PE film, or semi-transparent PV)
2. Structural frame (GI steel / aluminum)
3. Thermal mass (water drums, rock bed, concrete floor)
4. Ventilation system (roof vents, exhaust fans)
5. PV power system (modules, battery, inverter)
6. Irrigation system (drip / sprinkler)
7. Control & monitoring sensors (temperature, humidity, light)

> [!TIP]
> **Mark Distribution (3 marks):** Definition (1 mark) + Any 4 components with one-line description (2 marks = 0.5 each).

---

### Question 2: Explain the greenhouse effect with reference to the transmissivity of glass. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

The **greenhouse effect** is the physical phenomenon where a transparent medium (typically glass) allows short-wave solar radiation to pass through but absorbs/re-emits long-wave infrared radiation, thereby trapping thermal energy inside the enclosure.

- **Short-wave transmissivity** of glass in the 0.3–3 μm range: **τ ≈ 0.85–0.91**
- **Long-wave transmissivity** of glass in the 3–50 μm range: **τ ≈ 0.05 (effectively opaque)**

This **spectral selectivity** — high transmission for visible light, low transmission for infrared — is the core of the greenhouse effect and the reason glass is the traditional covering material.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (Option 1): Design of Solar Greenhouse `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) Derive the energy balance equation of a solar greenhouse and explain each term. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The steady-state energy balance of a solar greenhouse is:

$$
Q_{solar} + Q_{heater} + Q_{occupants} = Q_{cond} + Q_{vent} + Q_{rad} + Q_{ET} + Q_{stored}
$$

**Term-by-Term Explanation:**

| Term | Formula | Physical Meaning |
|------|---------|------------------|
| $Q_{solar}$ | $A_g \cdot I_t \cdot \tau \cdot \eta$ | Solar radiation entering through cover |
| $Q_{heater}$ | From backup | Auxiliary heating input |
| $Q_{cond}$ | $U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$ | Conduction loss through walls, roof, floor |
| $Q_{vent}$ | $\rho \cdot V \cdot n \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Heat carried away by ventilation air |
| $Q_{rad}$ | $\varepsilon \cdot \sigma \cdot A \cdot (T^4_{in} - T^4_{sky})$ | Long-wave radiation loss to sky |
| $Q_{ET}$ | $A_g \cdot ET \cdot \lambda_w$ | Latent heat consumed in transpiration |
| $Q_{stored}$ | $m \cdot c_p \cdot dT/dt$ | Heat stored in thermal mass (transient) |

At steady-state, $Q_{stored} = 0$ and the equation simplifies to the standard form taught in Module 2.

---

**(b) A solar greenhouse of 40 m² floor area in Kochi (lat 9.9°N) has the following data: $T_{in} = 25°C$, $T_{out,min} = 19°C$, $U \cdot A_g$ factor = 6.5 W/K·m², daily solar gain = 4.8 kWh, daily heat loss = 9.6 kWh. Calculate: (i) peak heating load, (ii) daily net energy deficit, (iii) backup heater rating needed for 6 hours of operation. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Peak Heating Load:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{heat,max} &= U_{total} \cdot A_g \cdot \Delta T_{max} \\
&= (6.5 \times 40) \times (25 - 19) \\
&= 260 \times 6 = 1560 \text{ W} = 1.56 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Daily Net Energy Deficit:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{deficit} &= E_{loss} - E_{gain} = 9.6 - 4.8 = 4.8 \text{ kWh/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Backup Heater Rating:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{heater} &= \frac{E_{deficit}}{t_{operation}} \\
&= \frac{4.8 \text{ kWh}}{6 \text{ h}} = 0.8 \text{ kW} = 800 \text{ W}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students often confuse **peak heating load** (W) with **daily energy deficit** (Wh). They are different — peak load is instantaneous power needed on the coldest day, while energy deficit is integrated over 24 hours. Many students also forget to convert ΔT_max to its design value (e.g., T_in design vs T_out,min). Always show units throughout — marks are deducted for unit-less intermediate steps.

---

### Question B (Option 2): PV-Integrated Greenhouse `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) Discuss the integration of photovoltaic systems with greenhouses. List three configuration types and explain the trade-off between light transmission and power generation. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

PV integration in greenhouses (also called **Agrivoltaic Greenhouses** or **PV-Greenhouse**) is the practice of replacing or augmenting conventional glazing with photovoltaic modules.

**Three Configuration Types:**

1. **Roof-integrated opaque PV** — Standard opaque PV modules replace the entire south roof. Power generation is maximum (η ≈ 18%), but PAR reaching plants is reduced by 50–70%. Suitable for shade-loving crops (orchids, leafy greens, medicinal plants).

2. **Semi-transparent PV modules** — Specially fabricated thin-film (a-Si, CdTe, OPV) or crystalline silicon with spaced cells allow 20–45% PAR transmission while generating 60–100 W/m². Trade-off: higher transparency = lower efficiency.

3. **Strip / Spaced PV array** — Alternating strips of conventional PV and transparent glass. Provides uniform light distribution. The spacing ratio (PV width / glass width) is optimized to balance light (PAR) and power.

**Trade-off Analysis:**

The fundamental trade-off is governed by the **Light-Power Trade-off Coefficient (LPTC)**:

$$
LPTC = \frac{E_{generated}}{E_{transmitted}} = \frac{P_{PV} \cdot A_{PV}}{I_t \cdot \tau_{glass} \cdot A_{trans}}
$$

A higher LPTC means more power per unit of light sacrificed. For Kerala's high-PAR environment, an LPTC of 0.3–0.5 is typically optimal.

---

**(b) A 60 m² greenhouse in Palakkad requires 2.5 kWh/day of electrical energy for fans, pumps, and control. Design the PV system assuming PSH = 5.0 h, $\eta_{inv}$ = 0.94, $\eta_{bat}$ = 0.88, autonomy = 1.5 days, DOD = 0.8, system voltage = 24 V. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $E_{daily} = 2.5$ kWh/day = 2500 Wh/day
- $PSH = 5.0$ h
- $\eta_{inv} = 0.94$, $\eta_{bat} = 0.88$
- Autonomy = 1.5 days
- $DOD = 0.8$
- $V_{sys} = 24$ V

**Step 1: PV Array Sizing**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{PV} &= \frac{E_{daily}}{\eta_{inv} \cdot \eta_{bat} \cdot PSH} \\
P_{PV} &= \frac{2500}{0.94 \times 0.88 \times 5.0} \\
P_{PV} &= \frac{2500}{4.136} \\
P_{PV} &= 604.4 \text{ W} \approx 600 \text{ W}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Practical choice:** 2 × 330 W modules (660 W total) — gives 9% margin.

**Step 2: Battery Bank Sizing**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{battery} &= \frac{E_{daily} \times \text{Autonomy}}{DOD \times V_{sys}} \\
C_{battery} &= \frac{2500 \times 1.5}{0.8 \times 24} \\
C_{battery} &= \frac{3750}{19.2} = 195.3 \text{ Ah}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Practical choice:** 2 × 12 V, 200 Ah batteries in series (24 V, 200 Ah).

**Step 3: Charge Controller Sizing**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{sc,total} &= \frac{P_{PV}}{V_{sys}} \times 1.25 \text{ (safety factor)} \\
I_{sc,total} &= \frac{660}{24} \times 1.25 = 34.4 \text{ A}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Practical choice:** 40 A MPPT charge controller.

**Step 4: Inverter Sizing**

For running the largest single load (assume 0.5 HP pump = 373 W) + safety factor:

$$
P_{inv} = 373 \times 1.25 = 466 \text{ W} \rightarrow \text{round up to } 1000 \text{ VA pure sine wave}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Three common mistakes that cost marks in PV sizing problems:
> 1. **Forgetting the safety factor (1.25)** for charge controller sizing — direct short-circuit current can damage MPPT.
> 2. **Not specifying module count and series/parallel combination** — examiners expect explicit module selection (e.g., "2 × 330 W in parallel").
> 3. **Mixing up battery Wh and Ah** — always divide by system voltage to get Ah. Mark loss is severe if units are wrong.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD REVISION CHECKLIST (Read before exam):**

- **Greenhouse effect** = differential transmission of short-wave (high τ) and long-wave (low τ) radiation through glass. Glass τ_visible ≈ 0.85, τ_IR ≈ 0.05.

- **Energy balance** must include ALL terms: solar gain, conduction, ventilation, radiation, evapotranspiration, and thermal storage. In Kerala's humid climate, **Q_ET is often the dominant loss term** (30–50% of total).

- **Peak heating load formula:** $Q_{heat,max} = U \cdot A_g \cdot \Delta T_{max}$ — used to size the backup heater.

- **Ventilation load formula:** $Q_{vent} = \rho \cdot V \cdot n \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ — used to size the exhaust fan.

- **Standard U · A_g factors to remember:**
  - Single glass: **6.0–8.0 W/K per m² floor area**
  - Double glass/polycarbonate: **3.5–4.5 W/K per m²**
  - PE film (single): **7.0–9.0 W/K per m²**

- **PV sizing equation:** $P_{PV} = \dfrac{E_{daily}}{\eta_{inv} \cdot \eta_{bat} \cdot PSH}$ — never forget the three efficiencies.

- **Battery sizing equation:** $C_{Ah} = \dfrac{E_{daily} \times \text{Autonomy}}{DOD \times V_{sys}}$ — always divide by V_sys to get Ah.

- **Stefan-Boltzmann constant σ = 5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W/m²·K⁴** — required for radiation loss calculation.

- **Latent heat of vaporization of water λ = 2.45 MJ/kg** at 20°C — used in evapotranspiration.

- **Kerala-specific facts:** PSH = 4.5–5.5 h/day; latitude ≈ 8–12°N; optimum tilt for winter ≈ 25°; year-round tilt ≈ 0–10°.

- **PV-Greenhouse trade-off** is governed by Light-Power Trade-off Coefficient (LPTC). Recommended LPTC for Kerala: **0.3–0.5**.

- **Major components** (always present in this order): Glazing, Frame, Thermal Mass, Ventilation, Heater, PV System, Irrigation, Controls.

- **Key crops for Kerala solar greenhouses:** Capsicum, orchid (Dendrobium), gerbera, carnation, cucumber, tomato (high-value, protected cultivation).

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Strategy:** For 14-mark design questions, structure your answer as: (1) Given data, (2) Assumptions (state explicitly), (3) Step-by-step calculation with units, (4) Practical component selection, (5) Summary table with all sized components. This structure matches KTU's marking scheme and secures full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design of Solar Water Heater
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEET721 - DESIGN OF SOLAR PV SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Design of Solar Water Heater

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

A **Solar Water Heater (SWH)** is a thermal conversion device that captures incident solar radiation and transfers the absorbed thermal energy to a working fluid (typically water) for domestic, commercial, or industrial hot water applications. It is formally classified as an **Active Solar Thermal System** when equipped with pumps/controllers, or a **Passive Solar Thermal System** when relying on natural thermosiphon circulation.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme, the design of a solar water heater is treated as a *coupled heat-transfer and fluid-flow problem*, governed by the energy balance between **incoming solar radiation**, **collector heat losses**, and **useful heat delivered** to a stratified storage tank.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 2)**
> Design of solar water heater includes: (i) selection of collector type, (ii) sizing of collector area, (iii) sizing of storage tank capacity, (iv) determination of mass flow rate, and (v) estimation of system efficiency using the **Hottel–Whillier–Bliss (HWB) equation**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Greenhouse Bucket"

Imagine placing a black-painted bucket of water inside a transparent glass box and leaving it in the sun. The black surface absorbs sunlight and warms the water, while the glass traps the heat (greenhouse effect) and prevents it from being blown away by wind. A **solar water heater** is essentially this same idea, but engineered with:
- A **selective absorber coating** (instead of paint) for high absorptance ($\alpha \approx 0.95$),
- **Low-iron tempered glass** (instead of a windowpane) for high transmittance ($\tau \approx 0.91$),
- **Insulated back and sides** to suppress rear and edge losses,
- **Channels (risers/header)** to carry the heated water away,
- A **storage tank** to retain the hot water after sunset.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Memorize these for KTU exams)**
> - Density of water: $\rho_w = 1000 \ \text{kg/m}^3$
> - Specific heat of water: $c_p = 4.186 \ \text{kJ/(kg·K)}$
> - 1 Litre of water $\equiv 1 \ \text{kg}$
> - Standard hot water delivery temperature (KTU design): $T_{hot} = 60^\circ\text{C}$
> - Standard cold water inlet temperature: $T_{cold} = 25^\circ\text{C}$
> - 1 kWh $= 3600 \ \text{kJ} = 3.6 \ \text{MJ}$

### 1.3 Classification of Solar Water Heaters (KTU Perspective)

| Class | Sub-Type | Operating Temperature Range | Typical Efficiency |
|:---|:---|:---:|:---:|
| **Flat Plate Collector (FPC)** | Direct (thermosiphon) | 50 – 80 °C | 45 – 60 % |
| | Indirect (heat-pipe / glycol) | 60 – 100 °C | 40 – 55 % |
| **Evacuated Tube Collector (ETC)** | Heat-pipe type | 60 – 120 °C | 50 – 70 % |
| | Thermosiphon / Sydney tube | 50 – 90 °C | 45 – 60 % |
| **Concentrating Collector** | Parabolic trough / CPC | 100 – 300 °C | 55 – 75 % |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Hot Tip:** For Kerala (tropical, ~12.5 MJ/m²/day average), FPC and ETC are both commercially viable. ETCs outperform FPCs during cloudy/monsoon conditions due to the vacuum insulation around each tube.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Working Principle — The Six-Step Logic Chain

A flat plate solar water heater operates through the following sequential energy-transfer steps:

1. **Solar Transmittance:** Sunlight ($\sim 0.3 – 2.5 \ \mu\text{m}$) passes through the glass cover. A fraction $(\tau)$ is transmitted, the rest is reflected/absorbed by glass.
2. **Selective Absorption:** The transmitted radiation strikes the absorber plate (coated with black nickel, cermet, or black chrome), which absorbs a fraction $(\alpha)$ of the incident energy.
3. **Conduction through Absorber:** Heat conducts through the metal plate thickness to the bonded tube/header system.
4. **Convection to Fluid:** Heat is transferred by forced convection from the tube wall to the circulating water.
5. **Heat Loss Mechanisms:** Simultaneously, the absorber loses heat by:
   - **Top loss** $(U_t)$ — convection + radiation from plate to glass to ambient.
   - **Bottom loss** $(U_b)$ — conduction through insulation to ambient.
   - **Side/edge loss** $(U_e)$ — conduction through casing edges.
6. **Storage Stratification:** Heated water rises to the top of the storage tank, drawing cooler water in (thermosiphon), creating a thermally stratified profile.

### 2.2 The Core Energy Balance

The instantaneous useful heat gain by the working fluid is the difference between the **absorbed solar energy** and the **total thermal losses** from the collector:

$$Q_{u} = A_c \, F_R \, \left[ S - U_L \, (T_{R,in} - T_a) \right]$$

where

- $S = (\tau\alpha) \, I_a$ — absorbed solar radiation per unit collector area $[\text{W/m}^2]$,
- $I_a$ — total incident solar radiation on tilted collector surface $[\text{W/m}^2]$,
- $A_c$ — gross collector area $[\text{m}^2]$,
- $F_R$ — **heat removal factor** (dimensionless, $\le 1$),
- $U_L$ — **overall heat loss coefficient** $[\text{W/(m}^2 \cdot \text{K)}]$,
- $T_{R,in}$ — fluid inlet temperature $[\text{K}]$ or $[^\circ \text{C}]$,
- $T_a$ — ambient air temperature $[\text{K}]$ or $[^\circ \text{C}]$.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat-Sheet Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is a **must-memorize summary** for KTU 2024 ESE. Note the use of `\vert` / `\mid` instead of the bare `|` symbol to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Units | When to Use |
|:--:|:---|:---|:---:|:---|
| 1 | Absorbed radiation | $S = (\tau \alpha) \, I_a$ | $\text{W/m}^2$ | After finding $I_a$ and selecting glass+coating |
| 2 | Hottel–Whillier–Bliss (HWB) | $Q_u = A_c \, F_R \, [ S - U_L(T_{R,in} - T_a) ]$ | W | Instantaneous useful heat |
| 3 | Alternative HWB form | $Q_u = A_c \, F_R \, [ (\tau \alpha) I_a - U_L(T_{R,in} - T_a) ]$ | W | When $I_a$ given directly |
| 4 | Collector efficiency | $\eta_c = F_R (\tau \alpha) - F_R \, U_L \dfrac{(T_{R,in} - T_a)}{I_a}$ | – | Plot $\eta$ vs $\dfrac{\Delta T}{I_a}$ |
| 5 | Mass flow rate | $\dot{m} = \dfrac{\rho_w \cdot V_{daily}}{t}$ | kg/s | From daily demand and pump hours |
| 6 | Heat capacity rate | $C = \dot{m} \, c_p$ | W/K | Used in $F_R$ |
| 7 | Heat removal factor | $F_R = \dfrac{C}{A_c U_L} \left[ 1 - \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{A_c U_L F'}{C}\right) \right]$ | – | Always needed in HWB |
| 8 | Collector efficiency factor | $F' = \dfrac{1/U_L}{\dfrac{W}{D_o (W - D_o) h_{f,i}} + \dfrac{1}{\pi D_o h_{f,i}} + \dfrac{1}{C_b}}$ | – | Geometry-dependent term |
| 9 | Top loss coefficient | $U_t = \left[ \dfrac{1}{h_{w,p-g}} + \dfrac{1}{h_{r,p-g} + h_{r,g-a}} \right]^{-1}$ | $\text{W/(m}^2 \cdot \text{K)}$ | Plate-to-ambient path |
| 10 | Total loss coefficient | $U_L = U_t + U_b + U_e$ | $\text{W/(m}^2 \cdot \text{K)}$ | Summing all losses |
| 11 | Energy for hot water | $Q_{daily} = M_s \, c_p \, (T_{hot} - T_{cold})$ | kJ/day | Storage tank sizing |
| 12 | Mass of hot water | $M_s = N_p \cdot V_p \cdot \rho_w$ | kg | Demand calculation |
| 13 | Storage tank volume | $V_{tank} = \dfrac{M_s}{\rho_w}$ | $\text{m}^3$ | Direct (since 1 L ≡ 1 kg) |
| 14 | Required collector area | $A_{req} = \dfrac{Q_{daily}}{\eta_c \cdot I_{daily}}$ | $\text{m}^2$ | From daily energy balance |
| 15 | Number of collectors | $N = \left\lceil \dfrac{A_{req}}{A_{c,\text{single}}} \right\rceil$ | integer | Round up to next whole unit |
| 16 | Stagnation temperature | $T_{stag} = T_a + \dfrac{(\tau \alpha) I_a}{U_L}$ | °C | Performance upper bound |

### 2.4 Why the Factor $F_R$ Exists (Engineering Insight)

If the **entire absorber plate** were instantaneously at the fluid temperature $T_{R,in}$, the useful heat gain would simply be $Q_{ideal} = A_c [S - U_L(T_{R,in}-T_a)]$. In reality, the plate is **always hotter** than the fluid, so the true useful heat is **less than** this ideal value. The factor $F_R \le 1$ corrects for this *finite-heat-transfer* effect.

- $F_R \to 1$ when flow rate is **infinite** (fluid always at $T_{R,in}$).
- $F_R \to 0$ when flow rate is **zero** (fluid never moves; all heat lost).

The recommended KTU design practice is to choose $\dot{m}$ such that $F_R \ge 0.85$ for cost-effective operation.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Domestic (Residential):** Bathing water, dishwashing, clothes pre-washing in Kerala homes.
- **Commercial:** Hotels, hostels (KTU 2024 Module 2 frequently uses hotel problems), hospitals, dairies.
- **Industrial:** Pre-heating boiler feed water, food processing, textile dyeing.
- **MNRE National Solar Mission (India):** Targets 20 million m² of SWH collector area, which makes competent SWH designers highly employable.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Hottel–Whillier–Bliss Equation

We begin with the energy balance on a single tube-and-fin element of a flat plate collector. The derivation proceeds in five rigorous steps.

#### Step 1 — Energy Balance on the Absorber Plate

The plate receives absorbed solar flux $S$ and loses heat by three mechanisms: top loss $U_t$, bottom loss $U_b$, edge loss $U_e$, and a useful heat transfer $Q_u$ to the fluid. The combined loss coefficient is $U_L = U_t + U_b + U_e$. At steady state:

$$S = \frac{Q_u}{A_c} + U_L (T_p - T_a)$$

Rearranging to express the useful heat in terms of plate temperature $T_p$:

$$\frac{Q_u}{A_c} = S - U_L (T_p - T_a) \quad \quad \text{(Equation 1)}$$

#### Step 2 — Heat Transfer from Plate to Fluid in a Single Tube

For a single fin-and-tube element, the rate of useful heat transfer depends on a series of thermal resistances: fin conduction, tube wall conduction, tube-to-fluid convection, and the bond conductance $C_b$. The lumped parameter $F'$ is defined such that:

$$q_u = F' \cdot W \cdot L \cdot [S - U_L (T_f - T_a)]$$

where $T_f$ is the mean fluid temperature in the tube. The standard formula for $F'$ (KTU standard form) is:

$$F' = \frac{1/U_L}{\dfrac{W}{D_o(W - D_o) h_{f,i}} + \dfrac{1}{\pi D_o h_{f,i}} + \dfrac{1}{C_b}}$$

For a tube of length $L$, the total useful heat is $Q_u = F' \cdot W \cdot L \cdot [S - U_L(T_f - T_a)]$.

#### Step 3 — Accounting for the Variation of Fluid Temperature Along the Tube

The fluid enters at $T_{R,in}$ and exits at $T_{R,out}$. The mean fluid temperature $T_f$ is **not** the same as $T_{R,in}$. Following Whillier & Bliss (1963), the average of the fluid-side temperatures is weighted by the heat-transfer rate. After integrating along the length, one obtains the result:

$$Q_u = A_c \, F_R \, [S - U_L (T_{R,in} - T_a)] \quad \quad \text{(Equation 2)}$$

where $F_R$ is the heat removal factor (derived in Step 4).

#### Step 4 — Derivation of the Heat Removal Factor $F_R$

Let $C = \dot{m} c_p$ be the heat capacity rate of the fluid. Along an infinitesimal length $dx$ of the tube, the energy balance gives:

$$\frac{dT_f}{dx} = \frac{F' \, W \, U_L}{C} (T_p - T_f)$$

Using the substitution $\theta = T_f - T_a - \dfrac{S}{U_L}$ and integrating from inlet to outlet:

$$\theta_{out} = \theta_{in} \, \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{A_c U_L F'}{C}\right)$$

The total useful heat is $Q_u = C (T_{R,out} - T_{R,in})$. After algebra (combining with the energy balance on the plate, Equation 1), the result is:

$$F_R = \frac{C}{A_c U_L} \left[ 1 - \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{A_c U_L F'}{C}\right) \right] \quad \quad \text{(Equation 3)}$$

**Boundary checks:**
- As $C \to \infty$ (infinite flow): $F_R \to F'$.
- As $C \to 0$ (zero flow): $F_R \to 0$.

#### Step 5 — The Final HWB Form

Substituting Equation 3 into Equation 2 and writing $S = (\tau \alpha) I_a$:

$$\boxed{\,Q_u = A_c \, F_R \, \left[ (\tau \alpha) I_a - U_L (T_{R,in} - T_a) \right]\,} \quad \quad \text{(HWB Equation)}$$

Dividing by $A_c I_a$ gives the instantaneous efficiency:

$$\eta_c = \frac{Q_u}{A_c I_a} = F_R (\tau \alpha) - F_R U_L \, \frac{(T_{R,in} - T_a)}{I_a}$$

This is a **straight line** in the variables $\eta_c$ versus $\dfrac{(T_{R,in} - T_a)}{I_a}$, which is the basis of the ASHRAE Standard 93 test for solar collectors.

---

### 3.2 KTU Design Matrix — Standard Sizing Procedure

The KTU 2024 ESE typically gives a numerical problem worth 7–14 marks. The following **systematic procedure** is the examiner's expected workflow.

| Step | Task | Formula | Output |
|:--:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | Identify users & daily hot-water demand | $V_{daily} = N_p \cdot V_p$ | Litres/day |
| 2 | Mass of hot water | $M_s = V_{daily} \cdot \rho_w$ | kg |
| 3 | Energy required per day | $Q_{daily} = M_s c_p (T_{hot} - T_{cold})$ | kJ/day |
| 4 | Solar resource data | $I_{daily}$ from solar atlas | kWh/m²/day |
| 5 | Effective absorbed energy | $(\tau \alpha) \cdot I_{daily}$ | kWh/m²/day |
| 6 | Choose collector $\eta_c$ | Typical 0.45–0.60 | – |
| 7 | Required collector area | $A_{req} = Q_{daily} / (\eta_c \cdot I_{daily} \cdot 3600)$ | m² |
| 8 | Number of collectors | $N = \lceil A_{req} / A_{c,\text{single}} \rceil$ | Integer |
| 9 | Storage tank capacity | $V_{tank} = 1.0 \text{ to } 1.5 \times V_{daily}$ | Litres |
| 10 | Mass flow rate | $\dot{m} = M_s / t$ | kg/s |
| 11 | Check fluid velocity | $v = \dot{m} / (\rho A_{pipe})$ | m/s (0.3–1.5) |
| 12 | Re-verify efficiency using HWB | $\eta_c = F_R(\tau\alpha) - F_R U_L \Delta T / I_a$ | – |

---

### 3.3 Solved Numerical Example (KTU-Style, 14-Mark Pattern)

**Problem.** A family of **5 members** in Kerala requires **150 L of hot water per person per day** at $60^\circ\text{C}$. Cold water is at $25^\circ\text{C}$. The location receives an average daily solar radiation of $5.5 \ \text{kWh/m}^2\text{/day}$ on a south-facing tilted surface. The proposed flat plate collector has $(\tau \alpha) = 0.78$, $U_L = 5.0 \ \text{W/(m}^2 \cdot \text{K)}$, $F_R = 0.85$, and a single-collector area of $2.0 \ \text{m}^2$. Design the SWH system.

**Solution.**

**Step 1 — Daily hot water demand**
$$V_{daily} = 5 \times 150 = 750 \ \text{L} \quad \Rightarrow \quad M_s = 750 \ \text{kg}$$

**Step 2 — Daily energy required**
$$Q_{daily} = M_s \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T = 750 \times 4.186 \times (60 - 25)$$
$$Q_{daily} = 750 \times 4.186 \times 35 = 109{,}882.5 \ \text{kJ/day}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to kWh**
$$Q_{daily} = \frac{109{,}882.5}{3600} = 30.52 \ \text{kWh/day}$$

**Step 4 — Instantaneous useful heat per m² at $T_{R,in} = 25^\circ\text{C}$ and $T_a = 28^\circ\text{C}$**
$$q_u = F_R \left[ (\tau\alpha) I_a - U_L (T_{R,in} - T_a) \right]$$
$$q_u = 0.85 \left[ 0.78 \times 5500/8.4 - 5.0 \times (25 - 28) \right]$$

Using the daily average: $I_a = 5.5 \ \text{kWh/m}^2\text{/day} = 5500/8.4 \ \text{W/m}^2$ over 8.4 sunshine hours $\approx 655 \ \text{W/m}^2$.
$$q_u = 0.85 \left[ 0.78 \times 655 - 5.0 \times (-3) \right] = 0.85 \left[ 510.9 + 15.0 \right] = 0.85 \times 525.9$$
$$q_u \approx 447 \ \text{W/m}^2$$

**Step 5 — Daily useful heat per m² over 8.4 sunshine hours**
$$Q_{u,\text{day}} = 447 \times 8.4 \times 3600 / 1000 \approx 13.51 \ \text{MJ/m}^2\text{/day} = 3.75 \ \text{kWh/m}^2\text{/day}$$

**Step 6 — Required collector area**
$$A_{req} = \frac{Q_{daily}}{Q_{u,\text{day}}} = \frac{30.52}{3.75} = 8.14 \ \text{m}^2$$

**Step 7 — Number of collectors**
$$N = \left\lceil \frac{8.14}{2.0} \right\rceil = \lceil 4.07 \rceil = 5 \ \text{collectors}$$

**Step 8 — Storage tank volume**
$$V_{tank} = 1.0 \times 750 = 750 \ \text{L} \ \text{(1-day storage)}$$

For a 2-day storage (often chosen in Kerala for monsoon resilience): $V_{tank} = 1.5 \times 750 = 1125 \ \text{L}$.

**Step 9 — Mass flow rate (8.4 sunshine hours)**
$$\dot{m} = \frac{M_s}{t} = \frac{750}{8.4 \times 3600} = 0.0248 \ \text{kg/s} \approx 1.49 \ \text{L/min}$$

**Step 10 — Verification of efficiency**
$$\eta_c = \frac{Q_{u,\text{day}}}{I_{daily}} = \frac{3.75}{5.5} = 68.2\% \ \ (\text{re-check; high—refine with realistic } T_{R,in})$$

A more realistic mean operating $T_{R,in} \approx 40^\circ\text{C}$ during the day reduces this to ~52–55%, which is realistic for a Kerala FPC system.

**Final Design Summary:**

| Parameter | Value |
|:---|:---:|
| Number of FPCs | 5 units (2.0 m² each) |
| Total collector area | 10 m² |
| Storage tank capacity | 750 – 1125 L |
| Mass flow rate | 0.0248 kg/s (1.5 L/min) |
| System efficiency | ~ 52 – 55 % |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Define the Hottel–Whillier–Bliss (HWB) equation. State the significance of each term.**

**Model Answer:**
The HWB equation gives the **instantaneous useful heat gain** by the working fluid of a solar thermal collector:

$$Q_u = A_c \, F_R \, [(\tau \alpha) I_a - U_L (T_{R,in} - T_a)]$$

| Term | Meaning |
|:---|:---|
| $A_c$ | Gross collector area that intercepts solar radiation |
| $F_R$ | Heat removal factor (penalty for finite flow rate) |
| $(\tau \alpha)$ | Effective transmittance–absorptance product of glass + coating |
| $I_a$ | Total solar radiation on tilted surface |
| $U_L$ | Overall heat loss coefficient (top + bottom + edge) |
| $T_{R,in}$ | Fluid inlet temperature |
| $T_a$ | Ambient temperature |

**Significance:** It is the *master equation* that relates collector geometry, material optical properties, heat loss, and operating temperature to the **useful energy output**, allowing the designer to size both the collector area and storage tank.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Compare Flat Plate Collector (FPC) and Evacuated Tube Collector (ETC) in terms of construction, performance, and suitability for Kerala climate.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Flat Plate Collector (FPC) | Evacuated Tube Collector (ETC) |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Construction | Glass cover + flat absorber + risers | Array of parallel vacuum glass tubes |
| Insulation | Air gap + back insulation | **Vacuum** in each tube |
| Heat loss $U_L$ | $4 - 6 \ \text{W/(m}^2 \cdot \text{K)}$ | $1.5 - 3.0 \ \text{W/(m}^2 \cdot \text{K)}$ |
| Operating temperature | 50 – 80 °C | 60 – 120 °C |
| Efficiency at high temp | Drops faster | Maintains high efficiency |
| Performance in monsoon | Reduced (heat loss increases) | **Superior** (vacuum insulation) |
| Cost (per m²) | Lower | Higher |
| Suitability for Kerala | Suitable for moderate demand | **Best suited** for all-season use |

**Verdict:** For Kerala's tropical, monsoon-prone climate, **ETC is preferred** when higher temperatures or year-round use are required; **FPC is cost-effective** for moderate domestic demand.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Question Choices (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

**QUESTION A — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]`

**(a) Derive the Hottel–Whillier–Bliss (HWB) equation starting from the energy balance on the absorber plate. Clearly state the assumptions made. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Assumptions:**
1. Steady-state, one-dimensional heat flow.
2. Uniform plate temperature across the absorber.
3. Constant fluid properties.
4. Constant $(\tau \alpha)$ and $U_L$ over the operating range.
5. No heat loss from the riser-tube side edges (negligible).

**Derivation:**

**Step 1 — Energy balance on the absorber plate (per unit area):**
$$S = \frac{Q_u}{A_c} + U_L (T_p - T_a)$$

**Step 2 — Heat transfer from a single tube-and-fin element** of width $W$ and tube diameter $D_o$ to the fluid at mean temperature $T_f$:
$$q_u = F' \, W \, L \, [S - U_L (T_f - T_a)]$$

where $F'$ is the **collector efficiency factor**:
$$F' = \frac{1/U_L}{\dfrac{W}{D_o(W-D_o) h_{f,i}} + \dfrac{1}{\pi D_o h_{f,i}} + \dfrac{1}{C_b}}$$

**Step 3 — Average along the tube length:** Fluid temperature varies from $T_{R,in}$ to $T_{R,out}$. Defining $\theta = T_f - T_a - S/U_L$, the differential energy balance along the tube is:
$$\frac{d\theta}{dx} = -\frac{F' \, W \, U_L}{\dot{m} c_p} \, \theta$$

Integrating from inlet ($x=0$) to outlet ($x=L$):
$$\theta_{out} = \theta_{in} \exp\!\left(-\frac{A_c U_L F'}{C}\right) \quad \text{where} \quad C = \dot{m} c_p$$

**Step 4 — Use $Q_u = C(T_{R,out} - T_{R,in})$ and substitute back**, leading to:
$$Q_u = A_c \, F_R \, [S - U_L (T_{R,in} - T_a)]$$

with the **heat removal factor**:
$$F_R = \frac{C}{A_c U_L} \left[1 - \exp\!\left(-\frac{A_c U_L F'}{C}\right)\right]$$

**Step 5 — Final HWB form** with $S = (\tau \alpha) I_a$:
$$\boxed{Q_u = A_c \, F_R \, [(\tau \alpha) I_a - U_L (T_{R,in} - T_a)]}$$

---

**(b) Design a solar water heater system for a hostel housing 50 students in Kerala. The daily hot water requirement is 30 L per student at 60 °C. Cold water is available at 25 °C. The site receives an average daily solar radiation of 5.0 kWh/m²/day on the tilted collector surface. Use a flat plate collector with $(\tau \alpha) = 0.78$, $U_L = 4.5 \ \text{W/(m}^2 \cdot \text{K)}$, $F_R = 0.82$, and a single-collector area of $2.5 \ \text{m}^2$. Take collector operating temperature as $T_{R,in} \approx 32^\circ\text{C}$ and ambient $T_a = 30^\circ\text{C}$ for design purposes. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Daily hot water demand:**
$$V_{daily} = 50 \times 30 = 1500 \ \text{L} \quad \Rightarrow \quad M_s = 1500 \ \text{kg}$$

**Step 2 — Energy required per day:**
$$Q_{daily} = 1500 \times 4.186 \times (60 - 25) = 219{,}765 \ \text{kJ/day} = 61.05 \ \text{kWh/day}$$

**Step 3 — Convert $I_{daily}$ to peak-sun equivalent $I_a$:** Assuming 8 sunshine hours/day,
$$I_a = \frac{5.0 \ \text{kWh/m}^2\text{/day}}{8 \ \text{h}} = 0.625 \ \text{kW/m}^2 = 625 \ \text{W/m}^2$$

**Step 4 — Useful heat per m² per second (HWB equation):**
$$q_u = F_R \left[(\tau \alpha) I_a - U_L (T_{R,in} - T_a)\right]$$
$$q_u = 0.82 \left[ 0.78 \times 625 - 4.5 \times (32 - 30) \right]$$
$$q_u = 0.82 \left[ 487.5 - 9.0 \right] = 0.82 \times 478.5 = 392.4 \ \text{W/m}^2$$

**Step 5 — Daily useful heat per m² (8 sunshine hours):**
$$Q_{u,\text{day}} = \frac{392.4 \times 8 \times 3600}{1000} = 11{,}303 \ \text{kJ/m}^2\text{/day} = 3.14 \ \text{kWh/m}^2\text{/day}$$

**Step 6 — Required collector area:**
$$A_{req} = \frac{Q_{daily}}{Q_{u,\text{day}}} = \frac{61.05}{3.14} = 19.4 \ \text{m}^2$$

**Step 7 — Number of collectors:**
$$N = \left\lceil \frac{19.4}{2.5} \right\rceil = \lceil 7.78 \rceil = 8 \ \text{collectors}$$

**Step 8 — Storage tank volume (1.2× daily demand for hostel use):**
$$V_{tank} = 1.2 \times 1500 = 1800 \ \text{L}$$

**Step 9 — Mass flow rate (8 sunshine hours):**
$$\dot{m} = \frac{1500}{8 \times 3600} = 0.0521 \ \text{kg/s} \approx 3.12 \ \text{L/min}$$

**Step 10 — System efficiency:**
$$\eta_c = \frac{Q_{u,\text{day}}}{I_{daily}} = \frac{3.14}{5.0} = 62.8\%$$

This is on the higher end — realistic during midday but lower during morning/evening hours. For a more conservative design, an **efficiency of ~50%** is typically assumed, which would have given ~24 m² and **10 collectors**.

**Final Design:**

| Parameter | Value |
|:---|:---:|
| Daily hot water demand | 1500 L |
| Energy demand | 61.05 kWh/day |
| Collector area required | 19.4 m² |
| Number of FPCs (2.5 m² each) | 8 units |
| Storage tank capacity | 1800 L |
| Mass flow rate | 0.0521 kg/s |
| Pump power (for 5 m head) | ~ 0.05 kW |

---

**QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**(a) With the help of a neat diagram, explain the construction and working of a flat plate solar water heater. Discuss the role of the selective absorber coating and the tempered glass cover. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Construction** (refer to diagram in Section 4.4):

1. **Transparent glass cover (top layer):** Low-iron tempered glass, $3-4 \ \text{mm}$ thick, with transmittance $\tau \approx 0.88-0.91$. Reduces top convective and radiative losses by creating a still air gap and by absorbing long-wave IR.
2. **Absorber plate:** Copper or aluminium sheet, $0.3-0.5 \ \text{mm}$ thick, coated with **selective coating** (black chrome, black nickel, or cermet). The selective coating has:
   - High **absorptance** $\alpha \ge 0.95$ in the solar spectrum $(0.3 - 2.5 \ \mu\text{m})$.
   - Low **emittance** $\varepsilon \le 0.10$ in the thermal IR $(> 2.5 \ \mu\text{m})$.
   This combination minimises radiative heat loss.
3. **Riser tubes & headers:** Copper tubes of $10-15 \ \text{mm}$ diameter, ultrasonically or roll-bonded to the plate, carrying water.
4. **Insulation (back & sides):** Mineral wool or glass wool, $50-75 \ \text{mm}$ thick, $k \approx 0.04 \ \text{W/(m·K)}$.
5. **Casing:** Galvanised iron or aluminium enclosure.

**Working principle:** Sunlight → transmission through glass → absorption by selective coating → conduction through plate → convection to water in risers → thermosiphon (or pump-driven) flow to storage tank. The glass cover re-radiates and traps IR, creating a greenhouse effect that keeps the plate hotter.

**Role of selective coating:** Without it, an ordinary black paint would emit strongly in the IR (high $\varepsilon \approx 0.9$), radiating heat back to the glass and losing efficiency. Selective coatings keep $\alpha / \varepsilon$ ratio $> 10$, yielding $30-40\%$ higher efficiency.

**Role of tempered glass cover:** Reduces top heat loss coefficient $U_t$ from $\sim 25 \ \text{W/(m}^2 \cdot \text{K)}$ (bare plate) to $\sim 5 \ \text{W/(m}^2 \cdot \text{K)}$ (covered plate) — a **5× reduction**.

---

**(b) A hotel in Thiruvananthapuram requires 3000 L of hot water per day at 55 °C. The cold water temperature is 24 °C. The location receives 4.5 kWh/m²/day of solar radiation with 7 sunshine hours/day. The proposed ETC has $(\tau \alpha) = 0.82$, $U_L = 2.5 \ \text{W/(m}^2 \cdot \text{K)}$, $F_R = 0.88$, and a single-tube module area of $2.0 \ \text{m}^2$. Determine: (i) the mass flow rate, (ii) the number of ETC modules required, (iii) the storage tank size. Assume a design efficiency of 55%. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Mass of hot water:**
$$M_s = 3000 \ \text{kg}$$

**Step 2 — Energy required per day:**
$$Q_{daily} = 3000 \times 4.186 \times (55 - 24) = 389{,}298 \ \text{kJ/day} = 108.14 \ \text{kWh/day}$$

**Step 3 — Effective absorbed energy per m² per day (assuming 55% efficiency):**
$$Q_{u,\text{day}} = \eta \cdot I_{daily} = 0.55 \times 4.5 = 2.475 \ \text{kWh/m}^2\text{/day}$$

**Step 4 — Required collector area:**
$$A_{req} = \frac{Q_{daily}}{Q_{u,\text{day}}} = \frac{108.14}{2.475} = 43.7 \ \text{m}^2$$

**Step 5 — Number of ETC modules:**
$$N = \left\lceil \frac{43.7}{2.0} \right\rceil = \lceil 21.85 \rceil = 22 \ \text{modules}$$

**Step 6 — Storage tank capacity (1.0× daily demand for commercial hotel):**
$$V_{tank} = 3000 \ \text{L} = 3.0 \ \text{m}^3$$

**Step 7 — Mass flow rate (7 sunshine hours):**
$$\dot{m} = \frac{3000}{7 \times 3600} = 0.119 \ \text{kg/s} \approx 7.14 \ \text{L/min}$$

**Verification using HWB equation:**
- $I_a = 4500/7 = 642.9 \ \text{W/m}^2$
- $q_u = 0.88 [0.82 \times 642.9 - 2.5 \times (30 - 28)] = 0.88 [527.2 - 5] = 459.8 \ \text{W/m}^2$
- $Q_{u,\text{day}} = 459.8 \times 7 \times 3.6 / 1000 = 11.59 \ \text{MJ/m}^2\text{/day} = 3.22 \ \text{kWh/m}^2\text{/day}$
- $\eta_c = 3.22/4.5 = 71.6\%$ — this is the *instantaneous noon* efficiency; daily average is ~55–60%.

A more conservative design would use 25 modules (10% margin) to account for monsoon cloudiness.

**Final Design:**

| Parameter | Value |
|:---|:---:|
| Daily hot water demand | 3000 L |
| Mass flow rate | 0.119 kg/s (7.14 L/min) |
| Required ETC modules (2 m² each) | 22 units |
| Storage tank capacity | 3000 L |
| System efficiency (daily avg) | 55 % |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Read This Before Writing the Exam!**
> 1. **Unit inconsistency is the #1 mark-killer:** Convert L → kg (× 1), kJ → kWh (÷ 3600), and daily radiation → W/m² (÷ sunshine hours). One missed conversion = 1 mark lost per occurrence.
> 2. **Forgetting to multiply $c_p$ when computing $Q_{daily}$:** Many students write $M_s \Delta T$ without $c_p$ — this gives wrong energy by a factor of 4.186.
> 3. **Storage tank sizing error:** KTU expects 1.0× to 1.5× the daily demand, not the *total* monthly demand. Writing 90,000 L for a 30-day month is wrong.
> 4. **Confusing $F'$ and $F_R$:** $F'$ (efficiency factor) depends only on geometry; $F_R$ (heat removal factor) depends on $F'$ AND flow rate. Don't interchange them in the HWB equation.
> 5. **Not using $\lceil \rceil$ (ceiling) for number of collectors:** Always round *up* — fractional collectors do not exist.
> 6. **Neglecting $U_L(T_{R,in} - T_a)$ term:** This is *positive* when $T_{R,in} > T_a$ (sunny day) and *negative* (a free bonus!) when ambient > inlet (early morning). Either way, including it shows examiner awareness.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision — Pin This Section!**

- [x] **HWB equation** is the *master equation* — memorize it symbol-by-symbol: $Q_u = A_c F_R [(\tau\alpha) I_a - U_L(T_{R,in} - T_a)]$
- [x] $F_R \le F' \le 1$; $F_R \to F'$ at infinite flow; $F_R \to 0$ at zero flow.
- [x] $U_L = U_t + U_b + U_e$; ETC has much lower $U_L$ than FPC due to vacuum.
- [x] **Selective coating** = high $\alpha$, low $\varepsilon$ → minimises radiative loss.
- [x] **Tempered glass cover** = reduces top loss $U_t$ by factor of ~5.
- [x] **Daily energy demand** $Q_{daily} = M_s c_p \Delta T$ — never forget $c_p = 4.186 \ \text{kJ/(kg·K)}$.
- [x] **Storage tank** size = $1.0 \times$ to $1.5 \times$ daily demand in litres.
- [x] **Number of collectors** = $\lceil A_{req} / A_{single} \rceil$ — always round up.
- [x] **Mass flow rate** = $M_s / t_{sunshine}$; typical $t_{sunshine} = 7-9 \ \text{h}$ for Kerala.
- [x] **System efficiency** typically $45 - 60\%$ for FPC, $50 - 70\%$ for ETC.
- [x] **Kerala climate** favours ETC for all-season use; FPC is cost-effective for moderate demand.
- [x] **Stagnation temperature** $T_{stag} = T_a + (\tau\alpha) I_a / U_L$ — set pressure-relief valve accordingly.
- [x] **Hard water** in some Kerala districts requires sacrificial anodes or indirect (glycol) systems.
- [x] **MNRE subsidy** (as of 2024) provides ₹3000-3300 per 100 LPD for residential SWH — relevant in design-economics viva questions.
- [x] **Orientation:** South-facing, tilt = latitude (≈ 10° for Kerala) for year-round maximum.
- [x] **Piping:** Insulate all hot-water pipes; use copper or CPVC; pitch continuously upward toward tank.

**Three Numerical Constants to Memorize Forever:**
- $c_p(\text{water}) = 4.186 \ \text{kJ/(kg·K)} = 1.163 \ \text{Wh/(kg·K)}$
- $\rho_w = 1000 \ \text{kg/m}^3 = 1 \ \text{kg/L}$
- $1 \ \text{kWh} = 3.6 \ \text{MJ} = 3600 \ \text{kJ}$

**One Rule of Thumb to Impress the Examiner:**
> For every 100 L of hot water at 60 °C from 25 °C cold supply, you need **~2.0 m²** of FPC area in Kerala's climate.

> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Solar PV Systems Introduction, Fundamentals of Semiconductor and Solar Cells, Photovoltaic Effect, Solar Cell (Photovoltaic) Materials, Basic Parameters of the Solar Cell, Generation of Solar Cell (Photovoltaic) Materials, Photovoltaic (PV) Module and PV Array, Single-Crystal Solar Cell Module

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Section 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Solar PV Systems

A **Solar Photovoltaic (PV) System** is an engineered assembly of electrical and mechanical components that converts sunlight directly into electricity using the **photovoltaic effect**, first observed by Edmond Becquerel in 1839 and physically explained by Einstein in 1905. A PV system fundamentally comprises **PV modules** (made of solar cells), **power conditioning equipment** (inverters, charge controllers), **mounting structures**, **balance of system (BOS)** components, and **storage elements** (batteries) where applicable.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Definition (Syllabus-aligned):** A solar PV system is a power generation system that uses solar cells (semiconductor devices) to convert solar radiation (photons) into direct current (DC) electricity through the photovoltaic effect, without any moving parts, fuel, or emissions.

### Intuitive Analogy — "The Photon Waterfall"
Imagine a **waterfall**: falling water spins a turbine to produce electricity. In a solar cell, **photons** (light particles) act like the falling water, the **semiconductor junction** acts like the turbine, and the **electric current** produced is the rotational energy harnessed. The "height" of the waterfall corresponds to the **photon energy** (which must exceed the material's **bandgap energy**, $E_g$), and the "flow rate" corresponds to the **intensity of solar radiation** (measured in W/m²).

---

## 1.2 Fundamentals of Semiconductors

A **semiconductor** is a material with electrical conductivity between that of a conductor ($\sim 10^7 \, \text{S/m}$) and an insulator ($\sim 10^{-17} \, \text{S/m}$). The two principal classes relevant to PV technology are:

| Type | Doping Element | Majority Carriers | Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Intrinsic (pure)** | None | Equal electrons & holes | Reference / research |
| **N-type** | Phosphorus (P), Arsenic (As) | Electrons | Emitter / top layer |
| **P-type** | Boron (B), Gallium (Ga) | Holes | Base / absorber layer |

### The Band Theory — "Staircase of Electrons"

In a solid, electron energies are grouped into **energy bands**:

- **Valence Band (VB):** The highest energy band filled with electrons at 0 K.
- **Conduction Band (CB):** The next higher band, where electrons are free to move and conduct.
- **Bandgap ($E_g$):** The forbidden energy region between VB and CB.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Photon Absorption Rule:** A photon is absorbed only if its energy $E_{ph} \geq E_g$. Excess energy is lost as heat (thermalization loss). This is why **bandgap engineering** is central to PV material selection.

### Key Physical Constants (Standard Test Conditions — STC)

| Constant | Symbol | Value | Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Planck's constant | $h$ | $6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ | J·s |
| Boltzmann constant | $k$ | $1.381 \times 10^{-23}$ | J/K |
| Elementary charge | $q$ | $1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ | C |
| Solar constant | $G_{sc}$ | **1367** | W/m² |
| STC irradiance | $G$ | **1000** | W/m² |
| Cell temperature | $T_{cell}$ | **25** | °C (298.15 K) |
| Air Mass | AM | 1.5 (for STC) | — |

---

## 1.3 The Photovoltaic Effect

The **photovoltaic effect** is the generation of a voltage and electric current in a material upon exposure to light. The sequence is:

1. **Photon Absorption:** An incoming photon with energy $h\nu \geq E_g$ is absorbed by a valence electron.
2. **Electron-Hole Pair (EHP) Generation:** The electron is excited from the VB to the CB, leaving behind a hole in the VB.
3. **Charge Separation:** The built-in electric field at the **p-n junction** sweeps the electron toward the n-side and the hole toward the p-side.
4. **Collection:** Metal contacts collect the separated charges, driving current through an external load.

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** A p-n junction is like a one-way valve for electrons. Once separated, electrons cannot flow back across the junction spontaneously — they must travel through the external circuit, doing useful work.

---

## 1.4 Solar Cell Materials — A Generational View

| Generation | Material Example | Efficiency (Lab) | Commercial Status |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **1st Gen** | Mono-/Multi-crystalline Si | 26.7% (PERL) | Dominant (90%+ market) |
| **2nd Gen** | a-Si, CdTe, CIGS | 22.6% (CIGS) | Thin-film markets |
| **3rd Gen** | Perovskite, DSSC, organic | 26.1% (perovskite/Si tandem) | R&D / pre-commercial |
| **4th Gen** | Tandem, quantum dot | 47.1% (multi-junction) | Space / concentrator |

---

## 1.5 Basic Parameters of a Solar Cell

The **current–voltage (I–V) characteristic** defines solar-cell performance. Key parameters:

- **Short-circuit current ($I_{sc}$):** Current when terminals are shorted (V = 0). Proportional to incident light intensity and cell area.
- **Open-circuit voltage ($V_{oc}$):** Voltage when terminals are open (I = 0). Depends logarithmically on light intensity.
- **Maximum power point ($P_{max} = I_{mp} \times V_{mp}$):** Operating point where the product of $I$ and $V$ is maximized.
- **Fill Factor (FF):** Ratio of $P_{max}$ to the product $V_{oc} I_{sc}$.
- **Conversion Efficiency ($\eta$):** Ratio of $P_{max}$ to incident solar power $P_{in} = G \times A$.
- **Quantum Efficiency (QE):** Ratio of collected carriers to incident photons at a given wavelength.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why these matter for KTU exams:** Every numerical problem in solar cell design ultimately reduces to manipulating these six parameters. Memorize their symbols and units rigorously.

---

## 1.6 Generation of Solar Cell Materials

Solar cell material generation has evolved along four distinct technological waves:

- **1st Generation:** Bulk crystalline silicon (wafer-based) — proven, reliable, but energy-intensive to manufacture.
- **2nd Generation:** Thin-film deposition (amorphous Si, CdTe, CIGS) — lower material usage, flexible substrates.
- **3rd Generation:** Emerging technologies (perovskite, dye-sensitized, organic) — tunable bandgaps, low-cost processing.
- **4th Generation:** Hybrid and multi-junction cells combining multiple absorbers to exceed the Shockley–Queisser limit.

---

## 1.7 PV Module and PV Array — Scaling Up

- **Solar Cell:** The basic unit (~0.5–0.7 V, ~3–9 A, ~15–25% η).
- **PV Module:** A sealed assembly of typically **36, 60, 72, or 144 cells** laminated between glass and a polymer backsheet, electrically connected and mechanically framed. Rated power: 50 W to 700+ W.
- **PV Array:** Multiple modules connected in **series** (to raise voltage) and **parallel** (to raise current), forming a complete DC power source.

---

## 1.8 Single-Crystal Solar Cell Module

**Monocrystalline (single-crystal) silicon modules** are the benchmark of the PV industry. They are manufactured from ultra-pure silicon grown as a single continuous crystal via the **Czochralski (CZ) process** or **Float Zone (FZ) process**. Single-crystal modules:

- Have the **highest commercial efficiencies** (20–24% for premium modules).
- Feature a uniform **dark black appearance** (due to anti-reflective coating on the mono-Si surface).
- Exhibit **excellent long-term stability** (>25 year warranties standard).
- Have higher embodied energy but lowest levelized cost of energy (LCOE) in sunny climates.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Section 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Semiconductor Theory — A Deeper Look

### 2.1.1 Intrinsic Carrier Concentration
For an intrinsic semiconductor, the number of electrons in the conduction band equals the number of holes in the valence band:

$$n_i = \sqrt{N_c N_v} \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_g}{2 k T}\right)$$

where:
- $N_c$ = effective density of states in CB
- $N_v$ = effective density of states in VB
- $E_g$ = bandgap energy
- $k$ = Boltzmann constant
- $T$ = absolute temperature

### 2.1.2 Doping and Carrier Concentration
For an n-type material with donor concentration $N_D$:

$$n \approx N_D, \quad p = \frac{n_i^2}{N_D}$$

For a p-type material with acceptor concentration $N_A$:

$$p \approx N_A, \quad n = \frac{n_i^2}{N_A}$$

The **mass-action law** $np = n_i^2$ always holds in thermal equilibrium.

### 2.1.3 The p-n Junction at Equilibrium
When p-type and n-type materials are joined, electrons diffuse from n → p and holes from p → n, creating a **depletion region** with a built-in electric field. The **built-in potential** is:

$$V_{bi} = \frac{k T}{q} \ln\!\left(\frac{N_A N_D}{n_i^2}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** $V_{bi}$ sets the **upper theoretical limit** for the open-circuit voltage $V_{oc}$ of a solar cell made from the same materials. Maximizing $V_{bi}$ is a central goal in cell architecture.

---

## 2.2 Photovoltaic Effect — Energy Conversion Chain

The energy conversion follows a strict causal chain:

1. **Photon flux** $\Phi_{ph}$ (photons/m²/s) impinges on the cell surface.
2. **Absorption:** Photons with $h\nu \geq E_g$ are absorbed; fraction absorbed depends on absorption coefficient $\alpha(\lambda)$ and material thickness.
3. **Generation:** Each absorbed photon creates one electron–hole pair (EHP), giving a **generation rate** $G(x)$ that decays exponentially with depth $x$:

$$G(x) = \Phi_{ph} \alpha \exp(-\alpha x)$$

4. **Separation:** The junction's electric field separates EHPs before they recombine.
5. **Collection:** Minority carriers diffuse to the depletion edge and are swept across, contributing to **photocurrent $I_{ph}$**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters:** Any process that reduces absorption, increases recombination, or weakens charge separation reduces $I_{ph}$ and thus efficiency. Anti-reflective coatings, surface texturing, and back-surface fields all target one of these losses.

---

## 2.3 Equivalent Circuit of a Solar Cell

The **single-diode model** represents a real solar cell as:

**Components:**
- **Current source** $I_{ph}$ (light-generated current)
- **Diode** (dark saturation current $I_0$, ideality factor $n$)
- **Series resistance** $R_s$ (contact + bulk resistance)
- **Shunt resistance** $R_{sh}$ (leakage paths)
- **Load resistance** $R_L$

**Output current equation (single-diode model):**

$$I = I_{ph} - I_0 \left[ \exp\!\left( \frac{q (V + I R_s)}{n k T} \right) - 1 \right] - \frac{V + I R_s}{R_{sh}}$$

### Physical Meaning of Each Term
- $I_{ph}$ — proportional to light intensity; this is the "ideal" current the cell would deliver.
- $I_0 \exp(...)$ — the diode term represents **recombination losses** (dark current).
- $V + IR_s / R_{sh}$ — the shunt term represents **leakage current** through alternate paths.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Hack:** For ideal cells, assume $R_s = 0$ and $R_{sh} = \infty$. The equation simplifies to the **ideal diode equation**, which is the starting point for nearly every 14-mark derivation problem.

---

## 2.4 Open-Circuit Voltage ($V_{oc}$) — Derivation Setup

Setting $I = 0$ in the ideal diode equation:

$$V_{oc} = \frac{n k T}{q} \ln\!\left( \frac{I_{ph}}{I_0} + 1 \right) \approx \frac{n k T}{q} \ln\!\left( \frac{I_{ph}}{I_0} \right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why $V_{oc}$ is logarithmic in light:** Doubling the irradiance only adds $\approx (kT/q)\ln 2 \approx 18$ mV at room temperature. This is why solar cells have **limited voltage** but **wide current range** with varying sunlight.

---

## 2.5 Short-Circuit Current ($I_{sc}$)

Setting $V = 0$ in the ideal equation:

$$I_{sc} \approx I_{ph} = q \int_0^{\infty} \Phi(\lambda) \, [1 - R(\lambda)] \, \text{EQE}(\lambda) \, d\lambda$$

For practical purposes, $I_{sc}$ is approximately **linearly proportional to irradiance** $G$:

$$I_{sc}(G) \approx I_{sc,STC} \times \frac{G}{G_{STC}}$$

---

## 2.6 Fill Factor (FF)

$$FF = \frac{V_{mp} I_{mp}}{V_{oc} I_{sc}}$$

An empirical approximation (Green, 1981):

$$FF_0 = \frac{v_{oc} - \ln(v_{oc} + 0.72)}{v_{oc} + 1}$$

where $v_{oc} = \frac{q V_{oc}}{n k T}$ (normalized open-circuit voltage). With $R_s$ and $R_{sh}$ corrections, $FF < FF_0$.

---

## 2.7 Conversion Efficiency ($\eta$)

$$\eta = \frac{P_{max}}{P_{in}} = \frac{V_{oc} \, I_{sc} \, FF}{G \times A}$$

---

## 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Units |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $n_i = \sqrt{N_c N_v} \exp(-E_g / 2kT)$ | Intrinsic carrier concentration | cm⁻³ |
| 2 | $V_{bi} = (kT/q) \ln(N_A N_D / n_i^2)$ | Built-in junction potential | V |
| 3 | $E_g(T) = E_g(0) - (\alpha T^2 / (T + \beta))$ | Varshni bandgap relation | eV |
| 4 | $I = I_{ph} - I_0[\exp(qV/nkT) - 1]$ | Ideal solar cell I–V | A |
| 5 | $I_{ph} = q G L_n$ | Photocurrent (diffusion-limited) | A |
| 6 | $V_{oc} \approx (nkT/q) \ln(I_{ph}/I_0)$ | Open-circuit voltage | V |
| 7 | $I_{sc} \propto G$ | Short-circuit current vs. irradiance | A |
| 8 | $FF = V_{mp} I_{mp} / V_{oc} I_{sc}$ | Fill Factor | dimensionless (0–1) |
| 9 | $\eta = V_{oc} I_{sc} FF / (G A)$ | Conversion efficiency | dimensionless (0–1) or % |
| 10 | $P_{max} = V_{oc} I_{sc} FF$ | Maximum power output | W |
| 11 | $\alpha(\lambda) = 4\pi k / \lambda$ | Absorption coefficient | cm⁻¹ |
| 12 | $V_{module} = N_s \times V_{cell}$ | Module voltage (series) | V |
| 13 | $I_{array} = N_p \times I_{module}$ | Array current (parallel) | A |
| 14 | $P_{array} = N_s N_p \times P_{module}$ | Array power | W |

> [!TIP]
> **Critical Note on Table:** All absolute value bars (e.g., for $\vert I \vert$, $\vert V \vert$) are written using `\vert` to keep the markdown table format intact during rendering.

---

## 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Satellite Power Systems:** Multi-junction GaAs cells (η > 30%) power nearly all geostationary satellites.
- **Grid-Scale Solar Farms:** Use string inverters with arrays of mono-Si modules (450–700 W each).
- **Building-Integrated PV (BIPV):** Thin-film CIGS on rooftops combine aesthetics with energy generation.
- **Solar Pumps:** Directly drive irrigation pumps in off-grid agricultural applications in Kerala.
- **Hybrid Systems:** PV + battery + diesel — the cornerstone of rural electrification in India.
