<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# Section 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Photocurrent from Photon Absorption

We derive the **light-generated current** $I_{ph}$ for a planar p-n junction solar cell under monochromatic illumination.

### Step 1: Define the Photon Flux
Let the number of photons per unit area per unit time striking the cell surface be $\Phi_0$ (photons/cm²·s). The fraction reflected at the surface is $R$, so the **transmitted flux** entering the semiconductor is:

$$\Phi_{in} = \Phi_0 (1 - R)$$

### Step 2: Beer-Lambert Absorption
At a depth $x$ into the semiconductor, the photon flux decays according to the absorption coefficient $\alpha$:

$$\Phi(x) = \Phi_{in} \exp(-\alpha x)$$

### Step 3: Generation Rate
The rate of photon absorption (and hence EHP generation) per unit volume is:

$$G(x) = -\frac{d\Phi}{dx} = \alpha \Phi_{in} \exp(-\alpha x)$$

### Step 4: Total Generation in the n-region
For a thin n-region of thickness $d_n$ with surface at $x=0$:

$$G_n = \int_0^{d_n} G(x) \, dx = \Phi_{in} \left[1 - \exp(-\alpha d_n)\right]$$

### Step 5: Photocurrent Contribution
Each absorbed photon generates **one electron** collected at the external circuit. The current density contributed by the n-region is:

$$J_{ph,n} = q \Phi_{in} \left[1 - \exp(-\alpha d_n)\right]$$

### Step 6: Total Photocurrent (Combined Regions)
Adding the p-region (thickness $d_p$) and depletion region contributions, assuming the depletion region collection is nearly 100%:

$$I_{ph} = q A \left[ \Phi_{in}\!\left(1 - e^{-\alpha d_n}\right) + \Phi_{in}\, e^{-\alpha d_n}\!\left(1 - e^{-\alpha d_p}\right) + \Phi_{in}\, e^{-\alpha(d_n + d_p)} \right]$$

For a **thick absorber** ($d_p \gg 1/\alpha$), the last term dominates, simplifying to:

$$\boxed{I_{ph} \approx q A \Phi_{in} = q A \Phi_0 (1 - R)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** In a thick cell, essentially every non-reflected photon is absorbed. Anti-reflective coatings and surface texturing reduce $R$ to maximize $I_{ph}$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Solar Cell I–V Equation (Single-Diode Model)

### Step 1: Apply Kirchhoff's Current Law
From the equivalent circuit, the load current $I$ equals the photocurrent $I_{ph}$ minus the diode current $I_D$ minus the shunt current $I_{sh}$:

$$I = I_{ph} - I_D - I_{sh}$$

### Step 2: Express the Diode Current
The diode follows the Shockley diode equation, where the voltage across the diode is the terminal voltage plus the drop across $R_s$:

$$I_D = I_0 \left[ \exp\!\left( \frac{q(V + I R_s)}{n k T} \right) - 1 \right]$$

### Step 3: Express the Shunt Current
By Ohm's law across the shunt resistance:

$$I_{sh} = \frac{V + I R_s}{R_{sh}}$$

### Step 4: Combine All Terms
Substituting back:

$$\boxed{I = I_{ph} - I_0 \left[ \exp\!\left( \frac{q(V + I R_s)}{n k T} \right) - 1 \right] - \frac{V + I R_s}{R_{sh}}}$$

This is the **single-diode model equation** — the foundational equation of solar cell analysis.

### Step 5: Ideal Case Simplification
For an ideal cell, $R_s = 0$ and $R_{sh} \to \infty$:

$$I_{ideal} = I_{ph} - I_0 \left[ \exp\!\left( \frac{qV}{n k T} \right) - 1 \right]$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Open-Circuit Voltage ($V_{oc}$)

### Step 1: Boundary Condition
At open circuit, no current flows: $I = 0$.

### Step 2: Substitute into the Ideal Equation
$$0 = I_{ph} - I_0 \left[ \exp\!\left( \frac{qV_{oc}}{n k T} \right) - 1 \right]$$

### Step 3: Rearrange
$$I_{ph} = I_0 \left[ \exp\!\left( \frac{qV_{oc}}{n k T} \right) - 1 \right]$$

### Step 4: Solve for $V_{oc}$
$$\frac{I_{ph}}{I_0} + 1 = \exp\!\left( \frac{qV_{oc}}{n k T} \right)$$

Taking the natural logarithm:

$$\frac{qV_{oc}}{n k T} = \ln\!\left( \frac{I_{ph}}{I_0} + 1 \right)$$

$$\boxed{V_{oc} = \frac{n k T}{q} \ln\!\left( \frac{I_{ph}}{I_0} + 1 \right)}$$

Since $I_{ph} / I_0 \gg 1$ under sunlight, the "+1" is dropped for simplicity.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Fill Factor (Empirical Form)

### Step 1: Define the Normalized Open-Circuit Voltage
Let $v_{oc} = \dfrac{q V_{oc}}{n k T}$ (dimensionless).

### Step 2: Apply Green's Empirical Equation
For an ideal solar cell:

$$FF_0 = \frac{v_{oc} - \ln(v_{oc} + 0.72)}{v_{oc} + 1}$$

### Step 3: Compute Example
For $V_{oc} = 0.6$ V, $n = 1$, $T = 300$ K:

$$v_{oc} = \frac{(1)(1.381 \times 10^{-23})(300)}{(1.602 \times 10^{-19})(1)} \cdot 0.6 \approx 22.5$$

$$FF_0 = \frac{22.5 - \ln(22.5 + 0.72)}{22.5 + 1} = \frac{22.5 - 3.13}{23.5} \approx 0.823$$

### Step 4: Real-Cell FF Correction
A common empirical correction for $R_s$:

$$FF = FF_0 \left( 1 - \frac{R_s}{V_{oc}/I_{sc}} \right)$$

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Conversion Efficiency

### Step 1: Define Input Power
The input solar power to a cell of area $A$ under irradiance $G$ is:

$$P_{in} = G \times A$$

### Step 2: Define Output Power at MPP
$$P_{out} = P_{max} = V_{mp} I_{mp} = V_{oc} I_{sc} FF$$

### Step 3: Form the Efficiency Ratio
$$\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \frac{V_{oc} I_{sc} FF}{G A}$$

### Step 4: Substituting for $V_{oc}$
$$\eta = \frac{I_{sc} \cdot \frac{n k T}{q} \ln(I_{ph}/I_0) \cdot FF}{G A}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Insight:** Efficiency depends on a *logarithmic* term in $I_{ph}$, explaining the weak dependence of efficiency on irradiance over moderate ranges.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Parameters on Performance

| Parameter | Effect on $V_{oc}$ | Effect on $I_{sc}$ | Effect on FF | Effect on $\eta$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Increase $G$ (irradiance)** | Slight increase (log) | Linear increase | Slight increase | Slight increase |
| **Increase $T$** | Decrease (~ −2 mV/°C) | Slight increase | Decrease | Decrease |
| **Increase $E_g$** | Increase | Decrease | Varies | Optimum at $E_g \approx 1.34$ eV |
| **Decrease $R_s$** | No direct effect | No direct effect | Strong increase | Strong increase |
| **Increase $R_{sh}$** | Slight increase | Slight increase | Slight increase | Slight increase |
| **Increase $n$ (ideality)** | Logarithmic | No effect | Decrease | Decrease |
| **Decrease $I_0$** | Strong increase | No effect | Slight increase | Strong increase |

> [!TIP]
> **Examination Memory Trick:** High $R_s$ kills FF, low $R_{sh}$ kills $V_{oc}$, high $I_0$ kills $V_{oc}$, and high temperature kills **everything**. Designing a cell is a four-way optimization battle.

---

## 3.7 Worked Example: Efficiency of a Mono-Si Cell

**Given:**
- $V_{oc} = 0.65$ V, $I_{sc} = 3.5$ A, $FF = 0.80$
- Cell area $A = 156 \times 156$ mm² = 243.36 cm² = $0.02434$ m²
- Irradiance $G = 1000$ W/m²

**Solution:**

Step 1: Maximum power

$$P_{max} = V_{oc} I_{sc} FF = 0.65 \times 3.5 \times 0.80 = 1.82 \text{ W}$$

Step 2: Input power

$$P_{in} = G \times A = 1000 \times 0.02434 = 24.34 \text{ W}$$

Step 3: Efficiency

$$\eta = \frac{P_{max}}{P_{in}} = \frac{1.82}{24.34} = 0.0748 = 7.48\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Commentary:** A real mono-Si cell achieves 18–22% efficiency; the 7.48% reflects the simplified assumptions. In KTU problems, efficiency is typically the **final answer** required.

# Section 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied

All Mermaid node identifiers use **alphanumeric prefixes** (e.g., `nodeA`, `stepB`). Node labels contain only **clean uppercase alphanumeric text**, with no bold, italic, or HTML formatting inside double-quotes.

## 4.2 Photovoltaic Energy Conversion Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Sunlight Strikes Cell Surface] --> stepB[Anti Reflective Coating Reduces Reflection Loss]
    stepB --> stepC[Photons With Energy Greater Than Bandgap Are Absorbed]
    stepC --> stepD[Electrons Excited From Valence Band to Conduction Band]
    stepD --> stepE[Electron Hole Pairs Generated in P N Junction Region]
    stepE --> stepF[Built In Electric Field Separates Charges]
    stepF --> stepG[Electrons Travel Through External Load]
    stepG --> stepH[DC Electrical Power Delivered to Circuit]
    stepH --> stepI[Inverter Converts DC to AC for Load or Grid]
    stepI --> endJ[Usable AC Power For Applications]
```

## 4.3 Solar Cell Equivalent Circuit (Block Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    lightA[Solar Irradiance G] --> genB[Photocurrent Source Iph]
    genB --> juncC[P N Junction Diode]
    juncC --> rsD[Series Resistance Rs]
    rsD --> termE[Output Terminal to Load]
    termF[Output Terminal Return] --> rshG[Shunt Resistance Rsh Parallel]
    rshG --> juncC
    loadH[External Load RL] --> termE
    loadH --> termF
```

## 4.4 PV Module and Array Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    cellA1[Solar Cell 1] --> cellA2[Solar Cell 2]
    cellA2 --> cellA3[Solar Cell N]
    cellA3 --> modB[PV Module: 36 To 144 Cells Sealed and Framed]
    modB --> modC[Module Produces 30 To 60 V DC Typical]
    modC --> arrD[Series Connection Increases Array Voltage]
    arrD --> arrE[Parallel Connection Increases Array Current]
    arrE --> arrF[PV Array: Multiple Modules Form Complete DC Source]
    arrF --> invG[Inverter Converts DC to AC]
    invG --> loadH[AC Load or Grid]
```

## 4.5 Single-Crystal Solar Cell Manufacturing Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rawA[High Purity Metallurgical Grade Silicon] --> purB[Purification to Solar Grade 99.9999 Percent Pure]
    purB --> czC[Czochralski Process: Single Crystal Ingot Growth]
    czC --> waferD[Wafer Slicing Into Thin Discs]
    waferD --> etchE[Surface Etching and Texturing]
    etchE --> diffF[Doping To Form P N Junction]
    diffG[Anti Reflective Coating Deposition] --> diffF
    diffF --> contH[Front and Back Metal Contact Printing]
    contH --> testI[Cell Testing and Sorting]
    testI --> modJ[Module Assembly With Multiple Cells]
    modJ --> lamK[Lamination Between Glass and Backsheet]
    lamK --> frL[Framing and Junction Box Installation]
    frL --> finalM[Finished Mono Crystalline PV Module]
```

## 4.6 Generations of Solar Cell Materials Timeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    gen1A[First Generation: Wafer Based Crystalline Silicon] --> gen1B[Examples: Mono Si and Multi Si]
    gen2A[Second Generation: Thin Film Technologies] --> gen2B[Examples: Amorphous Si, CdTe, CIGS]
    gen3A[Third Generation: Emerging Materials] --> gen3B[Examples: Perovskite, DSSC, Organic]
    gen4A[Fourth Generation: Hybrid and Multi Junction] --> gen4B[Examples: Tandem Cells, Quantum Dots]
    gen1B --> gen2A
    gen2B --> gen3A
    gen3B --> gen4A
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Each diagram is a **block-level functional flow** representing the physical/electrical process. Use these to answer KTU diagram-drawing questions (typically 4–6 marks each).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# Section 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Define the photovoltaic effect. List the four sequential steps in the energy conversion process of a solar cell.**

**Model Answer:**

> The photovoltaic effect is the phenomenon of generating a voltage and electric current in a semiconductor material upon exposure to light, due to the absorption of photons and subsequent separation of charge carriers at a p-n junction.
>
> The four sequential steps are:
> 1. **Photon Absorption:** A photon with energy $h\nu \geq E_g$ is absorbed by a valence electron in the semiconductor.
> 2. **Electron–Hole Pair Generation:** The electron is excited from the valence band to the conduction band, leaving a hole in the valence band.
> 3. **Charge Separation:** The built-in electric field at the p-n junction sweeps electrons toward the n-side and holes toward the p-side.
> 4. **Collection:** Metal contacts collect the separated charges, producing a photocurrent in the external circuit.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Distinguish between intrinsic, n-type, and p-type semiconductors with one example dopant each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Intrinsic | N-type | P-type |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Doping** | None (pure) | Donor (P, As) | Acceptor (B, Ga) |
| **Majority carriers** | Electrons = Holes | Electrons | Holes |
| **Minority carriers** | — | Holes | Electrons |
| **Example dopant** | — | Phosphorus | Boron |
| **Fermi level** | Mid-gap | Near CB | Near VB |
| **Role in solar cell** | Reference | Emitter (top) | Base (absorber) |

> **Marks Distribution:** Definition (1 mark), distinction table (2 marks).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**(a)** Derive the ideal solar cell I–V equation from first principles, starting from the equivalent circuit and applying Kirchhoff's Current Law. Clearly state the assumptions made. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A silicon solar cell at 300 K has $I_{ph} = 3.2$ A, $I_0 = 1.5 \times 10^{-9}$ A, and ideality factor $n = 1.2$. Calculate the open-circuit voltage $V_{oc}$ and the short-circuit current $I_{sc}$. Given $FF = 0.78$ and cell area $A = 0.024$ m² under STC ($G = 1000$ W/m²), determine the conversion efficiency. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1 — Equivalent Circuit Setup:**
The single-diode equivalent circuit consists of a current source $I_{ph}$, a diode with saturation current $I_0$, a series resistance $R_s$, and a shunt resistance $R_{sh}$.

**Step 2 — Apply KCL at the Output Node:**

$$I = I_{ph} - I_D - I_{sh}$$

**Step 3 — Express Diode Current (Shockley Equation):**

$$I_D = I_0 \left[\exp\!\left(\frac{q V_D}{n k T}\right) - 1\right]$$

where $V_D = V + I R_s$ (voltage across the diode, accounting for the series drop).

**Step 4 — Express Shunt Current (Ohm's Law):**

$$I_{sh} = \frac{V + I R_s}{R_{sh}}$$

**Step 5 — Combine All Terms:**

$$I = I_{ph} - I_0 \left[\exp\!\left(\frac{q (V + I R_s)}{n k T}\right) - 1\right] - \frac{V + I R_s}{R_{sh}}$$

**Step 6 — Ideal-Case Assumptions:**

For an ideal cell: $R_s = 0$ and $R_{sh} \to \infty$. The equation reduces to:

$$I_{ideal} = I_{ph} - I_0 \left[\exp\!\left(\frac{q V}{n k T}\right) - 1\right]$$

> **Assumptions Stated:** (i) Uniform generation in the depletion region, (ii) No recombination in the depletion region, (iii) Boltzmann statistics valid, (iv) One-dimensional current flow.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1 — Compute $V_{oc}$:**
Setting $I = 0$ in the ideal equation:

$$V_{oc} = \frac{n k T}{q} \ln\!\left(\frac{I_{ph}}{I_0} + 1\right)$$

Substituting values:

$$V_{oc} = \frac{(1.2)(1.381 \times 10^{-23})(300)}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}} \ln\!\left(\frac{3.2}{1.5 \times 10^{-9}} + 1\right)$$

$$V_{oc} = (0.03102) \times \ln(2.133 \times 10^{9})$$

$$V_{oc} = 0.03102 \times 21.485 = 0.667 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $I_{sc}$:**
For an ideal cell, $I_{sc} = I_{ph} = 3.2$ A.

**Step 3 — Compute Maximum Power:**

$$P_{max} = V_{oc} \times I_{sc} \times FF = 0.667 \times 3.2 \times 0.78 = 1.665 \text{ W}$$

**Step 4 — Compute Input Power:**

$$P_{in} = G \times A = 1000 \times 0.024 = 24 \text{ W}$$

**Step 5 — Compute Efficiency:**

$$\eta = \frac{P_{max}}{P_{in}} = \frac{1.665}{24} = 0.0694 = 6.94\%$$

> **Final Answer:** $V_{oc} = 0.667$ V, $I_{sc} = 3.2$ A, $\eta = 6.94\%$

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain the construction and working of a single-crystal silicon solar cell with a neat cross-sectional diagram. Discuss the role of the anti-reflective coating, front and back contacts, and the back-surface field. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 72-cell mono-crystalline PV module has each cell rated at $V_{oc} = 0.65$ V, $I_{sc} = 9.5$ A, and $FF = 0.80$. The cells are connected in series. Calculate: (i) Module open-circuit voltage, (ii) Module short-circuit current, (iii) Maximum module power, (iv) Module efficiency, given cell area = $156 \text{ cm}^2$ and $G = 1000$ W/m². **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Construction of a Mono-Si Solar Cell:**

A monocrystalline silicon solar cell is a thin (~200 μm) disc cut from a single-crystal ingot grown by the Czochralski process. The key structural layers from top to bottom are:

1. **Anti-Reflective Coating (ARC):** A thin layer (~70 nm) of silicon nitride (SiNₓ) or titanium dioxide (TiO₂) deposited on the front surface. **Role:** Reduces reflection from ~30% (bare Si) to ~3%, increasing photon absorption.

2. **Front Contact (Grid):** A pattern of silver (Ag) fingers and busbars screen-printed on the ARC. **Role:** Collects electrons from the n-type emitter with minimal shadowing (typically < 5% area coverage).

3. **N-type Emitter:** A thin (~0.5 μm) n-region formed by phosphorus diffusion on top of the p-type base. **Role:** Forms the p-n junction and provides the built-in field for charge separation.

4. **P-type Base (Absorber):** The bulk of the cell, lightly boron-doped (10¹⁵–10¹⁶ cm⁻³). **Role:** Absorbs most of the incident photons and generates the majority of electron-hole pairs.

5. **Back-Surface Field (BSF):** A heavily doped p⁺ region at the back contact. **Role:** Creates a potential barrier that reflects minority carriers (electrons) away from the rear contact, reducing recombination.

6. **Back Contact:** A full-area aluminum (Al) layer screen-printed on the rear. **Role:** Collects holes and provides the back electrical contact.

**Working Principle:**
- Photons enter through the ARC and are absorbed in the base.
- Each absorbed photon generates one EHP.
- The p-n junction's built-in field separates the carriers.
- Electrons travel to the n-side and through the front grid, holes travel to the p-side and through the back contact, producing current in the external load.

> **Marks Distribution:** Construction details (3), diagram description (2), role of each component (2).

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given:** $N_s = 72$ cells in series, $V_{oc,cell} = 0.65$ V, $I_{sc,cell} = 9.5$ A, $FF = 0.80$, $A_{cell} = 156 \text{ cm}^2 = 0.0156 \text{ m}^2$, $G = 1000$ W/m².

**(i) Module Open-Circuit Voltage:**
Cells in series add voltages:

$$V_{oc,mod} = N_s \times V_{oc,cell} = 72 \times 0.65 = 46.8 \text{ V}$$

**(ii) Module Short-Circuit Current:**
Cells in series carry the same current:

$$I_{sc,mod} = I_{sc,cell} = 9.5 \text{ A}$$

**(iii) Maximum Module Power:**

$$P_{max,mod} = V_{oc,mod} \times I_{sc,mod} \times FF = 46.8 \times 9.5 \times 0.80 = 355.68 \text{ W}$$

**(iv) Module Efficiency:**
Total module area:

$$A_{mod} = 72 \times 0.0156 = 1.1232 \text{ m}^2$$

Input power:

$$P_{in,mod} = G \times A_{mod} = 1000 \times 1.1232 = 1123.2 \text{ W}$$

Module efficiency:

$$\eta_{mod} = \frac{P_{max,mod}}{P_{in,mod}} = \frac{355.68}{1123.2} = 0.3167 = 31.67\%$$

> **Sanity Check:** This efficiency is unrealistically high for a commercial module (typically 18–22%). The discrepancy arises because real modules have packing factor losses, mismatch, and resistive losses. In KTU, the **calculated value** is the expected answer.

> **Final Answer:** (i) 46.8 V, (ii) 9.5 A, (iii) 355.68 W, (iv) 31.67%

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Solar PV Problems:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the ideality factor $n$:** In $V_{oc}$ and I–V equations, students often drop the $n$ or use $n = 1$ always. For real cells, $n$ is between 1 and 2 — including it correctly earns the full mark.
>
> 2. **Unit Inconsistency:** Mixing $cm^2$ and $m^2$ for area. Always convert to SI ($m^2$) before plugging into $G \times A$.
>
> 3. **Sign Convention Errors:** Diode current is **subtracted** from $I_{ph}$. Forgetting the negative sign gives an answer of negative current — guaranteed zero.
>
> 4. **Confusing $I_{sc}$ with $I_{ph}$:** For ideal cells they are equal, but in real cells with finite $R_{sh}$, $I_{sc} < I_{ph}$.
>
> 5. **Series vs. Parallel Module Connections:** Series adds **voltage**; parallel adds **current**. Mixing these up in array problems costs 3–4 marks easily.
>
> 6. **Skipping the diagram:** In construction-type questions, a labeled diagram (even schematic) is worth 2–3 marks by itself. Always draw.
>
> 7. **Forgetting the $\ln(+1)$ term in $V_{oc}$:** The full equation is $V_{oc} = (nkT/q)\ln(I_{ph}/I_0 + 1)$. Although the +1 is often dropped, examiners may deduct a mark for omission if explicitly asked for the "complete" equation.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### High-Density Revision Checklist

- [ ] **Photovoltaic Effect** = Photon absorption → EHP generation → charge separation at p-n junction → current collection.
- [ ] **Bandgap Rule:** A photon is absorbed only if $h\nu \geq E_g$. Silicon's $E_g = 1.12$ eV.
- [ ] **Three Generations (legacy 4):** Crystalline Si (1st) → Thin-film (2nd) → Emerging (3rd) → Multi-junction/Tandem (4th).
- [ ] **Six Key Parameters:** $V_{oc}$, $I_{sc}$, $V_{mp}$, $I_{mp}$, $FF$, $\eta$.
- [ ] **Single-Diode Equation:** $I = I_{ph} - I_0[\exp(qV/nkT) - 1] - (V/R_{sh})$.
- [ ] **Open-Circuit Voltage:** $V_{oc} \approx (nkT/q)\ln(I_{ph}/I_0)$.
- [ ] **Short-Circuit Current:** $I_{sc} \approx I_{ph}$, proportional to $G$.
- [ ] **Fill Factor:** $FF = V_{mp} I_{mp} / V_{oc} I_{sc}$, typically 0.7–0.85.
- [ ] **Efficiency:** $\eta = V_{oc} I_{sc} FF / (G A)$.
- [ ] **Series vs. Parallel:** Modules in series add voltage; in parallel add current. Arrays combine both.
- [ ] **Single-Crystal Si:** Highest commercial efficiency, made by Czochralski process, dark black appearance.
- [ ] **STC Conditions:** $G = 1000$ W/m², AM 1.5, $T = 25°C$.
- [ ] **Anti-Reflective Coating:** Reduces reflection losses, typically SiNₓ of ~70 nm.
- [ ] **Back-Surface Field:** p⁺ layer that reflects minority carriers and reduces recombination.
- [ ] **Built-in Potential:** $V_{bi} = (kT/q)\ln(N_A N_D / n_i^2)$ — the upper limit for $V_{oc}$.
- [ ] **Temperature Coefficient:** $V_{oc}$ decreases ~2 mV/°C; $I_{sc}$ slightly increases; $\eta$ drops ~0.4–0.5%/°C.
- [ ] **Constant Memory:** $kT/q \approx 25.85$ mV at 300 K — used everywhere in solar cell equations.
- [ ] **Diode Ideality Factor $n$:** 1 for ideal diffusion current; 2 for recombination-dominated; real cells: 1.0–1.5.

### Quick-Recall Numerical Values

- Silicon bandgap: **1.12 eV**
- GaAs bandgap: **1.42 eV**
- CdTe bandgap: **1.45 eV**
- Perovskite (MAPbI₃) bandgap: **1.55 eV**
- Optimal single-junction bandgap (Shockley–Queisser): **~1.34 eV**
- Best lab cell efficiency (Si): **26.7%** (LONGi, 2024)
- Best multi-junction lab efficiency: **47.1%** (NREL, 2024)
- Typical commercial mono-Si module: **20–22%**
- Typical commercial poly-Si module: **17–19%**
- Typical thin-film module: **10–15%**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thin-Film PV Modules
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEET721 - DESIGN OF SOLAR PV SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Thin-Film PV Modules

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Thin-Film Photovoltaic (TFPV) Modules** are second-generation solar cells manufactured by depositing one or more thin layers (typically **1 µm to 10 µm** thick) of photovoltaic semiconductor material onto a low-cost substrate such as **glass, stainless steel, plastic, or flexible polymer foil**. Unlike first-generation crystalline silicon (c-Si) cells which use **180 µm – 300 µm** thick wafers, thin-film modules use direct deposition techniques (PVD, CVD, sputtering, evaporation) to form the absorber layer.

The four dominant thin-film technologies studied in the KTU 2024 syllabus are:

| Technology | Chemical Formula | Bandgap (eV) |
|---|---|---|
| Amorphous Silicon | a-Si:H | ~1.7 |
| Cadmium Telluride | CdTe | ~1.45 |
| Copper Indium Gallium Selenide | Cu(In,Ga)Se₂ | 1.0 – 1.7 (tunable) |
| Copper Indium Sulfide | CuInS₂ (CIS) | ~1.5 |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Thin-film modules are covered under the broader topic "Solar Cell Technologies" with emphasis on construction, working principle, efficiency, advantages, limitations, and comparison with crystalline silicon modules. Students must be able to identify the bandgap engineering principle behind CIS/CIGS and the Staebler-Wronski degradation in a-Si.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine building a wall:
- **Crystalline silicon** is like **stacking full-sized bricks** — each brick is heavy, takes a lot of raw material (silicon), and the wall is thick.
- **Thin-film** is like **spray-painting the wall** with a special photovoltaic "paint" — only a few micrometers thick, very little material needed, and you can even spray it on flexible surfaces like roll-up plastic!

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it matters:** Because thin films use **100 – 1000× less semiconductor material** than c-Si wafers, they promise **lower cost per watt** ($/W) — the holy grail of solar economics. The trade-off is **lower module efficiency** (typically 7% – 18%) compared to mono c-Si (20% – 24%).

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard reference values for thin-film calculations (use bold in derivations):**
> - **Solar constant (AM1.5G)**: **G_sc = 1361 W/m²** (extraterrestrial), **1000 W/m²** (STC standard)
> - **Planck's constant**: **h = 6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s**
> - **Electronic charge**: **q = 1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ C**
> - **Boltzmann constant**: **k_B = 1.381 × 10⁻²³ J/K**
> - **Bandgap of a-Si:H**: **E_g ≈ 1.7 eV**
> - **Bandgap of CdTe**: **E_g ≈ 1.45 eV**
> - **Bandgap of CIGS**: **E_g ≈ 1.1 – 1.2 eV** (typical for high-efficiency devices)
> - **Absorption coefficient of a-Si**: **α ≥ 10⁴ cm⁻¹** (100× higher than c-Si, enabling micron-thick absorbers)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1 — Substrate Preparation**
A low-cost carrier (soda-lime glass for rigid modules, polyimide/foil for flexible) is cleaned and acts as both mechanical support and (for CdTe/CIGS) as the source of Na which improves grain growth.

**Step 2 — Back Contact Deposition**
A metallic back contact (Mo for CIGS, SnO₂:F for CdTe) is sputtered or evaporated. This contact must form an **ohmic** (non-rectifying) junction with the absorber.

**Step 3 — Absorber Deposition**
The light-absorbing semiconductor is deposited via:
- **a-Si:H** → Plasma-Enhanced Chemical Vapor Deposition (PECVD) of silane (SiH₄)
- **CdTe** → Close-Spaced Sublimation (CSS), Vapor Transport Deposition (VTD), or electrodeposition
- **CIGS** → Co-evaporation of Cu, In, Ga, Se or selenization of metallic precursors

**Step 4 — Junction Formation**
A thin **n-type window layer** is added (CdS for CdTe/CIGS; a-SiC:H or µc-Si:H for a-Si tandem cells). The p-n (or p-i-n for a-Si) junction separates photogenerated carriers.

**Step 5 — Front Contact / TCO**
A **Transparent Conducting Oxide (TCO)** — typically **ITO** (Indium Tin Oxide) or **SnO₂:F** (FTO) — is sputtered on top. It transmits visible light (~80%–90%) while conducting current laterally.

**Step 6 — Encapsulation**
EVA (Ethylene-Vinyl Acetate) and a second glass cover provide environmental protection — required for the **25-year warranty** mandate.

## 2.2 The Core "Why" — Direct Bandgap Advantage

> [!NOTE]
> **Why thin-film needs only ~1 µm instead of ~200 µm:**
> The **absorption coefficient α** of direct-bandgap semiconductors (CdTe, CIGS, a-Si) is **>10⁴ cm⁻¹**, while indirect-bandgap c-Si has α ≈ 10³ cm⁻¹ near its band edge. Per the Beer-Lambert law, the penetration depth $1/\alpha$ for direct semiconductors is in the **sub-micron range**, so nearly all usable sunlight is absorbed in a **film thinner than a human hair (≈ 50 µm)**.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $I_{ph} = q \cdot A \cdot G \cdot (1 - R)$ | Photogenerated current (short-circuit approximation) | A |
| 2 | $I = I_{ph} - I_0 \left[ \exp\!\left(\dfrac{qV}{nk_BT}\right) - 1 \right]$ | Single-diode model (also applies to thin-film cells) | A |
| 3 | $V_{oc} = \dfrac{n k_B T}{q} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{I_{ph}}{I_0} + 1\right)$ | Open-circuit voltage | V |
| 4 | $I_{ph} \propto \int \alpha(\lambda) \cdot S(\lambda) \, d\lambda$ | Spectral current depends on absorption coefficient | A/m² |
| 5 | $\eta_{cell} = \dfrac{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc} \cdot FF}{P_{in}}$ | Cell efficiency | dimensionless (%) |
| 6 | $FF = \dfrac{V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp}}{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}}$ | Fill Factor | dimensionless |
| 7 | $E_g^{CIGS}(x) = 1.01 + 0.626x - 0.167x(1-x)$ | Bandgap vs Ga/(Ga+In) ratio $x$ | eV |
| 8 | $P_{max}(T) = P_{STC}\left[1 + \dfrac{\gamma}{100}(T - 25)\right]$ | Power vs temperature with temp. coefficient $\gamma$ | W |
| 9 | $\eta_{quantum} = 1 - e^{-\alpha d}$ | Fraction of photons absorbed in film of thickness $d$ | dimensionless |
| 10 | $C_{system} = \dfrac{C_{BOS}}{\eta_{module} \cdot A}$ | LCOE insight: low efficiency increases Balance-of-System cost | $/m² |

> [!WARNING]
> **Trick: Do NOT use the vertical pipe `|` symbol in formulas inside this table.** The Markdown parser will break the column structure. Always use `\vert` (e.g., $\vert V_{oc} \vert$) for absolute value or magnitude.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **BIPV (Building-Integrated PV)**: Flexible CIGS laminates are bonded to curved rooftops.
- **Space satellites**: a-Si:H triple-junction cells are used because of their **radiation hardness** (Staebler-Wronski effect saturates after ~1000 hours).
- **Utility-scale farms**: CdTe leader First Solar deploys >50 GW globally; CIGS holds the **world record for thin-film efficiency at 23.35% (NREL, 2023)**.
- **Off-grid consumer products**: Solar calculators, IoT sensors, and watches almost universally use a-Si:H due to its low cost and indoor-light sensitivity.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Beer-Lambert Absorption in Thin Film

**Goal:** Derive the fraction of incident photons absorbed in a thin-film layer of thickness $d$, starting from the intensity decay law.

**Step 1: Define incident monochromatic photon flux**
Let $S_0(\lambda)$ be the number of photons per second per unit area at wavelength $\lambda$ entering the top surface of the thin film. The corresponding energy flux is $G(\lambda) = S_0(\lambda) \cdot \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$.

**Step 2: Apply the differential form of Beer-Lambert's law**
The rate of photon loss with depth $x$ inside the semiconductor is proportional to the local intensity:

$$
\frac{dS(x)}{dx} = -\alpha(\lambda) \cdot S(x)
$$

This states that as light travels an infinitesimal distance $dx$, the loss in photon flux equals the local flux times the absorption coefficient $\alpha$.

**Step 3: Separate variables and integrate**

$$
\int_{S_0}^{S(d)} \frac{dS}{S} = -\alpha \int_{0}^{d} dx
$$

$$
\ln\!\left(\frac{S(d)}{S_0}\right) = -\alpha d
$$

**Step 4: Solve for transmitted flux**

$$
S(d) = S_0 \, e^{-\alpha d}
$$

**Step 5: Compute absorbed fraction (internal quantum efficiency component)**

$$
\eta_{abs}(\lambda) = \frac{S_0 - S(d)}{S_0} = 1 - e^{-\alpha d}
$$

**Step 6: Numerical illustration for a-Si:H**
For a-Si:H, $\alpha \approx 10^{4} \text{ cm}^{-1}$ at $\lambda = 600 \text{ nm}$. Take $d = 1 \mu\text{m} = 10^{-4} \text{ cm}$:

$$
\eta_{abs} = 1 - e^{-(10^{4})(10^{-4})} = 1 - e^{-1} = 1 - 0.3679 = 0.6321
$$

So **63% of 600-nm photons are absorbed in just 1 µm** of a-Si:H.

**Step 7: Same calculation for c-Si (indirect)**
For c-Si at the same wavelength, $\alpha \approx 3 \times 10^{3} \text{ cm}^{-1}$. With $d = 200 \mu\text{m} = 2 \times 10^{-2} \text{ cm}$:

$$
\eta_{abs} = 1 - e^{-(3 \times 10^{3})(2 \times 10^{-2})} = 1 - e^{-60} \approx 1.000
$$

c-Si must be 200× thicker to achieve **near-total absorption**.

## 3.2 Derivation — Tunable Bandgap of CIGS

The bandgap of Cu(In₁₋ₓGaₓ)Se₂ varies linearly-with-bow with Ga fraction $x$:

$$
E_g(x) = E_g^{CuInSe_2} + (E_g^{CuGaSe_2} - E_g^{CuInSe_2}) x + bx(1-x)
$$

Using experimentally fitted constants $E_g^{CuInSe_2} = 1.01$ eV, $E_g^{CuGaSe_2} = 1.68$ eV, and the optical bowing $b = -0.167$ eV, we get the KTU reference formula:

$$
E_g(x) = 1.01 + 0.626x - 0.167 x(1-x) \;\; [\text{eV}]
$$

**Numerical example — Pure CIGS with x = 0.3 (typical for record cells):**

$$
E_g(0.3) = 1.01 + 0.626(0.3) - 0.167(0.3)(0.7)
$$

$$
= 1.01 + 0.1878 - 0.0351 = 1.163 \;\text{eV}
$$

This matches the experimentally observed optimal bandgap near **1.15 eV** for maximum efficiency under AM1.5G.

## 3.3 Derivation — Temperature De-rating for Thin-Film Modules

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Thin-film modules have a **smaller temperature coefficient** than c-Si, making them preferred for hot climates (e.g., Kerala — average cell temperature 50–65 °C).

**Step 1: Power at any cell temperature T (°C)**

$$
P_{max}(T) = P_{STC} \left[ 1 + \frac{\gamma}{100}(T - 25) \right]
$$

**Step 2: Typical thin-film γ values**

| Technology | γ (%/°C) |
|---|---|
| c-Si (mono) | −0.40 to −0.45 |
| **CdTe** | **−0.25** |
| **CIGS** | **−0.30 to −0.36** |
| **a-Si:H** | **−0.20 to −0.25** |

**Step 3: Worked example**
A 100 W CdTe module operates at cell temperature 60 °C. Loss in output:

$$
\Delta P = 100 \times \left[1 + \frac{(-0.25)}{100}(60 - 25) \right] = 100 \times [1 - 0.0875] = 91.25 \;\text{W}
$$

So CdTe loses only **8.75 W**, whereas an equivalent mono c-Si module would lose **~15.75 W** — a critical advantage for Keralite rooftop installations.

## 3.4 Derivation — Staebler-Wronski Degradation (a-Si:H)

**Step 1: Phenomenon**
In 1977, Staebler & Wronski observed that a-Si:H's photoconductivity and dark conductivity decrease under prolonged illumination, stabilizing after **~1000 hours** at ~70%–80% of initial efficiency. The effect is reversible upon annealing at 150–200 °C.

**Step 2: Microscopic model**
Light generates mobile carriers that gain kinetic energy, breaking weak Si–Si bonds and creating **dangling-bond defects** (mid-gap states). These new defects act as recombination centers, reducing the **minority carrier lifetime τ** and therefore the **diffusion length L**:

$$
L = \sqrt{D \tau}
$$

**Step 3: Effect on cell performance**
With $L$ reduced below the absorber thickness, fewer carriers reach the junction, decreasing both $I_{sc}$ and $V_{oc}$. Typical degradation: from ~10% initial to ~7% stabilized.

**Step 4: Engineering solution — Multi-junction (tandem) cells**
By stacking cells of different bandgaps, each sub-cell operates at a thinner current-matched thickness, dramatically reducing the Staebler-Wronski loss. This is why modern a-Si modules are almost always **double- or triple-junction**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Cross-Sectional Layer Stack — Generic Thin-Film Module

Below is the layered physical structure of a CdTe/CIGS-style superstrate configuration. The Mermaid block renders a **block-level functional architecture** rather than a physical scale drawing (safer to compile).

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Top Cover: Low-iron Tempered Glass, 3.2 mm]
    B[Encapsulant Layer 1: EVA, 0.45 mm]
    C[Front Contact: TCO SnO2:F or ITO, 0.5 micrometer]
    D[Window Layer: CdS or ZnS, 50 nm]
    E[Absorber Layer: CdTe or CIGS, 1 to 3 micrometer]
    F[Back Contact: Mo or Graphite paste, 0.5 micrometer]
    G[Encapsulant Layer 2: EVA, 0.45 mm]
    H[Back Sheet: Tedlar or Glass, for weather protection]
    I[Junction Box with Bypass Diodes]

    A --> B --> C --> D --> E --> F --> G --> H
    F -.cable.-> I

    classDef glass fill:#cce6ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef semi fill:#ffe6cc,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000
    classDef contact fill:#e6e6e6,stroke:#333,color:#000
    classDef encap fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#996600,color:#000

    class A,H glass
    class D,E semi
    class C,F contact
    class B,G encap
```

## 4.2 Comparison Flow — Manufacturing Routes

```mermaid
flowchart LR

      A1[SiH4 Gas] --> A2[PECVD Chamber]
      A2 --> A3[p-i-n Stack on TCO Glass]
      A3 --> A4[Laser Scribing for Monolithic Interconnect]
    end

    subgraph TFT_CDTE[CdTe Route]
      B1[High-Purity Cd and Te] --> B2[Close-Spaced Sublimation]
      B2 --> B3[CdTe on CdS TCO]
      B3 --> B4[Heat Treatment in CdCl2 Ambient]
    end

    subgraph TFT_CIGS[CIGS Route]
      C1[Cu, In, Ga, Se Sources] --> C2[Co-evaporation OR Selenization]
      C2 --> C3[CIGS on Mo-coated Soda Lime Glass]
      C3 --> C4[Chemical Bath Deposition of CdS]
    end

    A4 --> MOD[Final Module Assembly]
    B4 --> MOD
    C4 --> MOD

    classDef route fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366,color:#000

```

## 4.3 Performance Decision Tree — Selecting a Thin-Film Technology

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Application?}
    Q1 -->|Rooftop curved surface| CIS[CIGS flexible laminate]
    Q1 -->|Utility-scale flat ground| CDTE[CdTe rigid glass-glass]
    Q1 -->|Indoor IoT, calculators| ASI[a-Si:H low cost]
    Q1 -->|High temperature climate| HOT[Prioritize low temp coefficient: a-Si or CdTe]

    CIS --> C1{Budget flexible?}
    C1 -->|Yes| CIGSOK[Choose CIGS]
    C1 -->|No| MONO[Use mono c-Si instead]

    CDTE --> D1{Land area constraint?}
    D1 -->|Plenty of land| CDTEOK[Choose CdTe for LCOE]
    D1 -->|Space-limited| MONO2[Use mono c-Si]

    HOT --> H1[Pick a-Si or CdTe with gamma less than -0.30]
```

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List any three advantages of thin-film PV modules over crystalline silicon modules.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Drastically reduced semiconductor material usage** — only 1 µm to 3 µm of absorber versus 180 µm wafer; lower raw-material cost (key enabler of low $/W) *(1 mark)*.
2. **Lower temperature coefficient** (typically −0.25 %/°C for CdTe and a-Si versus −0.45 %/°C for mono c-Si), giving better performance in hot climates like Kerala *(1 mark)*.
3. **Flexible and lightweight form factor** when deposited on polyimide/steel foil, enabling Building-Integrated PV (BIPV) on curved roofs and portable applications *(1 mark)*.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is the Staebler-Wronski effect? How is it mitigated in modern a-Si:H modules?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 marks):** The Staebler-Wronski effect is the light-induced degradation of a-Si:H in which prolonged illumination creates dangling-bond defects, reducing photoconductivity and stabilizing efficiency at **~70–80%** of the initial value.
- **Mitigation (1.5 marks):** Modern modules use **multi-junction (tandem/triple) cells** in which each sub-cell is thinner and operates at its own optimal bandgap. Combined with hydrogen dilution during PECVD and protocrystalline silicon growth, the stabilized efficiency improves to ~10%–12%.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**a)** With a neat layered diagram, explain the construction and working principle of a **Copper Indium Gallium Selenide (CIGS) thin-film solar cell**. Discuss the role of the CdS window layer and Mo back contact. *(7 marks)*

**b)** Derive the expression for **bandgap tuning** of Cu(In₁₋ₓGaₓ)Se₂ as a function of Ga fraction $x$. Calculate $E_g$ for $x = 0.25$ and comment on why CIGS achieves higher $V_{oc}$ than pure CuInSe₂. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Construction and Working (7 marks)**

*Layered structure (top to bottom):* (Use the diagram from Section 4.1 as reference.)

1. **Substrate**: Soda-lime glass (3 mm). Na diffuses from glass into CIGS, enhancing grain growth and p-type doping *(1 mark)*.
2. **Back contact**: Molybdenum (~0.5 µm) sputtered. Forms ohmic contact with CIGS; its work function matches the valence band of Cu(In,Ga)Se₂. *(1 mark)*
3. **Absorber**: CIGS layer (1–2 µm) deposited by co-evaporation. This p-type layer absorbs photons and generates electron-hole pairs. The bandgap can be tuned from 1.01 eV (pure CIS) to 1.68 eV (pure CGS) by adjusting Ga/In ratio. *(1 mark)*
4. **Buffer/Window layer**: CdS (~50 nm) deposited by chemical bath deposition. Acts as n-type heterojunction partner, has wide bandgap (~2.4 eV) so it lets visible light pass through to the absorber. *(1 mark)*
5. **TCO front contact**: ZnO:Al or ITO (~0.5 µm) sputtered. Provides lateral conduction while being >85% transparent to sunlight. *(1 mark)*
6. **Working principle**: Photons with energy $h\nu > E_g$ generate electron-hole pairs in CIGS. The built-in electric field at the p-CIGS/n-CdS junction sweeps electrons toward CdS and TCO; holes travel to Mo back contact. *(1 mark)*
7. **Role of Na**: Diffuses from glass → Mo (via pinholes) → CIGS grain boundaries, increasing hole concentration and grain size. *(1 mark)*

**Part (b) — Bandgap Derivation (7 marks)**

Step 1 — Start from the general expression for a quaternary alloy with bowing parameter:

$$
E_g(x) = E_g^{A} + (E_g^{B} - E_g^{A})x + b x(1-x)
$$

Step 2 — Substitute $A$ = CuInSe₂ with $E_g^{A} = 1.01$ eV; $B$ = CuGaSe₂ with $E_g^{B} = 1.68$ eV; bowing coefficient $b = -0.167$ eV:

$$
E_g(x) = 1.01 + (1.68 - 1.01)x - 0.167 x(1-x)
$$

Step 3 — Simplify:

$$
E_g(x) = 1.01 + 0.67x - 0.167x + 0.167x^2
$$

$$
E_g(x) = 1.01 + 0.503x + 0.167x^2 \;\; [\text{eV}]
$$

> **Note**: The widely tabulated KTU form $E_g(x) = 1.01 + 0.626x - 0.167x(1-x)$ comes from a refined polynomial fit. We use that version:

$$
E_g(x) = 1.01 + 0.626x - 0.167 x(1-x)
$$

Step 4 — Substitute $x = 0.25$:

$$
E_g(0.25) = 1.01 + 0.626(0.25) - 0.167(0.25)(0.75)
$$

$$
= 1.01 + 0.1565 - 0.0313 = 1.1352 \;\text{eV}
$$

Step 5 — Comment on $V_{oc}$ improvement *(2 marks)*:
Per the diode equation $V_{oc} \approx (n k_B T/q) \ln(I_{ph}/I_0)$, increasing $E_g$ exponentially reduces the reverse saturation current $I_0$ (since $I_0 \propto \exp(-E_g/k_BT)$). Hence moving from pure CIS ($E_g = 1.01$ eV) to CIGS with $x = 0.25$ ($E_g = 1.135$ eV) **raises $V_{oc}$ by roughly 50–80 mV**, improving overall efficiency. The optimal $x \approx 0.3$ balances the higher $V_{oc}$ against the loss in $I_{sc}$ (broader bandgap absorbs fewer photons).

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**a)** Compare **amorphous silicon (a-Si:H)**, **CdTe**, and **CIGS** thin-film technologies in terms of bandgap, typical efficiency, temperature coefficient, and degradation mechanism. *(7 marks)*

**b)** A 200 W CdTe module has a temperature coefficient of power of −0.25 %/°C. If the cell operates at 65 °C under STC irradiance of 1000 W/m², calculate (i) the actual power output and (ii) the equivalent loss in daily energy if it operates for 6 peak-equivalent hours. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Comparison Table (7 marks)**

| Property | a-Si:H | CdTe | CIGS |
|---|---|---|---|
| Bandgap (eV) | 1.7 | 1.45 | 1.0 – 1.7 (tunable) |
| Typical module η | 6 – 9 % | 16 – 19 % | 14 – 18 % |
| Record cell η | 14 % | 22.1 % | **23.35 %** (NREL) |
| Temperature coefficient (γ) | −0.20 to −0.25 %/°C | −0.25 %/°C | −0.30 to −0.36 %/°C |
| Degradation mechanism | **Staebler-Wronski** (light-induced) | Cu back-contact diffusion; not a major concern | Moisture-induced; needs proper encapsulation |
| Toxicity | None | **Cd is toxic** | None |
| Abundance of raw materials | Excellent (silicon) | Te is scarce | In is scarce |

*(1.5 marks for table; remaining 5.5 marks for narrative)*

Narrative:
- **a-Si:H** uses the most abundant raw material (silicon) but suffers from the Staebler-Wronski effect that initially drops efficiency by ~20–30%, mitigated by tandem/triple-junction designs *(1.5 marks)*.
- **CdTe** currently holds the largest market share among thin films due to its near-optimum bandgap of 1.45 eV and the lowest LCOE in utility-scale plants. The cadmium toxicity and tellurium scarcity are engineering challenges *(1.5 marks)*.
- **CIGS** offers the highest laboratory efficiency of any thin-film technology (23.35%) and a tunable bandgap via Ga fraction. It has good radiation tolerance and is used in space and BIPV *(1 mark)*.

**Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks)**

(i) **Actual power output** *(3 marks)*:

$$
P_{max}(T) = P_{STC}\left[1 + \frac{\gamma}{100}(T - 25)\right]
$$

$$
P_{max}(65) = 200 \left[1 + \frac{(-0.25)}{100}(65 - 25)\right] = 200 \left[1 - 0.10\right] = 180 \;\text{W}
$$

(ii) **Daily energy loss** *(4 marks)*:

Daily energy at STC for 6 hours:

$$
E_{STC} = 200 \times 6 = 1200 \;\text{Wh}
$$

Actual daily energy:

$$
E_{actual} = 180 \times 6 = 1080 \;\text{Wh}
$$

Loss:

$$
\Delta E = 1200 - 1080 = 120 \;\text{Wh/day} = 0.12 \;\text{kWh/day}
$$

Percentage loss:

$$
\frac{\Delta E}{E_{STC}} \times 100 = \frac{120}{1200} \times 100 = 10\%
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Sign of γ**: Always substitute γ with its **negative** value (e.g., −0.25, NOT +0.25). Forgetting the minus sign is the most common 1-mark error.
> 2. **Don't confuse cell temperature with ambient temperature**: The cell is typically 20–30 °C hotter than ambient. If the question gives ambient temperature, you must add the NOCT correction (typically +25–30 °C).
> 3. **Units of γ**: Must be in **%/°C**, not fraction/°C. If a question gives γ = −0.0025/°C, convert to −0.25 %/°C before plugging into the equation.
> 4. **Be precise**: KTU expects the answer to two decimal places for $E_g$ calculations and to one decimal for power; rounding too aggressively loses marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Thin-Film PV Modules**

- ✅ **Definition**: Thin-film modules use **1 – 10 µm** absorber layers, deposited on glass/foil substrates, versus ~200 µm c-Si wafers.
- ✅ **Four major types**: **a-Si:H**, **CdTe**, **CIGS**, **CIS**. Bandgaps are **1.7, 1.45, 1.0–1.7, 1.5 eV** respectively.
- ✅ **Direct bandgap advantage**: $\alpha > 10^4$ cm⁻¹ gives $\eta_{abs} > 60\%$ in just **1 µm**.
- ✅ **Standard structure (top → bottom)**: Glass → EVA → TCO → Window (CdS) → Absorber → Back contact (Mo) → EVA → Back sheet.
- ✅ **CIGS bandgap formula (KTU-mandated)**:
  $E_g(x) = 1.01 + 0.626x - 0.167 x(1-x)$ eV, where $x$ = Ga/(Ga+In).
- ✅ **Optimal Ga fraction**: $x \approx 0.3$ gives $E_g \approx 1.15$ eV (best efficiency match to AM1.5G).
- ✅ **Temperature coefficient ranking** (best to worst): a-Si:H (~ −0.20) > CdTe (−0.25) > CIGS (−0.30 to −0.36) > mono c-Si (−0.45).
- ✅ **Staebler-Wronski effect**: Reversible light-induced degradation of a-Si:H; mitigated by **multi-junction (tandem)** architectures.
- ✅ **Power temperature formula**:
  $P_{max}(T) = P_{STC}[1 + (\gamma/100)(T - 25)]$. **Sign of γ is negative**.
- ✅ **Efficiency record holders**: CdTe cell 22.1 % (First Solar); CIGS cell **23.35 %** (NREL); a-Si:H cell ~14 %.
- ✅ **Market dominance**: CdTe holds ~50% of thin-film market; total thin-film share of global PV ~5–10% (c-Si dominates ~90%).
- ✅ **Kerala-specific application**: Choose CdTe or a-Si:H for rooftops because of the **low temperature coefficient** — significant energy retention in tropical heat.
- ✅ **Toxicity & recycling**: CdTe requires specialized recycling (EU WEEE directive). CIGS and a-Si:H are environmentally benign.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEET721 - DESIGN OF SOLAR PV SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** III-V Single Junction and Multifunction PV Modules, Emerging and New PV Systems, Packing Factor of the PV Module, Efficiency of the PV Module, Energy Balance Equations for PV Modules, Series and Parallel Combination of PV Modules, Effect of Shadowing, MPPT Techniques, P&O and Incremental Conductance Method, Maximum Power Point Tracker (MPPT) Using Buck-Boost Converter

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 III-V Single Junction and Multi-Junction PV Modules

### Formal Definition
**III-V Semiconductor PV Modules** are photovoltaic devices fabricated from compound semiconductors belonging to Groups III and V of the periodic table (e.g., Gallium Arsenide — GaAs, Indium Phosphide — InP, Gallium Indium Phosphide — GaInP). A **Single Junction (SJ)** cell uses one p-n junction to absorb photons, while a **Multi-Junction (MJ)** cell stacks multiple p-n junctions (sub-cells) of different bandgap materials to capture a broader spectrum of solar radiation.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Multi-junction cells are the workhorse of space PV and concentrated PV (CPV) systems, achieving the **highest demonstrated efficiencies (over 47% under concentration)**.

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine filling a bucket with water from a tap. A single junction cell is like a bucket with only **one opening at one fixed height** — water above the opening spills, and water below it is missed. A multi-junction cell is like a **stacked series of buckets at different heights** — each bucket captures the water that flows past the one above it. Higher-energy photons (shorter wavelengths) are absorbed by the top (larger bandgap) cell, while lower-energy photons (longer wavelengths) pass through and are absorbed by lower (smaller bandgap) cells.

## 1.2 Emerging and New PV Systems

### Formal Definition
**Emerging PV Systems** refer to next-generation photovoltaic technologies that are not yet in widespread commercial production but represent significant advances in efficiency, flexibility, or cost. These include **Perovskite cells, Organic Photovoltaics (OPV), Dye-Sensitized Solar Cells (DSSC), Copper Indium Gallium Selenide (CIGS), Cadmium Telluride (CdTe), and Quantum Dot Solar Cells**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Perovskite/Silicon Tandems** have crossed **33.9% certified efficiency** (NREL 2024 chart), making them the fastest-advancing PV technology in history.

### Conceptual Analogy
Think of emerging PV as the **"electric vehicle evolution"** of solar — just as EVs are replacing traditional internal combustion engines, perovskites and tandems are replacing single-junction silicon in the long-term efficiency race.

## 1.3 Packing Factor of the PV Module

### Formal Definition
**Packing Factor (PF)** or **Fill Factor of the Module** is the ratio of the **active solar cell area** to the **total module area** (including the inactive areas such as the border, gaps between cells, and busbars). It is a dimensionless quantity between **0 and 1** (typically **0.7 to 0.9**).

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine packing square cookies into a rectangular lunchbox. The **packing factor** is the fraction of the lunchbox bottom actually covered by cookies. The empty space between cookies is like the gaps and borders in a PV module.

## 1.4 Efficiency of the PV Module

### Formal Definition
**PV Module Efficiency ($\eta_{mod}$)** is the ratio of the **electrical power output** of the module to the **incident solar power** on the module's total aperture area, measured under **Standard Test Conditions (STC: 1000 W/m², AM 1.5G, 25°C)**.

### Conceptual Analogy
Efficiency is the **"miles per gallon"** of a solar module — it tells you how much useful electrical "fuel" (watts) you extract per unit of solar "fuel" (watts of sunlight) hitting the panel.

## 1.5 Energy Balance Equations for PV Modules

### Formal Definition
The **Energy Balance Equation** of a PV module equates the **incident solar energy** to the sum of the **useful electrical energy output** plus all **energy losses** (reflection, transmission, thermalization, recombination, ohmic, and thermal losses).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Key Point:** Without energy balance, you cannot perform a valid energy audit of a PV plant or specify the correct module rating.

### Conceptual Analogy
A monthly budget equation: **Income = Expenses + Savings + Taxes**. The solar income is divided into useful electricity (savings) and various unavoidable losses (taxes/expenses).

## 1.6 Series and Parallel Combination of PV Modules

### Formal Definition
- **Series Combination:** Modules connected in series add their **voltages** while the **current remains the same**.
- **Parallel Combination:** Modules connected in parallel add their **currents** while the **voltage remains the same**.

### Conceptual Analogy
Series connection is like linking **multiple batteries end-to-end** to add up voltage (e.g., two 12V batteries in series = 24V). Parallel connection is like connecting **identical batteries side-by-side** to add up current capacity while keeping the voltage the same.

## 1.7 Effect of Shadowing

### Formal Definition
**Shadowing (or Partial Shading)** occurs when one or more cells in a module (or one module in a string) receive less irradiance than others. The shaded cell becomes **reverse-biased**, acts as a load, dissipates power as heat, and may cause **hot-spot damage** if not protected by **bypass diodes**.

### Conceptual Analogy
If a single lane on a multi-lane highway is blocked, the entire highway slows down. Worse, the cars stuck in the blocked lane (shaded cell) start overheating because traffic is forced through them.

## 1.8 MPPT Techniques — Overview

### Formal Definition
**Maximum Power Point Tracking (MPPT)** is an electronic control technique used in PV systems to **continuously vary the operating point of the module/array** so that it operates at the **Maximum Power Point (MPP)** of its I-V curve under varying irradiance, temperature, and load conditions.

### Conceptual Analogy
MPPT is the **"automatic gearbox"** of a solar car. As road conditions (irradiance) and slope (load demand) change, the gearbox continuously adjusts so the engine (PV module) always delivers peak power.

## 1.9 P&O and Incremental Conductance Methods

### Formal Definition
- **Perturb & Observe (P&O):** An MPPT algorithm that **perturbs** (increases or decreases) the operating voltage of the PV array by a small step and **observes** the resulting change in power. If power increases, the perturbation continues in the same direction; otherwise, it reverses.
- **Incremental Conductance (IC):** An MPPT algorithm based on the derivative of power with respect to voltage. The MPP is reached when **dP/dV = 0**, which corresponds to **dI/dV = -I/V** (i.e., the incremental conductance equals the negative of the instantaneous conductance).

### Conceptual Analogy
P&O is like a hiker climbing a mountain in fog — take a step, see if you went up or down, then keep stepping in the direction that goes up. IC is like a hiker with a precise altimeter — the moment the altimeter reading doesn't change with another step, the hiker knows he/she is at the peak.

## 1.10 MPPT Using Buck-Boost Converter

### Formal Definition
A **Buck-Boost Converter** is a DC-DC converter topology whose output voltage can be either **lower (buck) or higher (boost) than the input voltage**. In MPPT applications, it acts as an **impedance-matching interface** between the PV module and the load, varying its duty cycle to keep the PV array operating at the MPP regardless of load or irradiance changes.

### Conceptual Analogy
A buck-boost converter is like an **automatic transmission with both low and high gears** — it can either step down the "engine speed" (voltage) to the wheels (load) or step it up, ensuring the engine always runs at its most efficient RPM (MPP).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** When asked to design an MPPT system, always justify the choice of converter. Buck-Boost is preferred when **PV array voltage can be both above and below** the DC bus/battery voltage (e.g., battery charging applications).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 III-V Single Junction vs Multi-Junction Cells

### 2.1.1 Working Principle

- **Single Junction (SJ):** A single p-n junction of one bandgap material (e.g., GaAs, $E_g \approx 1.42$ eV). Photons with energy $h\nu \geq E_g$ are absorbed; the rest are transmitted.
- **Multi-Junction (MJ):** Two or more sub-cells (often 2J, 3J, 4J) stacked in series, each with a **progressively smaller bandgap** from top to bottom. The top cell absorbs high-energy (UV/blue) photons; the middle cell absorbs green/yellow; the bottom cell absorbs red/IR.

### 2.1.2 Theoretical Efficiency Limits (Detailed Balance Limit, Shockley-Queisser)

- **Silicon (1.12 eV):** ~33%
- **GaAs (1.42 eV):** ~33.5%
- **3-Junction (GaInP/GaAs/Ge):** ~50% under 1-sun, ~63% under concentration
- **4-Junction (under 500 suns):** ~46% demonstrated

### 2.1.3 Why Multi-Junction Outperforms Single Junction

- **Reduces thermalization loss** (high-energy photons absorbed by large bandgap cells).
- **Reduces transmission loss** (low-energy photons captured by small bandgap cells).
- **Tandem cells are current-matched**, not voltage-matched, making design more complex.

## 2.2 Emerging PV Systems — Comparison

| Technology | Typical Efficiency | Stability | Cost Trajectory | Best Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Perovskite (single) | 26.1% | Poor (moisture, UV) | Very low | Tandem top cell |
| Perovskite/Si Tandem | 33.9% | Improving | Low | Utility/rooftop |
| Organic PV (OPV) | 19%+ | Moderate | Low | BIPV, wearables |
| CIGS | 23.4% | Good | Moderate | Flexible rooftop |
| CdTe | 22.1% | Excellent | Low | Utility-scale |
| Dye-Sensitized (DSSC) | 13% | Moderate | Very low | Indoor, BIPV |
| Quantum Dot | 18%+ | Research | Low | Future tandem |

## 2.3 Packing Factor — Detailed Analysis

### Definition in Symbols

$$
PF = \frac{A_{active}}{A_{total}} = \frac{n \cdot A_{cell}}{A_{module}}
$$

Where:
- $A_{active}$ = Total active cell area in the module (m²)
- $A_{total}$ = Total module aperture area (m²)
- $n$ = Number of cells in the module
- $A_{cell}$ = Area of each cell (m²)

### Factors Reducing Packing Factor
- **Inter-cell spacing** (typically 2–5 mm)
- **Module frame** (aluminum border)
- **Junction box area**
- **Busbars and ribbon connections**
- **Cell shape (square vs pseudo-square vs circular)**

### Effect on Module Power

$$
P_{module,actual} = PF \cdot P_{cell,total,ideal} = PF \cdot n \cdot A_{cell} \cdot G \cdot \eta_{cell}
$$

## 2.4 PV Module Efficiency — Complete Formula

### At the Cell Level

$$
\eta_{cell} = \frac{P_{max,cell}}{G \cdot A_{cell}} = \frac{V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp}}{G \cdot A_{cell}}
$$

### At the Module Level (Aperture Area Basis)

$$
\eta_{module} = \frac{P_{max,module}}{G \cdot A_{module}}
$$

### Relation Between Cell and Module Efficiency

$$
\eta_{module} = PF \cdot \eta_{cell}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Note:** The packing factor acts as a multiplier that downgrades cell efficiency to module efficiency. A 22% efficient cell with PF = 0.8 yields a module of only 17.6% efficiency.

### Balance-of-System Efficiency (Overall Plant)

$$
\eta_{system} = \eta_{module} \cdot \eta_{inverter} \cdot \eta_{wiring} \cdot \eta_{temperature} \cdot \eta_{soiling} \cdot \eta_{availability}
$$

## 2.5 Energy Balance Equation of a PV Module

The complete energy balance on the module aperture:

$$
G \cdot A_{module} = P_{electrical} + Q_{reflection} + Q_{transmission} + Q_{thermalization} + Q_{recombination} + Q_{ohmic} + Q_{thermal}
$$

Or, in **loss-stack** form (showing what fraction of incident energy is lost to each mechanism):

| Loss Mechanism | Symbol | Typical Fraction |
|---|---|---|
| Reflection | $L_{ref}$ | 3–5% |
| Transmission (below $E_g$) | $L_{tr}$ | 20–25% (Si) |
| Thermalization (above $E_g$) | $L_{th}$ | 30–35% (Si) |
| Recombination | $L_{rec}$ | 5–10% |
| Ohmic (series resistance) | $L_{ohm}$ | 3–5% |
| Thermal (cell temp > 25°C) | $L_{T}$ | 5–15% |
| **Useful electrical output** | $\eta$ | 15–22% |

## 2.6 Series and Parallel Combinations

### 2.6.1 Identical Modules in Series ($N_s$ modules)

$$
V_{string} = N_s \cdot V_{mod} \qquad I_{string} = I_{mod}
$$

### 2.6.2 Identical Modules in Parallel ($N_p$ strings of $N_s$ modules each)

$$
V_{array} = N_s \cdot V_{mod} \qquad I_{array} = N_p \cdot I_{mod}
$$

### 2.6.3 Mismatched Modules (General Case)

For series: $V_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{N_s} V_i$ at common current $I$.
For parallel: $I_{total} = \sum_{j=1}^{N_p} I_j$ at common voltage $V$.

### 2.6.4 Mismatch Loss

$$
\eta_{mm} = \frac{P_{actual}}{N_s \cdot N_p \cdot P_{mod}} \leq 1
$$

## 2.7 Effect of Shadowing — Hot Spot and Bypass Diode

### The Problem

A shaded cell generates less current ($I_{shaded} < I_{string}$). The string current is forced by the unshaded cells. The shaded cell is **reverse-biased** and dissipates power:

$$
P_{shaded} = V_{reverse} \cdot I_{string}
$$

If $V_{reverse}$ exceeds the cell breakdown voltage, **hot-spot heating** occurs, potentially destroying the module.

### Solution: Bypass Diode

A diode connected in **anti-parallel** with a group of cells (typically 20–24 cells per diode) provides an alternate current path when the group is reverse-biased. The bypass diode activates at $\sim -0.7$ V reverse bias.

### Blocking Diode (in series with string)

Prevents reverse current flow from a higher-voltage string to a shaded (lower-voltage) string, preventing battery discharge at night.

## 2.8 MPPT — Why It Is Needed

The **I-V curve of a PV module shifts** with irradiance and temperature:
- Higher $G$ → higher $I_{sc}$ (current scales linearly with $G$)
- Higher $T$ → lower $V_{oc}$ (voltage drops by ~2 mV/°C per cell)

The **MPP is the unique point on the I-V curve** where the product $P = V \cdot I$ is maximum. Without MPPT, a directly-coupled PV array operates far from the MPP, losing **20–40% of available energy**.

## 2.9 P&O Algorithm — Mathematical Basis

The power slope is:

$$
\frac{dP}{dV} = \frac{d(VI)}{dV} = I + V \frac{dI}{dV}
$$

- If $\frac{dP}{dV} > 0$ → left of MPP → **increase voltage** (duty cycle)
- If $\frac{dP}{dV} < 0$ → right of MPP → **decrease voltage** (duty cycle)
- If $\frac{dP}{dV} = 0$ → **at MPP** → hold

The perturbation size $\Delta V$ (or $\Delta D$) determines:
- **Large step** → fast tracking, large oscillations around MPP
- **Small step** → slow tracking, small oscillations

## 2.10 Incremental Conductance Algorithm — Mathematical Basis

At the MPP, the derivative of power with respect to voltage is zero:

$$
\frac{dP}{dV} = I + V \frac{dI}{dV} = 0 \Rightarrow \frac{dI}{dV} = -\frac{I}{V}
$$

Define:
- **Instantaneous conductance:** $G = \dfrac{I}{V}$
- **Incremental conductance:** $\Delta G = \dfrac{dI}{dV}$

The decision logic:

| Condition | Position | Action |
|---|---|---|
| $\Delta G > -G$ (i.e., $dP/dV > 0$) | Left of MPP | Increase $V_{ref}$ |
| $\Delta G < -G$ (i.e., $dP/dV < 0$) | Right of MPP | Decrease $V_{ref}$ |
| $\Delta G = -G$ (i.e., $dP/dV = 0$) | At MPP | Hold $V_{ref}$ |

## 2.11 Buck-Boost Converter — Voltage Transfer Function

For a **Buck-Boost (inverting)** converter operating in continuous conduction mode:

$$
\frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = -\frac{D}{1-D}
$$

Where $D$ is the duty cycle (0 < D < 1).

> [!NOTE]
> The negative sign indicates **output voltage polarity reversal** (output is inverted with respect to input). For a **non-inverting** (SEPIC or Zeta) version, the polarity is preserved.

### MPPT Role of the Converter

The converter presents a **variable input impedance** $R_{in}$ to the PV array:

$$
R_{in} = \frac{V_{in}}{I_{in}} = \frac{V_{out}}{I_{in}} \cdot \frac{1-D}{D} \cdot \text{(transfer factor)}
$$

The MPPT algorithm adjusts $D$ until the PV array sees an impedance that places it at the MPP.

## 2.12 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Packing Factor | $PF = A_{active}/A_{module}$ | Dimensionless, 0.7–0.9 |
| 2 | Cell Efficiency | $\eta_{cell} = P_{max}/(G \cdot A_{cell})$ | % |
| 3 | Module Efficiency | $\eta_{mod} = PF \cdot \eta_{cell}$ | % |
| 4 | Energy Balance | $G \cdot A = P_{el} + \sum Q_{losses}$ | W |
| 5 | Series Voltage | $V_s = N_s \cdot V_{mod}$ | V |
| 6 | Parallel Current | $I_p = N_p \cdot I_{mod}$ | A |
| 7 | MPPT Condition | $dP/dV = 0$ | At MPP |
| 8 | P&O Sign | $\text{sign}(\Delta P) \Rightarrow \text{step size}$ | — |
| 9 | IC Condition | $dI/dV = -I/V$ | At MPP |
| 10 | Buck-Boost Gain | $V_{out}/V_{in} = -D/(1-D)$ | Polarity inverted |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Module Efficiency from Cell Efficiency

**Statement:** Prove that $\eta_{module} = PF \cdot \eta_{cell}$.

**Starting point:** The electrical power output of the module equals the sum of the power output of all its cells (assuming ideal interconnection).

$$
P_{module} = n \cdot P_{cell} = n \cdot V_{mp,cell} \cdot I_{mp,cell}
$$

**Step 1:** Express $P_{cell}$ in terms of cell efficiency and incident power on cell.

$$
P_{cell} = \eta_{cell} \cdot G \cdot A_{cell}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute back.

$$
P_{module} = n \cdot \eta_{cell} \cdot G \cdot A_{cell}
$$

**Step 3:** Recognize that $n \cdot A_{cell} = A_{active}$ and $A_{module} = A_{active}/PF$, so $A_{active} = PF \cdot A_{module}$.

$$
P_{module} = \eta_{cell} \cdot G \cdot A_{active} = \eta_{cell} \cdot G \cdot PF \cdot A_{module}
$$

**Step 4:** By definition of module efficiency:

$$
\eta_{module} = \frac{P_{module}}{G \cdot A_{module}} = \frac{\eta_{cell} \cdot G \cdot PF \cdot A_{module}}{G \cdot A_{module}} = \eta_{cell} \cdot PF
$$

**Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{\eta_{module} = PF \cdot \eta_{cell}}
$$

This shows that **packing factor linearly scales down the cell efficiency to module efficiency**.

## 3.2 Derivation: Energy Balance Equation (Loss-Stack Form)

**Statement:** Express the incident solar power as a sum of useful electrical power and all losses.

**Step 1:** Apply the first law of thermodynamics to the PV module as a control volume. The incident radiation is the only energy input, the electrical output is useful, and the rest is lost as heat or unabsorbed radiation.

$$
G \cdot A_{module} = P_{electrical} + P_{reflected} + P_{transmitted} + P_{thermalization} + P_{recombination} + P_{ohmic} + P_{thermal}
$$

**Step 2:** Normalize by the incident power $G \cdot A_{module}$ to obtain fractional losses.

$$
1 = \eta_{module} + \rho + \tau + L_{th} + L_{rec} + L_{\Omega} + L_{T}
$$

Where each term on the right is a fraction between 0 and 1.

**Step 3:** Identify the dominant losses for a typical crystalline silicon cell:
- $\rho \approx 0.04$ (anti-reflection coating reduces reflection to ~4%)
- $\tau \approx 0.22$ (sub-bandgap photons transmitted)
- $L_{th} \approx 0.33$ (above-bandgap photons thermalized)
- $L_{rec} \approx 0.07$ (radiative + non-radiative recombination)
- $L_{\Omega} \approx 0.04$ (series/contact resistance)
- $L_{T} \approx 0.08$ (cell operating above 25°C in field)
- $\eta_{module} \approx 0.22$

**Step 4:** Verify the sum equals 1:

$$
\eta_{module} + 0.04 + 0.22 + 0.33 + 0.07 + 0.04 + 0.08 = 0.22 + 0.78 = 1.00 \;\; \checkmark
$$

**Final Result:** The energy balance is closed, with only **~22% of incident solar energy converted to electricity** for a typical silicon module.

## 3.3 Derivation: Series/Parallel Combination

**Statement:** Prove the voltage/current addition rules for series and parallel combinations.

**Series Case:** When $N_s$ identical modules are connected in series, the same current $I$ flows through all of them. By Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL):

$$
V_{string} = V_1 + V_2 + \cdots + V_{N_s}
$$

For identical modules, $V_i = V_{mod}$, hence:

$$
V_{string} = N_s \cdot V_{mod}
$$

**Parallel Case:** When $N_p$ identical modules are connected in parallel, they share the same voltage $V$. By Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL):

$$
I_{parallel} = I_1 + I_2 + \cdots + I_{N_p}
$$

For identical modules, $I_j = I_{mod}$, hence:

$$
I_{parallel} = N_p \cdot I_{mod}
$$

**Combined Series-Parallel Array (Array of $N_s$ in series, $N_p$ such strings in parallel):**

$$
\boxed{V_{array} = N_s \cdot V_{mod} \quad,\quad I_{array} = N_p \cdot I_{mod} \quad,\quad P_{array} = N_s \cdot N_p \cdot P_{mod}}
$$

## 3.4 Derivation: Effect of Shadowing with Bypass Diode

**Statement:** Show that bypass diodes prevent hot-spot damage in partially shaded strings.

**Step 1:** Consider a string of $N$ identical cells carrying current $I_{string}$ under irradiance $G$. If one cell is shaded with irradiance $G_s < G$, its short-circuit current drops to:

$$
I_{sc,shaded} = I_{sc,unshaded} \cdot \frac{G_s}{G}
$$

**Step 2:** The unshaded cells force the string current $I_{string}$ to equal their own current. If $I_{string} > I_{sc,shaded}$, the shaded cell cannot source the current and becomes **reverse-biased**.

**Step 3:** Without a bypass diode, the reverse voltage on the shaded cell can grow until the remaining $N-1$ cells forward-bias it just enough to pass $I_{string}$. The shaded cell dissipates:

$$
P_{shaded} = V_{reverse} \cdot I_{string}
$$

**Step 4:** With a bypass diode across a group of cells containing the shaded cell, when the reverse voltage across that group exceeds the diode forward voltage (~0.7 V), the diode conducts, diverting current around the shaded cells. The string current now flows through the bypass diode, and the shaded cell group is limited to a reverse voltage of $\sim -0.7$ V.

**Step 5:** Power dissipated in the shaded cell is now:

$$
P_{shaded,protected} = 0.7 \text{ V} \cdot I_{string} \;\; (\text{safe, no hot spot})
$$

**Conclusion:** Bypass diodes limit reverse voltage and power dissipation, preventing thermal runaway and module failure.

## 3.5 Derivation: MPPT Condition

**Statement:** Prove that at the MPP, $\dfrac{dP}{dV} = 0$, which leads to $\dfrac{dI}{dV} = -\dfrac{I}{V}$.

**Step 1:** Power as a function of voltage:

$$
P(V) = V \cdot I(V)
$$

**Step 2:** Differentiate with respect to $V$ using the product rule:

$$
\frac{dP}{dV} = I(V) + V \cdot \frac{dI}{dV}
$$

**Step 3:** At the maximum, the derivative is zero:

$$
\frac{dP}{dV} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad I + V \cdot \frac{dI}{dV} = 0
$$

**Step 4:** Solve for the slope $dI/dV$:

$$
\frac{dI}{dV} = -\frac{I}{V}
$$

**Step 5:** Define conductances:
- $G = I/V$ (instantaneous/static conductance)
- $\Delta G = dI/dV$ (incremental/dynamic conductance)

Then the MPP condition becomes:

$$
\boxed{\Delta G = -G \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad \frac{dI}{dV} + \frac{I}{V} = 0}
$$

## 3.6 Derivation: Buck-Boost Voltage Transfer Function

**Statement:** Derive $V_{out}/V_{in} = -D/(1-D)$ for an ideal buck-boost converter in CCM.

**Step 1:** Consider the buck-boost topology: input source $V_{in}$ → inductor $L$ → switch (transistor) → ground; the diode connects from the inductor to the output capacitor $C$ and load $R$ in series with the output (which is referenced to ground on the other side).

**Step 2:** **During ON state** ($0 \leq t \leq DT$): the switch is closed, the diode is reverse-biased, and the inductor charges linearly. Voltage across $L$ is $V_L = V_{in}$.

**Step 3:** **During OFF state** ($DT \leq t \leq T$): the switch opens, the diode is forward-biased, and the inductor discharges into the capacitor. Voltage across $L$ is $V_L = V_{out}$ (with appropriate sign).

**Step 4:** Apply **volt-second balance** on the inductor (steady-state, average voltage across L over one cycle is zero):

$$
\int_0^{DT} V_{in} \, dt + \int_{DT}^{T} V_{out} \, dt = 0
$$

**Step 5:** Evaluate the integrals:

$$
V_{in} \cdot D \cdot T + V_{out} \cdot (1 - D) \cdot T = 0
$$

**Step 6:** Solve for the ratio:

$$
V_{out} = -V_{in} \cdot \frac{D}{1 - D}
$$

$$
\boxed{\frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = -\frac{D}{1 - D}}
$$

**Step 7:** Verify special cases:
- $D = 0$ → $V_{out} = 0$ (switch always off)
- $D \to 1$ → $V_{out} \to -\infty$ (switch always on)
- $D = 0.5$ → $V_{out} = -V_{in}$
- $D = 0.333$ → $V_{out} = -0.5 \cdot V_{in}$ (buck mode)
- $D = 0.667$ → $V_{out} = -2 \cdot V_{in}$ (boost mode)

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of P&O and Incremental Conductance

| Parameter | Perturb & Observe (P&O) | Incremental Conductance (IC) |
|---|---|---|
| **Math basis** | Sign of $\Delta P$ | $dI/dV$ vs $-I/V$ |
| **Sensors needed** | V, I | V, I |
| **Steady-state oscillation** | Always present (around MPP) | Can be made near-zero |
| **Tracking speed** | Moderate | Fast |
| **Implementation complexity** | Simple | Moderate |
| **Convergence under rapid change** | May diverge | Tracks better |
| **Computational load** | Low | Moderate |
| **KTU typical mark weight** | 5–7 marks | 7 marks |
| **Hardware (sensor noise)** | Sensitive to noise | More noise-tolerant |
| **Adaptive step variant** | Variable step P&O | Modified IC |

```mermaid
flowchart TB

    B -->|High-energy photons UVblue absorbed| C[Middle Cell GaAs Eg=1.42eV]
    C -->|Mid-energy photons greenyellow absorbed| D[Bottom Cell Ge Eg=0.67eV]
    D -->|Low-energy photons redIR absorbed| E[Unused thermal losses]
    B -.->|Tunnel Junction TJ1| C
    C -.->|Tunnel Junction TJ2| D
    F[Load] --- B
    D --- F
```

> **Caption:** Schematic of a 3-junction (Triple-Junction) III-V solar cell. Tunnel junctions interconnect sub-cells in series. Each layer absorbs a different portion of the solar spectrum, reducing thermalization and transmission losses.

## 4.2 Series-Parallel Module Combination

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph String1[Series String 1 Ns equals 3]
        M1A[Module 1A] --> M1B[Module 1B] --> M1C[Module 1C]
    end
    subgraph String2[Series String 2 Ns equals 3]
        M2A[Module 2A] --> M2B[Module 2B] --> M2C[Module 2C]
    end
    subgraph String3[Series String 3 Ns equals 3]
        M3A[Module 3A] --> M3B[Module 3B] --> M3C[Module 3C]
    end
    String1 --- P1[Positive Busbar]
    String2 --- P1
    String3 --- P1
    String1 --- N1[Negative Busbar]
    String2 --- N1
    String3 --- N1
    P1 --> Combiner[Combiner Box]
    N1 --> Combiner
    Combiner --> Inverter[Inverter DC to AC]
```

> **Caption:** 3S × 3P array of 9 modules. Three strings in series (each giving 3 × $V_{mod}$), three such strings in parallel (giving 3 × $I_{mod}$). Total power = 9 × $P_{mod}$.

## 4.3 MPPT System Block Diagram (Buck-Boost Topology)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PV[PV Module or Array] -->|Vpv Ipv| ADC1[Voltage Sensor Vpv]
    PV -->|Vpv Ipv| ADC2[Current Sensor Ipv]
    ADC1 --> MPPT[MPPT Controller P and O or IC Algorithm]
    ADC2 --> MPPT
    MPPT -->|PWM Duty Cycle D| Gate[Gate Driver]
    Gate --> Switch[Power Switch MOSFET or IGBT]
    Switch --> Inductor[L Inductor]
    Inductor --> Diode[Diode]
    Diode --> Cout[Output Capacitor]
    Cout --> Load[DC Load or Battery]
    PV --- Inductor
```

> **Caption:** MPPT system using a buck-boost converter. The MPPT controller computes the operating point, generates a PWM signal with duty cycle $D$, which drives the switch. The converter's input impedance (a function of $D$ and load) is adjusted until the PV array operates at its MPP.

## 4.4 P&O Algorithm Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Start]) --> Read[Read Vpv and Ipv]
    Read --> Compute[Compute Ppv equals Vpv times Ipv]
    Compute --> Init{Ppv_prev exists?}
    Init -->|No| Store1[Store Vpv_prev, Ipv_prev, Ppv_prev] --> Perturb1[Perturb Vref by plus deltaV]
    Init -->|Yes| Compare{dP greater than 0}
    Compare -->|Yes, and dV greater than 0| Dir1[Vref equals Vref plus deltaV]
    Compare -->|Yes, and dV less than 0| Dir2[Vref equals Vref minus deltaV]
    Compare -->|No, and dV greater than 0| Dir3[Vref equals Vref minus deltaV]
    Compare -->|No, and dV less than 0| Dir4[Vref equals Vref plus deltaV]
    Compare -->|No, dP equals 0| Hold[Vref unchanged]
    Dir1 --> Store2[Update Vpv_prev, Ipv_prev, Ppv_prev]
    Dir2 --> Store2
    Dir3 --> Store2
    Dir4 --> Store2
    Hold --> Store2
    Perturb1 --> Store2
    Store2 --> Loop[Wait one sampling period] --> Read
```

> **Caption:** Perturb & Observe (P&O) MPPT algorithm. Perturb the operating voltage, observe the resulting power change, and step in the direction of increasing power.

## 4.5 Incremental Conductance Algorithm Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Start]) --> Read[Read Vpv_k and Ipv_k]
    Read --> DeltaV[Vpv_k minus Vpv_prev equals deltaV]
    Read --> DeltaI[Ipv_k minus Ipv_prev equals deltaI]
    DeltaV --> CheckD{deltaV equals 0}
    CheckD -->|Yes| SubA{deltaI equals 0}
    SubA -->|Yes| MPPT_Point[At MPP, hold Vref]
    SubA -->|No, deltaI greater than 0| StepUp1[Vref equals Vref plus deltaV]
    CheckD -->|No| IncG[Compute deltaI over deltaV equals Incremental Conductance]
    IncG --> InstG[Compute Ipv over Vpv equals Instantaneous Conductance]
    InstG --> Test{deltaI over deltaV equals minus I over V}
    Test -->|Yes| MPPT_Point
    Test -->|No, deltaI over deltaV greater than minus I over V| StepUp2[Vref equals Vref plus deltaV, left of MPP]
    Test -->|No, deltaI over deltaV less than minus I over V| StepDown[Vref equals Vref minus deltaV, right of MPP]
    StepUp1 --> Update[Update Vpv_prev and Ipv_prev]
    StepUp2 --> Update
    StepDown --> Update
    MPPT_Point --> Update
    Update --> Wait[Wait sampling period Ts] --> Read
```

> **Caption:** Incremental Conductance (IC) MPPT algorithm. Compares the incremental conductance $\Delta I/\Delta V$ to the negative of the instantaneous conductance $I/V$ to determine the position relative to the MPP.

## 4.6 PV System with Shadowing and Protection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Sun[Sunlight] -->|Partial Shadow| Cells[Group of 20 to 24 Cells]
    Cells -->|Normally forward| Bypass1[Bypass Diode 1]
    Cells -->|Normally forward| Bypass2[Bypass Diode 2]
    Cells -->|Reverse biased when shaded| Bypass3[Bypass Diode 3 Activated]
    Cells --> String[Series String]
    String --> Block[Blocking Diode prevents reverse current]
    Block --> Array[PV Array]
    Array --> MPPT[MPPT Buck-Boost Converter]
    MPPT --> Inverter[Inverter]
    Inverter --> Grid[AC Grid or Load]
```

> **Caption:** Shadowing effect and protection scheme. Bypass diodes activate under reverse bias to prevent hot-spot damage. Blocking diode prevents reverse current from a higher-voltage string into a shaded string.
