# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2023]
**Define Packing Factor of a PV module. How does it differ from the Fill Factor of a solar cell?**

**Model Answer:**
The **Packing Factor (PF)** of a PV module is defined as the ratio of the **total active cell area** within the module to the **total aperture area** of the module:

$$
PF = \frac{A_{active}}{A_{module}}
$$

It is a **geometric/area ratio** and is always less than 1 (typically 0.7–0.9) due to inter-cell gaps, borders, and junction box area.

In contrast, the **Fill Factor (FF)** of a *solar cell* is an **electrical parameter** defined as:

$$
FF = \frac{V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp}}{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}}
$$

It represents the "squareness" of the I-V curve and indicates the quality of the cell. It is dimensionless and typically lies between 0.6 and 0.85 for good silicon cells.

| Aspect | Packing Factor (Module) | Fill Factor (Cell) |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Active area / total area | $V_{mp} I_{mp} / V_{oc} I_{sc}$ |
| Domain | Geometry | Electrical |
| Typical range | 0.7–0.9 | 0.6–0.85 |
| Affected by | Cell spacing, frame | Series resistance, shunt |

**[3 Marks]**

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**List any three emerging PV technologies and state their highest reported efficiencies.**

**Model Answer:**
The three emerging PV technologies are:

1. **Perovskite single-junction cells** — Highest certified efficiency: **26.1%** (NREL chart, 2024).
2. **Perovskite/Silicon tandem cells** — Highest certified efficiency: **33.9%** (NREL, 2024), surpassing the Shockley-Queisser limit for single junctions.
3. **Organic Photovoltaics (OPV)** — Highest certified efficiency: **~19%** (research cells, 2024).

All three are research-stage or pre-commercial, with perovskite tandems being the most promising for near-term commercialization due to their compatibility with existing silicon manufacturing lines.

**[3 Marks]**

## 5.2 Part B — Full Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Warning:**
> - In MPPT derivations, **do not skip the step where you compute $dP/dV$** and set it to zero — many students lose 2 marks here.
> - For buck-boost converter, **always state the polarity inversion** explicitly; forgetting the negative sign is a 1-mark deduction.
> - In energy balance, **show the sum equals 1** to demonstrate closure — otherwise the examiner assumes you have not validated the model.
> - For bypass diode questions, **draw the circuit with current paths** — verbal descriptions without a circuit sketch lose 2–3 marks.

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the working of a multi-junction (tandem) III-V solar cell with a neat sketch. Compare its efficiency with single-junction cells. Why are tunnel junctions used between sub-cells?**

**Model Solution:**

**Working Principle:** A multi-junction (MJ) solar cell consists of two or more p-n junction sub-cells stacked in optical and electrical series. Each sub-cell is fabricated from a semiconductor with a **progressively decreasing bandgap** from top to bottom. The top cell (largest $E_g$) absorbs high-energy photons (UV/blue); the middle cell absorbs green/yellow; the bottom cell (smallest $E_g$) absorbs red/infrared photons.

**Why Multi-Junction Outperforms Single Junction:**
- **Reduced thermalization loss:** In a single-junction cell of bandgap $E_g$, any photon with energy $h\nu > E_g$ loses the excess energy $(h\nu - E_g)$ as heat. In a MJ cell, the topmost cell with the largest $E_g$ absorbs only the most energetic photons, so the average thermalization loss is much lower.
- **Reduced transmission loss:** In a single-junction cell, sub-bandgap photons ($h\nu < E_g$) pass through and are wasted. In a MJ cell, the bottom cell with the smallest $E_g$ captures these low-energy photons.
- **Higher theoretical (detailed-balance) limit:** A 3-junction cell under 1-sun has a theoretical efficiency of ~50% vs. ~33% for a single-junction cell.

**Tunnel Junctions:** Between sub-cells, heavily-doped p⁺-n⁺ tunnel junctions are placed. They serve two purposes:
1. **Electrical interconnection** (low-resistance ohmic contact between sub-cells in series).
2. **Optical coupling** — the tunnel junction is optically transparent in the absorption range of the lower sub-cell.

**Common material systems:**
- **GaInP / GaAs / Ge** (lattice-matched, 3-junction, ~30–32% efficiency)
- **GaInP / GaAs / GaInAs / Ge** (4-junction, ~35–38% under 1-sun, >46% under concentration)

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Single Junction | Multi-Junction |
|---|---|---|
| Theoretical max | ~33% | ~50% (3J) to 86% (infinite J) |
| Practical best | ~26% (Si) | ~47% (CPV, 4J) |
| Spectral utilization | Poor | Excellent |
| Complexity | Low | High |
| Cost | Low | Very high (mostly space/CPV) |

**Sketch:** (Refer to the Mermaid block diagram of MJ cell in Section 4.1.)

**[7 Marks]**

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**A PV module has 36 cells connected in series, each of size 156 mm × 156 mm. The module aperture is 1.65 m × 0.99 m. The cell efficiency under STC is 18.5%. Calculate (i) the Packing Factor and (ii) the Module Efficiency.**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Number of cells: $n = 36$
- Cell area: $A_{cell} = 0.156 \times 0.156 = 0.024336$ m²
- Module aperture area: $A_{module} = 1.65 \times 0.99 = 1.6335$ m²
- Cell efficiency: $\eta_{cell} = 18.5\% = 0.185$

**Step 1: Compute Active Area**

$$
A_{active} = n \cdot A_{cell} = 36 \times 0.024336 = 0.876096 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 2: Compute Packing Factor**

$$
PF = \frac{A_{active}}{A_{module}} = \frac{0.876096}{1.6335} = 0.5363
$$

**Result:** PF ≈ **0.536** (or 53.6%)

This is lower than the typical 0.7–0.9 range, indicating large inactive area (likely due to the 4×9 layout with substantial gaps).

**Step 3: Compute Module Efficiency**

$$
\eta_{module} = PF \cdot \eta_{cell} = 0.5363 \times 0.185 = 0.0992
$$

**Result:** Module Efficiency ≈ **9.92%** (or ~10%)

**Step 4: Verification (alternative method)**

The total incident power on the module at $G = 1000$ W/m²:

$$
P_{in} = G \cdot A_{module} = 1000 \times 1.6335 = 1633.5 \text{ W}
$$

Total active cell area power:

$$
P_{cells} = G \cdot A_{active} \cdot \eta_{cell} = 1000 \times 0.876096 \times 0.185 = 162.08 \text{ W}
$$

Module efficiency:

$$
\eta_{module} = \frac{P_{cells}}{P_{in}} = \frac{162.08}{1633.5} = 0.0992 = 9.92\% \;\; \checkmark
$$

**[7 Marks]**

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the Perturb & Observe (P&O) MPPT algorithm. Derive the condition for MPP. How does the choice of perturbation step size affect the algorithm?**

**Model Solution:**

**MPP Condition (Derivation):**
Power delivered by a PV module at a given operating voltage $V$ is $P = V \cdot I(V)$. The Maximum Power Point is where $P$ is maximized, i.e., where $dP/dV = 0$.

$$
\frac{dP}{dV} = \frac{d(VI)}{dV} = I + V \cdot \frac{dI}{dV}
$$

At MPP:

$$
I + V \cdot \frac{dI}{dV} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{dI}{dV} = -\frac{I}{V}
$$

This is the fundamental condition for the MPP and is the basis of the Incremental Conductance method.

**P&O Algorithm — Working Principle:**

The P&O algorithm operates by:
1. Measuring the PV voltage $V_k$ and current $I_k$ at the current time step $k$.
2. Computing the power $P_k = V_k \cdot I_k$.
3. Comparing $P_k$ with the previous power $P_{k-1}$.
4. Perturbing (incrementing or decrementing) the reference voltage $V_{ref}$ by a small step $\Delta V$ according to the following rules:

| $\Delta P = P_k - P_{k-1}$ | $\Delta V = V_k - V_{k-1}$ | Next Action |
|---|---|---|
| $> 0$ | $> 0$ | Increase $V_{ref}$ (move right, was on left of MPP) |
| $> 0$ | $< 0$ | Decrease $V_{ref}$ (move left, was on right of MPP) |
| $< 0$ | $> 0$ | Decrease $V_{ref}$ (was on right, overshooting) |
| $< 0$ | $< 0$ | Increase $V_{ref}$ (was on left, overshooting) |
| $= 0$ | — | Hold $V_{ref}$ (at MPP) |

**Effect of Perturbation Step Size $\Delta V$:**

- **Large $\Delta V$:** Faster dynamic response to changing irradiance, but causes **large steady-state oscillations** around the MPP, leading to **power loss** and possible instability. May diverge under rapid irradiance changes.
- **Small $\Delta V$:** Slower dynamic response (poor tracking of fast irradiance changes), but **smaller steady-state oscillations** and higher average efficiency at steady state.
- **Variable $\Delta V$ (Adaptive P&O):** A modern improvement where the step size is large far from the MPP and small near the MPP, combining fast tracking with low oscillation. This is the basis of the **modified P&O** method that overcomes the traditional drawbacks.

**Sketch:** (Refer to the P&O flow chart in Section 4.4.)

**[7 Marks]**

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**A PV array of 10 modules (each rated 36 V, 8 A under STC) is to charge a 240 V battery bank. Design a buck-boost MPPT converter. Determine the duty cycle range required.**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Module rating: $V_{mod} = 36$ V, $I_{mod} = 8$ A
- Number of modules in array: 10
- Battery voltage: $V_{batt} = 240$ V (assumed nominal; the converter must regulate for the battery's voltage range)

**Step 1: Array Voltage Calculation**

Assuming the modules are connected in series to step up the voltage to suit the battery (10 × 36 V = 360 V, which is above 240 V, but the array can be reconfigured; we will analyze both series and series-parallel options):

**Option 1:** All 10 modules in series.

$$
V_{array,max} = 10 \times 36 = 360 \text{ V}
$$

**Option 2:** Two parallel strings of 5 modules each.

$$
V_{array} = 5 \times 36 = 180 \text{ V} \quad \text{(with double current: 16 A)}
$$

**Step 2: Determine Buck-Boost Duty Cycle**

For a buck-boost converter: $V_{out} = -V_{in} \cdot \dfrac{D}{1-D}$

The required output magnitude is 240 V. The input (array voltage) varies between $V_{MP,min}$ and $V_{MP,max}$ depending on irradiance and temperature.

**Case A: Array at full power (360 V input, output = 240 V magnitude)**

$$
240 = 360 \cdot \frac{D}{1 - D} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{D}{1 - D} = \frac{2}{3} \quad \Rightarrow \quad D = 0.4
$$

**Case B: Array at 50% of STC voltage (~180 V input, output = 240 V magnitude)**

$$
240 = 180 \cdot \frac{D}{1 - D} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{D}{1 - D} = \frac{4}{3} \quad \Rightarrow \quad D = 0.571
$$

**Case C: Array at 80% voltage (~288 V input, output = 240 V)**

$$
D = \frac{240}{240 + 288} = \frac{240}{528} = 0.4545
$$

**Step 3: Range of Duty Cycle**

The required duty cycle range to cover battery charging (output = 240 V) for the input voltage range of 180–360 V is:

$$
D_{min} = 0.4 \;\; (\text{at } V_{in} = 360 \text{ V})
$$
$$
D_{max} = 0.571 \;\; (\text{at } V_{in} = 180 \text{ V})
$$

**Step 4: Design Considerations**

- **Polarity:** Buck-boost inverts the output voltage. For battery charging, the output polarity is reversed compared to input. Use a **SEPIC or Zeta** (non-inverting) topology if polarity preservation is required, or use a full H-bridge output.
- **MPPT Controller:** Implement IC algorithm (faster, no oscillation) to vary $D$ between 0.4 and 0.571.
- **Inductor Selection:** $L = \dfrac{V_{in} \cdot D \cdot (1-D)}{\Delta I_L \cdot f_s}$ (depends on switching frequency and acceptable ripple).
- **Capacitor Selection:** $C = \dfrac{I_{out} \cdot D}{f_s \cdot \Delta V_{out}}$ (depends on acceptable output ripple).

**Conclusion:** The buck-boost MPPT converter must operate in the duty cycle range of **D = 0.40 to 0.57** to maintain the PV array at MPP while delivering 240 V to the battery bank.

**[7 Marks]**

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Module 3 — Rapid Revision Checklist**

### III-V and Multi-Junction Cells
- [ ] III-V = compounds of groups III (Ga, In) and V (As, P) of the periodic table
- [ ] Single junction has one $E_g$; multi-junction stacks sub-cells with **decreasing** $E_g$ from top to bottom
- [ ] Tunnel junctions (heavily doped p⁺n⁺) interconnect sub-cells
- [ ] Best materials: **GaInP / GaAs / Ge** (3J), > 47% under CPV
- [ ] Multi-junction reduces both **thermalization** and **transmission** losses

### Emerging PV Systems
- [ ] **Perovskite** cells: best emerging, 26.1% single, 33.9% tandem with Si
- [ ] OPV (~19%), CIGS (~23%), CdTe (~22%), DSSC (~13%), Quantum Dots (~18%)
- [ ] Perovskites degrade with moisture and UV → encapsulation is critical

### Packing Factor & Efficiency
- [ ] $PF = A_{active}/A_{module}$, typically **0.7–0.9**
- [ ] $\eta_{module} = PF \cdot \eta_{cell}$ — direct multiplicative relationship
- [ ] Module rated power = $\eta_{module} \cdot G_{STC} \cdot A_{module}$ (with $G_{STC} = 1000$ W/m²)
- [ ] System efficiency = product of all BOS efficiencies

### Energy Balance
- [ ] $G \cdot A = P_{el} + Q_{reflection} + Q_{transmission} + Q_{thermalization} + Q_{recombination} + Q_{ohmic} + Q_{thermal}$
- [ ] Sum of fractional losses + module efficiency = 1 (closure check)
- [ ] Dominant losses in Si: thermalization (~33%), transmission (~22%), reflection (~4%)

### Series/Parallel Combinations
- [ ] Series adds **voltage**, parallel adds **current**
- [ ] $V_{array} = N_s \cdot V_{mod}$, $I_{array} = N_p \cdot I_{mod}$
- [ ] Mismatch loss typically 2–5% in real systems

### Shadowing
- [ ] Shaded cell becomes **reverse-biased** → dissipates power → **hot spot**
- [ ] **Bypass diode** (anti-parallel, 1 per 20–24 cells) prevents hot spot
- [ ] **Blocking diode** (in series with string) prevents reverse current flow

### MPPT Basics
- [ ] MPPT keeps PV array at its **Maximum Power Point** under varying conditions
- [ ] Condition: $dP/dV = 0$ ↔ $dI/dV = -I/V$
- [ ] MPPT can improve energy harvest by **20–40%** over direct coupling

### P&O Method
- [ ] Perturb $V_{ref}$ by $\Delta V$, observe $\Delta P$, step in direction of +$\Delta P$
- [ ] Always oscillates around MPP (trade-off: step size vs oscillation)
- [ ] Simple, but may diverge under rapid irradiance change

### Incremental Conductance Method
- [ ] Compare $\Delta I/\Delta V$ with $-I/V$ to determine position w.r.t. MPP
- [ ] Can be made oscillation-free (theoretically)
- [ ] More complex than P&O, but tracks rapid changes better

### Buck-Boost Converter for MPPT
- [ ] $V_{out}/V_{in} = -D/(1-D)$ — **polarity inverted**
- [ ] Allows both step-up and step-down → ideal when PV voltage crosses load voltage
- [ ] Acts as **impedance transformer** — MPPT varies $D$ to match load to PV
- [ ] Duty cycle range: 0 to 1, but practical 0.1 to 0.9

> **Last-Minute Mnemonic for MPPT Algorithm Selection:**
> **"P for Perturb (P&O), I for Incremental (IC)"** — P&O is like a hiker in fog, IC is like a hiker with an altimeter.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Solar PV System — Fundamental Classification

A **Solar Photovoltaic (PV) System** is an integrated arrangement of solar modules, power conditioning equipment, protection devices, mounting structures, and balance-of-system (BOS) components that converts incident solar irradiance directly into usable electrical energy. Based on the topology of power delivery, the system is classified into two fundamental architectures.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** The classification of PV systems into **Stand-Alone (Off-Grid)** and **Grid-Connected (On-Grid / Utility-Interactive)** forms the foundational taxonomy upon which all subsequent design steps, sizing equations, and life-cycle costing models are built.

| Architecture | KTU Formal Definition | Storage Element | Prime Application |
|--------------|----------------------|------------------|--------------------|
| **Stand-Alone (Off-Grid)** | A self-contained PV system that operates independently of the utility grid, requiring on-site energy storage to satisfy load demand during non-sunny hours. | **Mandatory** (Battery / Ultra-capacitor) | Remote electrification, islands, telecom, water pumping |
| **Grid-Connected (On-Grid)** | A utility-interactive PV system that operates in synchronism with the public electricity grid, injecting surplus power and importing deficit power. | **Not mandatory** (Net-metering acts as virtual storage) | Urban rooftops, solar farms, commercial buildings |

---

## 1.2 Stand-Alone (Off-Grid) PV System — Definition & Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** *A Stand-Alone PV System is a decentralized electrical generation system in which the PV array is the sole or primary energy source, and the load is supplied either directly during sunshine or indirectly through a battery bank when the array output is insufficient or unavailable.*

**Conceptual Analogy (The "Personal Water Tank" Analogy):**
Imagine your house is in a remote village with **no municipal water pipeline**. You install a **roof rainwater harvesting system** with an **overhead storage tank**. The roof acts as the **PV array**, the overhead tank acts as the **battery bank**, and the taps (faucets) act as your **loads** (lights, fans, pumps). The tank must be sized carefully — too small and you run dry in summer; too large and the capital cost becomes uneconomical. You also need a **float valve (charge controller)** to prevent overflow and a **pump/filter (inverter)** to deliver usable water pressure. This is exactly how an off-grid PV system works — energy is harvested when available and stored for later use.

**Block-Level Architecture of Stand-Alone PV System:**

```
[PV Array] → [Charge Controller] → [Battery Bank] → [DC Bus] → [Inverter] → [AC Load]
                                                          ↓
                                                       [DC Load]
```

---

## 1.3 Grid-Connected (On-Grid) PV System — Definition & Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** *A Grid-Connected PV System is a utility-interactive system in which the PV array feeds power into the local load through a grid-tie inverter that is phase-locked, voltage-matched, and frequency-matched with the public electricity network. The grid functions as an infinite sink or source, providing implicit storage via net-metering.*

**Conceptual Analogy (The "Bank Account" Analogy):**
Consider your **salary account** linked to a **flexible deposit**. On days you earn extra (sunny days), you **deposit** the surplus energy into the grid (the bank). On days you earn nothing (cloudy/night), you **withdraw** from the grid to run your loads. Your **net balance** (kWh imported − kWh exported) determines your electricity bill. You don't need a personal "tank" (battery), because the grid itself is the storage. However, the moment the grid fails, the inverter must **anti-island** and shut down — you cannot run loads without the grid as a reference (unless hybridized with batteries).

**Block-Level Architecture of Grid-Connected PV System:**

```
[PV Array] → [Grid-Tie Inverter (MPPT + Anti-Island)] ⇄ [AC Bus] ⇄ [Utility Grid]
                                                  ↓
                                               [Local AC Loads]
```

---

## 1.4 Critical Comparison Table (High-Yield for KTU)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Difference between Stand-Alone and Grid-Connected PV System (Direct KTU 2-Mark Favorite):**

| Parameter | Stand-Alone (Off-Grid) | Grid-Connected (On-Grid) |
|-----------|------------------------|---------------------------|
| **Grid Dependency** | Fully independent of grid | Must be synchronized with grid |
| **Battery Storage** | Essential (autonomy requirement) | Optional / not required |
| **Power Conditioning** | Charge controller + Inverter | Only Grid-Tie Inverter |
| **Anti-Islanding** | Not applicable | Mandatory (per IEEE 1547 / IEC 62116) |
| **Net-Metering** | Not applicable | Available (virtual storage) |
| **Capital Cost** | Higher (battery cost dominant) | Lower (no battery) |
| **Reliability** | Depends on battery sizing | Depends on grid availability |
| **Typical Efficiency** | 70 – 85 % (overall) | 95 – 98 % (inverter) |
| **Maintenance** | High (battery replacement every 3–7 yrs) | Low (no battery) |
| **Example** | Solar lantern, solar water pump | 1 kW rooftop, 50 MW solar farm |

---

## 1.5 Hybrid PV System — The Modern Compromise

> [!NOTE]
> A **Hybrid PV System** combines the PV array with both battery storage **and** grid connection. It can operate in off-grid mode during grid failure and in on-grid mode otherwise. This is the dominant architecture for modern residential installations under the KSEB (Kerala State Electricity Board) rooftop scheme.

---

## 1.6 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Kerala Context)

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard Value | Unit |
|--------|----------|----------------|------|
| $G_{STC}$ | Standard Test Condition Irradiance | **1000** | W/m² |
| $T_{STC}$ | Standard Test Condition Cell Temperature | **25** | °C |
| $AM$ | Air Mass (standard) | **1.5** | dimensionless |
| $\eta_{inv}$ | Typical Inverter Efficiency | **0.95** | (unitless) |
| $\eta_{bat}$ | Battery Round-Trip Efficiency | **0.80 – 0.85** | (unitless) |
| $\eta_{charge}$ | Charge Controller Efficiency | **0.95 – 0.98** | (unitless) |
| DoD | Depth of Discharge (Lead-acid) | **0.50 – 0.80** | (unitless) |
| $E_{d,Thiruvananthapuram}$ | Peak Sun Hours (Kerala average) | **4.5 – 5.0** | h/day |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Design Steps for a Stand-Alone PV System — Operational Logic

The design of a stand-alone PV system follows a **deterministic sequential algorithm** prescribed by KTU 2024 syllabus. The procedure is a closed-loop iterative process that converges on the smallest PV array and battery combination that satisfies load demand under the worst-case meteorological conditions.

### STEP 1 — Load Assessment (Energy Audit)

The first step is the **quantification of the load profile**. The total daily energy consumption is computed by summing the energy demand of each appliance over a 24-hour window.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** The student must always express the **total daily energy in Wh/day** (or kWh/day) and the **total peak power demand in W (or kW)**. These two numbers drive the entire design.

**Logic Steps:**
- List every load (lamp, fan, TV, refrigerator, pump, etc.).
- Note the rated power $P_i$ in watts.
- Note the daily operating hours $n_i$.
- Compute energy per load: $E_i = P_i \times n_i$.
- Sum: $E_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} P_i \times n_i$.
- Identify peak load: $P_{peak} = \max(P_i)$ (for inverter sizing).

---

### STEP 2 — Sizing of the PV Array (Wp)

The PV array must generate enough energy per day to compensate for **all system losses** (battery, charge controller, wiring, temperature, dust) and meet the load demand.

**Why losses matter:** A 1 kWp module does **not** deliver 1 kWh per peak sun hour in practice. Multiple derating factors are multiplied to account for real-world degradation.

**How it is computed:** The required array watt-peak is obtained by dividing the corrected daily energy by the **equivalent peak sun hours (PSH)** of the site, which in Kerala is typically **4.5 – 5.0 h/day**.

---

### STEP 3 — Sizing of the Battery Bank (Ah)

The battery bank must provide:
- **Days of Autonomy ($N_{aut}$):** Number of consecutive cloudy days the system must survive on battery alone.
- **Depth of Discharge (DoD):** Maximum fraction of battery capacity that can be safely discharged.
- **Temperature Correction Factor ($f_t$):** Battery capacity derates at low temperatures (relevant for hill stations like Munnar, Wayanad).

**Logic Steps:**
- Convert load energy to battery Ah: $E_{load} \times N_{aut}$.
- Apply DoD (typical 0.5 for lead-acid, 0.8 for lithium).
- Apply temperature correction.
- Divide by battery nominal voltage (12 V, 24 V, 48 V) to get Ah.

---

### STEP 4 — Sizing of the Charge Controller

The charge controller rating must exceed the **short-circuit current ($I_{sc}$) of the array multiplied by a safety factor of 1.25** (per IS/IEC standards).

---

### STEP 5 — Sizing of the Inverter

The inverter rating must handle the **continuous peak AC load** with a safety margin (typically 1.25 ×) and must be capable of handling **surge loads** (e.g., refrigerator compressor, water pump motor starting).

---

### STEP 6 — Cable & Protection Sizing

Voltage drop in DC cables must be limited to **≤ 3 %** (per KTU 2024 standard) to avoid excessive losses. Cable cross-section is derived from:

$$A = \frac{2 \times \rho \times L \times I}{V_{drop}}$$

where $\rho$ is resistivity (copper = $1.724 \times 10^{-8}$ Ω·m), $L$ is one-way length, $I$ is current, $V_{drop}$ is allowed voltage drop.

---

### STEP 7 — System Integration & Verification

Compute the **Performance Ratio (PR)** and **Capacity Utilization Factor (CUF)** to validate the design.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are KTU Board-Exam-High-Yield. Master this table — it solves ~70% of numerical problems asked in Part B.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Boundary / Notes |
|---|-----------|---------|-------|------------------|
| 1 | Daily Energy of Load | $E_{load} = \sum P_i \times n_i$ | Wh/day | Per appliance summation |
| 2 | Corrected Daily Energy | $E_{corr} = \dfrac{E_{load}}{\eta_{bat} \cdot \eta_{cc} \cdot \eta_{inv} \cdot \eta_{wire}}$ | Wh/day | Typical overall: 0.65 – 0.75 |
| 3 | PV Array Watt-Peak | $W_p = \dfrac{E_{corr}}{PSH \times \eta_{derate}}$ | Wp | $\eta_{derate} \approx 0.9$ (dust + temp) |
| 4 | Number of PV Modules | $N_m = \dfrac{W_p}{P_{module}}$ | integer | Round up to next integer |
| 5 | Modules in Series (String) | $N_s = \dfrac{V_{DC,system}}{V_{module,mp}}$ | integer | String voltage match |
| 6 | Strings in Parallel | $N_p = \dfrac{N_m}{N_s}$ | integer | Round up |
| 7 | Battery Capacity | $C_{bat} = \dfrac{E_{load} \times N_{aut}}{V_{bat} \times DoD \times \eta_{bat} \times f_t}$ | Ah | $f_t \approx 1.0$ for Kerala |
| 8 | Days of Autonomy | $N_{aut}$ | days | 1–3 days typical; 5 days for critical loads |
| 9 | Charge Controller Rating | $I_{cc} = 1.25 \times I_{sc,array}$ | A | NEC/IEC safety factor |
| 10 | Inverter Rating | $P_{inv} = 1.25 \times P_{peak,AC}$ | VA | Plus surge capability |
| 11 | Cable Cross-Section | $A = \dfrac{2 \rho L I}{\Delta V}$ | mm² | $\Delta V \leq 3\%$ of $V_{DC}$ |
| 12 | Performance Ratio | $PR = \dfrac{E_{actual}}{E_{ideal}}$ | fraction | 0.7 – 0.85 typical |
| 13 | Capacity Utilization Factor | $CUF = \dfrac{E_{annual,actual}}{P_{installed} \times 8760}$ | fraction | 0.15 – 0.20 for India |
| 14 | Battery Round-Trip Efficiency | $\eta_{bat} = \dfrac{E_{out}}{E_{in}}$ | fraction | 0.80 – 0.85 (Lead-acid) ; 0.92 – 0.95 (Li-ion) |
| 15 | State of Charge | $SoC = \dfrac{C_{remaining}}{C_{rated}}$ | fraction | $1 - DoD$ |
| 16 | Number of Batteries in Series | $N_{bat,s} = \dfrac{V_{bat,system}}{V_{bat,unit}}$ | integer | Voltage match |
| 17 | Number of Battery Strings | $N_{bat,p} = \dfrac{C_{bat}}{C_{bat,unit}}$ | integer | Round up |
| 18 | Life Cycle Cost (LCC) | $LCC = C_{cap} + \sum_{t=1}^{N} \dfrac{C_{O\&M,t}}{(1+r)^t} - \dfrac{S}{(1+r)^N}$ | ₹ | Sum of discounted cash flows |
| 19 | Levelized Cost of Energy (LCOE) | $LCOE = \dfrac{LCC}{\sum_{t=1}^{N} E_t / (1+r)^t}$ | ₹/kWh | Standard project metric |
| 20 | Discount Factor | $DF_t = \dfrac{1}{(1+r)^t}$ | unitless | $r$ = discount rate |

---

## 2.3 Storage Batteries — Theoretical Background

> [!NOTE]
> A **storage battery** in a PV system is an electrochemical device that stores the DC electrical energy generated by the PV array during sunshine and delivers it to the load during periods of low or zero irradiance. It is the **most critical and most failure-prone** component of any stand-alone PV system.

### 2.3.1 Key Battery Parameters (KTU High-Yield)

| Parameter | Definition | Typical Value (Lead-Acid) | Typical Value (Li-ion) |
|-----------|------------|---------------------------|-------------------------|
| **Nominal Voltage** | Operating voltage per cell/unit | 2 V (cell) / 12 V (unit) | 3.6 – 3.7 V (cell) |
| **Capacity (C)** | Total charge stored at specified discharge rate | 100 – 200 Ah | 50 – 200 Ah |
| **C-Rate** | Discharge current relative to capacity | C/10, C/20 | C/2, 1C, 2C |
| **Depth of Discharge (DoD)** | Maximum safe discharge fraction | 0.50 – 0.80 | 0.80 – 1.00 |
| **Cycle Life** | Number of charge-discharge cycles | 500 – 1500 | 2000 – 5000 |
| **Round-Trip Efficiency** | $\eta_{bat} = E_{out}/E_{in}$ | 0.80 – 0.85 | 0.92 – 0.95 |
| **Self-Discharge** | Loss per month at 25°C | 3 – 5 % | 1 – 2 % |
| **Operating Temperature** | Range for normal operation | -20 to +50 °C | -20 to +60 °C |
| **Energy Density** | Wh per kg | 30 – 50 | 100 – 250 |
| **Cost per kWh** | (India 2024) | ₹ 8,000 – 12,000 | ₹ 15,000 – 25,000 |

### 2.3.2 Battery Types Used in PV Systems (KTU Syllabus)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three primary battery chemistries dominate PV applications** (KTU 2024 Module 4 mandates coverage of these):

1. **Lead-Acid Batteries (Flooded / VRLA / AGM / Gel):**
   - *Cheapest* per kWh but *lowest* cycle life and lowest DoD.
   - Requires periodic water topping (flooded type) — not preferred for remote unmanned sites.
   - VRLA (Valve-Regulated Lead-Acid) is maintenance-free, popular for solar.

2. **Lithium-Ion Batteries (LiFePO₄ / NMC):**
   - *Highest* cycle life, *highest* DoD, *highest* efficiency.
   - Higher upfront cost but **lowest Levelized Cost of Storage (LCOS)** over lifetime.
   - **LiFePO₄** is the safest Li-ion variant — preferred for residential PV in India.

3. **Nickel-Based Batteries (NiCd, NiFe):**
   - Extremely rugged, can operate at extreme temperatures, very long cycle life.
   - Used in telecom towers and remote industrial sites.
   - Suffers from *memory effect* and contains toxic cadmium (banned in some regions).

### 2.3.3 Ultra-Capacitors (Supercapacitors) — KTU High-Yield

> [!NOTE]
> **Ultra-Capacitor (Electrochemical Double-Layer Capacitor, EDLC):** A high-capacity electrochemical capacitor with capacitance values of hundreds to thousands of Farads, storing energy electrostatically at the electrode-electrolyte interface (not electrochemically like a battery).

| Property | Battery | Ultra-Capacitor |
|----------|---------|------------------|
| Energy Storage Mechanism | Chemical (Faradaic) | Electrostatic (non-Faradaic) |
| Energy Density (Wh/kg) | 30 – 250 | 5 – 10 |
| Power Density (W/kg) | 100 – 1000 | 10,000 – 100,000 |
| Cycle Life | 500 – 5000 | 500,000 – 1,000,000 |
| Charge Time | 1 – 10 hours | 1 – 60 seconds |
| Discharge Time | 0.3 – 5 hours | 0.1 – 60 seconds |
| Operating Temperature | -20 to +50 °C | -40 to +85 °C |
| Cost per kWh | ₹ 8,000 – 25,000 | ₹ 5,00,000 – 10,00,000 |

**Why Ultra-Capacitors Cannot Replace Batteries in PV Systems:**
- Ultra-caps have **low energy density** — they store very little total energy.
- They are excellent for **bridging short power transients** (e.g., motor starting, cloud-edge effects).
- In PV systems, ultra-caps are **hybridized with batteries** to provide peak power assist and extend battery life.

**Hybrid Battery + Ultra-Capacitor Architecture:**

```
[PV Array] → [MPPT Charge Controller] → [DC Bus]
                                              ↓
                                  ┌───────────┴───────────┐
                                  ↓                       ↓
                          [Battery Bank]          [Ultra-Capacitor Bank]
                          (Energy Storage)         (Peak Power Buffer)
                                  └───────────┬───────────┘
                                              ↓
                                       [DC/AC Load]
```

**Engineering Utility:** Ultra-capacitors absorb the **inrush current** during motor starting in solar water pumps, reducing the cyclic stress on the battery bank and extending its cycle life by 30 – 50 %.

---

## 2.4 Design of PV-Powered DC Fan and Pump Without Battery

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Direct Question (Module 4):** "Design a directly-coupled PV water pumping system / DC fan without battery storage. Justify why the battery is not required."

### 2.4.1 Concept — Direct-Coupling (Battery-Less Operation)

In a **direct-coupled PV system**, the PV array is connected **directly** (or through a simple DC-DC converter with MPPT) to the DC load (fan / pump) without any battery or grid. The system operates **only when sunlight is available**.

**Why No Battery is Needed:**
- The application (fan / pump) is **solar-coincident** — the load is needed **only when the sun shines**.
- Examples: agricultural irrigation, daytime ventilation, solar water circulation.
- Eliminating the battery **reduces cost by 30 – 50 %** and **eliminates the weakest link** (battery life).
- System is simpler, more reliable, and has higher round-trip efficiency (no battery loss).

### 2.4.2 Design Procedure

**Step 1 — Load Specification:**
- DC Fan: $P_{fan}$ = 30 W to 75 W (typical BLDC solar fans)
- DC Pump: $P_{pump}$ = 0.5 HP (373 W) to 5 HP (3.73 kW) (typical solar submersible)

**Step 2 — Operating Voltage Selection:**
- Small DC fans: 12 V or 24 V DC.
- Pumps: 24 V, 48 V, 72 V, or 96 V DC.

**Step 3 — PV Array Sizing:**
For a direct-coupled pump/fan, sizing is based on **daily water volume (m³/day)** or **daily air delivery (m³/h)** rather than Wh/day. The hydraulic energy required is:

$$E_{hydraulic} = \rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot H \cdot t$$

where:
- $\rho$ = density of water = **1000 kg/m³**
- $g$ = acceleration due to gravity = **9.81 m/s²**
- $Q$ = flow rate in m³/s
- $H$ = total head in meters
- $t$ = daily pumping hours

**Step 4 — Array Sizing:**

$$W_p = \frac{E_{hydraulic}}{PSH \times \eta_{pump} \times \eta_{wire}}$$

For pumps, overall efficiency $\eta_{pump}$ is typically **0.30 – 0.50** (submersible centrifugal with DC motor + MPPT).

**Step 5 — Module Configuration:**
$$N_s = \frac{V_{system}}{V_{module}}, \quad N_p = \frac{W_p}{N_s \times P_{module}}$$

**Step 6 — MPPT Charge Controller Sizing:**
The controller must be rated for the array's short-circuit current with safety factor:
$$I_{controller} \geq 1.25 \times I_{sc,array}$$

### 2.4.3 Typical PV-Powered DC Pump Specification (KTU Sample)

**Given:** A farmer needs to pump **20,000 liters (20 m³) of water per day** from a depth of **30 m** using a DC submersible pump. Site is in **Thrissur, Kerala** with PSH = 4.8 h/day. Pump overall efficiency = 35 %. Find the PV array size.

**Solution Path:**
- Hydraulic energy: $E_{hyd} = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 20 \times 30 = 5.886 \times 10^6$ J = 1635 Wh
- Wait — the question expects the student to use a **simplified empirical method** where the energy per m³ of water per meter head is approximately **2.725 Wh**.
- $E_{hyd} = 20 \times 30 \times 2.725 = 1635$ Wh
- Array size: $W_p = 1635 / (4.8 \times 0.35) = 972$ Wp ≈ **1 kWp**
- Using 250 Wp modules: $N_m = 4$ modules.

---

## 2.5 Design of Standalone System with Battery and AC or DC Load

This is the **most comprehensive KTU 2024 design problem** — the full procedure is now applied with a battery and a mixed AC/DC load.

### 2.5.1 System Architecture

```
[PV Array] → [MPPT Charge Controller] → [Battery Bank] → [DC Bus]
                                                                ↓
                                                          [DC Loads]
                                                                ↓
                                                          [Inverter] → [AC Loads]
```

### 2.5.2 Complete Design Algorithm (KTU Step-by-Step)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 8-Step KTU Design Algorithm (Memorize this — it is the backbone of Module 4 numerical problems):**

1. **Energy Audit:** Compute $E_{load}$ (Wh/day) and $P_{peak}$ (W).
2. **System Voltage Selection:** Choose $V_{DC}$ = 12 V, 24 V, 48 V, or 96 V based on load size.
3. **PV Array Sizing:** Compute $W_p$ with all derating factors.
4. **Battery Sizing:** Compute $C_{bat}$ (Ah) with DoD, autonomy, and temperature correction.
5. **Charge Controller Sizing:** $I_{cc} = 1.25 \times I_{sc,array}$.
6. **Inverter Sizing:** $P_{inv} = 1.25 \times P_{peak,AC}$ (or 1.5× for motor loads).
7. **Cable Sizing:** Voltage drop ≤ 3 %.
8. **Performance Verification:** Compute PR and CUF.

### 2.5.3 Worked-Out Methodology for AC vs DC Loads

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Concept:** When the load is purely DC, the inverter is eliminated (efficiency gain ≈ 10 – 15 %). When the load is AC, an inverter is mandatory, and its efficiency (≈ 95 %) must be included in the loss budget.

**Modification of the basic energy equation:**

**For Pure DC Load:**
$$W_p = \frac{E_{DC,load}}{PSH \times \eta_{bat} \times \eta_{cc} \times \eta_{wire} \times \eta_{derate}}$$

**For Pure AC Load:**
$$W_p = \frac{E_{AC,load}}{PSH \times \eta_{bat} \times \eta_{cc} \times \eta_{inv} \times \eta_{wire} \times \eta_{derate}}$$

**For Mixed AC + DC Load:**
$$E_{total} = E_{DC,load} + \frac{E_{AC,load}}{\eta_{inv}}$$

---

## 2.6 Life Cycle Costing (LCC) — Engineering Economics of PV

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Life Cycle Costing (LCC)** is a systematic economic analysis method that sums up all the costs incurred over the entire operational lifetime of a PV system — from initial capital investment to operation, maintenance, replacement, and disposal — to determine the **total cost of ownership** and the **cost of energy generated**.

### 2.6.1 LCC Formula (Time Value of Money)

$$LCC = C_{cap} + \sum_{t=1}^{N} \frac{C_{O\&M,t}}{(1+r)^t} + \sum_{t=k,m,...} \frac{C_{replace,t}}{(1+r)^t} - \frac{S}{(1+r)^N}$$

where:
- $C_{cap}$ = Initial capital cost (₹) — modules, BOS, installation
- $C_{O\&M,t}$ = Operation & Maintenance cost in year $t$ (₹/year)
- $C_{replace,t}$ = Component replacement cost (e.g., battery at year 5, 10, 15) (₹)
- $S$ = Salvage value at end of life (₹)
- $r$ = Discount rate (typically 8 – 12 % for India)
- $N$ = Project life (25 years for PV modules, 5–7 years for batteries)

### 2.6.2 Levelized Cost of Energy (LCOE) — The Comparable Metric

$$LCOE = \frac{LCC}{\sum_{t=1}^{N} \frac{E_t}{(1+r)^t}}$$

where $E_t$ is the annual energy produced in year $t$ (kWh). The LCOE is the **single most important metric** for comparing PV with grid power, diesel generation, or wind.

**Typical LCOE Values (India, 2024):**
- Rooftop Solar (without battery): ₹ 2.5 – 4.0 /kWh
- Rooftop Solar (with battery): ₹ 6.0 – 10.0 /kWh
- Utility-Scale Solar: ₹ 2.0 – 3.0 /kWh
- Diesel Generation: ₹ 18 – 25 /kWh

### 2.6.3 Components of LCC for a Stand-Alone PV System

| Cost Head | Typical Share | Notes |
|-----------|---------------|-------|
| PV Modules | 35 – 45 % | One-time; 25-year life |
| Battery Bank | 20 – 30 % | Replaced 2 – 4 times over project life |
| Inverter + BOS | 15 – 20 % | Inverter replaced once at year 10 – 12 |
| Mounting & Civil | 8 – 12 % | One-time |
| Installation | 5 – 8 % | One-time |
| O&M (annual) | 1 – 2 % of capex/year | Module cleaning, battery maintenance |
| Replacement Battery | (recurring) | Year 5, 10, 15 (lead-acid); Year 12, 20 (Li-ion) |

### 2.6.4 Discounted Payback Period (DPP)

The DPP is the time (in years) required for the cumulative discounted cash savings to equal the initial capital investment. It is the **preferred metric** over simple payback when comparing PV with grid/diesel.

$$DPP = \min \left\{ t \mid \sum_{k=0}^{t} \frac{(C_{saving} - C_{O\&M})_k}{(1+r)^k} \geq C_{cap} \right\}$$

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — PV Array Watt-Peak Sizing from Load Energy

### Objective
Derive the closed-form expression for the required PV array watt-peak $W_p$ from the daily load energy $E_{load}$, incorporating all system losses.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Start with the daily load energy requirement:
$$E_{load} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} P_i \times n_i \quad \text{[Wh/day]}$$

> *Logic:* Each appliance $i$ consumes power $P_i$ for $n_i$ hours per day. The total daily energy is the sum of all appliance energies.

**Step 2:** The PV array must generate **more** energy than the load to compensate for system losses. Define the system efficiency $\eta_{sys}$ as the product of all sub-efficiencies:
$$\eta_{sys} = \eta_{derate} \times \eta_{cc} \times \eta_{bat} \times \eta_{inv} \times \eta_{wire}$$

> *Logic:* The derate factor accounts for temperature, dust, module mismatch, and aging (typical 0.85 – 0.90). The charge controller, battery, inverter, and wiring each introduce additional losses.

**Step 3:** The actual energy the PV array must deliver per day is:
$$E_{PV} = \frac{E_{load}}{\eta_{sys}}$$

> *Logic:* Since each stage of the system dissipates some energy as heat, the array must produce more than the load consumes to ensure the load receives exactly $E_{load}$.

**Step 4:** The daily energy harvested by a $W_p$-rated array under peak sun hours PSH is:
$$E_{PV} = W_p \times PSH$$

> *Logic:* At STC (1000 W/m²), a 1 Wp array generates 1 Wh in 1 hour. Over PSH hours, it generates $W_p \times PSH$ Wh.

**Step 5:** Equating Step 3 and Step 4:
$$W_p \times PSH = \frac{E_{load}}{\eta_{sys}}$$

**Step 6:** Solving for $W_p$:
$$\boxed{W_p = \frac{E_{load}}{PSH \times \eta_{sys}}}$$

> *Final Result:* The required PV array watt-peak is the daily load energy divided by the product of peak sun hours and overall system efficiency.

**Step 7:** Number of modules (with module rating $P_{mod}$):
$$N_m = \left\lceil \frac{W_p}{P_{mod}} \right\rceil$$

> *Logic:* The ceiling function `⌈ ⌉` ensures we round **up** to the next integer to avoid undersizing.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Battery Bank Capacity in Ampere-Hours

### Objective
Derive the expression for battery bank capacity $C_{bat}$ (Ah) considering autonomy, depth of discharge, and temperature derating.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Energy required from the battery over $N_{aut}$ autonomy days:
$$E_{bat} = E_{load} \times N_{aut} \quad \text{[Wh]}$$

> *Logic:* The battery must supply the full daily load for $N_{aut}$ consecutive days without any solar input (autonomous operation).

**Step 2:** Convert Wh to Ah by dividing by the battery system voltage $V_{bat}$:
$$C_{raw} = \frac{E_{bat}}{V_{bat}} = \frac{E_{load} \times N_{aut}}{V_{bat}} \quad \text{[Ah]}$$

> *Logic:* Battery capacity is fundamentally measured in Ampere-Hours (Ah = Wh / V), not Watt-Hours.

**Step 3:** Apply the **Depth of Discharge (DoD)** constraint. The battery cannot be discharged below $(1 - DoD)$ of its rated capacity. Hence the **usable** capacity is $DoD \times C_{rated}$. To get the required usable capacity, we must divide by DoD:
$$C_{bat,no\_temp} = \frac{C_{raw}}{DoD} = \frac{E_{load} \times N_{aut}}{V_{bat} \times DoD}$$

> *Logic:* If DoD = 0.5 (lead-acid), only 50% of the battery is usable. To deliver $C_{raw}$ Ah, the rated capacity must be twice as large.

**Step 4:** Apply **battery round-trip efficiency** $\eta_{bat}$. The charge stored must exceed the energy delivered:
$$C_{bat,no\_temp} = \frac{E_{load} \times N_{aut}}{V_{bat} \times DoD \times \eta_{bat}}$$

> *Logic:* During charge-discharge, 15 – 20% of the input energy is lost as heat. The battery must be charged with more than it delivers.

**Step 5:** Apply **temperature derating factor** $f_t$ (≥ 1; for Kerala ambient ≈ 1.0, for high-altitude stations like Munnar at sub-zero nights, $f_t$ may be 1.1 – 1.2):
$$\boxed{C_{bat} = \frac{E_{load} \times N_{aut}}{V_{bat} \times DoD \times \eta_{bat} \times f_t}}$$

> *Final Result:* The required battery bank capacity in Ampere-Hours.

**Step 6:** Number of battery units:
$$N_{bat,s} = \left\lceil \frac{V_{bat,system}}{V_{bat,unit}} \right\rceil \quad \text{(series, voltage match)}$$
$$N_{bat,p} = \left\lceil \frac{C_{bat}}{C_{bat,unit}} \right\rceil \quad \text{(parallel, capacity match)}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Inverter Sizing from AC Peak Load

### Objective
Derive the inverter rating $P_{inv}$ (VA) for a given AC peak load with motor surge considerations.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** The total continuous AC load power:
$$P_{AC,cont} = \sum_{j=1}^{M} P_{AC,j} \quad \text{[W]}$$

**Step 2:** Apply a **safety margin** of 25 % to handle sustained overloads and future load additions:
$$P_{inv,min} = 1.25 \times P_{AC,cont} \quad \text{[VA]}$$

**Step 3:** For motor loads (refrigerator compressor, water pump, washing machine), account for the **surge / starting current**, which is typically 3 – 7× the rated current for 1 – 5 seconds:
$$P_{inv,with\_surge} = P_{AC,cont} \times 1.25 + P_{motor,largest} \times k_{surge}$$

where $k_{surge}$ = 3 for small motors, 5 – 7 for large motors.

**Step 4:** Select the next standard inverter rating above $P_{inv,with\_surge}$:
$$\boxed{P_{inv} \geq \max(1.25 \times P_{AC,cont}, \, P_{AC,cont} + k_{surge} \times P_{motor,largest})}$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Charge Controller Sizing

### Objective
Derive the charge controller current rating from the PV array short-circuit current.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Each module has a short-circuit current $I_{sc,mod}$ (typically 8 – 12 A for a 250 Wp module). The array's total short-circuit current is:
$$I_{sc,array} = N_p \times I_{sc,mod}$$

**Step 2:** Per IS/IEC 62548, apply a safety factor of 1.25:
$$\boxed{I_{controller} \geq 1.25 \times I_{sc,array}}$$

> *Logic:* The safety factor compensates for the fact that $I_{sc}$ can exceed the STC value under "edge-of-cloud" focusing (up to 1.25 × STC irradiance).

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — DC Cable Cross-Section from Voltage Drop Limit

### Objective
Derive the cable cross-sectional area $A$ such that the voltage drop is ≤ 3 % of the system DC voltage.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** The voltage drop across a two-conductor (positive + negative) DC cable is:
$$\Delta V = \frac{2 \times I \times \rho \times L}{A}$$

where:
- $I$ = current in the cable (A)
- $\rho$ = resistivity of conductor ($\Omega \cdot m$); copper = $1.724 \times 10^{-8}$
- $L$ = one-way cable length (m)
- $A$ = cross-sectional area (m²)

**Step 2:** The maximum permissible voltage drop is:
$$\Delta V_{max} = 0.03 \times V_{DC,system}$$

**Step 3:** Equate and solve for $A$:
$$A = \frac{2 \times I \times \rho \times L}{\Delta V_{max}} = \frac{2 \times I \times \rho \times L}{0.03 \times V_{DC}}$$

**Step 4:** Convert to mm² (multiply by $10^6$):
$$\boxed{A = \frac{2 \times I \times \rho \times L}{0.03 \times V_{DC}} \times 10^6 \quad \text{[mm}²\text{]}}$$

---

## 3.6 Derivation 6 — Levelized Cost of Energy (LCOE)

### Objective
Derive the LCOE expression from the LCC.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** The Life Cycle Cost is the sum of discounted costs minus salvage:
$$LCC = C_{cap} + \sum_{t=1}^{N} \frac{C_{O\&M,t}}{(1+r)^t} + \sum_{t=k,...} \frac{C_{replace,t}}{(1+r)^t} - \frac{S}{(1+r)^N}$$

**Step 2:** The total discounted energy produced over the project life:
$$E_{total,disc} = \sum_{t=1}^{N} \frac{E_t}{(1+r)^t}$$

**Step 3:** The LCOE is defined as the LCC divided by the total discounted energy:
$$\boxed{LCOE = \frac{LCC}{\sum_{t=1}^{N} \frac{E_t}{(1+r)^t}} \quad \text{[₹/kWh]}}$$

**Step 4 (Simplified case):** If $E_t = E$ (constant annual energy) and we approximate the geometric series:
$$LCOE \approx \frac{C_{cap} \times CRF + C_{O\&M,annual}}{E_{annual}}$$

where $CRF$ (Capital Recovery Factor) is:
$$CRF = \frac{r(1+r)^N}{(1+r)^N - 1}$$

> *Logic:* The CRF converts the present-value capital cost into an equivalent uniform annual cost, which is then divided by the annual energy to get the per-kWh cost.

---

## 3.7 Comprehensive Numerical Example (KTU-Style 14-Mark Problem)

### Problem Statement
Design a stand-alone PV system for a remote home in **Palakkad, Kerala** (PSH = 5.0 h/day) with the following load profile:

| Appliance | Qty | Rating (W) | Hours/Day |
|-----------|-----|------------|-----------|
| LED Bulb | 6 | 9 | 5 |
| Ceiling Fan (BLDC) | 4 | 30 | 8 |
| Television (LED) | 1 | 60 | 4 |
| Refrigerator (Inverter) | 1 | 150 | 10 (with thermostat) |
| Water Pump (AC) | 1 | 750 | 1 |

**System Constraints:**
- System DC voltage: $V_{bat} = 48$ V
- Days of autonomy: $N_{aut} = 2$ days
- Depth of Discharge: $DoD = 0.5$ (Lead-acid)
- Battery efficiency: $\eta_{bat} = 0.85$
- Inverter efficiency: $\eta_{inv} = 0.93$
- Charge controller efficiency: $\eta_{cc} = 0.97$
- Wiring efficiency: $\eta_{wire} = 0.98$
- Derate factor: $\eta_{derate} = 0.9$
- Module rating: 250 Wp, $V_{mp}$ = 30 V, $I_{sc}$ = 8.5 A

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Daily Energy Consumption**

| Appliance | Computation | Energy (Wh/day) |
|-----------|-------------|------------------|
| LED Bulb | $6 \times 9 \times 5$ | 270 |
| Ceiling Fan | $4 \times 30 \times 8$ | 960 |
| Television | $1 \times 60 \times 4$ | 240 |
| Refrigerator | $1 \times 150 \times 10$ | 1500 |
| Water Pump | $1 \times 750 \times 1$ | 750 |
| **Total** | | **$E_{load} = 3720$ Wh/day** |

**Step 2: System Overall Efficiency**

$$\eta_{sys} = 0.9 \times 0.97 \times 0.85 \times 0.93 \times 0.98 = 0.679 \approx 0.68$$

**Step 3: PV Array Sizing**

$$W_p = \frac{3720}{5.0 \times 0.68} = \frac{3720}{3.4} = 1094 \text{ Wp}$$

Use $\lceil 1094/250 \rceil = 5$ modules of 250 Wp.

**Module Configuration:**
- $N_s = 48/30 = 1.6 \Rightarrow$ Use $N_s = 2$ (string voltage ≈ 60 V)
- $N_p = 5/2 = 2.5 \Rightarrow$ Use $N_p = 3$
- **Total modules = 2 × 3 = 6 modules = 1500 Wp** (with oversizing margin)

**Step 4: Battery Bank Sizing**

$$C_{bat} = \frac{E_{load} \times N_{aut}}{V_{bat} \times DoD \times \eta_{bat}} = \frac{3720 \times 2}{48 \times 0.5 \times 0.85} = \frac{7440}{20.4} = 364.7 \text{ Ah}$$

**Battery Configuration:**
- Battery unit: 12 V, 200 Ah (typical C/10 lead-acid)
- $N_{bat,s} = 48/12 = 4$ in series
- $N_{bat,p} = 365/200 = 1.83 \Rightarrow$ Use $N_{bat,p} = 2$
- **Total batteries = 4 × 2 = 8 batteries** → Bank capacity = 400 Ah at 48 V

**Step 5: Charge Controller Sizing**

$$I_{sc,array} = 3 \times 8.5 = 25.5 \text{ A}$$
$$I_{controller} = 1.25 \times 25.5 = 31.9 \text{ A} \Rightarrow \text{Use 40 A MPPT controller}$$

**Step 6: Inverter Sizing**

- Continuous AC load: $9 \times 6 + 30 \times 4 + 60 + 150 + 750 = 54 + 120 + 60 + 150 + 750 = 1134$ W (worst case all on)
- With 1.25 safety: $1.25 \times 1134 = 1417$ VA
- With pump surge (×3): $1134 - 750 + 750 \times 3 = 2634$ VA
- **Inverter rating: 3 kVA** (with at least 3× surge handling for 5 sec)

**Step 7: Cable Sizing (Example for 10 m PV-to-Controller run)**

$$A = \frac{2 \times 1.724 \times 10^{-8} \times 10 \times 25.5}{0.03 \times 48} \times 10^6 = \frac{8.79 \times 10^{-6}}{1.44} \times 10^6 = 6.1 \text{ mm}²$$

**Use 10 mm² cable** (next standard size up).

**Step 8: Performance Verification**

$$PR = \frac{E_{actual}}{W_p \times PSH \times 365} = \frac{3720 \times 365}{1500 \times 5.0 \times 365} = \frac{3720}{7500} = 0.496$$

> *Note:* PR of 0.50 is on the lower side because the array is oversized (1500 Wp vs required 1094 Wp). The actual PR in practice would be higher (0.70 – 0.85) because surplus energy is used and the array is operating below its rated capacity.

**Design Summary:**

| Component | Specification |
|-----------|---------------|
| PV Array | 6 × 250 Wp = 1500 Wp (2S × 3P) |
| Battery Bank | 8 × 12 V, 200 Ah = 48 V, 400 Ah |
| Charge Controller | 48 V, 40 A MPPT |
| Inverter | 3 kVA, 48 V DC → 230 V AC, pure sine wave |
| Total Load | 3720 Wh/day |
| Autonomy | 2 days (with 50% DoD) |

```mermaid
    SUN[Sunlight] --> PV[PV Array]
    PV --> DCBOX[DC Combiner Box]
    DCBOX --> GTI[Grid-Tie Inverter with MPPT and Anti-Island]
    GTI --> METER[Net Meter Bidirectional]
    METER --> GRID[Utility Grid 11kV or 230V]
    METER --> LOAD[Local AC Loads]
    GTI --> ISLAND[Anti-Islanding Protection per IEEE 1547]
    GTI --> SYNC[Grid Sync PLL]
    GTI --> MPPT[Maximum Power Point Tracking]
    GTI --> PROT[OV UV OF UF OF Protection]
```

---

## 4.4 Design Algorithm Flowchart — 8-Step KTU Stand-Alone Design

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1[Step 1 Load Energy Audit Wh/day] --> S2[Step 2 Select System DC Voltage]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3 Compute PV Array Wp]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4 Compute Battery Ah]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5 Size Charge Controller]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6 Size Inverter with Surge]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7 Cable and Protection Sizing]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8 Verify PR and CUF]
    S8 --> CHECK{Meets Load with Autonomy?}
    CHECK -->|Yes| DONE[Design Complete]
    CHECK -->|No| S3[Iterate Increase Wp or Battery]
```

---

## 4.5 Battery + Ultra-Capacitor Hybrid Storage Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    PV[PV Array] --> MPPT[MPPT Controller]
    MPPT --> DCBUS[DC Bus]
    DCBUS --> BMS[Battery Management System]
    BMS --> BATT[Li-ion Battery Bank Energy Buffer]
    DCBUS --> UC[Ultra-Capacitor Bank Power Buffer]
    BATT --> INVERTER[Inverter]
    UC --> INVERTER
    INVERTER --> LOAD[AC and DC Load]
    UC -.->|Absorbs Inrush| LOAD
    BATT -.->|Supplies Steady Energy| LOAD
```

---

## 4.6 Life Cycle Costing — Cash Flow Timeline (25 Years)

```mermaid
graph LR
    Y0[Year 0 Capital Cost] --> Y1[Year 1 O and M]
    Y1 --> Y2[Year 2 O and M]
    Y2 --> Y3[Year 3 O and M]
    Y3 --> Y4[Year 4 O and M]
    Y4 --> Y5[Year 5 Battery Replace 1]
    Y5 --> Y10[Year 10 Battery Replace 2 plus Inverter Replace]
    Y10 --> Y15[Year 15 Battery Replace 3]
    Y15 --> Y20[Year 20 Battery Replace 4 Li-ion Optional]
    Y20 --> Y25[Year 25 Salvage Value minus Decommissioning]
    Y0 --> DISCOUNT[All Discounted to Present Value at Rate r]
    Y1 --> DISCOUNT
    Y5 --> DISCOUNT
    Y10 --> DISCOUNT
    Y15 --> DISCOUNT
    Y20 --> DISCOUNT
    Y25 --> DISCOUNT
    DISCOUNT --> LCC[Life Cycle Cost LCC]
    DISCOUNT --> LCOE[Levelized Cost LCOE per kWh]
```

---

## 4.7 Direct-Coupled (Battery-Less) PV Pump System

```mermaid
graph LR
    SUN[Sunshine] --> PV[PV Array 1 to 5 kWp]
    PV --> MPPT[MPPT DC-DC Converter]
    MPPT --> PUMP[DC Submersible Pump Motor]
    PUMP --> WATER[Water Tank or Field]
    MPPT --> SW[DC Switch and Protection]
    SW --> SENSOR[Water Level Sensor Optional]
    SENSOR --> MPPT
    MPPT -->|Match V-I Curve| PUMP
```

> *Engineering note:* The MPPT block in a battery-less system is critical — it dynamically adjusts the operating point of the PV array to track the maximum power point as irradiance changes, ensuring the pump runs at optimal efficiency throughout the day.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A — 3-MARK QUESTIONS (Direct Short-Answer)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**
*Distinguish between stand-alone and grid-connected PV systems. Mention two applications of each.*

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Stand-Alone PV System | Grid-Connected PV System |
|---------|------------------------|---------------------------|
| Grid Connection | Not connected to the utility grid | Connected and synchronized to the utility grid |
| Battery Storage | Mandatory (autonomy requirement) | Not mandatory (net-metering acts as virtual storage) |
| Power Flow | One-way (PV → Load) | Bi-directional (PV ↔ Grid) |

**Applications of Stand-Alone:** (1) Remote village electrification, (2) Solar water pumps for agriculture, (3) Telecom tower power supply, (4) Street lighting.

**Applications of Grid-Connected:** (1) Urban rooftop installations under PM-Surya Ghar Yojana, (2) Utility-scale solar farms (e.g., Pavagada Solar Park, Karnataka), (3) Commercial building-integrated PV, (4) Industrial captive power.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**
*What is the role of ultra-capacitors in a PV system? Why are they not used as the sole energy storage element?*

**Model Answer:**

**Role of Ultra-Capacitors:**
1. **Peak Power Buffering:** They absorb/deliver high surge currents (e.g., during motor starting in a solar pump) within milliseconds, protecting the battery from high inrush stress.
2. **Cycle-Life Extension of Battery:** By handling transient load fluctuations (cloud-edge effects, switching surges), the ultra-capacitor reduces the number of charge-discharge cycles on the battery, extending its life by 30 – 50 %.
3. **Fast Charging:** Ultra-capacitors can absorb the full PV array output during brief irradiance peaks, preventing controller overload.

**Why Not Sole Energy Storage:**
1. **Very Low Energy Density:** Ultra-capacitors store only 5 – 10 Wh/kg, compared to 30 – 50 Wh/kg for lead-acid and 100 – 250 Wh/kg for Li-ion. They cannot sustain a load for hours.
2. **High Self-Discharge:** Up to 10 – 20 % per day, compared to 1 – 5 % per month for batteries.
3. **High Cost per kWh:** ₹ 5 – 10 lakh per kWh vs ₹ 8,000 – 25,000 per kWh for batteries.
4. **Voltage Discharge Curve:** Ultra-caps exhibit a linear voltage drop during discharge, which is unsuitable for direct DC bus operation.

**Conclusion:** Ultra-capacitors are **always used in hybrid configuration with batteries** — battery for energy, ultra-capacitor for power.

---

## PART B — 14-MARK QUESTIONS (Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Per KTU 2024 Scheme:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks and offers an internal choice. Students must answer either OR. Each question is split into two 7-mark sub-parts.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**

**(a)** Explain the step-by-step procedure for designing a stand-alone PV system for a remote home. State all design equations and the role of each component in the system. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A remote home in Idukki, Kerala has the following daily load: 4 LED bulbs (9 W each, 5 h/day), 3 ceiling fans (30 W each, 8 h/day), 1 television (80 W, 4 h/day), and 1 refrigerator (200 W, 10 h/day). Design the PV array and battery bank. Assume PSH = 4.5 h/day, system voltage 48 V, $N_{aut} = 2$ days, $DoD = 0.5$, $\eta_{bat} = 0.85$, $\eta_{inv} = 0.93$, $\eta_{cc} = 0.97$, $\eta_{wire} = 0.98$, $\eta_{derate} = 0.9$. **(7 marks)**

### Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) — Step-by-Step Design Procedure (7 marks)

> [!NOTE]
> The student should write the 8 steps with equations, followed by a clear description of each component. (Below is the KTU model answer — write concisely in 1 – 2 sentences per step.)

**Step 1 — Load Assessment:** Compute daily energy consumption and peak power:
$$E_{load} = \sum P_i \times n_i \quad ; \quad P_{peak} = \max(P_i)$$

**Step 2 — System DC Voltage Selection:** Choose $V_{DC}$ = 12 V (small), 24 V (medium), 48 V (large). The choice balances current (lower current → smaller cables) with safety.

**Step 3 — PV Array Sizing:** Required watt-peak:
$$W_p = \frac{E_{load}}{PSH \times \eta_{sys}}$$

**Step 4 — Battery Bank Sizing:** Required Ah capacity:
$$C_{bat} = \frac{E_{load} \times N_{aut}}{V_{bat} \times DoD \times \eta_{bat} \times f_t}$$

**Step 5 — Charge Controller Sizing:**
$$I_{cc} = 1.25 \times N_p \times I_{sc,mod}$$

**Step 6 — Inverter Sizing:**
$$P_{inv} = 1.25 \times P_{AC,peak} \text{ (with surge handling)}$$

**Step 7 — Cable Sizing:** For voltage drop ≤ 3 %:
$$A = \frac{2 \rho L I}{0.03 \times V_{DC}} \times 10^6 \text{ mm}²$$

**Step 8 — Performance Verification:** Compute PR and CUF.

**Role of Each Component:**
- **PV Array:** Converts sunlight to DC electricity.
- **Charge Controller:** Regulates charge to prevent battery overcharge; provides MPPT.
- **Battery Bank:** Stores energy for night and cloudy days.
- **Inverter:** Converts DC to AC for AC loads.
- **Protection Devices:** Fuses, MCBs, SPDs — ensure safety.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Design (7 marks)

**Step 1: Daily Energy**

| Appliance | Computation | Wh/day |
|-----------|-------------|--------|
| LED | $4 \times 9 \times 5$ | 180 |
| Fan | $3 \times 30 \times 8$ | 720 |
| TV | $1 \times 80 \times 4$ | 320 |
| Refrigerator | $1 \times 200 \times 10$ | 2000 |
| **Total** | | **3220 Wh/day** |

**Step 2: Overall System Efficiency**
$$\eta_{sys} = 0.9 \times 0.97 \times 0.85 \times 0.93 \times 0.98 = 0.679$$

**Step 3: PV Array Watt-Peak**
$$W_p = \frac{3220}{4.5 \times 0.679} = \frac{3220}{3.056} = 1053.5 \text{ Wp} \approx 1100 \text{ Wp}$$

With 250 Wp modules: $N_m = \lceil 1100/250 \rceil = \lceil 4.4 \rceil = 5$ modules.

**Module configuration (48 V system, 30 Vmp modules):**
- $N_s = 48/30 = 1.6 \Rightarrow 2$ in series
- $N_p = 5/2 = 2.5 \Rightarrow 3$ in parallel
- **Total: 6 modules (2S × 3P) = 1500 Wp** (with oversizing margin)

**Step 4: Battery Bank Sizing**
$$C_{bat} = \frac{3220 \times 2}{48 \times 0.5 \times 0.85 \times 1.0} = \frac{6440}{20.4} = 315.7 \text{ Ah}$$

Using 12 V, 150 Ah battery units:
- $N_{bat,s} = 48/12 = 4$ in series
- $N_{bat,p} = 315.7/150 = 2.1 \Rightarrow 3$ in parallel
- **Total: 4 × 3 = 12 batteries = 48 V, 450 Ah**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**(a)** Describe the construction and working of a Lead-Acid battery used in a PV system. Explain the terms Depth of Discharge (DoD), State of Charge (SoC), and C-rate. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design a directly-coupled (battery-less) PV water pumping system for a farm in Wayanad. The required daily water output is 30,000 liters from a total head of 25 m. The pump overall efficiency is 35 %, and PSH at the site is 4.5 h/day. Calculate the required PV array size and the number of modules. Use 330 Wp modules with $V_{mp}$ = 34 V, $I_{mp}$ = 9.7 A. **(7 marks)**

### Solution to Question B

#### Part (a) — Lead-Acid Battery (7 marks)

**Construction:** A lead-acid cell consists of:
- **Positive Plate:** Lead dioxide ($\text{PbO}_2$) on a lead-antimony grid.
- **Negative Plate:** Sponge lead ($\text{Pb}$) on a lead-antimony grid.
- **Electrolyte:** Dilute sulfuric acid ($\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$ + water), specific gravity 1.21 – 1.28.
- **Separator:** Microporous polyethylene or rubber to prevent short-circuit between plates.
- **Container:** Polypropylene or hard rubber; cells connected in series via lead straps.

**Working Principle — Discharge:**
$$\text{Pb} + \text{PbO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \rightarrow 2\text{PbSO}_4 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{Energy (electrical)}$$

**Working Principle — Charge:** Reverse of above (electrolysis).

**Key Terminology:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **DoD (Depth of Discharge):** The fraction of battery capacity that has been discharged. $\text{DoD} = (C_{discharged}/C_{rated}) \times 100\%$. For lead-acid PV batteries, DoD is limited to **50 – 80 %** to maximize cycle life.

> **SoC (State of Charge):** The remaining capacity as a fraction of rated capacity. $\text{SoC} = 1 - \text{DoD}$. SoC = 100 % when fully charged; SoC = 0 % at full discharge.

> **C-Rate:** The rate at which a battery is discharged relative to its rated capacity. A discharge current equal to the rated capacity is called the **1C rate**. A 100 Ah battery at C/10 delivers 10 A for 10 hours. Higher C-rates reduce usable capacity (Peukert's effect).

#### Part (b) — Direct-Coupled PV Pump Design (7 marks)

**Step 1: Hydraulic Energy Required**

Empirical energy per m³ per meter head ≈ **2.725 Wh** (for $\rho g / \eta_{wire}$ conversion).

$$E_{hyd} = Q \times H \times 2.725 = 30 \times 25 \times 2.725 = 2043.75 \text{ Wh/day}$$

*Alternative rigorous method:*
$$E_{hyd} = \rho g Q H t = 1000 \times 9.81 \times (30/86400) \times 25 \times (4.5 \times 3600) = 5512 \text{ kJ} = 1531 \text{ Wh}$$
*(Note: The two methods differ because the empirical factor already includes the dynamic viscosity and minor losses.)*

Using the rigorous method (more accepted in KTU):

$$E_{hyd} = 1531 \text{ Wh/day}$$

**Step 2: PV Array Sizing**

$$W_p = \frac{E_{hyd}}{PSH \times \eta_{pump} \times \eta_{wire}} = \frac{1531}{4.5 \times 0.35 \times 0.98} = \frac{1531}{1.5435} = 992 \text{ Wp}$$

**Step 3: Number of Modules**

$$N_m = \left\lceil \frac{992}{330} \right\rceil = \lceil 3.006 \rceil = 4 \text{ modules of 330 Wp} = 1320 \text{ Wp total}$$

**Step 4: Configuration (Pump operating at 72 V DC, 4-string series)**
- $N_s = 72/34 = 2.12 \Rightarrow 2$ in series
- $N_p = 4/2 = 2$ in parallel
- **Total: 2S × 2P = 4 modules (1320 Wp)**

**Step 5: MPPT Controller Sizing**
$$I_{cc} = 1.25 \times 2 \times I_{sc,mod} = 1.25 \times 2 \times 10.5 = 26.25 \text{ A} \Rightarrow \text{Use 30 A MPPT}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (HIGH-FREQUENCY DEDUCTION):**
> 1. **Forgetting the derate factor** (0.9) — costs 1 mark.
> 2. **Using peak power $P_{peak}$ instead of daily energy $E_{load}$** for battery sizing — costs 2 marks. Battery is sized in **Ah** (energy), not W (power).
> 3. **Forgetting to round UP** the number of modules or batteries. Always use ceiling $\lceil \cdot \rceil$, never floor.
> 4. **Incorrect DoD value** — using DoD = 1.0 for lead-acid is a critical error. Use 0.5 – 0.7.
> 5. **Omitting the 1.25 safety factor** for charge controller and inverter.
> 6. **Mixing up autonomy direction** — Autonomy = battery-only days (cloudy), NOT sun hours.
> 7. **LCC numerical errors:** Discount factor in the wrong year, salvage value added instead of subtracted, O&M not converted to present value.
> 8. **In PV pump problems:** Forgetting to apply pump efficiency $\eta_{pump}$ — the hydraulic energy is NOT the same as the electrical energy from the array.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Final Read Before Exam):**

### Quick-Recall Definitions
- **Stand-Alone PV System:** Off-grid; mandatory battery; one-way power flow.
- **Grid-Connected PV System:** On-grid; no battery; bidirectional; anti-islanding mandatory.
- **Peak Sun Hours (PSH):** Equivalent hours of 1000 W/m² irradiance; for Kerala = 4.5 – 5.0 h/day.
- **Depth of Discharge (DoD):** Fraction of battery discharged; 0.5 for lead-acid, 0.8 for Li-ion.
- **State of Charge (SoC):** $1 - DoD$.
- **Days of Autonomy ($N_{aut}$):** Days the battery can supply load without sun; typically 1 – 3 days.
- **Performance Ratio (PR):** Actual/ideal energy output; typical 0.70 – 0.85.
- **Capacity Utilization Factor (CUF):** Actual annual energy / (rated power × 8760); typical 0.15 – 0.20 for India.
- **LCC:** Total discounted cost over project life.
- **LCOE:** ₹/kWh — cost per unit of energy generated.

### Must-Memorize Formulas (Top 8)
1. $W_p = E_{load} / (PSH \times \eta_{sys})$
2. $C_{bat} = (E_{load} \times N_{aut}) / (V_{bat} \times DoD \times \eta_{bat})$
3. $I_{cc} = 1.25 \times I_{sc,array}$
4. $P_{inv} = 1.25 \times P_{AC,peak}$ (plus surge for motors)
5. $A = (2 \rho L I) / (0.03 \times V_{DC}) \times 10^6$ mm²
6. $LCC = C_{cap} + \sum C_{O\&M}/(1+r)^t - S/(1+r)^N$
7. $LCOE = LCC / \sum E_t/(1+r)^t$
8. $CRF = r(1+r)^N / ((1+r)^N - 1)$

### KTU High-Frequency Mistake Avoiders
- ✅ Always include **all** efficiency factors in $\eta_{sys}$.
- ✅ Always **round up** the number of modules and batteries.
- ✅ Use **PSH for the specific site** (do not assume 5.0 everywhere).
- ✅ Use $DoD = 0.5$ for lead-acid; $0.8$ for Li-ion.
- ✅ **PV pump** = battery-less; **PV home** = with battery.
- ✅ **Grid-tie inverter** has built-in MPPT + anti-island; **off-grid inverter** has neither.
- ✅ In LCC problems, **subtract** salvage value, do not add.
- ✅ Discount rate $r$ is positive (e.g., 0.10 for 10 %); higher $r$ → lower LCC (in present value).

### One-Line Last-Minute Memory Hooks
- *"Stand-alone needs a battery, like a remote village needs a tank of water."*
- *"Grid-connected needs no battery, like a salaried person needs no savings account."*
- *"Ultra-capacitor = Power, Battery = Energy — never confuse the two."*
- *"LCOE is the price per unit of sunshine converted to electricity — compare it with your electricity bill to know if PV is cheaper."*
- *"DoD and SoC are mirror twins — add them, you always get 1.0."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Growth Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEET721 - DESIGN OF SOLAR PV SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Growth Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition
In the context of **Design of Solar PV Systems**, the term **"Growth Models"** refers to the theoretical and physical frameworks that describe the **nucleation, coalescence, and microstructural evolution of thin films** deposited on substrates during the fabrication of photovoltaic absorber layers (e.g., CIGS, CdTe, amorphous-Si, perovskite, GaAs). These models predict the resulting **grain size, surface morphology, crystallographic orientation, defect density, and interfacial quality** of the solar absorber — properties that directly determine the cell's photovoltaic conversion efficiency.

The three classical thermodynamic growth modes, originally proposed by **Ernst Bauer (1958)**, are:
1. **Volmer–Weber (VW) growth** – 3D island growth
2. **Frank–van der Merwe (FM) growth** – 2D layer-by-layer growth
3. **Stranski–Krastanov (SK) growth** – layer-plus-island hybrid growth

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Growth models link the **deposition parameters** (substrate temperature, deposition rate, flux ratio, background pressure) to the **final device performance** (J<sub>sc</sub>, V<sub>oc</sub>, FF, η). This is a **frequently tested** concept in ESE questions.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine you are **sprinkling water droplets** on three different surfaces:

- **A Teflon-coated pan (non-wetting):** Droplets bead up into round balls that never spread. This is **Volmer–Weber** — the film "dislikes" the substrate and forms isolated 3D islands.
- **A clean glass slide (perfectly wetting):** Water spreads into a uniform, atomically thin sheet before the next layer forms. This is **Frank–van der Merwe** — the film "loves" the substrate.
- **A freshly painted wall:** The first layer of paint spreads nicely, but the second layer beads up. This is **Stranski–Krastanov** — a wetting monolayer forms first, then 3D islands take over.

> [!NOTE]
> The "wetting behaviour" is governed entirely by the **balance of surface energies** between the substrate, the film, and the vacuum/ambient. This is quantified by the **spreading coefficient** and **Young's contact angle**.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics
- Boltzmann constant: **k<sub>B</sub> = 1.380649 × 10⁻²³ J/K**
- Avogadro's number: **N<sub>A</sub> = 6.022 × 10²³ /mol**
- Standard thermal energy at 300 K: **k<sub>B</sub>T ≈ 0.02585 eV**
- Typical substrate temperatures for CIGS: **T<sub>sub</sub> = 400–600 °C**
- Typical deposition rates: **0.1–10 nm/s**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Bauer Growth Modes — Operational Logic

### 2.1.1 Volmer–Weber (3D Island Growth)
- Adatom–adatom bonds are **stronger** than adatom–substrate bonds.
- Atoms prefer to **bind to each other** rather than to the substrate.
- **Critical wetting condition:** Sum of substrate surface energy (γ<sub>s</sub>) and film–substrate interfacial energy (γ<sub>fs</sub>) is **less than** the film surface energy (γ<sub>f</sub>).
- **Result:** Isolated 3D nuclei form directly on the substrate; film grows by island coalescence.
- **PV application:** Often seen in **polycrystalline CdTe** and some CIGS depositions on Mo-coated glass.

### 2.1.2 Frank–van der Merwe (2D Layer-by-Layer Growth)
- Adatom–substrate bonds are **stronger** than adatom–adatom bonds.
- Atoms **wet the substrate completely**; each layer completes before the next begins.
- **Critical wetting condition:** γ<sub>s</sub> > γ<sub>f</sub> + γ<sub>fs</sub>.
- **Result:** Smooth, epitaxial, or highly oriented films; ideal for **heteroepitaxial III–V solar cells** (GaAs/Ge).
- **PV application:** MBE-grown GaAs/Si, AlGaAs/GaAs tandem cells.

### 2.1.3 Stranski–Krastanov (Layer + Island Growth)
- **First one or two monolayers** grow in 2D (wetting layer) because lattice mismatch strain is still tolerable.
- Beyond a **critical thickness**, accumulated **misfit strain energy** makes 2D growth unfavourable; 3D islands form to relieve elastic strain.
- **PV application:** **CIGS on Mo**, perovskite on TiO₂, quantum dot solar cells.

## 2.2 Why the Modes Matter for Solar PV
| Property Affected | Impact on PV Performance |
|---|---|
| **Grain size** | Larger grains → fewer grain-boundary recombination centres → higher V<sub>oc</sub> and J<sub>sc</sub> |
| **Surface roughness** | Excessive roughness (Volmer–Weber) → shunting paths, poor junction formation |
| **Strain / defects** | Misfit dislocations (SK mode) → deep-level traps, reduced carrier lifetime |
| **Interfacial quality** | Wetting layer (FM) → abrupt, low-defect heterojunction |
| **Composition uniformity** | VW island growth can cause **phase segregation** in multi-element absorbers (CIGS, CZTS) |

## 2.3 Nucleation Theory — The Thermodynamic Foundation

Film growth begins with the formation of **stable nuclei** from a supersaturated vapour phase. The **Classical Nucleation Theory (CNT)** describes the energetics of forming a small cluster of *n* atoms.

The total Gibbs free energy change for forming a spherical cap-shaped nucleus of radius *r* on a substrate is:

$$\Delta G(r) = -\frac{4}{3}\pi r^{3} \Delta G_{v} + 4\pi r^{2} \gamma_{fv} + \pi r^{2} \sin^{2}\theta \left(\gamma_{sf} - \gamma_{sv}\right)$$

where:
- **ΔG<sub>v</sub>** = bulk free energy per unit volume (negative during condensation)
- **γ<sub>fv</sub>** = film–vapour surface energy
- **γ<sub>sf</sub>** = substrate–film interfacial energy
- **γ<sub>sv</sub>** = substrate–vapour surface energy
- **θ** = contact angle (Young's equation)

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Relation | Symbol Meaning | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Young's equation:** $\gamma_{sv} = \gamma_{sf} + \gamma_{fv} \cos\theta$ | Surface energies, contact angle θ | Determines which growth mode will occur |
| 2 | **Spreading coefficient:** $S = \gamma_{sv} - \gamma_{sf} - \gamma_{fv}$ | Net "wetting drive" | $S \geq 0$ → FM ; $S < 0$ → VW or SK |
| 3 | **Contact angle criterion:** $\cos\theta = (S) / (2\gamma_{fv})$ | Equilibrium wetting angle | Predicts morphology |
| 4 | **Critical nucleus radius:** $r^{*} = \dfrac{-2\gamma_{fv}}{\Delta G_{v}}$ | Size of smallest stable cluster | Determines nucleus density |
| 5 | **Critical free-energy barrier:** $\Delta G^{*} = \dfrac{16 \pi \gamma_{fv}^{3}}{3 (\Delta G_{v})^{2}}$ | Activation energy for nucleation | Controls nucleation rate |
| 6 | **Nucleation rate:** $J = J_{0} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{\Delta G^{*}}{k_{B}T}\right) \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_{d}}{k_{B}T}\right)$ | Nuclei per unit area per second | Predicts grain density |
| 7 | **Adatom diffusion coefficient:** $D = D_{0} \exp(-E_{d}/k_{B}T)$ | Surface diffusivity | Higher T → larger grains |
| 8 | **Supersaturation:** $S = \dfrac{P}{P_{eq}(T)}$ | Vapour pressure ratio | Drives condensation |
| 9 | **Structure Zone Model (Thornton):** $T^{*}/T_{m}$ | Homologous temperature | Predicts columnar / equiaxed / recrystallized zones |
| 10 | **Lattice mismatch strain:** $f = \dfrac{\vert a_{s} - a_{f} \vert}{a_{f}}$ | Misfit strain | Triggers SK mode beyond critical thickness |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** The signs and physical meaning of the **spreading coefficient S** and **contact angle θ** are **THE most repeated** short-answer question in KTU ESE for this module. Memorize the three wetting conditions.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **CIGS production lines** (Solar Frontier, MiaSolé): Engineers tune Se flux and substrate temperature to switch from SK to FM-like growth, achieving >22% efficient cells.
- **Perovskite solar cells**: Anti-solvent and hot-casting techniques modify nucleation rate **J** to grow millimetre-scale single crystals.
- **III–V multi-junction space cells**: Strict FM growth in MBE/MOCVD reactors ensures lattice-matched GaInP/GaAs/Ge stacks with >32% efficiency.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Critical Nucleus Radius (r*) and Energy Barrier (ΔG*)

### Step 1 — Set up the free energy expression
We consider a spherical-cap nucleus of radius **r** with contact angle **θ** on a flat substrate. The total free-energy change relative to the supersaturated vapour has three contributions:

1. **Volume term (negative, favourable):** the cluster replaces supersaturated vapour with condensed phase.
2. **Film–vapour surface term (positive, unfavourable):** creates new surface area on top of the cap.
3. **Substrate–film interfacial term (positive or negative):** replaces the substrate–vapour interface.

$$
\Delta G(r) = -\frac{4}{3}\pi r^{3} \Delta G_{v} + 4\pi r^{2} \gamma_{fv} + \pi r^{2} \sin^{2}\theta \left(\gamma_{sf} - \gamma_{sv}\right)
$$

### Step 2 — Apply Young's equation
Young's equation (derived from horizontal force balance at the three-phase contact line) gives:

$$
\gamma_{sv} = \gamma_{sf} + \gamma_{fv}\cos\theta
$$

Solving for the substrate-related term:

$$
\gamma_{sf} - \gamma_{sv} = -\gamma_{fv}\cos\theta
$$

### Step 3 — Substitute back
Insert the Young's-equation result into the substrate term:

$$
\Delta G(r) = -\frac{4}{3}\pi r^{3} \Delta G_{v} + 4\pi r^{2} \gamma_{fv} - \pi r^{2} \sin^{2}\theta \, \gamma_{fv}\cos\theta
$$

### Step 4 — Use the geometric identity for a spherical cap
The surface area of a spherical cap projected onto the substrate is $\pi r^{2} \sin^{2}\theta$. The volume is $\tfrac{2}{3}\pi r^{3}(2 - 3\cos\theta + \cos^{3}\theta)$. After substituting the identity $2 - 3\cos\theta + \cos^{3}\theta = (2 + \cos\theta)(1 - \cos\theta)^{2}$, the expression simplifies to:

$$
\Delta G(r) = \left[-\frac{4}{3}\pi r^{3} \Delta G_{v} + 4\pi r^{2} \gamma_{fv}\right] \cdot f(\theta)
$$

where the **shape factor** is $f(\theta) = \dfrac{(2 + \cos\theta)(1 - \cos\theta)^{2}}{4}$.

### Step 5 — Find the critical radius by differentiation
The critical nucleus is the size at which $\dfrac{d(\Delta G)}{dr} = 0$ (a saddle point — a maximum of $\Delta G$ before the cluster becomes stable and grows).

$$
\frac{d(\Delta G)}{dr} = \left[-4\pi r^{2} \Delta G_{v} + 8\pi r \gamma_{fv}\right] \cdot f(\theta) = 0
$$

Solve for **r**:

$$
-4\pi r^{2} \Delta G_{v} + 8\pi r \gamma_{fv} = 0
$$

$$
4\pi r \gamma_{fv} = 4\pi r^{2} \Delta G_{v}
$$

$$
r^{*} = \frac{2 \gamma_{fv}}{-\Delta G_{v}} = \frac{-2\gamma_{fv}}{\Delta G_{v}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Note: $\Delta G_{v} < 0$ during condensation, so $r^{*} > 0$ — physically meaningful.

### Step 6 — Substitute r* back to obtain ΔG*
Insert $r = r^{*}$ into the simplified $\Delta G$ expression:

$$
\Delta G^{*} = \left[-\frac{4}{3}\pi (r^{*})^{3} \Delta G_{v} + 4\pi (r^{*})^{2} \gamma_{fv}\right] f(\theta)
$$

Substitute $r^{*} = -2\gamma_{fv}/\Delta G_{v}$:

$$
\Delta G^{*} = \left[\frac{32\pi \gamma_{fv}^{3}}{3 (\Delta G_{v})^{2}} + \frac{16\pi \gamma_{fv}^{3}}{(\Delta G_{v})^{2}}\right] f(\theta)
$$

Simplify:

$$
\Delta G^{*} = \frac{16\pi \gamma_{fv}^{3}}{3(\Delta G_{v})^{2}} \cdot f(\theta)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> For a **homogeneous** nucleus in free space (no substrate), $\theta \to 0$ and $f(\theta) \to 1$, recovering the textbook expression: $\Delta G^{*}_{homo} = \dfrac{16\pi \gamma_{fv}^{3}}{3(\Delta G_{v})^{2}}$. For a **fully wetting** film ($\theta = 0$, $f(\theta) = 0$), the barrier vanishes and growth proceeds spontaneously (Frank–van der Merwe).

## 3.2 Derivation: The Three Growth Modes from Surface Energy Balance

Starting from Young's equation:

$$
\gamma_{sv} = \gamma_{sf} + \gamma_{fv}\cos\theta
$$
