### Step 1 — Rearrange
$$
\gamma_{sv} - \gamma_{sf} = \gamma_{fv}\cos\theta
$$

### Step 2 — Define the spreading coefficient
$$
S = \gamma_{sv} - \gamma_{sf} - \gamma_{fv}
$$

### Step 3 — Connect S and θ
From Young's equation, $\cos\theta = (\gamma_{sv} - \gamma_{sf})/\gamma_{fv} = (S + \gamma_{fv})/\gamma_{fv} = 1 + S/\gamma_{fv}$, hence:

$$
\cos\theta = 1 + \frac{S}{\gamma_{fv}}
$$

### Step 4 — Classify the modes
- **If $S \geq 0$:** $\cos\theta \geq 1$ ⇒ $\theta = 0$ ⇒ **complete wetting** ⇒ **Frank–van der Merwe** (2D layer-by-layer).
- **If $S < 0$ and $\theta > 0$:** **partial wetting** ⇒ **Volmer–Weber** (3D islands).
- **If $S < 0$ initially but strain energy dominates after a few monolayers:** **Stranski–Krastanov**.

## 3.3 Derivation: Adatom Mean-Free-Path and Critical Nucleation Density

The **nucleation density N** on the substrate depends on the **impingement flux F** (atoms per unit area per second) and the adatom surface lifetime **τ**:

$$
N \propto F \cdot \tau \quad \text{where} \quad \tau = \frac{1}{\nu} \exp\!\left(\frac{E_{d}}{k_{B}T}\right)
$$

with **ν** = adatom vibration frequency (~10¹²–10¹³ Hz) and **E<sub>d</sub>** = activation energy for surface diffusion.

The **mean adatom diffusion length** before re-evaporation is:

$$
\lambda = \sqrt{D \tau} = \sqrt{D_{0} \tau} \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_{d}}{2k_{B}T}\right)
$$

**Engineering implication:** A higher substrate temperature **T** gives a longer diffusion length, fewer but larger grains — exactly the trend observed in the **Thornton Structure Zone Model** (Zones 1, T, 2, 3 as T<sub>sub</sub>/T<sub>melt</sub> increases).

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — The Three Growth Modes

| Parameter | Volmer–Weber (3D) | Frank–van der Merwe (2D) | Stranski–Krastanov (Mixed) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Wetting** | Non-wetting ($\theta > 0$) | Complete wetting ($\theta = 0$) | Wetting layer, then dewetting |
| **Spreading coefficient S** | $S < 0$ | $S \geq 0$ | Initially $\geq 0$, later $< 0$ |
| **Driving force for switch** | Surface energy unbalance | Surface energy balance | Misfit strain energy accumulation |
| **Morphology** | Rough, granular, columnar | Smooth, layered, epitaxial | Wetting layer + 3D islands |
| **Critical parameter** | $\theta$, $\gamma_{fv}/\gamma_{sf}$ | Lattice match, $\gamma_{sv}$ | Critical thickness, misfit $f$ |
| **Typical PV use** | CdTe, polycrystalline Si | GaAs/Ge, AlGaAs/GaAs | CIGS/Mo, perovskite/TiO₂ |
| **Recombination** | High (grain boundaries) | Low (defect-free) | Moderate (interface states) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Classification of Thin-Film Growth Modes

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Atoms from vapour phase arrive at substrate] --> B{Surface energy balance}
    B -- Strong film film and weak film substrate --> C[VOLMER WEBER 3D ISLAND GROWTH]
    B -- Strong film substrate and weak film film --> D[FRANK VAN DER MERWE 2D LAYER GROWTH]
    B -- Wetting first layer then strain dominates --> E[STRANSKI KRASTANOV HYBRID GROWTH]
    C --> F[Polycrystalline absorber high grain boundary density]
    D --> G[Epitaxial film low defect density high efficiency]
    E --> H[Heteroepitaxial film wetting layer plus 3D islands]
    F --> I[Solar cell output low to moderate]
    G --> J[Solar cell output high to very high]
    H --> K[Solar cell output moderate to high]
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Nucleation and Growth Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Stage 1: Substrate preparation and cleaning] --> P2[Stage 2: Adatom adsorption]
    P2 --> P3[Stage 3: Surface diffusion and re evaporation]
    P3 --> P4{Cluster size compared to r star}
    P4 -- Smaller than r star --> P5[Sub critical cluster dissolves]
    P4 -- Equal to r star --> P6[Critical nucleus stable]
    P6 --> P7[Stage 4: Island nucleation]
    P7 --> P8[Stage 5: Island coalescence]
    P8 --> P9[Stage 6: Continuous film formation]
    P9 --> P10[Stage 7: Bulk film growth and grain coarsening]
    P10 --> P11[Final solar absorber layer for PV cell]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Effect of Substrate Temperature on Film Morphology (Thornton Zone Model)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Z1[Zone 1: T sub T melt less than 0.3] --> Z2[Porous tapered crystallites with domed tops low adatom mobility]
    Z3[Zone T: 0.3 to 0.5] --> Z4[Dense columnar grains with smooth surface]
    Z5[Zone 2: 0.5 to 0.75] --> Z6[Columnar grains with faceted surfaces]
    Z7[Zone 3: above 0.75] --> Z8[Equiaxed recrystallized grains bulk diffusion dominates]
    Z2 --> Z3
    Z4 --> Z5
    Z6 --> Z7
```

## 4.4 Energy Landscape — ΔG vs. Cluster Size

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Small cluster size n less than n star] --> S2[Delta G increases with n metastable region]
    S2 --> S3[Critical nucleus n equals n star Delta G equals Delta G star peak of barrier]
    S3 --> S4[Cluster larger than n star Delta G decreases spontaneously stable growth]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The peak at $n = n^{*}$ is the **activation barrier** $\Delta G^{*}$. The steeper the bulk free-energy driving force $|\Delta G_{v}|$, the smaller $n^{*}$ — meaning nucleation is easier and denser. The flatter the curve, the rarer and larger the nuclei.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define the three classical thin-film growth modes proposed by Bauer and state the surface-energy condition that distinguishes each.

**Model Answer:**
The three Bauer growth modes are:
1. **Volmer–Weber (3D island):** Adatoms bind more strongly to each other than to the substrate; **S = γ<sub>sv</sub> − γ<sub>sf</sub> − γ<sub>fv</sub> < 0** and contact angle θ > 0. Nuclei grow as 3D islands.
2. **Frank–van der Merwe (2D layer):** Adatoms wet the substrate completely; **S ≥ 0** and θ = 0. Growth proceeds layer-by-layer.
3. **Stranski–Krastanov (hybrid):** A 2D wetting layer forms first; after a critical thickness, strain energy forces a switch to 3D island growth. Typical of lattice-mismatched systems like CIGS/Mo.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** With the help of Young's equation, derive the spreading-coefficient criterion that predicts complete wetting (2D growth) versus partial wetting (3D growth).

**Model Answer:**
Young's equation (horizontal force balance at the three-phase line):

$$\gamma_{sv} = \gamma_{sf} + \gamma_{fv}\cos\theta$$

Define the **spreading coefficient:**

$$S = \gamma_{sv} - \gamma_{sf} - \gamma_{fv}$$

From Young's equation, $\cos\theta = 1 + S/\gamma_{fv}$. Since $\vert\cos\theta\vert \leq 1$ in physical reality:
- **If S ≥ 0:** $\cos\theta \geq 1$ ⇒ θ = 0, **complete wetting** → **Frank–van der Merwe**.
- **If S < 0:** θ > 0, **partial wetting** → **Volmer–Weber** (or Stranski–Krastanov after strain build-up).

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** Starting from the Gibbs free energy of formation of a spherical-cap nucleus, derive the expressions for the **critical radius r\*** and the **critical free-energy barrier ΔG\***. State the assumptions clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Stranski–Krastanov growth mode** with reference to (i) lattice misfit strain, (ii) critical layer thickness, and (iii) a relevant solar-cell material system. Discuss how this mode affects the photovoltaic efficiency. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

We consider a spherical-cap nucleus of radius **r** and contact angle **θ** forming on a flat substrate from a supersaturated vapour. The total free-energy change has three terms:

$$
\Delta G(r) = -\frac{4}{3}\pi r^{3} \Delta G_{v} + 4\pi r^{2} \gamma_{fv} + \pi r^{2} \sin^{2}\theta \left(\gamma_{sf} - \gamma_{sv}\right)
$$

**Assumptions:**
- The nucleus is in the shape of a spherical cap (isotropic surface energy).
- Bulk thermodynamic properties apply even to small clusters (capillarity approximation).
- The substrate is rigid, smooth, and chemically uniform.

**Applying Young's equation:** $\gamma_{sv} = \gamma_{sf} + \gamma_{fv}\cos\theta$ gives $\gamma_{sf} - \gamma_{sv} = -\gamma_{fv}\cos\theta$. Substituting and using the spherical-cap geometry leads to:

$$
\Delta G(r) = \left[-\frac{4}{3}\pi r^{3} \Delta G_{v} + 4\pi r^{2} \gamma_{fv}\right] f(\theta), \quad f(\theta) = \frac{(2+\cos\theta)(1-\cos\theta)^{2}}{4}
$$

**Differentiate with respect to r and set to zero:**

$$
\frac{d(\Delta G)}{dr} = \left[-4\pi r^{2} \Delta G_{v} + 8\pi r \gamma_{fv}\right] f(\theta) = 0
$$

Solving:

$$
\boxed{\, r^{*} = \frac{-2\gamma_{fv}}{\Delta G_{v}} \,}
$$

**Substitute r* back:**

$$
\boxed{\, \Delta G^{*} = \frac{16\pi \gamma_{fv}^{3}}{3(\Delta G_{v})^{2}} \cdot f(\theta) \,}
$$

For homogeneous nucleation ($f(\theta) = 1$), the well-known result is recovered. For complete wetting ($\theta \to 0$, $f(\theta) \to 0$), the barrier vanishes — consistent with Frank–van der Merwe growth.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**(i) Lattice misfit strain:** When a film of lattice constant $a_{f}$ is grown epitaxially on a substrate of lattice constant $a_{s}$, the misfit is $f = (a_{s} - a_{f})/a_{f}$. The associated strain energy per unit area scales as $E_{strain} \propto f^{2} h$ for a film of thickness *h*.

**(ii) Critical layer thickness:** The total free energy per unit area is $\gamma_{f} + \gamma_{sf} - \gamma_{s} + E_{strain}(h)$. Initially this is negative (2D wetting layer is favourable). Beyond a critical thickness $h_{c}$, the strain term dominates, making further 2D growth energetically costly. The system lowers its energy by forming 3D islands (which can relax elastically through their free sidewalls).

**(iii) Solar-cell example:** The prototypical SK system in PV is **Cu(In,Ga)Se₂ (CIGS) grown on Mo-coated soda-lime glass**. The first 1–2 monolayers wet the Mo substrate, but the lattice mismatch and Cu–Se / In–Ga inter-diffusion favour 3D grain growth above ~50 nm, producing the columnar grains desirable for efficient carrier collection.

**Effect on PV efficiency:**
- **Positive:** The 3D grains reduce grain-boundary density in the absorber, lowering recombination and increasing J<sub>sc</sub>.
- **Negative:** Strain-induced dislocations and compositional inhomogeneities in the islands create deep-level traps that reduce V<sub>oc</sub> and fill factor.
- **Net:** The strain must be optimized via Ga/(Ga+In) grading — a key engineering knob in record-efficiency CIGS cells (22.6%, Solar Frontier).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Derive **Young's equation** from interfacial force balance and explain how the contact angle θ is used to predict the growth mode. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **Thornton Structure Zone Model (SZM)** and explain how substrate temperature (homologous temperature T\*/T<sub>m</sub>) influences the resulting film morphology, grain size, and PV device performance. Give one example for each zone. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

At the three-phase contact line where film (f), substrate (s), and vapour (v) meet, the three interfacial tensions act as forces per unit length. Resolving tangentially (along the substrate plane) gives:

$$
\gamma_{sv} = \gamma_{sf} + \gamma_{fv}\cos\theta
$$

This is **Young's equation**, where:
- $\gamma_{sv}$ — substrate–vapour surface tension (pulls the contact line toward the bare substrate)
- $\gamma_{sf}$ — substrate–film interfacial tension (pulls the line toward the film-covered region)
- $\gamma_{fv}$ — film–vapour surface tension (acts along the cap surface)

The contact angle θ is then used together with the spreading coefficient to predict the growth mode:
- $\theta = 0$ → $S \geq 0$ → **FM growth** (perfect wetting)
- $0 < \theta < \pi$ → $S < 0$ → **VW growth** (islands)
- After initial 2D layer → **SK growth** (islands after wetting layer)

In Young's equation, if $\gamma_{sv} < \gamma_{sf}$, then $\cos\theta < 0$ and $\theta > \pi/2$ (strongly non-wetting), which is the thermodynamic signature of strong Volmer–Weber behaviour. If $\gamma_{sv} > \gamma_{sf} + \gamma_{fv}$, the inequality is satisfied only by $\theta = 0$, signalling spontaneous layer-by-layer spreading.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

The **Thornton Structure Zone Model (1974)** classifies sputter-deposited thin films into four morphological zones based on the **homologous temperature** $T_{h} = T_{sub}/T_{melt}$ (in Kelvin) and the **argon working pressure**.

| Zone | $T_{h}$ range | Morphology | Example in PV |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Zone 1** | $T_{h} < 0.3$ | Porous, tapered, domed-top crystallites. Adatom mobility is low; shadowing dominates. | Amorphous or nanocrystalline Si:H at low T |
| **Zone T** | $0.3 \leq T_{h} < 0.5$ | Dense, smooth, fibrous grains with limited surface diffusion. | CdTe deposited at moderate T |
| **Zone 2** | $0.5 \leq T_{h} < 0.75$ | Columnar grains with faceted surfaces; surface diffusion dominates. | High-quality CIGS at 550 °C |
| **Zone 3** | $T_{h} \geq 0.75$ | Equiaxed, recrystallized grains; bulk diffusion and grain-boundary migration. | Poly-Si absorbers in thin-film Si tandems |

**Effect on PV performance:**
- **Lower T<sub>h</sub> (Zone 1):** High defect density, dangling bonds, low carrier lifetime → poor V<sub>oc</sub> and J<sub>sc</sub>.
- **Moderate T<sub>h</sub> (Zone T/2):** Optimal columnar morphology with vertical grain boundaries that act as benign current conduits → highest η.
- **High T<sub>h</sub> (Zone 3):** Grain coarsening and possible phase segregation → mixed device behaviour; useful in some CIGS and CZTS processing.

The SZM demonstrates that **substrate temperature is the single most powerful process parameter** controlling thin-film PV absorber morphology and hence the final conversion efficiency.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in KTU ESE:**
> 1. **Sign error in the spreading coefficient:** Many students write $S = \gamma_{sf} - \gamma_{sv} - \gamma_{fv}$. The correct KTU-board-accepted form is $S = \gamma_{sv} - \gamma_{sf} - \gamma_{fv}$. Wrong sign = full 3-mark loss in Part A.
> 2. **Confusing ΔG<sub>v</sub> with ΔG\*:** ΔG<sub>v</sub> is the **volume** free energy (J/m³, negative for condensation), while ΔG\* is the **critical barrier** (J, positive). Examiners will deduct if these are mixed up.
> 3. **Skipping the shape factor $f(\theta)$:** When asked for the heterogeneous nucleation barrier, students often quote the homogeneous result ($f(\theta) = 1$) without the contact-angle term. Always write $f(\theta) = (2 + \cos\theta)(1 - \cos\theta)^{2}/4$ explicitly.
> 4. **Mixing up the zones of the Thornton model:** Zone 1 is **not** at high temperature — it is the **cold** end. Remember: Zone 1 (porous) → Zone T (transition) → Zone 2 (columnar) → Zone 3 (recrystallized) as T<sub>h</sub> increases.
> 5. **Forgetting the Stranski–Krastanov trigger:** A common oversight is that SK is triggered by **strain energy accumulation** beyond a critical thickness — not by a simple surface-energy argument. Mention "lattice misfit" explicitly.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Bauer's three growth modes** — Volmer–Weber (3D island), Frank–van der Merwe (2D layer), Stranski–Krastanov (mixed) — are the cornerstone of thin-film PV absorber engineering.
- **Young's equation** $\gamma_{sv} = \gamma_{sf} + \gamma_{fv}\cos\theta$ and the **spreading coefficient** $S = \gamma_{sv} - \gamma_{sf} - \gamma_{fv}$ together determine the wetting behaviour.
- **S ≥ 0 → FM** (2D layer growth); **S < 0 → VW or SK** (3D or mixed).
- **Critical nucleus radius** $r^{*} = -2\gamma_{fv}/\Delta G_{v}$; **critical barrier** $\Delta G^{*} = \dfrac{16\pi \gamma_{fv}^{3}}{3(\Delta G_{v})^{2}} f(\theta)$.
- **Nucleation rate** $J = J_{0} \exp(-\Delta G^{*}/k_{B}T) \exp(-E_{d}/k_{B}T)$ — increases with supersaturation and temperature.
- **Higher substrate temperature → longer adatom diffusion length → fewer, larger grains → higher V<sub>oc</sub>** (Thornton SZM, Zones 1 → T → 2 → 3).
- **SK growth** is triggered by misfit strain beyond a critical thickness; common in **CIGS/Mo** and **perovskite/TiO₂**.
- **VW growth** dominates when the film "dislikes" the substrate (e.g., Au on NaCl, CdTe on bare glass).
- **FM growth** is ideal for **epitaxial III–V tandem solar cells** (GaAs/Ge).
- **Practical knobs:** substrate temperature, deposition rate, working-gas pressure, flux ratio (e.g., Se/metal ratio in CIGS), and substrate surface preparation all shift the system from one growth mode to another.
- **Always quote SI units** in derivations: γ in J/m², ΔG in J, r in m, ΔG<sub>v</sub> in J/m³, k<sub>B</sub>T in J (or eV with conversion 1 eV = 1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ J).
- **Memorize the four-zone SZM table** — it is one of the highest-yield 7-mark questions in the module.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Annual Payment and Present Worth Factor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEET721 - DESIGN OF SOLAR PV SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Annual Payment and Present Worth Factor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Present Worth Factor (PWF)** is an economic time-value-of-money multiplier used in engineering economy to convert a stream of future cash flows (uniform annual payments, single future payments, or perpetuities) into an equivalent single lump-sum **Present Worth (PW)** value evaluated at year zero (the present). The factor depends on two key parameters: the **discount rate (i)** expressed as a decimal per year, and the **number of compounding periods (n)** in years.

**Annual Payment Factor (APF)**, more rigorously known in engineering economy as the **Capital Recovery Factor (CRF)**, is the inverse operation of the PWF. It converts a present value (initial investment) into an equivalent uniform series of **annual end-of-year payments (A)** spread over **n** periods at discount rate **i**. CRF includes both the return of principal and the periodic interest on the outstanding balance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:** In the design of Solar PV systems, these two factors are the mathematical backbone of **Levelized Cost of Energy (LCOE)**, **Net Present Value (NPV)**, and **Life-Cycle Cost (LCC)** analysis. Examiners almost always test the relationship:
> $$\text{CRF} = \text{PWF}_{\text{uniform}} \times \text{SPWF}$$

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you buy a 1 kW rooftop solar PV system for **₹50,000** today. The system will produce electricity worth approximately **₹8,000** every year for 25 years. The question is: *Is this deal profitable?* The trouble is, **₹8,000 in 2035 is not the same as ₹8,000 in 2025** — inflation, the opportunity cost of money, and the bank's interest rate mean a rupee in the future is worth less than a rupee today.

- The **Present Worth Factor** is like a *time machine* that pulls all 25 years of future savings back into 2025 rupees, so you can add them up and compare with the ₹50,000 investment.
- The **Annual Payment (Capital Recovery) Factor** is the *reverse* — it spreads the ₹50,000 lump-sum cost into 25 equal annual instalments (very similar to a home-loan **EMI**), so you can directly compare it against the ₹8,000 yearly savings.

If the annual saving (₹8,000) is greater than the recovered annual cost (EMI), the project is profitable. This is exactly how KTU examiners frame solar PV economic problems.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Parameters for KTU Numerical Problems:**
> - Domestic rooftop solar discount rate (bank lending rate): **i = 10% to 12% per annum**
> - Solar PV module useful life: **n = 25 years**
> - Inverter replacement (mid-life): typically at **n = 12 to 13 years**
> - O&M escalation rate: **g = 5% to 6% per annum**

### 1.3 The Two Standard Factor Families

| Factor Family | Symbol | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Single Payment Present Worth Factor | **SPWF** | $F \rightarrow P$ for a single future sum |
| Uniform Series Present Worth Factor | **USPWF** | $A \rightarrow P$ for a stream of equal annual payments |
| Capital Recovery Factor | **CRF** | $P \rightarrow A$ (inverse of USPWF) |
| Sinking Fund Factor | **SFF** | $F \rightarrow A$ (the interest portion) |
| Single Payment Future Worth Factor | **F/P, (F/P, i, n)** | $P \rightarrow F$ |
| Uniform Series Future Worth Factor | **F/A** | $A \rightarrow F$ |

Where **P = Present value, F = Future value, A = Uniform annual payment, i = interest/discount rate, n = number of years**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic of Each Factor

**Step 1 — Single Payment Present Worth Factor (SPWF / Single Discount Factor)**
- A single future payment $F$ occurring at the end of year $n$ is discounted back to year 0 by the compound interest identity.
- The logic: ₹1 received after 1 year is worth $\frac{1}{(1+i)}$ today; after 2 years it is $\frac{1}{(1+i)^2}$, and so on.
- Multiplying $F$ by this factor collapses the time gap.

**Step 2 — Uniform Series Present Worth Factor (USPWF / Present Worth Annuity Factor)**
- A stream of equal payments $A$ at the end of years 1, 2, …, n is the geometric sum of n discounted values.
- The factor converts this stream into a single present lump-sum $P$.

**Step 3 — Capital Recovery Factor (CRF / Annual Payment Factor)**
- The mathematical reciprocal of USPWF.
- It answers: *"What equal annual payment A over n years at rate i is exactly equivalent to spending P today?"*
- In solar PV economics, CRF is used to convert the **total life-cycle cost** (panel cost + inverter + BOS + installation + present worth of O&M) into an **annualized cost**, which is then divided by **annual energy yield (kWh/yr)** to obtain **LCOE (₹/kWh)**.

**Step 4 — Sinking Fund Factor (SFF)**
- Computes the *interest-only savings* portion of CRF.
- CRF = SFF + i (this identity is a **favourite KTU trick question**).
- Used when one needs to find how much to deposit annually to accumulate a known future replacement cost (e.g., inverter replacement after 12 years).

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Factor Name | Symbol | Algebraic Formula | Engineering Use in Solar PV |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Single Payment Present Worth Factor | $(P/F, i, n)$ | $\dfrac{1}{(1+i)^n}$ | Discounting inverter replacement cost at year 12 back to year 0 |
| 2 | Single Payment Future Worth Factor | $(F/P, i, n)$ | $(1+i)^n$ | Escalating a present scrap/salvage value to end-of-life year |
| 3 | Uniform Series Present Worth Factor | $(P/A, i, n)$ | $\dfrac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i(1+i)^n}$ | Converting 25 years of annual electricity savings to PW |
| 4 | Capital Recovery Factor (Annual Payment) | $(A/P, i, n)$ | $\dfrac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1}$ | Annualized cost of initial investment + financed O&M |
| 5 | Sinking Fund Factor | $(A/F, i, n)$ | $\dfrac{i}{(1+i)^n - 1}$ | Annual reserve for inverter replacement |
| 6 | Uniform Series Future Worth Factor | $(A/F, i, n)$ reciprocal: $(F/A, i, n)$ | $\dfrac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i}$ | Future scrap value of annual O&M savings |
| 7 | Arithmetic Gradient Present Worth | $(P/G, i, n)$ | $\dfrac{(1+i)^n - i n - 1}{i^2 (1+i)^n}$ | Tariff escalation with linear annual increase |
| 8 | Geometric Gradient Present Worth | $P_{geo}$ | $A_1 \cdot \dfrac{1 - \left(\frac{1+g}{1+i}\right)^n}{i - g}$ | Realistic electricity tariff hike of 5% per year |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Conditions to Remember:**
> - When $i = 0$: USPWF = n, CRF = 1/n, SPWF = 1 (no time value of money)
> - When $n \rightarrow \infty$ and $i > 0$: USPWF → 1/i, CRF → i (perpetuity case)
> - **Key Identity:** $\text{CRF} = \text{SFF} + i$

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

In a **grid-tied rooftop solar PV design**, the engineer must justify the investment to the homeowner. The economic feasibility report uses:
- **CRF** to compute the *Equivalent Annual Cost (EAC)* of the system.
- **USPWF** to compute the *Present Worth of Lifetime Savings* from electricity bills.
- **LCOE** (which uses CRF in the numerator) to compare solar ₹/kWh with grid ₹/kWh.
- **NPV = PW(Savings) − PW(Costs)** to determine profitability.

For a **utility-scale solar farm** (e.g., 1 MW plant), the same factors are applied to project cash flows, debt servicing, accelerated depreciation benefits, and PPA (Power Purchase Agreement) tariff streams.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Single Payment Present Worth Factor (SPWF)

**Statement:** Given a future sum $F$ at the end of year $n$, find its present equivalent $P$ at discount rate $i$.

By the definition of compound interest, ₹1 invested today grows to $(1+i)$ in 1 year, $(1+i)^2$ in 2 years, and $(1+i)^n$ in $n$ years.

Therefore, the present equivalent of ₹1 received in year $n$ is the reciprocal:

$$
P = F \cdot \frac{1}{(1+i)^n}
$$

Hence, $\text{SPWF} = (P/F, i, n) = \dfrac{1}{(1+i)^n}$.

> **Logic step:** If money grows forward by multiplication, it must shrink backward by division. The factor is simply the *inverse compound-growth multiplier*.

### 3.2 Derivation of Uniform Series Present Worth Factor (USPWF)

**Statement:** A uniform series of payments $A$ is made at the end of years 1 through $n$. Find the present worth $P$.

The present worth of each annual payment is obtained by discounting individually using SPWF:

$$
P = \frac{A}{(1+i)^1} + \frac{A}{(1+i)^2} + \frac{A}{(1+i)^3} + \dots + \frac{A}{(1+i)^n}
$$

Factor out $A$:

$$
P = A \left[ \frac{1}{(1+i)^1} + \frac{1}{(1+i)^2} + \dots + \frac{1}{(1+i)^n} \right]
$$

The bracketed term is a finite geometric series with first term $a = \dfrac{1}{(1+i)}$ and common ratio $r = \dfrac{1}{(1+i)}$. Using the geometric series sum formula $\displaystyle S_n = a \cdot \frac{1 - r^n}{1 - r}$:

$$
P = A \cdot \frac{1}{(1+i)} \cdot \frac{1 - \left(\dfrac{1}{1+i}\right)^n}{1 - \dfrac{1}{1+i}}
$$

Simplify the denominator:

$$
1 - \frac{1}{1+i} = \frac{(1+i) - 1}{1+i} = \frac{i}{1+i}
$$

Substitute back:

$$
P = A \cdot \frac{1}{(1+i)} \cdot \frac{1 - \dfrac{1}{(1+i)^n}}{\dfrac{i}{1+i}}
$$

The $(1+i)$ terms cancel:

$$
P = A \cdot \frac{1 - \dfrac{1}{(1+i)^n}}{i} = A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i(1+i)^n}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\text{USPWF} = (P/A, i, n) = \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i(1+i)^n}}
$$

> **Logic step:** We summed the individual present values of n equal payments. Since each payment is the same, we applied the closed-form geometric series to avoid writing 25 terms.

### 3.3 Derivation of Capital Recovery Factor (CRF / Annual Payment Factor)

**Statement:** Given a present value $P$, find the equivalent uniform annual payment $A$ over $n$ years at rate $i$.

CRF is simply the algebraic inverse of USPWF. Starting from $P = A \cdot \text{USPWF}$:

$$
A = P \cdot \frac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1}
$$

Hence:

$$
\boxed{\text{CRF} = (A/P, i, n) = \frac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1}}
$$

**The KTU Identity — CRF = SFF + i:**

Starting from the CRF expression, split the fraction:

$$
\text{CRF} = \frac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1} = \frac{i[(1+i)^n - 1] + i}{(1+i)^n - 1}
$$

Simplify:

$$
\text{CRF} = \frac{i[(1+i)^n - 1]}{(1+i)^n - 1} + \frac{i}{(1+i)^n - 1} = i + \frac{i}{(1+i)^n - 1}
$$

The second term is precisely the **Sinking Fund Factor (SFF)**. Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\text{CRF} = i + \text{SFF}}
$$

> **Logic step:** The annual payment $A$ on a loan of $P$ covers two things — (1) recovering the principal portion (SFF × P) and (2) paying interest on the outstanding balance (i × P). Their sum is the total annual instalment.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A 2 kW rooftop solar PV system costs **₹1,20,000** to install. It produces electricity savings of **₹15,000 per year** for 25 years. Annual O&M is **₹2,000** (escalating at 5% per year). The bank lending (discount) rate is **i = 10%**. Determine the Net Present Value (NPV) and the LCOE.

**Step A — Capital Recovery Factor at i = 10%, n = 25 years:**

$$
\text{CRF} = \frac{0.10(1.10)^{25}}{(1.10)^{25} - 1}
$$

Compute $(1.10)^{25}$:

$$
(1.10)^{25} = 10.8347
$$

Substitute:

$$
\text{CRF} = \frac{0.10 \times 10.8347}{10.8347 - 1} = \frac{1.08347}{9.8347} = 0.11017
$$

**Step B — Annualized Total Cost (using CRF + escalating O&M via geometric gradient):**

The geometric series present worth for escalating O&M with $g = 5\%$, $i = 10\%$:

$$
P_{O\&M} = A_1 \cdot \frac{1 - \left(\dfrac{1.05}{1.10}\right)^{25}}{0.10 - 0.05}
$$

Compute the ratio:

$$
\frac{1.05}{1.10} = 0.9545
$$

Compute $(0.9545)^{25}$:

$$
(0.9545)^{25} = 0.2953
$$

Substitute:

$$
P_{O\&M} = 2000 \times \frac{1 - 0.2953}{0.05} = 2000 \times \frac{0.7047}{0.05} = 2000 \times 14.094 = 28{,}188 \text{ ₹}
$$

**Step C — Total Present Worth of Cost:**

$$
P_{\text{cost}} = 1{,}20{,}000 + 28{,}188 = 1{,}48{,}188 \text{ ₹}
$$

**Step D — Annualized Cost (using CRF):**

$$
A_{\text{cost}} = P_{\text{cost}} \times \text{CRF} = 1{,}48{,}188 \times 0.11017 = 16{,}322 \text{ ₹/year}
$$

**Step E — Net Annual Cash Flow:**

$$
A_{\text{net}} = 15{,}000 - 16{,}322 = -1{,}322 \text{ ₹/year}
$$

Since annual net is negative, NPV will be negative — this system is **not** financially attractive at these parameters.

**Step F — LCOE Calculation:**

Assume the system produces $2 \text{ kW} \times 4.5 \text{ sun-hours/day} \times 365 \times 0.80 \text{ performance ratio} = 2{,}628 \text{ kWh/year}$.

$$
\text{LCOE} = \frac{A_{\text{cost}}}{\text{Annual kWh}} = \frac{16{,}322}{2{,}628} = 6.21 \text{ ₹/kWh}
$$

If the grid tariff is **₹7/kWh**, the system is marginally competitive despite the negative NPV, because electricity prices will keep rising.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineer's Insight:** A negative NPV today does not always mean a bad investment if electricity tariffs rise faster than the discount rate. Many KTU problems test this *time-shifting* of break-even conditions.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Time-Value-of-Money Conversion Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Lump Sum Present Value P] -->|multiply by CRF = i(1+i)^n / (1+i)^n - 1| B[Uniform Annual Payment A]
    B -->|multiply by USPWF = (1+i)^n - 1 / i(1+i)^n| A
    A -->|multiply by F/P = (1+i)^n| C[Future Value F at year n]
    C -->|multiply by SPWF = 1 / (1+i)^n| A
    B -->|multiply by F/A = (1+i)^n - 1 / i| C
    C -->|multiply by SFF = i / (1+i)^n - 1| B
    D[Arithmetic Gradient G] -->|multiply by P/G factor| A
    E[Geometric Gradient with rate g] -->|special formula| A
    F[Real-World Solar PV Cash Flows] -->|discounted via SPWF/USPWF| A
    F -->|annualized via CRF| B
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Solar PV Economic Analysis Engine

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUTS
        P0[Initial Capital Cost]
        A1[Annual Electricity Savings]
        Aom[Annual O and M Cost]
        INV[Inverter Replacement at yr 12]
        SAL[Salvage Value at yr 25]
        IR[Discount Rate i]
        NY[Project Life n]
    end

    subgraph PROCESSING
        CRF1[CRF Calculator]
        SPWF1[SPWF Calculator]
        USPWF1[USPWF Calculator]
        GEO[Geometric Gradient Block]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUTS
        LCOE[Levelized Cost of Energy]
        NPV[Net Present Value]
        EAC[Equivalent Annual Cost]
        PB[Payback Period]
    end

    P0 --> CRF1
    CRF1 --> EAC
    A1 --> USPWF1
    A1 --> USPWF1
    Aom --> GEO
    GEO --> USPWF1
    INV --> SPWF1
    SAL --> SPWF1
    USPWF1 --> NPV
    P0 --> NPV
    EAC --> LCOE
    NPV --> PB
    IR --> CRF1
    IR --> SPWF1
    IR --> USPWF1
    NY --> CRF1
    NY --> SPWF1
    NY --> USPWF1
```

### 4.3 Decision-Tree for KTU Problem Solving

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[Given a solar PV economics problem] --> Q2{What is the unknown?}
    Q2 -->|Annual equivalent of a lump sum| R1[Use CRF = A/P factor]
    Q2 -->|Present worth of equal annual savings| R2[Use USPWF = P/A factor]
    Q2 -->|Present worth of single future cost e.g. inverter| R3[Use SPWF = P/F factor]
    Q2 -->|Annual reserve for future replacement| R4[Use SFF = A/F factor]
    Q2 -->|Tariff rising every year by g percent| R5[Use Geometric Gradient formula]
    Q2 -->|Comparing solar to grid tariff| R6[Compute LCOE and compare]
    R1 --> OUT[Express final answer in rupees per year]
    R2 --> OUT
    R3 --> OUT
    R4 --> OUT
    R5 --> OUT
    R6 --> OUT
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip:** Notice the symmetry in the flowchart — the arrow from A → P uses USPWF, and the arrow from P → A uses CRF. They are exact reciprocals. Memorizing this *mirror-image* structure is the fastest way to recall formulas in the exam hall.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper Q.4a]**
*Define Capital Recovery Factor (CRF) and state its mathematical relationship with the Sinking Fund Factor (SFF). Why is CRF preferred over simple division by project life for solar PV cost analysis?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Capital Recovery Factor (CRF) is the factor that converts a present value (P) into an equivalent uniform annual payment (A) over n years at discount rate i:
$$(A/P, i, n) = \frac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1}$$
- **Relationship (1 mark):** $\text{CRF} = i + \text{SFF}$, where $\text{SFF} = \dfrac{i}{(1+i)^n - 1}$.
- **Reason (1 mark):** CRF accounts for the time value of money (interest on outstanding capital and systematic return of principal), whereas simple division $P/n$ ignores this. Hence CRF gives the *true* annualized cost of the solar PV system.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4, Q.1f]**
*A solar PV system provides a uniform annual savings of ₹10,000 for 20 years. If the discount rate is 8%, calculate the present worth of these savings using the appropriate factor.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- Identify the factor: Uniform Series Present Worth Factor (USPWF) = (P/A, 8%, 20).
- Compute (1.08)^20 = 4.6610.
- USPWF = (4.6610 − 1) / [0.08 × 4.6610] = 3.6610 / 0.3729 = **9.8181**.
- Present Worth = 10,000 × 9.8181 = **₹98,181** (1 mark formula, 1 mark substitution, 1 mark final answer).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sample Q.8]**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**
*Derive the Uniform Series Present Worth Factor (USPWF) starting from the definition of compound interest. Show every algebraic step clearly.*

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**
*A 5 kW grid-tied solar PV system costs ₹2,50,000. The expected annual electricity bill saving is ₹30,000 for 25 years. Annual O&M is ₹3,000 in year 1, escalating at 4% per year. The discount rate is 9%. Using the appropriate factors, determine whether the project is financially viable (compute NPV).*

---

**Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]:**

The derivation has been shown in **Section 3.2** of these notes. Key marking distribution:
- 1 mark — Setting up the geometric series with n terms.
- 2 marks — Identifying first term $a = 1/(1+i)$ and common ratio $r = 1/(1+i)$.
- 2 marks — Applying the geometric series sum formula.
- 1 mark — Algebraic simplification leading to the final boxed expression.
- 1 mark — Final boxed formula with proper notation $(P/A, i, n) = \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i(1+i)^n}$.

---

**Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]:**

**Step 1 — Initial Cost PW (already at year 0):** ₹2,50,000.

**Step 2 — PW of Annual Savings (uniform):**
- i = 9%, n = 25. Compute (1.09)^25 = 8.6231.
- USPWF = (8.6231 − 1) / [0.09 × 8.6231] = 7.6231 / 0.7761 = **9.8226**.
- PW(Savings) = 30,000 × 9.8226 = **₹2,94,678**.

**Step 3 — PW of O&M (geometric gradient, g = 4%, i = 9%):**
- Ratio (1+g)/(1+i) = 1.04/1.09 = 0.9541.
- (0.9541)^25 = 0.3144.
- $P_{O\&M} = 3000 \times \dfrac{1 - 0.3144}{0.09 - 0.04} = 3000 \times \dfrac{0.6856}{0.05} = 3000 \times 13.712 = $ **₹41,136**.

**Step 4 — Net Present Value:**
$$
\text{NPV} = \text{PW(Savings)} - \text{PW(Initial Cost)} - \text{PW(O\&M)}
$$
$$
\text{NPV} = 2{,}94{,}678 - 2{,}50{,}000 - 41{,}136 = \mathbf{₹3{,}542}
$$

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Since NPV = +₹3,542 > 0, the solar PV project is **marginally financially viable** at the assumed discount rate.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Part B (b):**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Using the *wrong factor*. O&M is **escalating** (geometric), so students who blindly apply USPWF lose **3 marks** out of 7.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Forgetting to **subtract** the initial cost from PW(Savings). Many students compute only PW(Savings) and call it NPV. This loses **2 marks**.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Reporting NPV as positive without a **clear conclusion statement**. Examiners deduct **1 mark** for missing interpretation.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Writing 0.09 as "9" in the formula without converting to decimal. This causes a 10× error in the final answer.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Sample Q.9] — Alternative Choice**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**
*Explain the concept of the Capital Recovery Factor. Derive the relationship CRF = i + SFF, clearly defining each term.*

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**
*Using the CRF, calculate the Equivalent Annual Cost (EAC) of a 1 kW rooftop solar system with the following data: Initial cost ₹60,000; Inverter replacement at year 12 = ₹20,000; O&M = ₹1,500/year for 25 years; Discount rate i = 10%; Salvage value at year 25 = ₹5,000. Also compute the LCOE if the annual energy yield is 1,500 kWh.*

---

**Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]:**
- 2 marks — Definition of CRF and its formula $(A/P, i, n) = \frac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1}$.
- 2 marks — Definition of SFF and its formula $(A/F, i, n) = \frac{i}{(1+i)^n - 1}$.
- 2 marks — Algebraic manipulation: start with CRF, add and subtract $i$ in the numerator, factor out $i[(1+i)^n - 1]$, simplify to obtain $i + \text{SFF}$.
- 1 mark — Engineering interpretation: CRF's two components are the *interest charge* (i × P) and the *principal recovery* (SFF × P).

---

**Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]:**

**Step 1 — CRF at i = 10%, n = 25:**
- (1.10)^25 = 10.8347.
- CRF = (0.10 × 10.8347) / (10.8347 − 1) = 1.08347 / 9.8347 = **0.11017**.

**Step 2 — Annualized Initial Cost:** 60,000 × 0.11017 = **₹6,610/yr**.

**Step 3 — SPWF for inverter at year 12:** (1.10)^12 = 3.1384; SPWF = 1/3.1384 = 0.3186.
- PW of inverter replacement = 20,000 × 0.3186 = ₹6,372.
- Annualized inverter cost = 6,372 × 0.11017 = **₹702/yr**.

**Step 4 — Annual O&M (uniform, no escalation):** 1,500 × 0.11017 = wait — for *uniform* series, the annual value is already the annual value, so we need PW then annualize:
- PW of O&M = 1,500 × USPWF = 1,500 × 9.0770 = ₹13,616.
- Annualized O&M = 13,616 × 0.11017 = **₹1,500/yr** (as expected — sanity check passes).

**Step 5 — Salvage value recovery (subtract at year 25):**
- PW of salvage = 5,000 × 0.0923 = ₹462.
- Annualized salvage recovery = 462 × 0.11017 = **−₹51/yr**.

**Step 6 — Total Equivalent Annual Cost:**
$$
\text{EAC} = 6{,}610 + 702 + 1{,}500 - 51 = \mathbf{₹8{,}761/yr}
$$

**Step 7 — LCOE:**
$$
\text{LCOE} = \frac{8{,}761}{1{,}500} = \mathbf{₹5.84/kWh}
$$

**Conclusion (1 mark):** The solar PV electricity costs ₹5.84 per kWh, which is competitive if the grid tariff exceeds ₹6/kWh.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Question B Part (b):**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Computing annualized salvage as *additive* instead of *subtractive* (saves a future cost, so it reduces the EAC).
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Treating the inverter cost as a year-0 expense instead of discounting it to year 0 first via SPWF. This gives an inflated EAC.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Mixing up the *annual* O&M (already A) with the *initial* cost (P) when applying CRF. Remember: CRF converts **P → A**, not A → A.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Dividing by the *installed capacity* (1 kW) instead of by the *annual kWh yield* (1,500 kWh). LCOE is **₹/kWh**, not ₹/kW.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Annual Payment and Present Worth Factor**

- **SPWF** = $\dfrac{1}{(1+i)^n}$ — used for *one-time* future costs (inverter, battery replacement, salvage).
- **USPWF** = $\dfrac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i(1+i)^n}$ — used for *constant* annual cash flows (uniform tariff savings, level O&M).
- **CRF** = $\dfrac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1}$ — used to *annualize* any present-worth cost; central to LCOE calculation.
- **SFF** = $\dfrac{i}{(1+i)^n - 1}$ — used for *future-value-to-annual* conversions; reserve fund for replacement.
- **Golden Identity:** $\text{CRF} = i + \text{SFF}$ — guaranteed 1- to 2-mark short-answer question in KTU exams.
- **Geometric Gradient** is the *right* choice for tariff and O&M escalation, **not** USPWF.
- **LCOE** is *always* ₹/kWh = (Annualized Total Cost) / (Annual Energy Yield in kWh).
- **Standard KTU parameters to memorize:** i = 8% to 12%, n = 25 years, inverter replacement at n = 12, PR = 0.75 to 0.80.
- **Sign convention:** Savings are *positive*, costs are *negative*; salvage values *reduce* total cost.
- **Sanity check:** USPWF × CRF = 1 (exact reciprocals).
- **Boundary behaviour:** At $i = 0$, USPWF = n and CRF = 1/n (simple division case).
- **Memory trick:** "*P goes to A via CRF; A comes back to P via USPWF — they are mirror twins.*"
- **Exam tip:** Always convert percentages to decimals (9% → 0.09) before substituting into formulas.
- **Numerical error trap:** $(1+i)^n$ grows very fast for $n = 25$ — keep at least 4 decimal places during intermediate steps.
- **Common KTU distractors:** Arithmetic Gradient (P/G) is rarely used in solar PV problems — do not over-engineer.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Payback Period
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEEET721 - DESIGN OF SOLAR PV SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** Payback Period ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) The **Payback Period (PBP)** in the context of Solar PV System Design is formally defined as the **length of time required for the cumulative net cash inflows (annual monetary savings generated by the solar PV system) to recover the initial capital investment (total installed cost) of the photovoltaic system**. It is one of the most critical and widely used financial metrics in the techno-economic evaluation of solar photovoltaic installations, including grid-tied, off-grid, and hybrid systems. In the KTU 2024 Scheme, the Payback Period is studied as a key economic indicator within Module 4 (Economic Analysis and Feasibility of Solar PV Systems), and it is treated as a fundamental parameter used by engineers, project developers, and policy makers to assess the financial viability of solar projects. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition Highlight:** Payback Period is the time at which the **cumulative cash flow** equals the **initial investment**, and beyond which the project starts generating *pure profit*. There are two principal variants studied in the KTU curriculum: 1. **Simple Payback Period (SPP)** — Does NOT account for the time value of money. 2. **Discounted Payback Period (DPP)** — Accounts for the time value of money by discounting future cash flows. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Explanation Imagine you borrow **₹3,00,000** from a bank to set up a small solar PV plant on your rooftop. The plant generates electricity that you no longer have to buy from the utility. Every year, you "save" a certain amount of money on your electricity bills. The **Payback Period** is simply the question: > *"After how many years will my total savings have added up to ₹3,00,000?"* If you save **₹50,000 every year**, then mathematically: $$3,00,000 \div 50,000 = 6 \text{ years}$$ So your **Payback Period is 6 years**. After the 6th year, every unit of electricity your panels generate is essentially **"free money"** in your pocket. ### 1.3 Real-World Context in Solar PV In India, typical residential rooftop solar PV systems (1 kW – 10 kW) have a **Simple Payback Period of 4 to 7 years**, while utility-scale solar farms have a payback of around **6 to 9 years**. This is because: - Residential electricity tariffs are higher (₹6 – ₹10/kWh) - Utility-scale projects have lower tariffs (₹2.5 – ₹4/kWh) but benefit from economies of scale > [!NOTE] > **Standard Benchmark:** The Ministry of New and Renewable Energy (MNRE), Government of India, often references payback periods of **4–6 years for residential rooftop solar systems** under the PM-Surya Ghar Yojana scheme. ### 1.4 Key Physical & Economic Parameters The following parameters (all highlighted in **bold**) influence the Payback Period of a Solar PV System: - **Initial Investment (C₀)** — Total capital cost (modules, inverter, BOS, installation, civil work) in ₹ or $. - **Annual Energy Yield (E)** — Energy generated per year in kWh, computed as: $$E = P_{rated} \times PSH \times 365 \times \eta_{sys}$$ where $P_{rated}$ is the system rating in kW, $PSH$ is the Peak Sun Hours, and $\eta_{sys}$ is the system efficiency (including temperature, dust, wiring, inverter losses). - **Electricity Tariff (T)** — Cost per kWh paid to the grid (₹/kWh). - **Annual Tariff Escalation (e)** — Year-on-year increase in electricity tariff (typically 3%–6% in India). - **O&M Cost (M)** — Annual Operation and Maintenance cost (typically 1% of initial cost for solar PV). - **Discount Rate (r)** — Used in Discounted Payback Period (typically 8%–12% in India). - **Module Degradation (d)** — Annual reduction in output (~0.5%–0.7% per year for crystalline silicon). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Module Degradation is Critical:** Modern crystalline silicon PV modules degrade at approximately **0.5% per year**. This means a 5 kW system installed today will produce ~4.75 kW equivalent energy after 25 years. This degradation directly extends the Payback Period if not accounted for. ### 1.5 Classification of Payback Period in Solar PV | Variant | Accounts for Time Value of Money? | Accounts for Tariff Escalation? | Accounts for Degradation? | KTU Exam Relevance | |---|---|---|---|---| | Simple Payback Period (SPP) | ❌ No | ❌ No | ❌ No | Very High (3 & 14 mark questions) | | Discounted Payback Period (DPP) | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes (via discounting) | ✅ Yes (via cash flow) | High (14 mark problems) | | Levelized Payback | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | Moderate (theoretical only) | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Theoretical Framework: Why Payback Period Matters The Payback Period is essentially a **risk indicator**. A shorter payback period implies: - Faster recovery of invested capital. - Lower exposure to policy, tariff, and technology risks. - Greater attractiveness for financing institutions (banks, NBFCs). - Higher financial feasibility for residential and commercial adopters. From a **systems engineering perspective**, the Payback Period bridges two domains: 1. **Technical Domain** — How much energy does the system produce? (kWh/year) 2. **Economic Domain** — How much is that energy worth in monetary terms? (₹/year) The interaction can be visualized as: $$\text{Technical Output (kWh)} \xrightarrow{\times \text{Tariff (₹/kWh)}} \text{Annual Savings (₹)} \xrightarrow{\div \text{Initial Cost (₹)}} \text{Payback Period (years)}$$ ### 2.2 Mathematical Foundations #### 2.2.1 Simple Payback Period (SPP) The **Simple Payback Period** is the most basic and frequently tested variant in KTU examinations. It assumes: - Cash flows are uniform every year. - No time value of money. - No degradation. - No tariff escalation. The governing equation is: $$SPP = \frac{C_0}{A_{net}}$$ where: - $C_0$ = Initial Capital Investment (₹) - $A_{net}$ = Annual Net Cash Flow / Annual Net Savings (₹/year) The **Annual Net Cash Flow** is computed as: $$A_{net} = A_{gross} - M_{O\&M}$$ where $A_{gross} = E \times T$ is the gross annual savings (Energy generated × Tariff) and $M_{O\&M}$ is the annual Operation & Maintenance cost. #### 2.2.2 Discounted Payback Period (DPP) The **Discounted Payback Period** is more realistic because it incorporates the **time value of money**. A rupee earned today is worth more than a rupee earned in 10 years due to inflation, opportunity cost, and risk. The discounted cash flow for year $n$ is: $$DCF_n = \frac{CF_n}{(1 + r)^n}$$ The cumulative discounted cash flow is: $$CDCF_n = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{CF_i}{(1 + r)^i}$$ The **Discounted Payback Period** is the smallest integer $n$ for which: $$CDCF_n \geq C_0$$ where $C_0$ is the initial investment. For fractional years, linear interpolation is used: $$DPP = n - 1 + \frac{C_0 - CDCF_{n-1}}{DCF_n}$$ #### 2.2.3 Payback Period with Tariff Escalation When the electricity tariff escalates annually at rate $e$, the savings in year $n$ become: $$A_n = A_1 \times (1 + e)^{n-1}$$ where $A_1$ is the first-year savings. The cumulative savings after $N$ years are: $$\text{Cumulative Savings} = \sum_{n=1}^{N} A_1 (1+e)^{n-1} = A_1 \cdot \frac{(1+e)^N - 1}{e}$$ The Payback Period is the value of $N$ that makes cumulative savings equal to $C_0$. #### 2.2.4 Payback Period with Module Degradation When the module degrades annually at rate $d$, the energy generated in year $n$ becomes: $$E_n = E_1 \times (1 - d)^{n-1}$$ The cumulative energy is: $$\text{Cumulative Energy} = \sum_{n=1}^{N} E_1 (1-d)^{n-1} = E_1 \cdot \frac{1 - (1-d)^N}{d}$$ ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — Memorize These:** | # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Variables | Units | When to Use | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Simple Payback Period | $SPP = C_0 / A_{net}$ | $C_0$ = Initial cost, $A_{net}$ = Annual net savings | years | When cash flows are uniform, no time value of money | | 2 | Annual Energy Yield | $E = P_{rated} \times PSH \times 365 \times \eta_{sys}$ | $P_{rated}$ = kW, $PSH$ = h/day, $\eta_{sys}$ = dimensionless | kWh/year | Energy estimation | | 3 | Annual Gross Savings | $A_{gross} = E \times T$ | $E$ = kWh, $T$ = ₹/kWh | ₹/year | Computing yearly monetary benefit | | 4 | Annual Net Savings | $A_{net} = A_{gross} - M_{O\&M}$ | $A_{gross}$ = ₹/year, $M_{O\&M}$ = ₹/year | ₹/year | SPP denominator | | 5 | Discounted Cash Flow | $DCF_n = CF_n / (1+r)^n$ | $CF_n$ = Cash flow year n, $r$ = discount rate | ₹ | Time-value-of-money analysis | | 6 | Discounted Payback (Integer) | Smallest $n$ such that $\sum DCF_i \geq C_0$ | — | years | DPP with uniform cash flows | | 7 | Discounted Payback (Fractional) | $DPP = (n-1) + [C_0 - CDCF_{n-1}] / DCF_n$ | All in ₹ | years | DPP with linear interpolation | | 8 | Tariff Escalation Factor | $A_n = A_1 (1+e)^{n-1}$ | $e$ = annual tariff escalation rate | ₹/year | When tariffs change yearly | | 9 | Module Degradation Factor | $E_n = E_1 (1-d)^{n-1}$ | $d$ = annual degradation rate | kWh/year | Long-term energy estimation | | 10 | Geometric Sum (Savings) | $S_N = A_1 \cdot [(1+e)^N - 1] / e$ | $A_1$ = first year savings | ₹ | Total savings with tariff escalation | > [!WARNING] > **Critical Notation Rule:** In the formulas above, the discount rate $r$, escalation rate $e$, and degradation rate $d$ are **decimal values** (e.g., 8% = 0.08), **NOT percentage values** (do NOT write 8). This is a common error in KTU exams that costs full marks. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility The Payback Period is the **primary metric** used by: 1. **MNRE (Ministry of New and Renewable Energy)** for benchmarking rooftop solar programs. 2. **Banks and NBFCs** (e.g., SBI, HDFC, IREDA) to sanction solar loans. 3. **DISCOMs** (Distribution Companies) for net-metering viability assessment. 4. **Industrial consumers** (factories, malls, hospitals) for CAPEX justification. 5. **Project developers** for bid evaluation in solar tenders (e.g., SECI auctions). > [!NOTE] > **Industry Standard:** A solar PV project is generally considered **financially attractive** if its Simple Payback Period is **less than 5 years** for residential systems and **less than 7 years** for commercial/industrial systems. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Simple Payback Period from First Principles The **Simple Payback Period** is derived by equating cumulative net cash inflows to the initial capital investment. **Step 1:** Let the initial capital investment at year 0 be $C_0$ (in ₹). **Step 2:** Assume uniform annual net cash flow of $A_{net}$ (in ₹/year) for each year $n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots, N$. **Step 3:** The cumulative cash flow after $N$ years is: $$\text{Cumulative Cash Flow} = A_{net} \times N$$ **Step 4:** The Payback Period is reached when the cumulative cash flow first equals (or exceeds) the initial investment: $$A_{net} \times N_{payback} = C_0$$ **Step 5:** Solving for $N_{payback}$: $$N_{payback} = \frac{C_0}{A_{net}}$$ $$\boxed{SPP = \frac{C_0}{A_{net}}}$$ This derivation assumes uniform cash flows and no time value of money. ### 3.2 Derivation: Discounted Payback Period **Step 1:** A future cash flow $CF_n$ received at the end of year $n$ is worth less in present-day terms due to the discount rate $r$. The Present Value (PV) of $CF_n$ is: $$PV(CF_n) = \frac{CF_n}{(1 + r)^n}$$ **Step 2:** The cumulative discounted cash flow over $N$ years is the sum of the present values: $$CDCF_N = \sum_{n=1}^{N} \frac{CF_n}{(1 + r)^n}$$ **Step 3:** The Discounted Payback Period is the smallest $N$ such that: $$CDCF_N \geq C_0$$ **Step 4:** For fractional accuracy, linear interpolation between year $n-1$ and year $n$ is used: $$DPP = (n - 1) + \frac{C_0 - CDCF_{n-1}}{DCF_n}$$ ### 3.3 Exhaustive Worked Example — Simple Payback Period > **Problem Statement:** > A 5 kW rooftop solar PV system is installed at a residential building in Kerala. The following data is provided: > - Total installed cost: ₹3,00,000 > - Average Peak Sun Hours (PSH) in Kerala: 4.5 h/day > - System efficiency (overall): 0.85 > - Electricity tariff: ₹7.50/kWh > - Annual O&M cost: ₹3,000/year > > **Compute the Simple Payback Period.** **Step 1: Compute the annual energy generation (E).** Using the standard energy yield formula: $$E = P_{rated} \times PSH \times 365 \times \eta_{sys}$$ Substituting the values: $$E = 5 \, \text{kW} \times 4.5 \, \text{h/day} \times 365 \, \text{days} \times 0.85$$ $$E = 5 \times 4.5 \times 365 \times 0.85$$ $$E = 5 \times 1642.5 \times 0.85$$ $$E = 8212.5 \times 0.85$$ $$E = 6980.625 \, \text{kWh/year}$$ **Step 2: Compute the annual gross savings (A_gross).** $$A_{gross} = E \times T = 6980.625 \times 7.50 = ₹52,354.69 \, \text{per year}$$ **Step 3: Compute the annual net savings (A_net).** $$A_{net} = A_{gross} - M_{O\&M} = 52,354.69 - 3,000 = ₹49,354.69 \, \text{per year}$$ **Step 4: Compute the Simple Payback Period.** $$SPP = \frac{C_0}{A_{net}} = \frac{3,00,000}{49,354.69} \approx 6.08 \, \text{years}$$ **Conclusion:** The system recovers its cost in approximately **6.08 years (~6 years and 1 month)**. ### 3.4 Exhaustive Worked Example — Discounted Payback Period > **Problem Statement:** > Using the same 5 kW system from the previous example, compute the Discounted Payback Period assuming a discount rate of $r = 8\%$ per annum. The annual net cash flow is constant at ₹49,354.69/year. **Step 1: Compute the Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) for each year.** The DCF for year $n$ is: $$DCF_n = \frac{A_{net}}{(1 + r)^n} = \frac{49,354.69}{(1.08)^n}$$ Let us compute the DCF and cumulative DCF year by year. **Year 1:** $$DCF_1 = \frac{49,354.69}{(1.08)^1} = \frac{49,354.69}{1.08} = ₹45,698.79$$ $$CDCF_1 = 45,698.79$$ **Year 2:** $$DCF_2 = \frac{49,354.69}{(1.08)^2} = \frac{49,354.69}{1.1664} = ₹42,313.69$$ $$CDCF_2 = 45,698.79 + 42,313.69 = ₹88,012.48$$ **Year 3:** $$DCF_3 = \frac{49,354.69}{(1.08)^3} = \frac{49,354.69}{1.2597} = ₹39,179.34$$ $$CDCF_3 = 88,012.48 + 39,179.34 = ₹1,27,191.82$$ **Year 4:** $$DCF_4 = \frac{49,354.69}{(1.08)^4} = \frac{49,354.69}{1.3605} = ₹36,277.17$$ $$CDCF_4 = 1,27,191.82 + 36,277.17 = ₹1,63,468.99$$ **Year 5:** $$DCF_5 = \frac{49,354.69}{(1.08)^5} = \frac{49,354.69}{1.4693} = ₹33,589.05$$ $$CDCF_5 = 1,63,468.99 + 33,589.05 = ₹1,97,058.04$$ **Year 6:** $$DCF_6 = \frac{49,354.69}{(1.08)^6} = \frac{49,354.69}{1.5869} = ₹31,101.90$$ $$CDCF_6 = 1,97,058.04 + 31,101.90 = ₹2,28,159.94$$ **Year 7:** $$DCF_7 = \frac{49,354.69}{(1.08)^7} = \frac{49,354.69}{1.7138} = ₹28,797.13$$ $$CDCF_7 = 2,28,159.94 + 28,797.13 = ₹2,56,957.07$$ **Year 8:** $$DCF_8 = \frac{49,354.69}{(1.08)^8} = \frac{49,354.69}{1.8509} = ₹26,664.01$$ $$CDCF_8 = 2,56,957.07 + 26,664.01 = ₹2,83,621.08$$ **Year 9:** $$DCF_9 = \frac{49,354.69}{(1.08)^9} = \frac{49,354.69}{1.9990} = ₹24,687.05$$ $$CDCF_9 = 2,83,621.08 + 24,687.05 = ₹3,08,308.13$$ **Step 2: Identify the Payback Year.** The cumulative discounted cash flow $CDCF_8 = ₹2,83,621.08$ is **less than** $C_0 = ₹3,00,000$. The cumulative discounted cash flow $CDCF_9 = ₹3,08,308.13$ is **greater than** $C_0 = ₹3,00,000$. Therefore, the payback occurs between Year 8 and Year 9. **Step 3: Apply Linear Interpolation for Fractional Payback.** $$DPP = (n - 1) + \frac{C_0 - CDCF_{n-1}}{DCF_n}$$ $$DPP = (9 - 1) + \frac{3,00,000 - 2,83,621.08}{24,687.05}$$ $$DPP = 8 + \frac{16,378.92}{24,687.05}$$ $$DPP = 8 + 0.6635$$ $$DPP = 8.66 \, \text{years}$$ **Conclusion:** The **Discounted Payback Period is 8.66 years**, which is **2.58 years longer** than the Simple Payback Period (6.08 years). This is because discounting reduces the present value of future cash flows. ### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix: SPP vs DPP vs Real-World PBP | Metric | Simple Payback Period | Discounted Payback Period | Real-World PBP (with all factors) | |---|---|---|---| | Time Value of Money | Ignored | Discounted via $r$ | Discounted via $r$ | | Tariff Escalation | Ignored | Implicit (if cash flows vary) | Explicit modeling | | Module Degradation | Ignored | Ignored (if constant cash flow) | Explicit modeling | | O&M Inflation | Ignored | Ignored | Explicit modeling | | KTU Exam Likelihood | ★★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★ (advanced problems) | | Typical Value (5 kW Kerala) | 5 – 7 years | 7 – 10 years | 6 – 8 years | | Investor Preference | Quick screening | Risk-aware assessment | Bankable project report | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Observation:** DPP > SPP always, because discounting reduces future cash flows, requiring more years to recover the initial investment. ## 4.
