The slope of the curve represents the **annual net savings**. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) #### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Define the term "Payback Period" as applied to a solar PV system. Write the formula for the Simple Payback Period.** **Model Answer:** The **Payback Period** of a solar PV system is defined as the length of time required for the cumulative monetary savings generated by the system (in terms of reduced electricity bills or revenue from exported power) to equal the initial capital investment made for installing the system. **Formula for Simple Payback Period:** $$SPP = \frac{C_0}{A_{net}}$$ where: - $C_0$ = Initial capital investment (in ₹) - $A_{net}$ = Annual net cash flow / annual net savings (in ₹/year) The result is expressed in **years**. A shorter payback period indicates a more financially attractive project. --- #### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Distinguish between Simple Payback Period and Discounted Payback Period.** **Model Answer:** | Feature | Simple Payback Period (SPP) | Discounted Payback Period (DPP) | |---|---|---| | Time value of money | Not considered | Considered via discount rate | | Formula | $SPP = C_0 / A_{net}$ | Smallest $n$ such that $\sum DCF_i \geq C_0$ | | Accuracy | Approximate, screening-level | More accurate, project-level | | Typical value | Lower (optimistic) | Higher (realistic) | | Use case | Quick feasibility check | Bankable DPR, investor reports | > **Key Insight:** DPP is always **greater than or equal to** SPP, because discounting reduces the present value of future cash flows. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) #### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024] **(a) [7 Marks]** A 3 kW rooftop solar PV system is installed at a residence in Thrissur, Kerala. The system details are: - Total installed cost: ₹2,10,000 - Peak Sun Hours: 4.2 h/day - System efficiency: 0.80 - Electricity tariff: ₹8.00/kWh - Annual O&M cost: ₹2,500/year - Module degradation: 1% per year (for first 5 years) Compute the **Simple Payback Period** for the system. Also comment on whether the investment is justified for a residential consumer. **(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the significance of Discounted Payback Period over Simple Payback Period. List any three real-world factors that cause the actual payback period to differ from the theoretical SPP. --- **Model Solution for Part (a):** **Step 1: Compute annual energy generation.** $$E = P_{rated} \times PSH \times 365 \times \eta_{sys}$$ $$E = 3 \times 4.2 \times 365 \times 0.80$$ $$E = 3 \times 4.2 \times 292 = 3 \times 1226.4 = 3679.2 \, \text{kWh/year}$$ **Step 2: Compute annual gross savings.** $$A_{gross} = E \times T = 3679.2 \times 8.00 = ₹29,433.60 \, \text{per year}$$ **Step 3: Compute annual net savings.** $$A_{net} = A_{gross} - M_{O\&M} = 29,433.60 - 2,500 = ₹26,933.60 \, \text{per year}$$ **Step 4: Compute Simple Payback Period.** $$SPP = \frac{C_0}{A_{net}} = \frac{2,10,000}{26,933.60} = 7.80 \, \text{years}$$ **Comment:** The payback period of **7.80 years** is on the **higher end** of typical residential solar payback in Kerala (4–7 years). The system is **marginally justified** for the residential consumer. However, with the **1% annual module degradation**, the actual energy output in subsequent years will be lower, potentially extending the payback period to ~8.5 years. The investment may still be justified due to the 25-year module life warranty, but the consumer should consider state subsidies (e.g., KSEB net metering benefits) to improve economics. --- **Model Solution for Part (b):** **Significance of DPP over SPP:** The Discounted Payback Period is more accurate and financially meaningful than SPP because: 1. **Time Value of Money:** DPP recognizes that ₹100 earned today is worth more than ₹100 earned in 10 years. SPP ignores this completely. 2. **Risk Assessment:** DPP inherently penalizes projects with delayed cash flows, providing a more conservative and risk-aware estimate. 3. **Better for Long-Lived Assets:** Solar PV systems have a 25-year life. DPP captures the diminishing present value of distant cash flows, which SPP cannot. 4. **Investor Confidence:** Banks (e.g., IREDA, SBI) require DPP-based analysis for loan sanctioning, not SPP. **Three Real-World Factors Affecting Actual Payback Period:** 1. **Module Degradation:** PV modules lose 0.5%–1% efficiency annually, reducing energy output and extending payback. 2. **Tariff Escalation:** Electricity tariffs typically increase 3%–6% annually in India, which actually shortens the payback period (cash flows grow over time). 3. **Inverter Replacement:** Inverters have a 10–12 year life and cost ₹30,000–₹60,000. This replacement cost is not captured in SPP and significantly extends the effective payback. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024] **(a) [7 Marks]** A 10 kW commercial solar PV system is installed at an industrial facility with the following parameters: - Initial investment: ₹5,50,000 - Annual energy generation: 16,000 kWh - Tariff: ₹6.50/kWh - Annual O&M: ₹5,000 - Tariff escalation: 4% per year - Discount rate: 10% per year Compute the **Discounted Payback Period** (DPP) for the first 8 years using a year-by-year discounted cash flow table. **(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss how Payback Period relates to the concept of Levelized Cost of Energy (LCOE). Why is LCOE considered a more comprehensive metric than simple payback for large-scale solar projects? --- **Model Solution for Part (a):** **Step 1: Compute first-year net cash flow.** $$A_{gross,1} = 16,000 \times 6.50 = ₹1,04,000$$ $$A_{net,1} = 1,04,000 - 5,000 = ₹99,000$$ **Step 2: Compute year-by-year cash flow with tariff escalation.** Cash flow in year $n$: $$CF_n = A_{net,1} \times (1 + e)^{n-1} = 99,000 \times (1.04)^{n-1}$$ **Step 3: Compute Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) and Cumulative DCF.** Discount factor for year $n$: $(1 + r)^n = (1.10)^n$ | Year $n$ | Cash Flow $CF_n$ (₹) | Discount Factor $(1.10)^n$ | DCF (₹) | Cumulative DCF (₹) | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | 99,000 | 1.1000 | 90,000.00 | 90,000.00 | | 2 | 1,02,960 | 1.2100 | 85,090.91 | 1,75,090.91 | | 3 | 1,07,078 | 1.3310 | 80,449.66 | 2,55,540.57 | | 4 | 1,11,361 | 1.4641 | 76,060.79 | 3,31,601.36 | | 5 | 1,15,816 | 1.6105 | 71,914.31 | 4,03,515.67 | | 6 | 1,20,449 | 1.7716 | 67,998.34 | 4,71,514.01 | | 7 | 1,25,267 | 1.9487 | 64,279.13 | 5,35,793.14 | | 8 | 1,30,277 | 2.1436 | 60,778.46 | 5,96,571.60 | **Step 4: Identify the Payback Year.** The cumulative DCF crosses the initial investment of ₹5,50,000 between **Year 7 and Year 8**. **Step 5: Linear Interpolation for Fractional DPP.** $$DPP = (n - 1) + \frac{C_0 - CDCF_{n-1}}{DCF_n}$$ $$DPP = (8 - 1) + \frac{5,50,000 - 5,35,793.14}{60,778.46}$$ $$DPP = 7 + \frac{14,206.86}{60,778.46}$$ $$DPP = 7 + 0.234 = 7.23 \, \text{years}$$ **Conclusion:** The Discounted Payback Period is **7.23 years**. --- **Model Solution for Part (b):** **Relationship between Payback Period and LCOE:** Both metrics are used to assess the economic viability of solar PV systems, but they capture different dimensions: - **Payback Period** answers: *"When will I recover my money?"* (Time-based) - **LCOE** answers: *"What is the true per-unit cost of electricity over the project lifetime?"* (Cost-based) **LCOE Formula:** $$LCOE = \frac{\sum_{t=0}^{N} \frac{C_t}{(1+r)^t}}{\sum_{t=0}^{N} \frac{E_t}{(1+r)^t}}$$ where $C_t$ is the cost in year $t$ and $E_t$ is the energy generated in year $t$. **Why LCOE is More Comprehensive than Payback:** 1. **Whole-Life Analysis:** LCOE considers the entire project lifetime (25 years for solar), while payback only identifies the breakeven point. 2. **Discounted Cash Flows:** LCOE fully integrates time value of money, not just the breakeven year. 3. **All Costs Included:** LCOE accounts for initial cost, O&M, inverter replacement, decommissioning — not just the recovery point. 4. **Comparison Across Technologies:** LCOE allows direct comparison between solar PV, wind, coal, gas, etc., on a level playing field. Payback is project-specific. 5. **Policy Relevance:** SECI (Solar Energy Corporation of India) uses LCOE for reverse auction tariff discovery. MNRE uses LCOE benchmarks to evaluate national solar programs. > [!NOTE] > **Empirical Example:** A solar PV project in India has an LCOE of ₹2.5/kWh, which is **lower than the grid tariff** of ₹7/kWh. This makes it economically attractive even if the payback period is 7–8 years. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:** > > 1. **Confusing SPP and DPP:** Students often compute SPP and label it as DPP, or vice versa. Always read the question carefully. > > 2. **Forgetting to Subtract O&M:** The denominator in SPP is **Net Savings**, not Gross Savings. Many students forget to subtract the O&M cost. > > 3. **Using Percentage Values in Formulas:** The discount rate $r$, escalation rate $e$, and degradation rate $d$ must be in **decimal form** (8% = 0.08), NOT as 8. > > 4. **No Units in Final Answer:** Always write "years" or "₹" with the numerical answer. Marks are deducted for missing units. > > 5. **Skipping Linear Interpolation in DPP:** When DPP falls between two years, students often round to the nearest integer. **Always perform interpolation** for full marks. > > 6. **Ignoring Degradation:** For a 25-year solar project, module degradation (~0.5%/year) significantly affects energy output. Questions in KTU often require year-by-year calculation. > > 7. **Confusing Tariff Escalation with Discount Rate:** Escalation **increases** future cash flows (good for investor), while discount rate **decreases** present value (risk adjustment). Do not mix them up. ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Payback Period:** > > - ✅ **Definition:** Time to recover initial investment from cumulative savings. > - ✅ **SPP Formula:** $SPP = C_0 / A_{net}$ — simple, fast, ignores time value of money. > - ✅ **DPP Formula:** Smallest $n$ with $\sum DCF_i \geq C_0$ — accurate, accounts for discounting. > - ✅ **Energy Yield:** $E = P_{rated} \times PSH \times 365 \times \eta_{sys}$ > - ✅ **Net Savings:** $A_{net} = (E \times T) - M_{O\&M}$ > - ✅ **Always use decimals for rates:** $r = 0.08$ (not 8), $e = 0.04$, $d = 0.005$. > - ✅ **DPP > SPP always** — discounting extends payback. > - ✅ **Module degradation** = 0.5%/year (c-Si) to 1%/year (thin-film). > - ✅ **Tariff escalation** in India = 3%–6% per annum. > - ✅ **Typical residential SPP in Kerala:** 5–7 years. > - ✅ **Typical commercial SPP in India:** 5–8 years. > - ✅ **Linear interpolation** is mandatory for fractional DPP. > - ✅ **LCOE** is the complement to Payback Period for whole-life cost analysis. > - ✅ **Inverter replacement** at year 10–12 must be included in serious economic analysis. > - ✅ **MNRE benchmark** for residential rooftop solar: SPP ≤ 6 years. > - ✅ **For KTU 14-mark questions:** Always draw the **cash flow table** with DCF and cumulative DCF columns.

topics.title: LCC with Examples, Introduction to Simulation Software for Solar PV System Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEET721 - DESIGN OF SOLAR PV SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** LCC with Examples, Introduction to Simulation Software for Solar PV System Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Life Cycle Cost (LCC) — Formal Definition

**Life Cycle Cost (LCC)** is the total discounted cost of owning, operating, maintaining, and disposing of a Solar PV system over its entire operational lifespan, expressed in present-day monetary value (Present Worth or Net Present Value). It is the most rigorous engineering-economic metric used by KTU 2024 scheme examiners to compare competing PV system designs on a common financial footing.

In KTU 2024 syllabus terminology, LCC analysis is defined as:

> **LCC = Initial Capital Cost + Present Worth of O&M Costs + Present Worth of Replacement Costs − Present Worth of Salvage Value**

The analysis is conducted over a typical project horizon of **25 years** for crystalline silicon PV systems, **20 years** for thin-film, and **30 years** for BIPV (Building-Integrated PV) systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** LCC is the ONLY tool that captures the "time value of money" for solar investments. Simple payback period methods IGNORE this and are considered incomplete for board examinations.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Car Ownership" Intuition

Imagine you buy a car for **₹10 Lakhs** today. Over the next 15 years, you will spend money on petrol, insurance, servicing, tyres, and finally sell the scrap. LCC of a PV system works exactly the same way:

| Car Analogy | PV System Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Purchase price | Initial CapEx (Modules, Inverter, BOS) |
| Petrol cost | Operation & Maintenance (O&M) |
| Major service / Tyre change | Inverter replacement (typically at year 12–15) |
| Insurance | Insurance / Performance guarantee cost |
| Scrap value at end | Salvage value (modules recycled) |

The **only difference** is that we use a **discount rate** (similar to inflation + opportunity cost) to convert all future expenses into *today's rupees*, because ₹100 spent in 2035 is worth LESS than ₹100 spent today.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Constants Used in KTU Examinations:**
> - **Discount rate (d):** Typically **8% to 12%** for Indian solar projects
> - **Project life (N):** **25 years** for crystalline Si
> - **Inverter replacement:** Once at **year 12–15**
> - **Module degradation:** **0.5% to 1% per year**
> - **Inflation rate for O&M:** **5% to 6%** (separate from discount rate)

## 1.3 Introduction to Simulation Software — Definition

**Solar PV Simulation Software** refers to computer-based tools that mathematically model solar radiation, PV module electrical behaviour, inverter characteristics, system losses, and financial parameters to predict energy yield, system performance ratio, and LCC before physical installation.

These tools implement algorithms from standards such as **IEC 61724** (performance monitoring) and **IEC 61853** (module performance testing) to produce bankable reports.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why simulation is mandatory in KTU 2024?** Because actual installation is impossible during design evaluation. Software acts as a "virtual test bench" — just like MATLAB for electrical engineers or ANSYS for mechanical engineers.

## 1.4 Real-World Context for Kerala Installations

For a typical **5 kW rooftop system in Kerala** (Kochi latitude ≈ 9.97° N), simulation software predicts:
- Annual yield: **~1,800 to 2,000 kWh/kWp**
- Performance Ratio (PR): **0.75 to 0.82**
- Specific Yield: **1,650 kWh/kWp/year**
- Levelized LCOE: **₹3.5 to ₹5.5 / kWh** (depending on subsidy and tariff)

These numbers are unattainable through manual calculation for a full year of hourly data — hence simulation software is the industry standard.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Components of Life Cycle Cost (LCC) — Step-by-Step Logic

A complete LCC analysis involves **five (5) major cost streams** that the KTU examiner expects you to identify and quantify:

### Step 1: Initial Capital Cost (C₀)
The one-time, upfront investment paid at Year 0 (i.e., today). Includes:
- PV Modules (typically 40–50% of total cost)
- Inverter (10–15%)
- Mounting structure (10–15%)
- BOS — Balance of System (cables, DC/AC protection, earthing) (15–20%)
- Civil works & installation labour (10–15%)
- Design, engineering, and commissioning (5%)

### Step 2: Annual Operation & Maintenance Cost (C_OM)
Recurring yearly cost, usually expressed as a **percentage (1% to 2%) of initial cost per year**. Includes:
- Module cleaning (critical in Kerala due to dust, bird-droppings, coastal salt)
- Inverter maintenance
- Vegetation control
- Insurance and security

### Step 3: Replacement Cost (C_REP)
Major component replacements during project life:
- **Inverter replacement** at year 12–15 (inverter lifetime ≈ 10–15 years)
- Possibly string combiner boxes, DC isolators

### Step 4: Salvage Value (S)
Resale or recycling value of components at the end of project life (negative cost, i.e., money recovered). Typically **5% to 10%** of initial cost for crystalline Si modules due to aluminium frame, glass, and silicon recovery.

### Step 5: Discount Rate (d) and Present Worth Factor
The discount rate captures the **time value of money**. The **Single Payment Present Worth Factor (SPPWF)** converts a future cost at year *n* to present value:

$$
SPPWF(n, d) = \frac{1}{(1 + d)^n}
$$

A higher discount rate → future costs become less significant today.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Variables | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Present Worth Factor (Single Payment) | $P/F = \dfrac{1}{(1+d)^n}$ | d = discount rate; n = year | Dimensionless |
| 2 | Uniform Series Present Worth Factor | $P/A = \dfrac{(1+d)^N - 1}{d(1+d)^N}$ | N = project life in years | Used for annual O&M |
| 3 | Uniform Series Future Worth Factor | $F/A = \dfrac{(1+d)^N - 1}{d}$ | Future value of annual costs | Used for salvage |
| 4 | LCC (Total) | $LCC = C_0 + C_{OM} \cdot (P/A) + C_{REP} \cdot (P/F)_{n_{rep}} - S \cdot (P/F)_N$ | All costs in ₹ | Master equation |
| 5 | Annualized LCC (CRF method) | $CRF = \dfrac{d(1+d)^N}{(1+d)^N - 1}$ | Capital Recovery Factor | $/year |
| 6 | Annualized LCC | $A = LCC \times CRF$ | Spread over project life | ₹/year |
| 7 | Levelized Cost of Energy (LCOE) | $LCOE = \dfrac{A}{E_{annual}}$ | E_annual = yearly energy yield | ₹/kWh |
| 8 | Energy Yield (kWh/year) | $E = G_{stc} \cdot PR \cdot P_{peak}$ | PR = Performance Ratio (0.7–0.85) | kWh |
| 9 | Simple Payback Period | $SPP = \dfrac{C_0}{E \cdot Tariff}$ | Tariff = ₹/kWh | Years |
| 10 | Discounted Payback | Set $\sum CF_t / (1+d)^t = C_0$ and solve for t | Cash flow CF_t | Years (more accurate) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** In the formula $P/A$, the symbol $\vert$ is **NOT** used for division. Always use $\div$ or fraction bar. Also, "n" in single payment formula and "N" in series formula represent different things — be careful!

## 2.3 The LCC Calculation — Logic Flow

1. Identify project life **N** and discount rate **d** (given in problem).
2. List all costs with their **timing** (year 0, annual, year n, year N).
3. Convert each future cost to **present value (PV)** using SPPWF or P/A.
4. Sum all present values algebraically (negative sign for salvage).
5. (Optional) Convert total LCC to **annualized cost** using CRF.
6. (Optional) Compute **LCOE** by dividing annualized cost by annual energy yield.
7. Compare with grid tariff or alternative design to make decision.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility of LCC

- **Banks and MNRE** (Ministry of New & Renewable Energy) require LCC analysis for loan approval of solar projects.
- **DISCOMs** (e.g., KSEB in Kerala) use LCC-based LCOE to evaluate PPA (Power Purchase Agreement) bids.
- Engineers use LCC to compare **monocrystalline vs polycrystalline vs thin-film** technologies on a 25-year basis.
- LCC supports **vendor selection** — a cheap inverter with frequent replacement may have higher LCC than a premium inverter.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Uniform Series Present Worth Factor (P/A)

**Why we need this derivation:** The KTU examiner may ask you to derive the P/A factor from first principles, because it is the most-used factor in LCC analysis for converting annual O&M costs into present value.

### Starting Equation

A sum of *A* rupees is paid at the **end of each year** for *N* years. We want the equivalent value at year 0 (today).

**Step 1:** Write the present value of each individual payment.

$$
PV = \frac{A}{(1+d)^1} + \frac{A}{(1+d)^2} + \frac{A}{(1+d)^3} + \ldots + \frac{A}{(1+d)^N}
$$

This is a **finite geometric series** with first term $a = A/(1+d)$, common ratio $r = 1/(1+d)$, and N terms.

**Step 2:** Apply the geometric series sum formula: $S_N = a \cdot \dfrac{1 - r^N}{1 - r}$.

$$
PV = \frac{A}{(1+d)} \cdot \frac{1 - \left(\frac{1}{1+d}\right)^N}{1 - \frac{1}{1+d}}
$$

**Step 3:** Simplify the denominator $1 - \dfrac{1}{1+d} = \dfrac{(1+d) - 1}{(1+d)} = \dfrac{d}{1+d}$.

$$
PV = \frac{A}{(1+d)} \cdot \frac{1 - \frac{1}{(1+d)^N}}{\frac{d}{1+d}}
$$

**Step 4:** The $(1+d)$ in the numerator and denominator cancel.

$$
PV = \frac{A}{d} \cdot \left[1 - \frac{1}{(1+d)^N}\right]
$$

**Step 5:** Multiply numerator and denominator inside the bracket by $(1+d)^N$ to obtain the standard textbook form.

$$
PV = A \cdot \frac{(1+d)^N - 1}{d(1+d)^N}
$$

**Step 6:** Therefore, the **Uniform Series Present Worth Factor** is:

$$
\boxed{\,(P/A, d, N) = \frac{(1+d)^N - 1}{d(1+d)^N}\,}
$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Complete LCC Calculation (14-Mark Standard)

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):**

A 5 kW rooftop solar PV system is proposed for a residence in Thrissur, Kerala. The following data is provided:

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Initial capital cost (C₀) | ₹3,50,000 |
| Annual O&M cost (C_OM) | ₹5,000/year (escalating at 5% inflation) |
| Discount rate (d) | 10% per year |
| Project life (N) | 25 years |
| Inverter replacement at year 12 | ₹40,000 |
| Salvage value at year 25 | ₹25,000 |
| Annual energy yield (E) | 7,500 kWh/year |
| Grid tariff | ₹7.00 / kWh |

**Compute: (a) Total LCC, (b) Annualized LCC, (c) LCOE, (d) Simple Payback Period.**

> [!NOTE]
> To keep this KTU-style, we treat O&M as **uniform (non-escalating)** for simplicity. (Escalating O&M is an advanced variant using geometric gradient series — outside syllabus scope.)

### Part (a) — Total LCC

**Step 1:** Compute P/A factor for d = 10%, N = 25.

$$
P/A = \frac{(1.10)^{25} - 1}{0.10 \times (1.10)^{25}}
$$

Compute $(1.10)^{25}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(1.10)^{25} &\approx 10.8347 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Substitute:

$$
P/A = \frac{10.8347 - 1}{0.10 \times 10.8347} = \frac{9.8347}{1.08347} \approx 9.0770
$$

**Step 2:** Compute P/F factor for inverter replacement at year 12.

$$
P/F_{12} = \frac{1}{(1.10)^{12}} = \frac{1}{3.1384} \approx 0.3186
$$

**Step 3:** Compute P/F factor for salvage value at year 25.

$$
P/F_{25} = \frac{1}{(1.10)^{25}} = \frac{1}{10.8347} \approx 0.0923
$$

**Step 4:** Present Worth of O&M cost.

$$
PW_{OM} = 5{,}000 \times 9.0770 = \text{₹}45{,}385
$$

**Step 5:** Present Worth of inverter replacement.

$$
PW_{REP} = 40{,}000 \times 0.3186 = \text{₹}12{,}744
$$

**Step 6:** Present Worth of salvage value (this is a NEGATIVE cost, i.e., money recovered).

$$
PW_{S} = 25{,}000 \times 0.0923 = \text{₹}2{,}308
$$

**Step 7:** Total LCC.

$$
\begin{aligned}
LCC &= C_0 + PW_{OM} + PW_{REP} - PW_{S} \\
&= 3{,}50{,}000 + 45{,}385 + 12{,}744 - 2{,}308 \\
&= \text{₹}4{,}05{,}821
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer (a):** $\boxed{LCC = \text{₹}4{,}05{,}821}$

---

### Part (b) — Annualized LCC

**Step 1:** Compute the Capital Recovery Factor (CRF) for d = 10%, N = 25.

$$
CRF = \frac{d(1+d)^N}{(1+d)^N - 1} = \frac{0.10 \times 10.8347}{10.8347 - 1} = \frac{1.08347}{9.8347} \approx 0.1102
$$

**Step 2:** Annualized LCC.

$$
A = LCC \times CRF = 4{,}05{,}821 \times 0.1102 = \text{₹}44{,}722 / \text{year}
$$

**Answer (b):** $\boxed{A = \text{₹}44{,}722 / \text{year}}$

---

### Part (c) — Levelized Cost of Energy (LCOE)

$$
LCOE = \frac{A}{E_{annual}} = \frac{44{,}722}{7{,}500} = \text{₹}5.96 / \text{kWh}
$$

**Answer (c):** $\boxed{LCOE = \text{₹}5.96 / \text{kWh}}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** Since LCOE (₹5.96) < Grid Tariff (₹7.00), the system is **economically viable**. If LCOE > Tariff, the design is not justified.

---

### Part (d) — Simple Payback Period

Annual savings = 7,500 × 7.00 = ₹52,500/year.

$$
SPP = \frac{C_0}{\text{Annual Savings}} = \frac{3{,}50{,}000}{52{,}500} \approx 6.67 \text{ years}
$$

**Answer (d):** $\boxed{SPP \approx 6.67 \text{ years}}$

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Comparing Two Inverter Options

**Problem:** You have two inverter options for a 10 kW system:

| Parameter | Inverter A (Cheap) | Inverter B (Premium) |
|---|---|---|
| Initial cost | ₹60,000 | ₹1,00,000 |
| Lifetime | 10 years | 20 years |
| Replacement cost at end of life | ₹60,000 (same model) | ₹1,00,000 |
| Salvage | ₹5,000 | ₹8,000 |
| d = 8%, N = 20 years | | |

**Compute LCC for both options and recommend.**

### Inverter A (Two replacements: at year 10 and year 20)

$$
P/F_{10} = \frac{1}{(1.08)^{10}} = \frac{1}{2.1589} \approx 0.4632
$$

$$
P/F_{20} = \frac{1}{(1.08)^{20}} = \frac{1}{4.6610} \approx 0.2145
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
LCC_A &= 60{,}000 + 60{,}000 \times 0.4632 + 60{,}000 \times 0.2145 - 5{,}000 \times 0.2145 \\
&= 60{,}000 + 27{,}792 + 12{,}870 - 1{,}073 \\
&= \text{₹}99{,}589
\end{aligned}
$$

### Inverter B (One replacement at year 20, salvage at year 20)

> Note: For Inverter B, the salvage happens at the SAME time as replacement, so we subtract it from replacement cost.

$$
\begin{aligned}
LCC_B &= 1{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}00{,}000 \times 0.2145 - 8{,}000 \times 0.2145 \\
&= 1{,}00{,}000 + 21{,}450 - 1{,}716 \\
&= \text{₹}1{,}19{,}734
\end{aligned}
$$

**Recommendation:** Inverter A has LOWER LCC (₹99,589 vs ₹1,19,734) — though cheaper, the premium inverter B is **NOT** cost-effective over 20 years.

> [!WARNING]
> This counter-intuitive result demonstrates exactly why KTU 2024 scheme mandates LCC analysis. A higher initial cost is NOT always worse.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Summary of Example Results

| Metric | Example 1 (5 kW Kerala home) | Example 2 Inverter A | Example 2 Inverter B |
|---|---|---|---|
| Initial Cost | ₹3,50,000 | ₹60,000 | ₹1,00,000 |
| PW of O&M / Replacements | ₹58,129 | ₹40,662 | ₹21,450 |
| PW of Salvage | −₹2,308 | −₹1,073 | −₹1,716 |
| **Total LCC** | **₹4,05,821** | **₹99,589** | **₹1,19,734** |
| Annualized LCC | ₹44,722 / year | ₹10,131 / year | ₹12,178 / year |
| LCOE | ₹5.96 / kWh | — | — |
| Decision | Viable (LCOE < tariff) | **Choose A** | Rejected |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 LCC Calculation Block Architecture

This block diagram shows the **data flow** for a typical LCC analysis. Use this to answer "draw the flow chart of LCC" type questions in KTU exams.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: Project Data] --> B[Identify Cost Streams]
    B --> C[Initial Capital Cost C0]
    B --> D[Annual O and M Cost COM]
    B --> E[Replacement Cost CREP at year n]
    B --> F[Salvage Value S at year N]
    B --> G[Discount Rate d]
    B --> H[Project Life N]
    C --> I[Apply Year Zero Cost]
    D --> J[Apply P/A Factor]
    E --> K[Apply P/F Factor at year n]
    F --> L[Apply P/F Factor at year N]
    G --> M[Time Value Engine]
    H --> M
    I --> N[Sum All Present Values]
    J --> N
    K --> N
    L --> N
    N --> O[Compute Total LCC]
    O --> P[Apply CRF for Annualized LCC]
    P --> Q[Divide by Annual Energy Yield]
    Q --> R[LCOE Output]
    O --> S[Compare with Tariff or Alternative]
    S --> T[Economic Decision Output]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Tip:** The Mermaid block above can be hand-drawn in the exam as a flowchart with 5 inputs (C0, COM, CREP, S, d, N) feeding into a "Conversion Engine" that outputs LCC and LCOE.

## 4.2 LCC Computation Pipeline — Step-by-Step Process

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Step 1: Data Collection] --> B[Step 2: Cost Categorization]
    B --> C[Step 3: Discount Rate Selection]
    C --> D[Step 4: Present Value Calculation]
    D --> E[Step 5: Summation of PVs]
    E --> F[Step 6: LCC Computation]
    F --> G[Step 7: Annualization CRF]
    G --> H[Step 8: LCOE Calculation]
    H --> I[Step 9: Decision Matrix]
    I --> J[Final Output: Go or No Go]
```

## 4.3 Simulation Software Functional Architecture Block

This block diagram maps the **internal engine structure** of a typical PV simulation software (e.g., PVsyst, HOMER, SAM). Use this to answer "Explain the architecture of PV simulation software" questions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Inputs] --> B[Input Module]
    B --> B1[Geographic Location lat lon]
    B --> B2[Climate Data TMY]
    B --> B3[System Configuration]
    B --> B4[Module and Inverter Database]
    B1 --> C[Core Simulation Engine]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    B4 --> C
    C --> C1[Irradiance Model]
    C --> C2[PV Array Model]
    C --> C3[Inverter Model]
    C --> C4[Loss Cascade Model]
    C5[Shading Analysis] --> C
    C1 --> D[Output Engine]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D
    C5 --> D
    D --> D1[Hourly Monthly Annual Yield]
    D --> D2[Performance Ratio PR]
    D --> D3[Loss Diagram Sankey]
    D --> D4[Economic Analysis LCC LCOE]
    D1 --> E[Final Reports and Graphs]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
```

## 4.4 Comparative Architecture of Major Simulation Tools

```mermaid
flowchart TB
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define Life Cycle Cost (LCC) of a solar PV system. List any four components of LCC.**

**Model Answer:**

Life Cycle Cost (LCC) is the total discounted monetary cost of a solar PV system over its entire operational life, expressed in present-day (Year 0) value. It captures the time value of money through the discount rate.

The four main components of LCC are:
1. **Initial Capital Cost (C₀):** One-time investment in modules, inverter, BOS, mounting, and installation.
2. **Operation and Maintenance Cost (C_OM):** Recurring annual cost for cleaning, repairs, and insurance.
3. **Replacement Cost (C_REP):** Cost of replacing components (e.g., inverter) that have shorter life than the project.
4. **Salvage Value (S):** Money recovered from recycling or resale of components at end-of-life.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**What is the Levelized Cost of Energy (LCOE)? State its mathematical formula and explain the significance of LCOE in PV system design.**

**Model Answer:**

Levelized Cost of Energy (LCOE) is the **per-unit cost of electricity** generated by a solar PV system over its entire lifetime, expressed in ₹/kWh. It is obtained by dividing the **annualized Life Cycle Cost** by the **annual energy yield**.

$$
LCOE = \frac{LCC \times CRF}{E_{annual}} = \frac{A}{E_{annual}}
$$

**Significance:**
- Allows direct comparison of solar electricity cost with grid tariff.
- If LCOE < grid tariff → system is **economically viable**.
- Used by DISCOMs and banks to evaluate solar project bids and loan viability.
- Independent of project size and lifetime, making it a universal benchmark.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**Q. (a)** Define Life Cycle Cost (LCC) analysis for a solar PV system. Explain any **six (6) components** that constitute the LCC of a 10 kW grid-connected solar PV system, with their typical percentage share. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 10 kW grid-tied solar PV system installed in Kochi has the following data:

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Initial capital cost | ₹6,00,000 |
| Annual O&M cost | ₹8,000 (uniform) |
| Discount rate (d) | 9% per year |
| Project life (N) | 25 years |
| Inverter replacement at year 12 | ₹70,000 |
| Salvage value at year 25 | ₹30,000 |

**Compute: (i) Total LCC, (ii) Annualized LCC, (iii) Simple Payback if annual energy = 15,000 kWh and tariff = ₹7.50/kWh.** **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Components of LCC:**

1. **PV Modules cost** (40–50% of total): Includes panels, mounting hardware, and DC cabling.
2. **Inverter cost** (10–15%): String or central inverter with MPPT and grid synchronization.
3. **Balance of System (BOS)** (15–20%): DC/AC switchgear, junction boxes, earthing, lightning arrestors.
4. **Mounting structure** (10–15%): GI or aluminium structures for rooftop/ground installation.
5. **Civil works & installation labour** (10–15%): Foundation, conduit laying, grid interconnection.
6. **Soft costs** (5%): Design, engineering, permits, net-metering application, commissioning.

O&M is **not a one-time** cost; it is recurring annually and converted to present value using the P/A factor.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute factors for d = 9%, N = 25**

$$
(1.09)^{25} \approx 8.6231
$$

$$
P/A = \frac{8.6231 - 1}{0.09 \times 8.6231} = \frac{7.6231}{0.77608} \approx 9.8226
$$

$$
P/F_{12} = \frac{1}{(1.09)^{12}} = \frac{1}{2.8127} \approx 0.3555
$$

$$
P/F_{25} = \frac{1}{8.6231} \approx 0.1160
$$

**Step 2: Present Worths**

$$
PW_{OM} = 8{,}000 \times 9.8226 = \text{₹}78{,}581
$$

$$
PW_{REP} = 70{,}000 \times 0.3555 = \text{₹}24{,}885
$$

$$
PW_{S} = 30{,}000 \times 0.1160 = \text{₹}3{,}480
$$

**Step 3: (i) Total LCC**

$$
\begin{aligned}
LCC &= 6{,}00{,}000 + 78{,}581 + 24{,}885 - 3{,}480 \\
&= \text{₹}6{,}99{,}986 \approx \text{₹}7{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**(i) Total LCC = ₹6,99,986**

**Step 4: CRF for d = 9%, N = 25**

$$
CRF = \frac{0.09 \times 8.6231}{8.6231 - 1} = \frac{0.77608}{7.6231} \approx 0.1018
$$

**Step 5: (ii) Annualized LCC**

$$
A = 6{,}99{,}986 \times 0.1018 = \text{₹}71{,}253 / \text{year}
$$

**(ii) Annualized LCC = ₹71,253 / year**

**Step 6: (iii) Simple Payback**

Annual savings = 15,000 × 7.50 = ₹1,12,500/year

$$
SPP = \frac{6{,}00{,}000}{1{,}12{,}500} = 5.33 \text{ years}
$$

**(iii) Simple Payback ≈ 5.33 years**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**Q. (a)** What is solar PV simulation software? List and briefly explain **any four (4) popular simulation tools** used for solar PV system design. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **loss cascade model** in PVsyst or any similar simulation software. Identify **at least six (6) system losses** and the typical percentage each contributes to the reduction of the Performance Ratio (PR). **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Simulation Software:**

Solar PV simulation software is a computer program that mathematically models the energy yield, performance, and economics of a PV system using location-specific meteorological data and component specifications.

**Four Popular Tools:**

1. **PVsyst (by PVsyst SA, Switzerland):** Industry gold standard for grid-connected PV. Provides hourly simulation, loss diagram (Sankey), shading analysis using 3D scenes, and detailed economic analysis. Used in bankable reports.
2. **HOMER Pro (by HOMER Energy, USA):** Optimizes hybrid systems (PV + battery + diesel). Performs sensitivity analysis on cost, fuel price, and load. Best for off-grid and microgrid design.
3. **SAM (System Advisor Model) by NREL (USA):** Free, open-source tool for utility-scale and distributed PV. Integrates financial models with energy models. Supports PVSAM, PVWatts, and detailed photovoltaic sub-models.
4. **RETScreen (by Natural Resources Canada):** Free pre-feasibility tool for renewable energy. Excel-based; used for quick go/no-go decisions and energy efficiency analysis. Does not perform detailed hourly shading.

**Part (b) — Loss Cascade Model:**

The loss cascade is a **Sankey-style diagram** that traces the flow of energy from the available solar resource (POA irradiance) to the final AC output at the grid connection point. Each "loss" is a percentage reduction in the total energy.

**Six System Losses:**

1. **Soiling loss (2–5%):** Dust, bird droppings, and pollution on module surface. Higher in industrial and arid areas.
2. **Module IAM (Incidence Angle Modifier) loss (1–3%):** Reflection losses when sun is at low angles (morning/evening).
3. **Thermal loss (8–12%):** Modules lose efficiency at high operating temperatures. Critical in Kerala due to tropical climate (modules reach 60–70°C).
4. **Module mismatch loss (1–2%):** Slight variation in I-V characteristics between modules in a string.
5. **DC and AC wiring loss (2–3%):** Resistive losses in cables; depends on cable length and cross-section.
6. **Inverter loss (4–6%):** Conversion efficiency of inverter (typically 96–98%); includes standby and night-time consumption.

**Cumulative PR = 0.75 to 0.82** for well-designed systems; lower for poorly designed or maintained systems.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in LCC Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing "n" and "N"**: Use *n* for the specific year of a one-time event (replacement at year 12), and *N* for the total project life. Mixing them gives a wrong factor and 7 marks lost.
> 2. **Forgetting to subtract salvage value**: Students often *add* salvage instead of *subtracting* it. Salvage is a NEGATIVE cost (money recovered). LCC formula has a MINUS sign.
> 3. **Using simple payback when discounted payback is asked**: If the question says "discounted payback," you must discount each year's cash flow. Simple payback = ₹C₀ / annual savings.
> 4. **Wrong units in LCOE**: LCOE is always in **₹/kWh**, not ₹/year. Dividing annualized cost by kWh gives ₹/kWh.
> 5. **Ignoring inverter replacement**: Forgetting this single line can change LCC by 5–10% and is a guaranteed mark deduction.
> 6. **Not mentioning the discount rate rationale**: Always write 1–2 lines justifying why 8% or 10% is used (typical WACC for solar projects in India).
> 7. **Mixing LCC and LCOE formulas**: LCC is in ₹ (present value), LCOE is in ₹/kWh. They are NOT the same; one is converted to the other using CRF and energy yield.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### 🔑 Rapid Revision Checklist

- **LCC Definition:** Total discounted cost over project life, expressed in present value (₹).
- **LCC Formula:** $LCC = C_0 + C_{OM}(P/A) + C_{REP}(P/F)_n - S(P/F)_N$
- **CRF Formula:** $CRF = \dfrac{d(1+d)^N}{(1+d)^N - 1}$
- **LCOE Formula:** $LCOE = \dfrac{LCC \times CRF}{E_{annual}}$
- **Simple Payback:** $SPP = \dfrac{C_0}{E \times Tariff}$
- **Project life (N):** 25 years for crystalline Si PV
- **Discount rate (d):** 8–12% typical for Indian solar
- **Inverter replacement:** Once at year 12–15
- **Performance Ratio (PR):** 0.75 to 0.82 (Kerala: ~0.78)
- **Specific yield in Kerala:** 1,650 to 1,850 kWh/kWp/year
- **Salvage value:** 5–10% of initial cost (subtracted, not added)
- **P/A factor table values** must be memorized for d = 8%, 9%, 10%, 12% and N = 20, 25, 30.
- **Simulation software mandate:** PVsyst (detailed), HOMER (hybrid), SAM (utility), RETScreen (pre-feasibility).
- **Loss cascade order:** Soiling → IAM → Thermal → Mismatch → Wiring → Inverter → Transformer.
- **LCC vs Payback:** LCC includes time value of money; simple payback does not. KTU 2024 prefers LCC.

### ⚠️ Critical Don'ts

- ❌ Don't use absolute value bars `|x|` in markdown tables — use `\vert` or `\mid`.
- ❌ Don't mix discount rate with inflation rate — they are separate concepts.
- ❌ Don't add salvage to LCC — always SUBTRACT.
- ❌ Don't forget units: LCC in ₹, LCOE in ₹/kWh, Energy in kWh, Yield in kWh/kWp.
- ❌ Don't recommend a system without computing LCOE vs grid tariff comparison.

### ✅ High-Yield Exam Tips

- Memorize P/A values for d = 10%, N = 25 → **9.077**.
- Memorize CRF for d = 10%, N = 25 → **0.1102**.
- For d = 8%, N = 25 → P/A ≈ 10.675, CRF ≈ 0.0937.
- Always show **factor calculation steps** — partial marking requires it.
- Draw the **LCC block diagram** in long-answer questions for **2 extra marks** (presentation marks).
- Mention at least **2 simulation software names** with their **specific use case** to score full marks in the software question.
